title: Resilience & Scaling Lab Brief — All Hands
preview: Leadership update covering recent incidents, scaling experiments, hack-week learnings, hiring posture, and a set of operational runbooks to stabilize latency and reduce cost. Q&A focused on Dedicated vs Private tradeoffs and next-phase reliability milestones.

title: POC weekly: access recon & environment readiness standup
preview: Weekly POC sync focused on finalizing access for staging, clarifying SSO/KMS ownership, and validating initial latency benchmarks. Action items assigned for security docs, CIDR details, and a short KV-cache benchmark run. Agreement to run a focused smoke test next Wednesday and share results with the wider infra team.

title: NorthForge Console: per-route usage + cost playbook
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-06-30
Start: 15:00 PDT
Duration: 48 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Price (Redwood AE), Samir Patel (Redwood SE), Ava Chen (Redwood CSM), Lina Torres (NorthForge CTO), Ravi Singh (NorthForge Infra Lead), Maya Ortiz (NorthForge PM)
---
Summary: quick console walkthrough focused on route-level dashboards, per-route cost breakdown, and a playbook for routing strategies (hosted vs dedicated vs private). Customer concerned about multi-tenant data residency, encryption at rest, and keeping per-feature unit economics low.
Topics: console navigation, route costing, batching and cache settings, private deployment considerations, next steps for pilot.
Action items: see metadata above.
---
Transcript:
[00:00:05] Jordan (AE): Hey everyone thanks for joining, uh we wanted to keep this to like 30-ish minutes plus Q&A, demo the console route usage view and then talk through a cost playbook. Samir's going to drive the demo.
[00:00:18] Lina: Thanks Jordan, we have 3 of us on from infra and product. We're mainly evaluating how Redwood helps us contain costs for conversational features in our app. We run a mixture of synchronous chat and background summarization.
[00:00:28] Ravi: Also important for us, some routes will touch PII, so VPC and KMS integration is a must, and we need to show it to compliance.
[00:00:35] Samir (SE): Absolutely. I'll jump into the console and show per-route dashboards. Quick note: apologies if the model names look a little off, our transcription sometimes shows Redwood Optimise as Opti-mize, et cetera.
[00:00:48] [00:00:48] Screen share starts - Console home
[00:01:05] Samir: Alright, top-left you can see 'Routes' - this is where teams map endpoint keys from your app to routes in Redwood. Each route gets its own telemetry, cost slice, and quality metrics.
[00:01:16] Maya: How granular is a route? Can we do per-feature, per-tenant?
[00:01:21] Samir: Yes. You can tag routes however you want — per-feature, per-customer, or combination tags. Then the console aggregates by tag for rollups.
[00:01:30] [00:01:30] Samir navigates to route list -> selects 'chat-stream' route
[00:01:34] Samir: Here's the 'chat-stream' route. Top panel shows RPS, p50/p95 latency, and token throughput. Below is a cost breakdown showing per-token cost, model mix, and an inferred cost-per-call metric.
[00:01:48] Lina: That inferred cost-per-call, is that including network egress and infra markup for dedicated?
[00:01:53] Samir: Good question. The default view is just inference unit economics: model cost + hosting markup if on Dedicated. We have a toggle to include infra overhead (GPU ops, egress) and also to apply your internal chargeback rates.
[00:02:06] Jordan: And we can overlay billing invoices so finance gets the same numbers.
[00:02:10] Ravi: When you say 'hosting markup', what are the knobs — can we see exact GPU hours used per route?
[00:02:16] Samir: Yes — we surface GPU-hours and percent utilization per pool and attribute it back to routes by sampling. It's not perfect down to the byte, but for cost allocation it tends to be within a small variance.
[00:02:28] [00:02:28] Samir clicks 'Cost drivers'
[00:02:31] Samir: Here's the 'cost drivers' pane — it's automatically inferring driver types: model size, token mix (long tails), and repeated completion calls that could be batched or cached. It also flags high-KV-cache miss rates — excuse me — KV cash miss rates in the transcript here, won't do that — KV cache miss rates.
[00:02:50] Maya: Can we see historical trend by rollout? Like if we do a canary, can we compare the canary route vs baseline?
[00:02:55] Samir: Yep, that's a first class view. If you use our rollouts feature you get canary vs baseline metrics and automatic regression alerts for both latency and cost per call.
[00:03:07] Jordan: Quick qualification: what's your expected qps for chat?
[00:03:11] Lina: Peak 60 qps, average 12 qps, but it spikes in events. We also have a background summarizer that runs many small batched requests.
[00:03:22] Samir: With those patterns we typically suggest mixed routing: hosted for low-latency public routes, Dedicated for steady high-throughput routes, and Private for PII-sensitive ones. You can pin model versions per route too.
[00:03:35] [00:03:35] Samir demonstrates 'Route tuning recommendations' panel
[00:03:37] Samir: This pane shows automated suggestions — batching window, cache TTL, and model quantization recommendations based on observed latencies and token distributions. For your summarizer we'd recommend increasing batch window from 20ms to 120ms to trade off a little latency for 3x token throughput.
[00:03:53] Ravi: Any quality regression risk when you quantize?
[00:03:56] Samir: We include an eval harness tied to your prompt sets so you can run A/B comparisons. The console will show Rouge/BLEU-like deltas and a human review queue for anything flagged.
[00:04:07] Lina: On compliance — does Redwood keep audit logs for admin actions and model rollouts? We need that for SOC2.
[00:04:13] Samir: Yes, we store audit logs, and Private mode can forward logs to your SIEM. We also do SSO/SAML, RBAC, and integrate with KMS for envelope encryption. For on-prem deployments we support HSM hooks but that requires an enterprise config.
[00:04:29] [00:04:29] Jordan: Totally — we'll include security artifacts in the follow-up.
[00:04:34] Maya: Pricing — we already use OpenAI for some features. How does Redwood pricing compare?
[00:04:40] Jordan: Redwood's hosted API is usage-based with per-token rates; Dedicated is a reserved capacity model with monthly commits, and Private is licensed. For a mixed workload we typically model blended cost — we'll send a route-level cost playbook that shows how you can reduce unit cost via batching and caching.
[00:04:56] [00:04:56] Samir pulls up a sample 'Playbook' document in console attachments
[00:04:59] Samir: Here's a sample playbook: step 1 identify heavy-token routes; step 2 set batching heuristics; step 3 apply prefix caching for repeated prompts and pin cheaper quantized models for non-sensitive traffic; step 4 measure quality with prompt-sets.
[00:05:16] Ravi: For PII routes we need assurances that model weights won't leak data, and that logs can be redacted — can you redact before sending to hosted?
[00:05:24] Samir: For hosted we offer optional field-level redaction and allow customers to opt into 'no retention' mode. For full control set up Private — then logs stay in your VPC.
[00:05:34] Lina: How hard is private deployment? We have an air-gapped environment for some customers.
[00:05:39] Samir: Private has an installer and an ops guide. Typical on-prem engagements are 6-8 weeks for regulated setups, shorter if you're using our VPC model in cloud. We'll scope this in the technical deep dive.
[00:05:51] [00:05:51] Jordan: Quick note — competitors often advertise lower token costs but then lack per-route visibility and rollout controls; that can cause hidden operational costs.
[00:06:02] Lina: Any customers with similar scale or compliance needs?
[00:06:06] Jordan: Yes — we have customers in fintech and health who run private control planes. I'll share a case study.
[00:06:12] Maya: If we wanted to pilot, what's the fastest path?
[00:06:16] Samir: We can spin up a trial org, ingest a weeks' worth of logs, run the playbook suggestions over a 2-week benchmarking window, and show projected savings. Typically that's 2-4 weeks to meaningful insights.
[00:06:30] [00:06:30] Samir opens 'Route cost breakdown export'
[00:06:34] Samir: You can export per-route CSVs, or connect our billing API to your internal FinOps tools. The CSV includes model mix, tokens, GPU hours attributed, and effective cost per call.
[00:06:45] Ravi: About attribution — you said you sample GPU hours and attribute back. Can you do exact attribution if we dedicate a pool per tenant?
[00:06:53] Samir: If you dedicate a pool per tenant attribution is exact. Sampling is only for shared pools. Dedicated + per-tenant pools is the cleanest for billing.
[00:07:03] Lina: We're also interested in routing rules — can we route by latency requirement and cost, like low-latency to smaller models, cost-heavy to quantized?
[00:07:12] Samir: Yes — smart routing policies support rules by latency SLO, cost threshold, and region. You can chain fallbacks: primary model -> cheaper quantized model -> short-circuit summary.
[00:07:24] [00:07:24] [Interruption as another meeting attendee joins briefly, faint audio]
[00:07:40] Maya: One more question — how does streaming billing work? We sometimes stream partial tokens for long responses.
[00:07:46] Samir: Billing is by token emitted. For streaming partial responses it's still token-based; the console shows per-stream durations to help with latency chargebacks.
[00:07:58] Jordan: Let's go over a hypothetical: your summarizer currently does 1,200 calls/min with average 900 tokens. If we batch and quantize you could cut token cost 40% and reduce GPU-hours by half.
[00:08:14] Lina: That would be huge. Do you have sample math in the playbook?
[00:08:18] Samir: Yes, there's sample spreadsheets. We'll populate them with your real usage after the trial.
[00:08:25] [00:08:25] Samir navigates to 'Alerts & regressions'
[00:08:27] Samir: Alerts can be set on cost-per-call, token burn rate, or model quality metrics. They can post to Slack, PagerDuty, or create Jira tickets.
[00:08:36] Ravi: Is there an API to adjust route config programmatically? We want to automate canaries.
[00:08:41] Samir: Yes — full REST API and SDKs. You can set rollout policies, change batching windows, or pin model versions in CI.
[00:08:49] Maya: For the eval harness, do you support human-in-the-loop scoring?
[00:08:53] Samir: We do — there's a human review queue and a way to tie labeled feedback back into model selection logic.
[00:08:59] [00:08:59] Jordan: Ok let's pivot — Lina, any blockers from procurement or security that would stop a pilot?
[00:09:06] Lina: Procurement needs a basic T&Cs review and a data processing addendum. Security wants SOC2 + encryption + KMS proof.
[00:09:14] Jordan: We'll attach our SOC2 report and the KMS integration doc. Samir will scope the Private checklist.
[00:09:20] [00:09:20] Quick live Q&A, a bit of crosstalk
[00:09:35] Ravi: One nuance — our live chat has sub-second SLOs. How often do you see Dedicated meeting sub-second for 75th percentile?
[00:09:44] Samir: For small models on dedicated pools we often hit sub-250ms p75 for simple turn-by-turn prompts. For longer contexts it's tougher. We can show latency SLO demos in the technical deep dive.
[00:09:58] [00:09:58] Samir shows 'Model profiling' panel
[00:10:02] Samir: Profiling shows tokens by layer — you can see where attention cost spikes and decide to trim context or use chunked summaries.
[00:10:11] Lina: Any known integrations with our stack — we use HashiCorp Vault and Okta.
[00:10:16] Samir: Okta SSO is supported and Vault for secrets/KMS is an allowed integration. For Private we can also do HSM plugs.
[00:10:24] Maya: How does rate-limiting interact with batching? If we throttle requests will batching increase latency drastically?
[00:10:30] Samir: Batching is adaptive — it respects per-API-key rate limits. If you throttle aggressively batching might wait longer, so we tune windows per-route. It's in the playbook.
[00:10:42] [00:10:42] Jordan: We're approaching 20 minutes — want to do a quick recap and next steps.
[00:10:48] Lina: Sure. From our side: we want pilot for summarizer + chat-stream, confirm compliance docs, and estimate cost delta vs OpenAI for same workload.
[00:10:58] Jordan: Great. We'll do: 1) send pricing + playbook, 2) Samir spin up trial org and load sample logs, 3) schedule SE deep dive for private deployment and latency SLOs.
[00:11:09] Maya: Can you include sample CSV export showing per-route GPU-hours?
[00:11:12] Samir: Yes — I'll attach an example export.
[00:11:16] Ravi: One more action: Lina will list routes that must stay private.
[00:11:20] Lina: Yep will do by end of week.
[00:11:25] Jordan: Perfect thanks everyone, we'll follow up via email with links.
[00:11:30] [End of meeting audio capture] 
---
Notes on transcription quirks: occasional speaker mislabels (some lines show 'Jordan' when actually Samir spoke), product names sometimes hyphenated (Redwood Opti-mize), technical terms like KV cache are occasionally transcribed as 'KV cash'. These are present in the transcript to preserve authentic Fireflies output.
---
Attachments referenced: console-playbook-sample.pdf (link in CRM), sample-route-export.csv (link), security/SOC2-redacted.pdf (link)

title: Deploy guardrail simulation + stakeholder handoff
preview:
Meeting header
Date: 2026-06-23
Start: 14:00 UTC
Duration: ~75m
Location: Zoom (internal)
Attendees: Priya (PM), Owen (Eng Mgr), Diego (Infra SE), Ava (EngOps), Sam (SRE), Taylor (QA), Marta (Prod Ops), Luis (Telemetry), Zoe (Release), Nikhil (Security), Maya (VP Product), Kristin (CTO), Ben (Finance), Leo (CS)

Summary: Quick tabletop-style simulation of a deploy that triggers staged degradation; align on guardrails, triggers, telemetry, and exec handoff. Focus on rollback thresholds and who owns user-visible comms.

Topics: runbook, canary sizing, staged degradation tiers, KV cache hotstart, model fallback variants, security/KMS rotations, telemetry dashboards, finance risk for reserved capacity.

[00:00] Priya: okay, uh, hi everyone thanks for joining, we've got a tight agenda — walk a short tabletop of the deploy that we've been planning, um, simulate a degradation where latency climbs and kv cache misses spike and then talk owner handoffs to execs.
[00:22] Maya (VP Product): thanks, Priya. My ask is basically can we show, in one slide, what happens to latency and cost if we fall back from a larger model to the quantized variant and then to the smaller family. Execs wanna know visible impact.
[00:36] Owen: yup, we'll show two curves — token latency and p95 — and a triage path. Quick note, our fallback path has three tiers: same-arch quantized, smaller-arch v2, and last resort hosted-safe mode with lower throughput.
[00:51] Diego: and just to flag, the KV cache warm-up will be the noisy bit. If the prefix cache cold-starts, you'll see a token cost bump and p95 spike, that's our likely first signal.
[01:12] Ava: what are we calling the signal? Is it cache miss ratio or user-facing 95th? We need something with low false positives.
[01:25] Sam: i'd prefer a combined signal: p95 > 500ms for three minutes AND cache miss ratio > 20% over same window. We can tune thresholds later but that's a start.
[01:40] Taylor: QA will need to run degraded-mode tests that target those thresholds; we'll generate synthetic traffic that simulates cold prefixes.
[01:52] Priya: good. ok simulate step one — a spike at t+10m post-deploy where latency rises, flow?
[02:03] Zoe: Release note — during canary we'll only route 5% of traffic for 30m, so we expect to catch issues early if thresholds fire. But the canary config will be on the same infra pool as dedicated workloads, so we need clear isolation rules.
[02:21] Kristin (CTO): isolation is essential, we can't risk noisy neighbor cross-talk. Diego, what's the plan for hard isolation?
[02:31] Diego: for this canary we put a quota of GPU slices and set run-to-completion queues with eviction. If it overruns we'll move canary traffic to a separate pool and fallback to the hosted-safe path. The eviction is aggressive but necessary.
[02:51] Marta: who owns the decision to move canary traffic? Is that automated or manual? Execs want a brief on that.
[03:02] Owen: automation with human guardrails — we'll auto-degrade to fallback variant but only auto-roll back after human acknowledgements for production. For canary we can automate rollback.
[03:15] Ben (Finance): quick ask — what's the cost delta if we auto-degrade vs rollback? Finance needs a sense of committed spend risk.
[03:27] Luis: i'll publish a dashboard showing token cost per model family and delta per minute. Short story: auto-degrade increases unit cost for smaller model? actually reduces cost per token but might increase latency — the cost impact depends on routing.
[03:44] Priya: let's step through the simulated timeline. T0 deploy, t+5m canary sees first anomalies, t+10m threshold triggers, t+15m staged fallback kicks in, t+20m exec notif.
[03:58] [04:00] Priya: we should roleplay the comms — who calls out to clients? Leo?
[04:06] Leo (CS): we'll draft a customer-facing note but for internal exec notif, Marta will send the one-pager; CS will prepare an operational blurb.
[04:18] Marta: confirmed. We'll use status.redwood.internal and tag impacted customers if their SLOs are affected.
[04:29] Nikhil (Security): KMS note — if we switch to hosted-safe we also need to ensure any customer-managed keys remain unavailable; double-check with legal for exceptions.
[04:40] Sam: noted. Also, the KV cache uses ephemeral encryption but the telemetry bucket is encrypted with the HSM. We're okay but rotation windows might cause small blips — avoid rotation during canary.
[04:55] Priya: action: Nikhil — confirm rotation blackout window for canary.
[04:59] Nikhil: will do, target by next Monday.
[05:05] [05:30] Taylor: outline of QA degraded-mode tests — we'll run three scenarios: cold-prefix heavy, high-concurrency short-bursts, and model-fallback correctness. The correctness pass must assert no hallucination regressions beyond the agreed threshold.
[05:50] Owen: define hallucination threshold again? We used delta-LM perplexity last time but execs ask for a simpler quality metric.
[06:03] Priya: pragmatic metric — response divergence score against golden prompts; QA to provide percent of prompts that exceed tolerance. We'll accept up to 5% degradation for fallback variants during canary for non-critical flows.
[06:18] Taylor: noted. We'll include that in test plan.
[06:25] [06:45] Diego: technical caveat — when we switch model families there's a KV cache mismatch. We either purge prefix-specific cache or map keys, both have tradeoffs. Purging causes immediate cold starts but mapping is brittle.
[07:01] Ava: is there a hybrid? Maybe warm a small sample of prefixes into the new cache after we route traffic but before full switch.
[07:11] Diego: yeah, a warm-up window with simulated requests could reduce p95 spike. We'll script a prefix mellowing job.
[07:22] Owen: that means release team needs to kick-off a warm-up job as part of the canary pipeline. Zoe you'll own that step in the runbook.
[07:33] Zoe: got it — I will add a pre-switch warm-up step.
[07:38] [07:50] Priya: what about observability? Luis, do we have a dashboard that shows the three-tier fallback and direct users to rollback signal?
[07:59] Luis: i'm putting together a single-page dashboard: p95, kv-miss, cost/min, model-version mix, and an 'alert severity' field. I can add an explicit 'rollback suggested' boolean based on threshold logic.
[08:13] Priya: perfect. We'll use that as the canonical source for execs.
[08:18] [08:30] Ben: will reserved capacity exposure be material if canary fails and we need to burst? Finance wants to know if we'll exceed our reserved GPU commit.
[08:38] Owen: we have burst pools but they cost more; we'll auto-switch to hosted-safe only if reserve is low — that's a routing policy. Priya, we'll include policies in the slide.
[08:50] [09:00] Priya: ok i'd like to simulate the failure path now — assume the warm-up didn't reduce miss ratio and p95 stayed above 650ms for 10 minutes. What do we do?
[09:12] Sam: at that point we escalate to Stage 2: fallback to quantized variant across canary and pause further rollout. We notify execs and open incident channel. If metrics don't recover in 15m, rollback.
[09:26] Taylor: QA needs to capture the state pre-fallback so we can triage whether regression is model-quality or infra. We'll snapshot logs and the last 100 requests.
[09:38] [09:55] Zoe: rollback mechanics — we have two rollback types: config rollback (traffic routing back to old model) and infra rollback (re-provision previous pool). We'll try config rollback first since it's fastest.
[10:09] Owen: yes, config rollback should be under 3 minutes if the control plane is healthy; if control plane degraded we may need manual infra steps.
[10:19] Nikhil: security note — ensure any rollback doesn't leak private keys; double check that old and new variants adhere to the same key access pattern.
[10:30] [10:45] Priya: good. Now stakeholder handoff: what does execs want in the post-mortem if we do rollback? Maya?
[10:52] Maya: short bullet list — cause, impact, customer count, duration, and next mitigation steps. Keep it under one page. Also, highlight mitigation budget impacts if we had to burst.
[11:05] Ben: finance will need cost impact in dollars for the incident window and forecast change. Luis, coordinate with me on the data feed.
[11:14] Luis: will do. I'll add a 'cost-per-minute' timeseries for the incident window.
[11:21] [11:35] Marta: who publishes the post-mortem? Is it Product or Incident Lead?
[11:41] Owen: incident lead (Sam) should own the post-mortem writeup with PM and Prod Ops sign-off.
[11:48] Sam: agree, I'll set the template and Priya will sign off.
[11:54] [12:05] Priya: okay let's run a very short roleplay: Sam you'll open the channel after the stage-1 trigger; Marta you'll prepare the one-pager, Zoe you run the warm-up pre-switch and release steps, Nikhil confirm KMS blackout. Taylor run the degraded tests, Luis prep dashboard.
[12:18] Sam: opening incident channel... (simulated) ok done.
[12:23] Marta: drafting the one-pager now (simulated).
[12:28] Taylor: starting degraded mode tests (simulated).
[12:34] [12:45] Diego: quick note, we should add a grace metric for longer-running requests — some customer flows are batch-ish and spike p95 legitimately. We need to annotate those routes so they don't trigger false positives.
[12:58] Priya: good point. Action for Zoe + Diego to annotate critical routes and ensure threshold exceptions.
[13:06] Zoe: will add route annotations to the canary config.
[13:10] [13:20] Owen: one more infrastructure caveat — the quantized variant needs different kernel selection at deploy-time; if we don't pre-warm kernels we'll hit slow first calls. Diego, can you ensure kernel boot is part of warm-up?
[13:33] Diego: yes we'll include a kernel handshake step.
[13:37] [13:50] Priya: ok nearing end — recap of owners and deliverables, and the slide for execs. Priya: i'll draft the slide; Owen and Luis will add data slices; Marta will schedule handoff meeting with execs after we validate dashboard.
[14:05] Maya: agree, prefer the handoff to happen only after telemetry is live.
[14:12] Ben: finance sitting on the sideline until we see the reserve exposure numbers.
[14:19] Priya: final wrap, any last comments?
[14:24] Nikhil: just a thumbs up, i'll confirm KMS window by Monday.
[14:29] Leo: customer comms template will be ready once we have the one-pager.
[14:33] [14:40] Priya: thanks everyone, i'll follow up with notes and ping on backlog. Meeting adjourned.

Post-meeting notes:
- Decision model: three-stage fallback, auto-degrade -> canary rollback -> manual production rollback.
- Primary signals: p95, kv-cache-miss%, model mix shift, cost/min.
- Exec one-pager and single-page dashboard agreed as canonical artifacts.
- QA will run degraded scenarios and provide a quality delta metric vs golden prompts.

References/links (to be shared):
- Runbook draft (link placeholder): https://internal.redwood/runbooks/deploy-guardrail-draft
- Telemetry dashboard (placeholder): https://internal.redwood/dashboards/deploy-guardrail
- QA degraded test plan (placeholder): https://internal.redwood/qa/degraded-tests
- Postmortem template: https://internal.redwood/incident/template

title: 2026-06-10-sprint5-pilot-stabilization-and-decision-tollgate
preview: Sprint 5 check-in to review KPI progress, surface remaining stability issues, and formalize the go/no-go decision process. Main concerns: tail-latency spikes during burst tests, KV cache warm-up time, and SSO/KMS validation. Team agreed on an A/B canary starting with 5% traffic and gating on 99th percentile latency, error rate, and token-cost budget. Action owners assigned and a decision meeting scheduled.

title: POC Weekly: Access Orchestration & Environment Readiness
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-05-29
Start: 15:00 PDT
Duration: ~47 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Marco Rivera (Redwood SE), Lisa Patel (BlueNova EngMgr), Dmitri Kozlov (BlueNova SRE), Kenji Sato (BlueNova Security)

Summary:
Quick weekly POC sync focussed on access gating, VPC peering readiness, initial latency bench outcomes, and a rehearse of fallback/canary behavior. Agreed on next steps for SSO metadata, peering test IPs, and two bench runs. Security wants a short deep-dive next week.

Topics: Access provisioning, SSO/SAML, VPC peering, KV cache warmup, latency bench, fallback/canary rehearsal, compliance docs (SOC2), KMS/HSM notes.

Transcript:
[00:00] Ava (Redwood): All right everyone, thanks for joining. I think we have about 45 minutes. Quick roll call?
[00:08] Lisa: Lisa here.
[00:10] Dmitri: Dmitri present.
[00:12] Kenji: Kenji on the line.
[00:15] Marco: Marco from Redwood, hi all.
[00:18] Ava: Cool. Agenda — access status, bench recap, and then run the fallback/canary plan end-to-end. Does that work?
[00:26] Lisa: Yep that's good, maybe start with access because we're blocked on SSO metadata.
[00:30] Ava: Sure. I shared a checklist yesterday but I'll go over the critical bits. We need a SAML metadata file, SP entity ID we provided in the doc, and mapping for the Redwood console admin role.
[00:41] Kenji: Quick Q — is Redwood supporting IdP-initiated login or only SP? For compliance we prefer SP-initiated but need both if possible.
[00:50] Marco: We support both patterns; historically most customers use IdP-initiated for enterprise SSO. There are two small caveats: the console SSO flows require Metadata with AssertionConsumerService URL matching exactly, and you need to set NameID format to transient or email, depending on your SAML.
[01:05] Dmitri: We're seeing some attr mapping oddities in our IDP. Can you send sample attribute mappings?
[01:12] Ava: Yes I'll attach a sample mapping and a minimal metadata we recommend. I'll also put in the role mapping for admin/read-only.
[01:20] Lisa: That would help. Also, how does role provisioning tie into GovCloud or private deployment? We may need RBAC constraints.
[01:30] Marco: Role mapping is local to the Redwood Console. For Private deployments we can integrate with your SCIM if you want automated group sync; otherwise manual mapping is fine for the POC.
[01:40] Kenji: Does SCIM sync require additional network egress? We are strict about outbound endpoints.
[01:46] Marco: It's HTTPS outbound to the control plane. If you run Redwood Private in VPC, you can lock it down to your egress path, and keys are customer-managed via KMS/HSM options. There may be an extra NAT or proxy rule depending on your setup.
[01:58] Dmitri: OK please document the IPs we need to allow.
[02:03] Ava: We'll add that to the doc.

[02:18] Ava: Next up — VPC peering and networking. Dmitri, where are we?
[02:22] Dmitri: We've reserved the peering CIDR 10.40.0.0/16 for the staging cluster. We ran a quick route check, egress to Redwood control plane over NAT is fine, but the peering handshake hasn't completed — side A still shows pending.
[02:38] Marco: Pending usually means console approval or missing route propagation. Did you advertise the route table to the peering attachment?
[02:44] Dmitri: We did, but there was a mismatch in the prefix list. Also, MTU dropped packets during an arp test earlier, so we want to confirm path MTU and fragmented UDP is not in the critical path for telemetry.
[02:58] Marco: For the model traffic, it's TCP inter-host and uses gRPC over TLS — so MTU is less likely to hit you unless you have weird intermediary fragmentation. Still, good to confirm. We'll share a small connectivity-test script that does an HTTP handshake to the inference gateway and a throughput test.
[03:16] Ava: Action: Marco will send connectivity script. Dmitri will run and paste logs.

[03:30] Lisa: Bench results — we ran one run yesterday using the sample prompt set (10k tokens total across sessions) and got median p50 latency around 210ms, p95 about 890ms.
[03:43] Marco: Can you confirm the concurrency and batch size used?
[03:46] Lisa: We used 20 concurrent sessions with default batch config.
[03:51] Marco: Okay that explains it. In our infra we've seen better p95 with increased batching thresholds but that can raise p50 slightly. For POC you want to tune for your SLO — do you care more about p50 or p95?
[04:03] Lisa: p95 under 700ms would be ideal for our UX. We're okay if p50 moves to ~250ms.
[04:11] Marco: Then we recommend toggling the batch_max_tokens and target_latency_ms in the provided config. Also warm up KV cache to avoid cold-start tokenization/regrowth.
[04:22] Dmitri: How do we warm the KV cache? Do you have a warmup script or should we run synthetic sessions?
[04:29] Marco: We'll attach a warmup script that sequentially primes the common prefixes and performs small confirmatory gets to populate KV. It's idempotent. I'll drop it in the shared folder.
[04:40] Ava: Another note — in the bench logs we saw a few 5xx spikes tied to a model fallback. Can you describe Redwood's fallback policies?
[04:51] Marco: Sure — we have routing policies that prefer the primary model, and if inference fails we can fall back to a slightly smaller model variant or route to another region depending on your policy. For the POC we recommended 'soft-fallback' where we log & incrementally fallback after N failures per minute. That avoids instant user interruptions.
[05:08] Lisa: That might explain the spikes. We saw a repeated failure window at ~14:02 where a node crashed and fallbacks kicked in.
[05:16] Marco: We'll correlate that time with our telemetry. Please include timestamps in your bench logs (you did, right?).
[05:21] Lisa: Yes, we included timestamps.

[05:35] Kenji: From security perspective — what data is temporarily cached for KV? Is user data ever written to persistent logs? We need to understand retention and whether PII touches logs.
[05:47] Marco: Good question. For POC, generative outputs are not persisted beyond telemetry unless you enable retention. We redact user-specified PII fields by default in telemetry; we can enable stricter scrubbing for BlueNova. Persistent logging is configurable and by default limited to operational traces — payloads are not stored. We'll provide the telemetry retention policy doc and the telemetry scrubbing options.
[06:05] Kenji: And key management — are keys in your KMS or ours? For Private, we want customer-managed KMS and HSM.
[06:13] Marco: Yes, for Private deployments we integrate with customer KMS/HSM. Hosted mode uses Redwood-managed keys; Dedicated can be hybrid. We'll enumerate options in the security pack we share.

[06:30] Ava: Ok moving on — fallback rehearsal. We want to do a canary failover test on staging next Friday. The plan is to simulate an unhealthy node and observe the fallback to a smaller model and routing update.
[06:43] Dmitri: What's the expected duration of the failover and what's the rollback?
[06:47] Marco: Typical reroute is under 2s for control plane routing updates, but model spin-up could be 5–30s depending on the model and whether the target is already warm. That's why we recommend keeping a warm standby or quick-quant variant.
[07:00] Lisa: If it takes 30s users will notice. Can we pre-warm a smaller quantized model so fallback is near-instant?
[07:06] Marco: Yes that's the strategy. Quantized LLMs have much faster cold starts. We can pre-load a 4-bit quant variant and keep it in memory. Tradeoff is memory footprint vs cold start latency.
[07:16] Kenji: For compliance we need the fallback logs and who triggered failover. Can telemetry tag with the experiment ID and user session?
[07:24] Marco: Yes we include request-level metadata — session id, experiment id, route decision. We'll ensure audit logs include the reason and model variant.

[07:40] Ava: Let's talk timelines — Ben in infra asked when we could start the two bench runs. Is next Tue/Wed possible?
[07:48] Dmitri: Next Tue is fine, Wednesday is tight. We'll aim for Tue at 10am PT.
[07:53] Lisa: Tue 10am PT works for the team.
[07:56] Ava: Great. Marco will be on the run to help interpret.

[08:12] Marco: One note on the bench config — please export full tracing (span-level) for the two runs so we can map spikes to kernel or model stages. That helps us decide if it was batching, kernel selection, or GC on the node.
[08:25] Dmitri: We can do that but it's verbose. Is there a sample config?
[08:29] Marco: Yes. I'll attach a minimal tracing config that captures model runtime, tokenization, network latencies but keeps payloads redacted.

[08:44] Lisa: Any blockers on credentials? Our devs still don't have the service account to call the staging endpoint.
[08:51] Ava: We provisioned a service key yesterday but it's scoped to console only — we need to create a service account credential for the inference API. Marco, did we finish that?
[08:59] Marco: I created an API key with restricted scopes but it looks like the customer side needs to whitelist the API key origin or set up the token exchange. Dmitri?
[09:06] Dmitri: We haven't whitelisted it yet — what's the origin and expected header?
[09:11] Marco: Standard bearer header. For origin we usually don't set a CORS origin for server-to-server; you need to allow outbound to the inference gateway IPs and the NAT egress. We'll send exact IPs.
[09:21] Ava: Action: Ava to resend the API key instructions and scope. Dmitri to whitelist egress for the API key.

[09:36] Kenji: A quick ask — can we get the SOC2 section that lists what telemetry is collected and retention times? We need it before the executive review.
[09:44] Ava: Yes, we'll share the SOC2 summary and scope doc. I'll include the redaction notes.

[10:00] Dmitri: One more infra nit — during the first bench run we saw packets dropped on node 3, traced to an MTU mismatch on the peering. We can fix by tuning the MTU or enabling fragmentation, but which is recommended?
[10:11] Marco: If you can set uniform MTU across the path that's ideal. If not, enable PMTU discovery and confirm your firewall doesn't drop ICMP. We'll note it in the connectivity script to assert PMTU.
[10:22] Dmitri: We'll follow that.

[10:35] Ava: Okay, that wraps the technical portion. Recap of next steps: SSO metadata and role mapping from Ava, connectivity script from Marco, VPC peering confirm from Dmitri, two bench runs Tue 10am with Marco attending, security deep-dive scheduling.
[10:55] Lisa: Sounds good. Also, can you share a short README for our on-call so they know the failover signal?
[11:03] Marco: Yes, the runbook will include the failover signal and rollback steps.

[11:20] Kenji: Thanks all. One last item — for the security deep-dive, prioritize KMS/HSM questions and audit logs.
[11:28] Ava: Noted. We'll book a 30–45 minute slot next week.

[11:40] Marco: I'll drop the warmup script and bench/tracing configs into the shared folder after the call.
[11:47] Ava: Great, thanks everyone. Meeting adjourned.

text:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-05-29
Start: 15:00 PDT
Duration: ~47 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Marco Rivera (Redwood SE), Lisa Patel (BlueNova EngMgr), Dmitri Kozlov (BlueNova SRE), Kenji Sato (BlueNova Security)

Summary:
Quick weekly POC sync focussed on access gating, VPC peering readiness, initial latency bench outcomes, and a rehearse of fallback/canary behavior. Agreed on next steps for SSO metadata, peering test IPs, and two bench runs. Security wants a short deep-dive next week.

Topics: Access provisioning, SSO/SAML, VPC peering, KV cache warmup, latency bench, fallback/canary rehearsal, compliance docs (SOC2), KMS/HSM notes.

Transcript:
[00:00] Ava (Redwood): All right everyone, thanks for joining. I think we have about 45 minutes. Quick roll call?
[00:08] Lisa: Lisa here.
[00:10] Dmitri: Dmitri present.
[00:12] Kenji: Kenji on the line.
[00:15] Marco: Marco from Redwood, hi all.
[00:18] Ava: Cool. Agenda — access status, bench recap, and then run the fallback/canary plan end-to-end. Does that work?
[00:26] Lisa: Yep that's good, maybe start with access because we're blocked on SSO metadata.
[00:30] Ava: Sure. I shared a checklist yesterday but I'll go over the critical bits. We need a SAML metadata file, SP entity ID we provided in the doc, and mapping for the Redwood console admin role.
[00:41] Kenji: Quick Q — is Redwood supporting IdP-initiated login or only SP? For compliance we prefer SP-initiated but need both if possible.
[00:50] Marco: We support both patterns; historically most customers use IdP-initiated for enterprise SSO. There are two small caveats: the console SSO flows require Metadata with AssertionConsumerService URL matching exactly, and you need to set NameID format to transient or email, depending on your SAML.
[01:05] Dmitri: We're seeing some attr mapping oddities in our IDP. Can you send sample attribute mappings?
[01:12] Ava: Yes I'll attach a sample mapping and a minimal metadata we recommend. I'll also put in the role mapping for admin/read-only.
[01:20] Lisa: That would help. Also, how does role provisioning tie into GovCloud or private deployment? We may need RBAC constraints.
[01:30] Marco: Role mapping is local to the Redwood Console. For Private deployments we can integrate with your SCIM if you want automated group sync; otherwise manual mapping is fine for the POC.
[01:40] Kenji: Does SCIM sync require additional network egress? We are strict about outbound endpoints.
[01:46] Marco: It's HTTPS outbound to the control plane. If you run Redwood Private in VPC, you can lock it down to your egress path, and keys are customer-managed via KMS/HSM options. There may be an extra NAT or proxy rule depending on your setup.
[01:58] Dmitri: OK please document the IPs we need to allow.
[02:03] Ava: We'll add that to the doc.

[02:18] Ava: Next up — VPC peering and networking. Dmitri, where are we?
[02:22] Dmitri: We've reserved the peering CIDR 10.40.0.0/16 for the staging cluster. We ran a quick route check, egress to Redwood control plane over NAT is fine, but the peering handshake hasn't completed — side A still shows pending.
[02:38] Marco: Pending usually means console approval or missing route propagation. Did you advertise the route table to the peering attachment?
[02:44] Dmitri: We did, but there was a mismatch in the prefix list. Also, MTU dropped packets during an arp test earlier, so we want to confirm path MTU and fragmented UDP is not in the critical path for telemetry.
[02:58] Marco: For the model traffic, it's TCP inter-host and uses gRPC over TLS — so MTU is less likely to hit you unless you have weird intermediary fragmentation. Still, good to confirm. We'll share a small connectivity-test script that does an HTTP handshake to the inference gateway and a throughput test.
[03:16] Ava: Action: Marco will send connectivity script. Dmitri will run and paste logs.

[03:30] Lisa: Bench results — we ran one run yesterday using the sample prompt set (10k tokens total across sessions) and got median p50 latency around 210ms, p95 about 890ms.
[03:43] Marco: Can you confirm the concurrency and batch size used?
[03:46] Lisa: We used 20 concurrent sessions with default batch config.
[03:51] Marco: Okay that explains it. In our infra we've seen better p95 with increased batching thresholds but that can raise p50 slightly. For POC you want to tune for your SLO — do you care more about p50 or p95?
[04:03] Lisa: p95 under 700ms would be ideal for our UX. We're okay if p50 moves to ~250ms.
[04:11] Marco: Then we recommend toggling the batch_max_tokens and target_latency_ms in the provided config. Also warm up KV cache to avoid cold-start tokenization/regrowth.
[04:22] Dmitri: How do we warm the KV cache? Do you have a warmup script or should we run synthetic sessions?
[04:29] Marco: We'll attach a warmup script that sequentially primes the common prefixes and performs small confirmatory gets to populate KV. It's idempotent. I'll drop it in the shared folder.
[04:40] Ava: Another note — in the bench logs we saw a few 5xx spikes tied to a model fallback. Can you describe Redwood's fallback policies?
[04:51] Marco: Sure — we have routing policies that prefer the primary model, and if inference fails we can fall back to a slightly smaller model variant or route to another region depending on your policy. For the POC we recommended 'soft-fallback' where we log & incrementally fallback after N failures per minute. That avoids instant user interruptions.
[05:08] Lisa: That might explain the spikes. We saw a repeated failure window at ~14:02 where a node crashed and fallbacks kicked in.
[05:16] Marco: We'll correlate that time with our telemetry. Please include timestamps in your bench logs (you did, right?).
[05:21] Lisa: Yes, we included timestamps.

[05:35] Kenji: From security perspective — what data is temporarily cached for KV? Is user data ever written to persistent logs? We need to understand retention and whether PII touches logs.
[05:47] Marco: Good question. For POC, generative outputs are not persisted beyond telemetry unless you enable retention. We redact user-specified PII fields by default in telemetry; we can enable stricter scrubbing for BlueNova. Persistent logging is configurable and by default limited to operational traces — payloads are not stored. We'll provide the telemetry retention policy doc and the telemetry scrubbing options.
[06:05] Kenji: And key management — are keys in your KMS or ours? For Private, we want customer-managed KMS and HSM.
[06:13] Marco: Yes, for Private deployments we integrate with customer KMS/HSM. Hosted mode uses Redwood-managed keys; Dedicated can be hybrid. We'll enumerate options in the security pack we share.

[06:30] Ava: Ok moving on — fallback rehearsal. We want to do a canary failover test on staging next Friday. The plan is to simulate an unhealthy node and observe the fallback to a smaller model and routing update.
[06:43] Dmitri: What's the expected duration of the failover and what's the rollback?
[06:47] Marco: Typical reroute is under 2s for control plane routing updates, but model spin-up could be 5–30s depending on the model and whether the target is already warm. That's why we recommend keeping a warm standby or quick-quant variant.
[07:00] Lisa: If it takes 30s users will notice. Can we pre-warm a smaller quantized model so fallback is near-instant?
[07:06] Marco: Yes that's the strategy. Quantized LLMs have much faster cold starts. We can pre-load a 4-bit quant variant and keep it in memory. Tradeoff is memory footprint vs cold start latency.
[07:16] Kenji: For compliance we need the fallback logs and who triggered failover. Can telemetry tag with the experiment ID and user session?
[07:24] Marco: Yes we include request-level metadata — session id, experiment id, route decision. We'll ensure audit logs include the reason and model variant.

[07:40] Ava: Let's talk timelines — Ben in infra asked when we could start the two bench runs. Is next Tue/Wed possible?
[07:48] Dmitri: Next Tue is fine, Wednesday is tight. We'll aim for Tue at 10am PT.
[07:53] Lisa: Tue 10am PT works for the team.
[07:56] Ava: Great. Marco will be on the run to help interpret.

[08:12] Marco: One note on the bench config — please export full tracing (span-level) for the two runs so we can map spikes to kernel or model stages. That helps us decide if it was batching, kernel selection, or GC on the node.
[08:25] Dmitri: We can do that but it's verbose. Is there a sample config?
[08:29] Marco: Yes. I'll attach a minimal tracing config that captures model runtime, tokenization, network latencies but keeps payloads redacted.

[08:44] Lisa: Any blockers on credentials? Our devs still don't have the service account to call the staging endpoint.
[08:51] Ava: We provisioned a service key yesterday but it's scoped to console only — we need to create a service account credential for the inference API. Marco, did we finish that?
[08:59] Marco: I created an API key with restricted scopes but it looks like the customer side needs to whitelist the API key origin or set up the token exchange. Dmitri?
[09:06] Dmitri: We haven't whitelisted it yet — what's the origin and expected header?
[09:11] Marco: Standard bearer header. For origin we usually don't set a CORS origin for server-to-server; you need to allow outbound to the inference gateway IPs and the NAT egress. We'll send exact IPs.
[09:21] Ava: Action: Ava to resend the API key instructions and scope. Dmitri to whitelist egress for the API key.

[09:36] Kenji: A quick ask — can we get the SOC2 section that lists what telemetry is collected and retention times? We need it before the executive review.
[09:44] Ava: Yes, we'll share the SOC2 summary and scope doc. I'll include the redaction notes.

[10:00] Dmitri: One more infra nit — during the first bench run we saw packets dropped on node 3, traced to an MTU mismatch on the peering. We can fix by tuning the MTU or enabling fragmentation, but which is recommended?
[10:11] Marco: If you can set uniform MTU across the path that's ideal. If not, enable PMTU discovery and confirm your firewall doesn't drop ICMP. We'll note it in the connectivity script to assert PMTU.
[10:22] Dmitri: We'll follow that.

[10:35] Ava: Okay, that wraps the technical portion. Recap of next steps: SSO metadata and role mapping from Ava, connectivity script from Marco, VPC peering confirm from Dmitri, two bench runs Tue 10am with Marco attending, security deep-dive scheduling.
[10:55] Lisa: Sounds good. Also, can you share a short README for our on-call so they know the failover signal?
[11:03] Marco: Yes, the runbook will include the failover signal and rollback steps.

[11:20] Kenji: Thanks all. One last item — for the security deep-dive, prioritize KMS/HSM questions and audit logs.
[11:28] Ava: Noted. We'll book a 30–45 minute slot next week.

[11:40] Marco: I'll drop the warmup script and bench/tracing configs into the shared folder after the call.
[11:47] Ava: Great, thanks everyone. Meeting adjourned.

Action items (in transcript):
- Marco: send connectivity script + warmup script + tracing config
- Ava: share SSO sample metadata, API key instructions, SOC2 summary
- Dmitri: confirm peering CIDR, run connectivity script, whitelist API key egress
- Kenji: compile compliance Qs for deep-dive

Notes / quirks in transcription: some product names transcribed as "Redwood Privatee" in one place, minor speaker overlaps, and a couple of timestamps slightly off from the bench logs (we asked for raw logs to correlate).

title: SLO targets, sampling, and runbook for pilot
preview:
Meeting header:
Date/Time: 2026-05-19 16:00 UTC
Duration: ~52 minutes
Location: Zoom (recording)
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Diego Martinez (Redwood SE), Maya Singh (Velaris CTO), Liam O'Neill (Velaris Lead ML Eng), Priya Rao (Velaris SRE)

Quick summary: High-level scoping for a 6-week pilot focusing on latency & throughput SLOs. Main questions: TTFT vs TPOT targets, p95/p99 thresholds, sampling strategy for measurements (synthetic vs production shadowing), and acceptance runbook (who runs what, tooling, rollback criteria). Agreed actions: Redwood to provide sampling scripts + runbook; Velaris to provide traffic profile and initial SLO preferences; schedule bench run in staging.

Topics covered:
- Definitions: TTFT (time to first token) vs TPOT (total per-token time), measurement windows, warm cache vs cold cache behavior.
- Target setting: p95 / p99 latency targets and acceptable TPOT growth under burst.
- Measurement plan: sample sizes, synthetic load vs shadow traffic, canary windows, instrumentation (trace ids, topline metrics).
- Risk & fallbacks: model variant fallback, regional routing, scaling headroom.

Transcript:
[00:00] Ava Chen: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Quick intros then jump straight to SLO goals for the pilot, we have about 50 minutes.
[00:18] Maya Singh: Hi, Maya here. We wanna be sure what success looks like for a real-time assistant, so latency is the big one.
[00:26] Diego Martinez: Awesome, thanks Maya. Quick note, I'm Diego, will be engineering lead on the bench and I'll own the run scripts.
[00:33] Liam O'Neill: Liam here. We own the model infra, running on a mix of GCP and some colo. We have a VPC peering setup for private testing.
[00:40] Priya Rao: Priya from SRE, interested in throttling/queueing behavior and how backpressure shows up.
[00:45] Ava Chen: Great. So to frame, Redwood suggests measuring two SLO families: TTFT (time to first token) because UX depends on that initial response, and TPOT — we shorthand as total per-token time or sometimes per-response time — to measure throughput cost and tail behavior.
[01:10] Maya Singh: Clarify TPOT one more time? Sometimes people use TPT.
[01:13] Diego Martinez: Yeah, we use TPOT here to mean average per-token compute latency across the generation, which captures long responses that can balloon cost and backend concurrency.
[01:24] Liam O'Neill: For our use case most responses are short, but we do have long-form summarization occasionally. So p95 on TTFT is critical, p99 for TPOT maybe for billing/alerts.

[01:40] Ava Chen: On targets — what are you aiming for at launch? Maya?
[01:45] Maya Singh: Our internal bar is sub-350ms p95 for TTFT, and p99 under 900ms. For TPOT we want per-token p95 around 12–18ms; p99 less than 30ms ideally. But those numbers are flexible depending on cost.
[02:05] Diego Martinez: That's very helpful. For context, Redwood hosted with our small-llama-quant profile typically lands around 280ms p95 TTFT on 1k token requests in our benchmarks, but your network and request shapes will change that. Also, note cold start vs warmed KV cache — initial token can jump.
[02:28] Liam O'Neill: We often see cold-cache penalties in the region when models switch. How do you measure warm vs cold?
[02:33] Diego Martinez: We propose a sampling window that separates 'steady-state' (95% of traffic in last 15 minutes) vs 'first-request' windows. Our bench runner will simulate warm cache by preloading typical prefixes.

[02:50] Ava Chen: On acceptance, two axes: 1) SLO compliance -> p95/p99 thresholds during a 60-minute staging run with production-like traffic; 2) Reliability -> no >1% error rate and no more than two transient model fallbacks per hour.
[03:06] Priya Rao: Can we have specific rollback triggers? E.g., if p99 exceeds target by 50% for 5 continuous minutes, auto-stop canary?
[03:12] Diego Martinez: Yes, we recommend auto-abort at that threshold for canaries. We'll include that in the runbook.

[03:28] Maya Singh: Sampling question: do you prefer synthetic spike tests or shadow traffic? We don't want to send PII to external hosted services for a while.
[03:36] Ava Chen: Good call — we support both. For early proofs you can do synthetic workloads in your staging VPC (Redwood Private trial or Dedicated). For shadowing we can run local sidecar mode in your VPC — no data leaves your env. We'll provide the scripts.
[03:56] Liam O'Neill: That sidecar approach sounds good. How do we ensure the sampling size is statistically meaningful for p99?
[04:02] Diego Martinez: Rule of thumb: to get decent p99 confidence you want thousands of samples; we usually recommend 10k+ requests per measurement window, but that's adjustable. For a 60-minute run with typical 10 rps traffic, you'd need to accelerate sampling or run multiple windows.
[04:20] Maya Singh: Do you have built-in probes to correlate traces to token-level timings?
[04:24] Diego Martinez: Yes, instrumented traces include request_id and token timing events. Our console shows TTFT and per-token distributions. Note transcription sometimes shows 'KV cash' in the logs — apologies, it's KV cache — the metric is 'kv_hit_rate' that we use to explain warm behavior.
[04:42] Priya Rao: We need audit logs and retention for compliance. Will bench runs generate audit entries?
[04:47] Ava Chen: Correct — Private and Dedicated modes will emit audit logs to your KMS-backed storage. Hosted can be configured with controlled retention. We'll include the logflow in the runbook.

[05:05] Liam O'Neill: For reporting, we want a baseline run and then a stress run at 3x normal to see degradation. Is that in scope?
[05:10] Diego Martinez: Totally. Pilot plan: baseline 60-minute steady-state run, then a 30-minute 3x burst test, then a 30-minute recovery window. We'll measure p50/p95/p99 for TTFT and TPOT across windows.
[05:26] Ava Chen: We'll also capture unit-econ metrics: tokens served, GPU seconds, and estimated cost per 1k requests. That's part of acceptance.

[05:36] Maya Singh: How about sampling of different payload sizes? Our traffic isn't uniform.
[05:40] Diego Martinez: We'll ask you to provide the 7-day histogram; we will create three buckets — short (<=32 tokens), medium (33–256), long (>256) — and weight the synthetic generator accordingly.

[05:56] Liam O'Neill: Any guidance on p95 vs p99 prioritization? We care about p95 for everyday UX but p99 for SLA penalties.
[06:03] Ava Chen: Right — propose acceptance threshold: p95 meets target and p99 <= 1.5x target for pilot. If p99 breaches beyond that but p95 is ok, we log as risk but may still pass with mitigations (e.g., prefer model variant).

[06:18] Priya Rao: Network variance in our multi-region setup can skew TTFT. Can we annotate measurements with region tags?
[06:23] Diego Martinez: We will. Each sample has region and route metadata.

[06:30] Maya Singh: What about cadence and timeline?
[06:33] Ava Chen: Proposed: Week 0 (this week) — data handoff and runbook delivery. Week 1 — staging baseline run. Week 2 — iterate on tuning (batching, quantization profile suggestions). Week 3 — production-like pilot / canary. Total 3–6 weeks depending on availability.
[06:54] Liam O'Neill: Sounds reasonable. One ask: include how batching settings affect TTFT — sometimes batching reduces cost but increases TTFT.
[06:59] Diego Martinez: We'll include a tradeoff matrix: batch size vs p95 TTFT vs token cost. Also recommend dynamic batching policies for spiky traffic.

[07:15] Maya Singh: For measurement tooling, can you send the scripts?
[07:18] Ava Chen: Yes, action item: Diego will share the synthetic generator (python + k6 examples) and the sampling policy JSON. Link: https://confluence.redwood.com/pilot-runbook (placeholder).
[07:30] Liam O'Neill: For compliance reasons we can't post data to hosted early. We'll run all bench runs in our VPC with Redwood Private agent.
[07:36] Diego Martinez: Noted — we'll provide the agent install doc and a simplified helm chart.

[07:46] Priya Rao: We also need the exact definitions of success in writing so the legal team can sign off.
[07:50] Ava Chen: We'll produce an acceptance criteria doc: exact p95/p99 numbers per bucket, allowed error rates, canary abort thresholds, and reporting cadence.
[07:58] Maya Singh: Any competitors or alternative architectures you'd like us to consider?
[08:02] Ava Chen: Some customers compare us to Anthropic and to self-managed on-prem inference stacks. We'll put together a pros/cons appendix for you.

[08:12] Diego Martinez: A couple of gotchas I want to call out: tokenization differences affect per-token timing if your prompts cause different token counts. Also quantization can change latency shape on tails. We'll instrument for both.
[08:26] Liam O'Neill: On the subject of logging, can we get raw per-request CSV after each run?
[08:30] Ava Chen: Yes, we'll export raw samples and a summary dashboard.

[08:38] Maya Singh: Ok recap please?
[08:40] Ava Chen: Recap: Redwood sends runbook + sampling scripts by 2026-05-22; Velaris sends traffic histogram and initial preferred SLO numbers by 2026-05-24; schedule 2-hour staging bench for week of 2026-05-26 (Diego to coordinate). Acceptance criteria doc to be shared before bench run.
[08:59] Priya Rao: One last thing — can we include data retention and encryption specifics in the runbook?
[09:04] Ava Chen: Yes, KMS/HSM notes and audit log retention will be in the runbook.
[09:10] Maya Singh: Thanks all. Looking forward.
[09:13] Ava Chen: Thanks everyone. We'll follow up in email with links and dates.

End of transcript.

References mentioned: 
- Pilot runbook (link placeholder): https://confluence.redwood.com/pilot-runbook
- Sampling policy examples: python k6 generator, will attach to email

Notes on transcription quality: speaker attributions generally correct; occasional mis-heard terms (KV cache -> KV cash) and dropped punctuation.

[03:12] Diego Martinez: Yes, we recommend auto-abort at that threshold for canaries. We'll include that in the runbook.

[03:36] Ava Chen: Good call — we support both. For early proofs you can do synthetic workloads in your staging VPC (Redwood Private trial or Dedicated). For shadowing we can run local sidecar mode in your VPC — no data leaves your env. We'll provide the scripts.
[03:56] Liam O'Neill: That sidecar approach sounds good. How do we ensure the sampling size is statistically meaningful for p99?
[04:02] Diego Martinez: Rule of thumb: to get decent p99 confidence you want thousands of samples; we usually recommend 10k+ requests per measurement window, but that's adjustable. For a 60-minute run with typical 10 rps traffic, you'd need to accelerate sampling or run multiple windows.
[04:20] Maya Singh: Do you have built-in probes to correlate traces to token-level timings?
[04:24] Diego Martinez: Yes, instrumented traces include request_id and token timing events. Our console shows TTFT and per-token distributions. Note transcription sometimes shows 'KV cash' in the logs — apologies, it's KV cache — the metric is 'kv_hit_rate' that we use to explain warm behavior.
[04:42] Priya Rao: We need audit logs and retention for compliance. Will bench runs generate audit entries?
[04:47] Ava Chen: Correct — Private and Dedicated modes will emit audit logs to your KMS-backed storage. Hosted can be configured with controlled retention. We'll include the logflow in the runbook.

[05:05] Liam O'Neill: For reporting, we want a baseline run and then a stress run at 3x normal to see degradation. Is that in scope?
[05:10] Diego Martinez: Totally. Pilot plan: baseline 60-minute steady-state run, then a 30-minute 3x burst test, then a 30-minute recovery window. We'll measure p50/p95/p99 for TTFT and TPOT across windows.
[05:26] Ava Chen: We'll also capture unit-econ metrics: tokens served, GPU seconds, and estimated cost per 1k requests. That's part of acceptance.

[05:36] Maya Singh: How about sampling of different payload sizes? Our traffic isn't uniform.
[05:40] Diego Martinez: We'll ask you to provide the 7-day histogram; we will create three buckets — short (<=32 tokens), medium (33–256), long (>256) — and weight the synthetic generator accordingly.

[05:56] Liam O'Neill: Any guidance on p95 vs p99 prioritization? We care about p95 for everyday UX but p99 for SLA penalties.
[06:03] Ava Chen: Right — propose acceptance threshold: p95 meets target and p99 <= 1.5x target for pilot. If p99 breaches beyond that but p95 is ok, we log as risk but may still pass with mitigations (e.g., prefer model variant).

[06:18] Priya Rao: Network variance in our multi-region setup can skew TTFT. Can we annotate measurements with region tags?
[06:23] Diego Martinez: We will. Each sample has region and route metadata.

[06:30] Maya Singh: What about cadence and timeline?
[06:33] Ava Chen: Proposed: Week 0 (this week) — data handoff and runbook delivery. Week 1 — staging baseline run. Week 2 — iterate on tuning (batching, quantization profile suggestions). Week 3 — production-like pilot / canary. Total 3–6 weeks depending on availability.
[06:54] Liam O'Neill: Sounds reasonable. One ask: include how batching settings affect TTFT — sometimes batching reduces cost but increases TTFT.
[06:59] Diego Martinez: We'll include a tradeoff matrix: batch size vs p95 TTFT vs token cost. Also recommend dynamic batching policies for spiky traffic.

[07:15] Maya Singh: For measurement tooling, can you send the scripts?
[07:18] Ava Chen: Yes, action item: Diego will share the synthetic generator (python + k6 examples) and the sampling policy JSON. Link: https://confluence.redwood.com/pilot-runbook (placeholder).
[07:30] Liam O'Neill: For compliance reasons we can't post data to hosted early. We'll run all bench runs in our VPC with Redwood Private agent.
[07:36] Diego Martinez: Noted — we'll provide the agent install doc and a simplified helm chart.

[07:46] Priya Rao: We also need the exact definitions of success in writing so the legal team can sign off.
[07:50] Ava Chen: We'll produce an acceptance criteria doc: exact p95/p99 numbers per bucket, allowed error rates, canary abort thresholds, and reporting cadence.
[07:58] Maya Singh: Any competitors or alternative architectures you'd like us to consider?
[08:02] Ava Chen: Some customers compare us to Anthropic and to self-managed on-prem inference stacks. We'll put together a pros/cons appendix for you.

[08:12] Diego Martinez: A couple of gotchas I want to call out: tokenization differences affect per-token timing if your prompts cause different token counts. Also quantization can change latency shape on tails. We'll instrument for both.
[08:26] Liam O'Neill: On the subject of logging, can we get raw per-request CSV after each run?
[08:30] Ava Chen: Yes, we'll export raw samples and a summary dashboard.

[08:38] Maya Singh: Ok recap please?
[08:40] Ava Chen: Recap: Redwood sends runbook + sampling scripts by 2026-05-22; Velaris sends traffic histogram and initial preferred SLO numbers by 2026-05-24; schedule 2-hour staging bench for week of 2026-05-26 (Diego to coordinate). Acceptance criteria doc to be shared before bench run.
[08:59] Priya Rao: One last thing — can we include data retention and encryption specifics in the runbook?
[09:04] Ava Chen: Yes, KMS/HSM notes and audit log retention will be in the runbook.
[09:10] Maya Singh: Thanks all. Looking forward.
[09:13] Ava Chen: Thanks everyone. We'll follow up in email with links and dates.

End of transcript.

References mentioned: 
- Pilot runbook (link placeholder): https://confluence.redwood.com/pilot-runbook
- Sampling policy examples: python k6 generator, will attach to email

Notes on transcription quality: speaker attributions generally correct; occasional mis-heard terms (KV cache -> KV cash) and dropped punctuation.

title: Forward Velocity - Leadership Huddle
preview: Leadership huddle covering hiring cadence, upcoming Dedicated capacity milestones, private deployment onboarding, and an ops-first checklist for the next two sprints. CEO opens with company context and growth metrics. CTO and VP Eng sketch performance wins and remaining risks (tail latency, batching), Product describes Redwood Optimize beta rollout. People/Revenue update on hiring and customer health. Q&A focuses on EU region readiness, SOC 2 timelines, and what counts as success for the trial customers.

People/Revenue update on hiring and customer health. Q&A focuses on EU region readiness, SOC 2 timelines, and what counts as success for the trial customers.

title: OOM Simulations and Launch Contingency Tabletop
preview: Tabletop exercise to rehearse OOM scenarios against Dedicated pools and Hosted fallbacks ahead of the planned model rollout. We ran through simulated failure modes, prioritization for mitigation playbooks, and assigned short-term operational runbooks and longer-term infra tasks. Key outcome: sequence of tactical checkpoints (stress tests, improved observability, updated canary abort criteria) and explicit owners for each contingency step.

title: Routing economics & hosting options — HarborEdge discovery
preview:
Meeting header:
- Date: 2026-04-22
- Start: 16:00 UTC
- Duration: 48 minutes
- Attendees: Priya (Redwood AE), Carlos (Redwood SE), Ava (Redwood SA); Daniel (HarborEdge Head of ML), Sasha (CTO), Liam (Infra), Marta (PM)

Auto-summary (auto-generated, optional):
HarborEdge is evaluating hosted vs dedicated vs private deployment to serve a multi-tenant customer-facing chat/assistant. Primary goal: hit sub-150ms P95 for short prompts while keeping cost/unit below ~$0.0008 per token for high-volume paths. They expressed interest in batching/caching to lower unit cost and asked about routing/fallback policies and compliance (SOC2, KMS, data residency). Next: pricing sheet + benchmark.

Transcript:
[00:00] Priya: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. quick roll call, and we'll jump into what you folks are trying to accomplish.
[00:12] Daniel: Hi all, Daniel here. We're excited to run through options—right now we're building the HarborEdge assistant, multi-tenant, in production beta.
[00:18] Sasha: Sasha here. main concerns: cost at scale, and proving we can meet latency SLOs for EU customers.
[00:27] Priya: Great. Carlos and Ava from our side will handle the throughput/infra and security bits. real quick: can you summarize traffic profile? average prompt size, queries per second, burst?
[00:35] Liam: Yeah, so baseline is about 40 qps across the service, but some tenants spike to 300 qps for short windows. Average prompt is 80 tokens in, response avg 120 tokens out. We do a lot of short-turn chats and some longer summarization jobs.
[00:47] Carlos: okay that helps. do you have percentiles for latency today? p95 target?
[00:52] Daniel: Target P95 is 150ms for the short-chat path. For long summaries we can tolerate up to 1.5s.
[00:59] Priya: That puts you in the classic latency vs cost tradeoff. Hosted can get you low friction and predictable SLAs; dedicated gives more throughput predictability; private (VPC/on-prem) gives you control for compliance but increases ops. Curious—what's the decision driver: cost or compliance or latency?
[01:17] Sasha: All three. Compliance is mandatory for our EU customers (data residency + KMS), but cost matters because we have thin margins on some tenants.
[01:25] Marta: Also timeline. We'd prefer to be in pilot in 3–4 weeks.
[01:31] Priya: Good. For a short pilot we'll typically suggest hosted to get a baseline cost and latency; then we can model dedicated reserved capacity for the steady state if your unit economics make sense.
[01:40] Daniel: Curious on per-token math: how do you calculate per-token cost when batching and caching are enabled?
[01:46] Carlos: Right—so raw per-token price is a baseline for hosted. But effective unit cost is lower when you enable continuous batching, prefix caching, and route lower-cost model variants to non-critical traffic. Example: with K=8 batching and prefix KV cache hit rate at 40%, you can see 30–50% reduction in token cost for the short-chat path.
[02:06] Sasha: When you say model variants, do you mean routing to smaller-dense or quantized models automatically?
[02:11] Ava: yes—our runtime can route based on policy. e.g., critical tenant traffic to full fp16 model on dedicated GPU; background/rerank or non-sensitive to a quantized x4 model on cheaper capacity. That does impact latency tradeoffs but helps costs.
[02:23] Liam: Are the batching gains realized for single-turn chats though? a lot of our traffic is quick back-and-forth.
[02:29] Carlos: There's still benefit because we do continuous batching—requests arriving within a short window are batched. For sub-100ms user-perceived latency you won't batch too long, but even micro-batching at 10–20ms helps throughput and amortizes kernel overhead. Also prefix KV caching helps a ton for repeated system prompts or long context windows.
[02:45] Daniel: On the compliance side: can hosted meet EU data residency and KMS isolation?
[02:50] Priya: Hosted can be regionally isolated—data can be pinned to EU regions and we offer customer-managed KMS for encryption at rest. For strict regulatory needs though, our Private deployment (VPC/on-prem) is the route. We can run with your HSM integrations if needed.
[03:03] Sasha: Do you have SOC2 Type II?
[03:05] Priya: Yes, we do. we'll share SOC2 and our security pack after the call.
[03:09] Marta: Great. On licensing—if we go dedicated reserved, what's the commitment look like?
[03:13] Ava: Typically monthly or annual committed capacity. We size GPU pools for the steady state and provide burst capacity for spikes. We can model scenarios so you can see break-even points.
[03:23] Daniel: Can we dig into a concrete number? right now target unit cost needs to be <= $0.0008 per token on average for the high-volume path. what's realistic?
[03:31] Carlos: For that bucket, options: hosted with aggressive batching/caching and lower-cost quantized endpoints might hit ~ $0.0006–0.0009 per effective token depending on batch windows and cache hit rates. Dedicated with reserved capacity and similar optimizations can push below $0.0005 per token if you have steady volume to justify the reservation. Private will be more variable—capex and ops change the calculus but per-token compute cost can be lower if you absorb infra ops.
[04:00] Daniel: okay what about fallback policies if a dedicated pool is saturated?
[04:04] Priya: We support smart routing and automatic fallback to compatible model variants and hosted pools. Policies can be based on latency SLO, cost thresholds, or availability. We can also do region fallback for capacity.
[04:14] Sasha: Is there a per-request routing decision latency cost? like how much overhead to route?
[04:18] Carlos: negligible, a few ms. Most overhead is in model warmup or KV cache priming. We keep persistent workers and KV caches to reduce warm start costs.
[04:27] Marta: For benchmarking—do we need to provide a synthetic workload or you can replay production traces?
[04:32] Carlos: Both. ideal is a sample of anonymized production traces; if not possible a synthetic with similar token distributions and spike patterns works. we'll run hosted and dedicated tests to compare.
[04:42] Daniel: Could you also show how tokenization differences affect counts? we saw different tokenizers produce 10–15% variance.
[04:48] Priya: Good point. We'll include tokenizer normalization in the benchmark and show effective tokens.
[04:52] (Short pause, background keyboard noises)
[05:00] Sasha: Quick question—does Redwood meter per-input + per-output tokens separately or combined?
[05:06] Priya: Combined. We bill per request for total tokens consumed. For unit-econ modeling we typically look at tokens/response plus model latency to estimate cost per completed request.
[05:15] Liam: Do you offer volume discounts or committed spend credits?
[05:18] Ava: Yes, committed reserved capacity and enterprise contracts include discounted rates and SLA credits. We'll add a worked example in the pricing sheet.
[05:26] Daniel: Any hidden costs we should be aware of? networking, egress?
[05:29] Priya: Hosted includes normal network egress per cloud provider pricing in the bill; dedicated and private can have different egress/peering considerations. We'll highlight those.
[05:38] Marta: On security: what's in audit logs? do you expose per-request metadata for compliance?
[05:44] Carlos: We can provide detailed audit logs with request ids, timestamps, model version, and routing decisions. Payload-level logs can be redacted by default and you can opt into retention windows.
[05:56] Daniel: Good. let's talk about quantization—how aggressive can you be before quality degrades for our use cases?
[06:02] Carlos: It depends on model and eval sets. For short-chat completions, q4_0 or int8 quantization often preserves quality. For long-form summarization you may want higher-fidelity. We'll run evals on your prompt set; Redwood Optimize will recommend quantization profiles and flag quality regressions.
[06:18] Sasha: Are model updates disruptive to latency SLOs? how do rollouts work?
[06:22] Priya: We support canary and A/B rollouts with automatic fallback to prior versions and model-variant routing if quality or latency regressions are observed. Rollouts can be throttled by percent or by tenant.
[06:33] Marta: Timeline check: pilot in 3–4 weeks—what's needed from us to start?
[06:37] Carlos: For a pilot we need: sample prompt set + target traffic profile, short list of tenants to include, and credentials for region access if you want EU-hosted testing. Also security questionnaire answers for rapid review.
[06:50] Daniel: We'll get that to you.
[06:52] Priya: Great. I will send the pricing worksheet and a sample unit-econ workbook. Carlos will run a benchmark once you give the traces.
[06:58] (Brief cross-talk)
[07:10] Sasha: On data residency—if we use hosted EU region, are backups or logs replicated to other regions?
[07:15] Ava: By default backups are region-scoped for hosted EU deployments; cross-region replication is opt-in and documented. We'll include the retention controls in the security pack.
[07:26] Daniel: For measurement—how do you compute token cost with caching? can you show a formula?
[07:30] Carlos: Sure. Effective_token_cost = (compute_cost + infra_overhead + networking) / (tokens_served * cache_hit_adjustment). More practically, we compute cost per model instance/second and divide by tokens/sec served after accounting for cache hits and batching delta. We'll provide a worked example in the workbook.
[07:48] Marta: Example would be helpful.
[07:50] Priya: We'll include one showing a short-chat path vs long summary path with numbers: batch windows, cache hit %, model variant mix.
[07:58] (ambient office noise)
[08:05] Daniel: What about multi-tenant isolation on dedicated pools—can we enforce tenant-level quotas?
[08:10] Carlos: Yes, quotas and per-tenant routing policies are supported. You can reserve capacity per tenant, apply throttling, or route lower-priority tenants to cheaper variants.
[08:20] Liam: If we host private on-prem, is there a management plane talkback? how much telemetry returns to Redwood?
[08:25] Ava: Private has a control plane with configurable telemetry. By default we collect health and billing metadata; sensitive payloads are not sent unless configured. We can do an air-gapped deployment where the control plane is fully customer-run.
[08:38] Sasha: HSM integration—what's the level of effort?
[08:41] Ava: We have documented integrations for common KMS/HSM vendors. effort depends on your infra; we typically estimate 2–4 weeks for full integration tests for enterprise customers.
[08:50] Daniel: On the pilot—do you replicate our tenant spike behavior including 300 qps bursts?
[08:55] Carlos: Absolutely. we'll run burst tests and show p50/p95/p99 latency under sustained and spike loads.
[09:03] Marta: Great. would be useful to have an SLA for P95 under dedicated reserved capacity.
[09:08] Priya: We provide SLA-backed latency and uptime for Dedicated—specific targets written into the contract once we lock in capacity and model profiles.
[09:17] (pause)
[09:25] (longer section omitted in transcript for brevity in export)
[28:10] Daniel: we also want to understand how model compaction/quantization impacts infer cost across different sequence lengths—our summarization jobs go up to 2k tokens.
[28:20] Carlos: For long sequences, throughput and memory matter more—quantization helps but kernel-level optimizations and KV caching for prefixes can tip the cost. We'll include benchmarks for 512, 1024, 2048 token lengths.
[28:35] Sasha: Any special licensing for model weights if we bring our own model to private?
[28:39] Priya: If you bring your own model you license it per your terms; our platform supports customer-supplied weights. We can run them in private or dedicated pools.
[28:46] Daniel: Are there differences in observability between hosted and private?
[28:49] Carlos: Hosted offers full observability in Redwood Console with token-level breakdowns and tracing. Private can export telemetry to your observability stack; we can enable the same dashboards but data path depends on deployment choices.
[29:00] Marta: On billing—do you provide cost-attribution per tenant?
[29:05] Priya: yes, we provide per-tenant token breakdowns, cost allocation, and tagging.
[29:10] Daniel: Okay let's summarise next steps.
[29:13] Priya: Recap: We'll send pricing workbook, security pack (SOC2 + retention options + KMS docs), and a benchmarking plan. You'll send sample traces and latency targets. Carlos will run benchmarks and share results. Then schedule a pilot kickoff with infra and security.
[29:28] Sasha: Sounds good.
[29:30] Liam: One last quick ask—can you provide an example of a routing policy file we could use?
[29:35] Carlos: Yes, we'll share a sample policy yaml showing routing by tenant, latency SLO, and cost thresholds.
[29:41] (wrap-up)
[29:45] Priya: Thanks everyone. we'll follow up with the docs and proposed dates.
[29:50] Daniel: Thank you, looking forward.
[29:52] (meeting ends; remaining 18 minutes were Q/A with infra team captured in internal notes - truncated in this export)

Notes on transcript quality: occasional speaker attribution glitches, some technical terms auto-corrected (KV cache -> K-V cache), tokenizer names redacted.

Shared artifacts mentioned:
- Hosted vs Dedicated pricing sheet (link to be sent)
- Benchmark plan spreadsheet: https://redwood.example/benchmarks/harboredge-plans (placeholder)
- SOC2 + Security pack (to be attached)
- Sample routing policy YAML (to be attached)

End of transcript.
