[07:50] Priya: We'll include one showing a short-chat path vs long summary path with numbers: batch windows, cache hit %, model variant mix.
[07:58] (ambient office noise)
[08:05] Daniel: What about multi-tenant isolation on dedicated pools—can we enforce tenant-level quotas?
[08:10] Carlos: Yes, quotas and per-tenant routing policies are supported. You can reserve capacity per tenant, apply throttling, or route lower-priority tenants to cheaper variants.
[08:20] Liam: If we host private on-prem, is there a management plane talkback? how much telemetry returns to Redwood?
[08:25] Ava: Private has a control plane with configurable telemetry. By default we collect health and billing metadata; sensitive payloads are not sent unless configured. We can do an air-gapped deployment where the control plane is fully customer-run.
[08:38] Sasha: HSM integration—what's the level of effort?
[08:41] Ava: We have documented integrations for common KMS/HSM vendors. effort depends on your infra; we typically estimate 2–4 weeks for full integration tests for enterprise customers.
[08:50] Daniel: On the pilot—do you replicate our tenant spike behavior including 300 qps bursts?
[08:55] Carlos: Absolutely. we'll run burst tests and show p50/p95/p99 latency under sustained and spike loads.
[09:03] Marta: Great. would be useful to have an SLA for P95 under dedicated reserved capacity.
[09:08] Priya: We provide SLA-backed latency and uptime for Dedicated—specific targets written into the contract once we lock in capacity and model profiles.
[09:17] (pause)
[09:25] (longer section omitted in transcript for brevity in export)
[28:10] Daniel: we also want to understand how model compaction/quantization impacts infer cost across different sequence lengths—our summarization jobs go up to 2k tokens.
[28:20] Carlos: For long sequences, throughput and memory matter more—quantization helps but kernel-level optimizations and KV caching for prefixes can tip the cost. We'll include benchmarks for 512, 1024, 2048 token lengths.
[28:35] Sasha: Any special licensing for model weights if we bring our own model to private?
[28:39] Priya: If you bring your own model you license it per your terms; our platform supports customer-supplied weights. We can run them in private or dedicated pools.
[28:46] Daniel: Are there differences in observability between hosted and private?
[28:49] Carlos: Hosted offers full observability in Redwood Console with token-level breakdowns and tracing. Private can export telemetry to your observability stack; we can enable the same dashboards but data path depends on deployment choices.
[29:00] Marta: On billing—do you provide cost-attribution per tenant?
[29:05] Priya: yes, we provide per-tenant token breakdowns, cost allocation, and tagging.
[29:10] Daniel: Okay let's summarise next steps.
[29:13] Priya: Recap: We'll send pricing workbook, security pack (SOC2 + retention options + KMS docs), and a benchmarking plan. You'll send sample traces and latency targets. Carlos will run benchmarks and share results. Then schedule a pilot kickoff with infra and security.
[29:28] Sasha: Sounds good.
[29:30] Liam: One last quick ask—can you provide an example of a routing policy file we could use?
[29:35] Carlos: Yes, we'll share a sample policy yaml showing routing by tenant, latency SLO, and cost thresholds.
[29:41] (wrap-up)
[29:45] Priya: Thanks everyone. we'll follow up with the docs and proposed dates.
[29:50] Daniel: Thank you, looking forward.
[29:52] (meeting ends; remaining 18 minutes were Q/A with infra team captured in internal notes - truncated in this export)

Notes on transcript quality: occasional speaker attribution glitches, some technical terms auto-corrected (KV cache -> K-V cache), tokenizer names redacted.

Shared artifacts mentioned:
- Hosted vs Dedicated pricing sheet (link to be sent)
- Benchmark plan spreadsheet: https://redwood.example/benchmarks/harboredge-plans (placeholder)
- SOC2 + Security pack (to be attached)
- Sample routing policy YAML (to be attached)

End of transcript.

title: Airlocked deployment readiness + update workflow — Meridian Diagnostics
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-04-09
Start: 14:30 PT
Duration: ~54 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE); Ravi Patel (Redwood SE); Mateo Gomez (Redwood SE); Dr. Priya Nair (CTO, Meridian); Liam O'Connor (Head of Infra); Sofia Ruiz (Compliance); Marcus Chen (ML Lead)

Summary: Initial discovery focused on feasibility of an airlocked / on-prem private deployment. Key themes: offline update workflow, signing/verification of update bundles, hardware constraints (GPU mix, NVMe capacity), compliance needs (FIPS, HSM/KMS, audit logs), and a proposed pilot scope.

Topics: deployment topology, update delivery, update validation, model lifecycle in airgapped environments, telemetry, rollout rollback, pilot scope.

[00:00:10] Ava (Redwood): Hey everyone — thanks for joining. Quick round robin and then we'll jump into the scenario you sent, the airlocked private deployment.
[00:00:18] Dr. Priya: Thanks. We wanted to make sure we cover how updates get into a network with zero external connectivity — basically our R&D clusters are airlocked, and any movement of models or binaries has to be through a controlled, signed process.
[00:00:30] Liam: Also hardware — we're mixing a few GPU types across racks, some older A10s and a handful of H100s on loan. We need to know how Redwood runtime behaves with heterogenous nodes.
[00:00:42] Ravi (Redwood): Totally — we see that a lot. I'll walk through runtime compatibility but short answer: runtime supports fallbacks and mixed pools but there are perf considerations, and we usually recommend homogenous pools for critical low-latency paths.
[00:00:55] Marcus: For our use-case, inference is per-patient triage — latency needs are tight for some endpoints, batchy for others. Also curious about KV cache persistence and disk-backed caches in airgapped mode.
[00:01:08] Mateo (Redwood): We have an offline cache spill to NVMe option; it's not enabled by default but for airgapped installs it's common. We should check your NVMe capacity and IOPS.

[00:03:12] Sofia (Meridian): On compliance, we need FIPS 140-2 validated crypto for key operations and an HSM for signing. Also long-retention audit logs for up to 7 years. How does Redwood Private integrate with customer KMS/HSM?
[00:03:26] Ava: Redwood Private supports HSM integration and customer-managed KMS; for completely offline setups, we provide tooling for signing update bundles with your HSM or with a hardware signing service. We also support audit log export to a local SIEM.

[00:04:40] Liam: Walk us through the update flow. Our security team insists any model or runtime change is reviewed, signed, and checksumed before it's allowed in. No direct pull from internet.
[00:04:51] Ravi: Common pattern: we prepare an update artifact (signed tarball) in your staging network or with Redwood in a controlled env; artifact includes signed manifests, model files, runtime image, and provenance metadata. On the airlocked side you run a verification utility that checks signatures and hashes against your root-of-trust before allowing install. This avoids outbound calls.
[00:05:10] Dr. Priya: And how do rollbacks work if a model shows regression post-deploy?
[00:05:17] Mateo: We keep a local model registry in the private deployment. Rollback is a local operation — the operator runs the signed manifest to revert to a previous version. We can also enable canary on a subset of nodes within the airlocked cluster.

[00:07:03] Marcus: Performance wise, if we pin a critical endpoint to H100 nodes only, will Redwood runtime respect node affinity and avoid routing to A10s?
[00:07:13] Ravi: Yes, routing policies allow affinity by node label (GPU type) or by tier. In an airlocked install the routing controller runs locally and honors the policies. But mixed pools can lead to head-of-line blocking if requests are routed to a slower node — so we account for that in SLO design.

[00:09:45] Liam: Storage — we currently have a mix of NVMe for hot models and a central Ceph cluster for larger artifacts. Does Redwood support streaming model loads from remote storage, or do models need to be local?
[00:09:58] Mateo: For best latency, models are staged locally on NVMe. We do have streaming for very large models, but in an airlocked, high-throughput setup we recommend local staging. Also advise pre-warming KV caches after a deployment to avoid cold-start latency.

[00:12:20] Sofia: Auditability — we need tamper-evident logs. If Redwood produces internal logs, can we forward them to our SIEM over an internal syslog?
[00:12:30] Ava: Yes, log forwarding to customer SIEMs via syslog or secure file drop is supported. Also we can configure log redaction rules to avoid PII leakage.

[00:14:00] Dr. Priya: Operationally, how often would we expect updates? Model updates are weekly; runtime patches maybe monthly. Is that manageable in an airlocked flow?
[00:14:12] Ravi: Weekly model updates are common. For airlocked sites the work is primarily around review + signing cadence. We provide a signed bundle tooling that produces a small update artifact and a manifest you can verify. If your ops team prefers, Redwood can host a signing service in an offline staging environment and hand over signed artifacts.

[00:15:33] Marcus: About telemetry — we can't let any PII or telemetry leave the airlocked environment. Can we limit telemetry to internal metrics only?
[00:15:43] Mateo: Absolutely. Telemetry is configurable: either no external telemetry, or local telemetry endpoints only. For hosted SLA we rely on Redwood console — for private airlocked we run the console locally and send only anonymized usage to support if you enable it.

[00:18:05] Liam: On hardware constraints — some racks only have PCIe x16 slots vs NVLink. Does Redwood runtime require NVLink for large context models or can it operate on PCIe-only nodes?
[00:18:18] Ravi: NVLink/NVSwitch helps for multi-GPU model parallel workloads. For single-GPU inference or sharded models that fit on one device, PCIe-only is fine. If you plan large-context LLMs that require tens of GB KV caches across GPUs we recommend NVSwitch. We'll size based on your target seq-len and QPS.

[00:20:40] Sofia: Encryption at rest — we need disk encryption and integrated key rotation. Can the runtime use disk encryption managed by our KMS?
[00:20:50] Ava: Yes — you can use customer-managed disk encryption keys; the runtime defers to the host OS or cloud provider KMS for disk encryption. For model encryption at rest we can integrate with your KMS/HSM for envelope encryption.

[00:23:12] Marcus: Any special considerations for quantized models? We might want int8 to save memory — does Redwood runtime handle quantized operators in the private build?
[00:23:22] Ravi: Yes, we ship quantization-friendly kernels. In airlocked installs we provide precompiled binaries for your target architecture or a build script. We recommend validating output quality as quantization can introduce small differences; Redwood Optimize can help propose quant profiles.

[00:26:05] Dr. Priya: Legal/procurement question: we have an internal procurement schedule and will need a contract for Private deployment — are there standard EULA/enterprise contracts or is this bespoke?
[00:26:16] Ava: We have standard enterprise templates and an addendum for airgapped deployments. For large customers we do minor redlines; we can share a redwood enterprise template. We'll include support SLAs for the pilot.

[00:28:30] Liam: For the pilot, our idea is two racks in a staging lab with mixed GPUs; run synthetic benchmarks and a small real workload with de-identified patient data. We want to validate throughput, latency, and the offline update process.
[00:28:45] Mateo: Great — typical pilot: 6 weeks. Week 1 prep and signing verification; Week 2 hardware bench and baseline; Weeks 3–4 model tuning and cache warm; Week 5 canary rollout; Week 6 wrap-up and SLO sign-off. We'll include a runbook for your ops.

[00:31:00] Sofia: One more on security: we require MFA for any admin console access and SSO via SAML. Can the private console be integrated with our SAML IdP?
[00:31:10] Ava: Yes SAML/SSO integration is supported for the private console, and MFA enforcement via your IdP is supported. We also support RBAC with scoped roles.

[00:33:20] Marcus: How are secrets handled inside the runtime? Are they held in memory only?
[00:33:28] Ravi: Secrets are pulled from KMS and held in memory; we avoid writing secrets to disk by design and use tmpfs for ephemeral caches when possible. For extra assurance we can enable HSM-backed key operations.

[00:35:40] Dr. Priya: Are there restrictions on which third-party models we can run? For licensing we may need provenance tracking.
[00:35:50] Mateo: We support operator-managed model registry and metadata for provenance. If you need licensing enforcement we can add attestations in the manifest and disallow models without required provenance.

[00:38:12] Liam: Let me ask about maintenance windows. If we apply a runtime patch, do all nodes need to be offline or is rolling update supported without full cluster downtime?
[00:38:21] Ravi: Rolling updates are supported; the scheduler drains and migrates in-flight work where possible. For some kernel-level driver updates you may need coordinated node reboots, which is usually planned in the maintenance window.

[00:41:00] Ava: Quick pricing note — private deployments have platform licensing plus per-node runtime licenses. Dedicated reserved capacity is an alternative if you want Redwood-managed hardware but isolated tenancy. We'll send a comparison.

[00:42:20] Marcus: Any gotchas you typically see with airlocked installs?
[00:42:28] Mateo: The usual: incorrect time sync causing signature verification issues, missing CA roots for the verification utility, insufficient NVMe IOPS leading to cache thrashing, and incomplete firewall rules inside the airlocked zone that block internal control traffic. We'll provide a checklist to avoid them.

[00:45:10] Sofia: Can you share an example signed bundle and a verification script ahead of pilot so our security team can pre-approve the flow?
[00:45:18] Ava: Yes — we'll include a sample signed artifact and a lightweight verification tool. Expect that in the security pack.

[00:47:50] Liam: Timing — procurement wants a pilot quote by next week ideally. Is that doable?
[00:47:57] Ava: We'll prioritize it. I'll email the pricing and pilot SOW draft by Monday EOD.

[00:49:30] Dr. Priya: Ok let's recap next steps. (short crosstalk)
[00:49:40] Ava: Recap: Redwood sends security pack + signed bundle example; Redwood provides hardware checklist + pilot SOW and cost; Meridian sends rack and GPU inventory; schedule hardware validation session.
[00:49:55] Sofia: Sounds good. I'll coordinate with procurement and security.

[00:51:05] Marcus: Minor nit — can we get the kernel ABI list you support? We have some older CUDA driver constraints.
[00:51:13] Ravi: We'll include supported CUDA/driver matrix and prebuilt runtime binaries for the common combinations (CUDA 12.x, 11.x, etc.).

[00:52:40] Ava: Thanks everyone. We'll follow up with the attachments. Any final Qs?
[00:52:47] Dr. Priya: None on my side. Thank you.
[00:52:52] Liam: Thanks.
[00:52:55] Meeting ended.

title: Pilot readiness & onboarding access playbook sync
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-03-31
Start: 09:30 PDT
Duration: ~48 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen (AE), Benji Kumar (SE), Sofia Martinez (CSM) -- Finova: Monica, Ethan, Priya, Daniel

Summary:
Quick sync on onboarding progress, access handoffs, and environment readiness. Covered initial bench results, a KV-cache regression that showed up in p95 at 1000rps, and next steps for KMS and VPC peering. Agreed to a short re-benchmark and to exchange an access playbook.

Topics:
- Onboarding status and outstanding creds
- Network / VPC peering checklist
- Security/KMS and audit concerns
- Benchmarks (p50/p95/p99), KV cache, batching profiles
- Next steps and canary timing

Transcript body:
[00:00] Ava Chen: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Quick roll call?
[00:07] Monica Reyes: Hi, Monica here. Got Ethan, Priya and Daniel on the call as well.
[00:10] Ethan Cole: Hey, Ethan.
[00:12] Priya Singh: Priya here.
[00:14] Daniel Park: Daniel.
[00:16] Benji Kumar: Benji from Redwood, hi all.
[00:20] Ava Chen: Cool. We're about 48 minutes, agenda sent in calendar: onboarding status, security handoff, results review, then plan for the next run. Anything to add?
[00:30] Monica: No, that's good. Maybe just to emphasize timeline — we still want to get to a small canary by mid April.
[00:36] Ava: Yes, week of Apr 13 we discussed. We'll confirm after today's items.
[00:40] Benji: I'll start with the bench notes — ran the first 2k token evals last Friday. p50 was 42 ms token, p95 337 ms token at 1000rps baseline with batching=manual small batch. However, we saw an odd spike when prefix cache was used on some requests — p95 climbed to ~520 ms for mixed prompt lengths. Suspect a KV cache invalidation edge where concurrent requests clobber the hot shard.
[01:12] Ethan: Can you clarify the test harness? Was this in our staging VPC or Redwood hosted sandbox?
[01:18] Benji: That run was against your staging in Redwood Dedicated pilot environment — so it's the same networking flows but we used staging CIDR. We did not enable the KMS integration yet (so token decryption locally) so that part was off.
[01:30] Priya: Wait, you said KMS off — that's intentional for initial bench, but for our security review we need to see KMS integrated before any canary. Also we need to verify the HSM-backed key usage in Private deployment — are we covered?
[01:44] Ava: Short answer: yes we support customer KMS via our Private option and the Dedicated flow can plug into your tenant KMS; we'll provide docs and a short config snippet. We'll put the detailed KMS playbook in the shared drive after this — action item for me.
[01:58] Daniel: Also need SAML/SSO onboarding for developer accounts — we have a separate onboarding ticket.
[02:04] Sofia Martinez: Noted, we've got the SSO ticket in process, but please send the exact attribute mapping that Redwood expects.
[02:12] Monica: Another question on data retention — can you confirm logs are kept X days by default and if we can configure?
[02:20] Benji: Default telemetry retention is 30 days in the console; enterprise packages can extend that. Audit logs can be exported to your S3/connector. We'll share the SOC2 package, which lists audit capabilities.
[02:34] Priya: SOC2 yes — send it, but also a short FAQ on what evidence we get during an audit would be helpful.
[02:42] Ava: We'll attach that.
[02:44] Benji: Back to the bench — when we toggled KV cache on, throughput improved for mostly identical prompt prefixes, but we saw the spike with mixed-length traffic. We reproduced it three times with different seed sets. My hunch is the cache eviction/rehydration path isn't parallel-safe under our current batching settings.
[03:06] Ethan: Could that be exacerbated by your prefix grouping algorithm? We send a lot of similar prompts but sometimes with small differences due to PII tags.
[03:14] Benji: Yes, prefix heuristics matter. For the next run I'll switch to continuous-batching and enable per-client shard isolation to see if that reduces clobbering.
[03:24] Monica: Sounds good. What do you need from us to re-run?
[03:28] Benji: Mostly the staging CIDR confirmation for the allowlists, and the test dataset — the previous runs used the sample_1 corpus you gave. If you can provide the production-ish prompt set (anonymized) that'll help. Also KMS toggle — once enabled, latency adds a small crypto overhead, we'll measure p95 delta.
[03:46] Daniel: I'll validate the CIDR, that's in progress. We'll also anonymize and push the prompt set to the shared pilot bucket; expect that today.
[03:54] Priya: On KMS integration, we need the admin contact. I can give you the email, but we also require a key rotation policy.
[04:02] Ava: Please send the contact and policy. We'll schedule a short runbook walk-through with your security team.
[04:08] Sofia: We'll include a step-by-step KMS integration doc and example terraform snippet.
[04:14] Monica: Good. On networking — VPC peering vs Private VPN? We've got corporate constraints that prefer VPC peering for regional traffic staying in-cloud.
[04:24] Benji: For Dedicated that's ideal — we support VPC peering and also Private (VPC) deployment. We'll give a peering checklist and required CIDR ranges. Note: for peering we need route table updates and the security groups for the Redwood egress IPs.
[04:38] Monica: Can you provide the egress ranges?
[04:40] Ava: Yes, we'll send exact CIDRs, and also a minimal IAM role and a sample security group. That's part of the access playbook.
[04:48] Ethan: We noticed a blacklist on outbound to certain IP ranges at our corp firewall; how do we coordinate the firewall exceptions?
[04:54] Benji: We'll provide a short list of endpoints and ports (443 mostly) and a phasing plan: first staging allowlist, then production allowlist. We'll also support a proxy if needed to funnel traffic through corp egress.
[05:06] Priya: Regarding telemetry, do you provide per-tenant logs that we can ship to our SIEM?
[05:10] Benji: Yes, we can stream logs to your SIEM via syslog or S3 ingestion. We'll include example lambda/connector.
[05:18] Monica: Ok let's talk timing: if Benji re-runs and we validate KMS by Apr 6, do you think canary mid-April is realistic?
[05:26] Benji: Yes if CIDR and KMS contact are provided quickly; the bench run should take 48h including post-analysis.
[05:32] Ava: We can't promise zero unknowns but it's feasible.
[05:36] Daniel: For the canary window, we'll need a rollback plan and runbook. Do you have an emergency fallback that shifts traffic away from Redwood to our prior model endpoint?
[05:44] Benji: We recommend an automated fallback policy — Redwood can route incompatible requests to a lower-latency but lower-accuracy model or back to your prior provider. We'll provide the canary runbook with stepwise rollback.
[05:54] Priya: Also want to confirm encryption-at-rest for embeddings?
[05:58] Benji: Embeddings stored in your namespace are encrypted; with KMS integration we use your key for the envelope. We'll outline this in the security playbook.
[06:06] Monica: Anything else on feature toggles — quantization or int8 profiles?
[06:10] Benji: We can run quantized profiles in Dedicated; that will reduce memory and lower per-token cost with small accuracy delta. For the pilot we'll hold on quant to match production accuracy early, but that can be a later optimization.
[06:22] Ava: Great. Let's recap next steps. Ava will send access playbook and peering checklist. Benji will re-run bench with KV shard isolation and continuous batching, then post results. Customer to confirm CIDR and KMS contact. We'll aim to schedule canary week of Apr 13.
[06:42] Monica: Sounds good. Thanks everyone.
[06:46] Benji: Thanks all, I'll ping the spreadsheet and upload logs.
[06:50] Ava: Meeting adjourned.

References/shared artifacts:
- Pilot spreadsheet (results): https://redwood.ai/pilot-results/finova-staging (placeholder)
- Access playbook (to be uploaded): https://redwood.ai/pilot-playbooks/FINOVA (will be populated)
- SOC2 / Security pack: https://redwood.ai/security/soc2-v2.pdf

Date: 2026-03-31
Start: 09:30 PDT
Duration: ~48 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen (AE), Benji Kumar (SE), Sofia Martinez (CSM) -- Finova: Monica, Ethan, Priya, Daniel

Summary:

Topics:
- Onboarding status and outstanding creds
- Network / VPC peering checklist
- Security/KMS and audit concerns

- Next steps and canary timing

[00:10] Ethan Cole: Hey, Ethan.
[00:12] Priya Singh: Priya here.
[00:14] Daniel Park: Daniel.
[00:16] Benji Kumar: Benji from Redwood, hi all.
[00:20] Ava Chen: Cool. We're about 48 minutes, agenda sent in calendar: onboarding status, security handoff, results review, then plan for the next run. Anything to add?

[00:36] Ava: Yes, week of Apr 13 we discussed. We'll confirm after today's items.

However, we saw an odd spike when prefix cache was used on some requests — p95 climbed to ~520 ms for mixed prompt lengths. Suspect a KV cache invalidation edge where concurrent requests clobber the hot shard.

[01:58] Daniel: Also need SAML/SSO onboarding for developer accounts — we have a separate onboarding ticket.
[02:04] Sofia Martinez: Noted, we've got the SSO ticket in process, but please send the exact attribute mapping that Redwood expects.
[02:12] Monica: Another question on data retention — can you confirm logs are kept X days by default and if we can configure?
[02:20] Benji: Default telemetry retention is 30 days in the console; enterprise packages can extend that. Audit logs can be exported to your S3/connector. We'll share the SOC2 package, which lists audit capabilities.
[02:34] Priya: SOC2 yes — send it, but also a short FAQ on what evidence we get during an audit would be helpful.
[02:42] Ava: We'll attach that.

We reproduced it three times with different seed sets. My hunch is the cache eviction/rehydration path isn't parallel-safe under our current batching settings.
[03:06] Ethan: Could that be exacerbated by your prefix grouping algorithm? We send a lot of similar prompts but sometimes with small differences due to PII tags.
[03:14] Benji: Yes, prefix heuristics matter. For the next run I'll switch to continuous-batching and enable per-client shard isolation to see if that reduces clobbering.
[03:24] Monica: Sounds good. What do you need from us to re-run?
[03:28] Benji: Mostly the staging CIDR confirmation for the allowlists, and the test dataset — the previous runs used the sample_1 corpus you gave. If you can provide the production-ish prompt set (anonymized) that'll help. Also KMS toggle — once enabled, latency adds a small crypto overhead, we'll measure p95 delta.

[03:54] Priya: On KMS integration, we need the admin contact. I can give you the email, but we also require a key rotation policy.
[04:02] Ava: Please send the contact and policy. We'll schedule a short runbook walk-through with your security team.
[04:08] Sofia: We'll include a step-by-step KMS integration doc and example terraform snippet.
[04:14] Monica: Good. On networking — VPC peering vs Private VPN? We've got corporate constraints that prefer VPC peering for regional traffic staying in-cloud.
[04:24] Benji: For Dedicated that's ideal — we support VPC peering and also Private (VPC) deployment. We'll give a peering checklist and required CIDR ranges. Note: for peering we need route table updates and the security groups for the Redwood egress IPs.
[04:38] Monica: Can you provide the egress ranges?
[04:40] Ava: Yes, we'll send exact CIDRs, and also a minimal IAM role and a sample security group. That's part of the access playbook.
[04:48] Ethan: We noticed a blacklist on outbound to certain IP ranges at our corp firewall; how do we coordinate the firewall exceptions?
[04:54] Benji: We'll provide a short list of endpoints and ports (443 mostly) and a phasing plan: first staging allowlist, then production allowlist. We'll also support a proxy if needed to funnel traffic through corp egress.
[05:06] Priya: Regarding telemetry, do you provide per-tenant logs that we can ship to our SIEM?
[05:10] Benji: Yes, we can stream logs to your SIEM via syslog or S3 ingestion. We'll include example lambda/connector.
[05:18] Monica: Ok let's talk timing: if Benji re-runs and we validate KMS by Apr 6, do you think canary mid-April is realistic?
[05:26] Benji: Yes if CIDR and KMS contact are provided quickly; the bench run should take 48h including post-analysis.
[05:32] Ava: We can't promise zero unknowns but it's feasible.
[05:36] Daniel: For the canary window, we'll need a rollback plan and runbook. Do you have an emergency fallback that shifts traffic away from Redwood to our prior model endpoint?
[05:44] Benji: We recommend an automated fallback policy — Redwood can route incompatible requests to a lower-latency but lower-accuracy model or back to your prior provider. We'll provide the canary runbook with stepwise rollback.
[05:54] Priya: Also want to confirm encryption-at-rest for embeddings?
[05:58] Benji: Embeddings stored in your namespace are encrypted; with KMS integration we use your key for the envelope. We'll outline this in the security playbook.
[06:06] Monica: Anything else on feature toggles — quantization or int8 profiles?
[06:10] Benji: We can run quantized profiles in Dedicated; that will reduce memory and lower per-token cost with small accuracy delta. For the pilot we'll hold on quant to match production accuracy early, but that can be a later optimization.
[06:22] Ava: Great. Let's recap next steps. Ava will send access playbook and peering checklist. Benji will re-run bench with KV shard isolation and continuous batching, then post results. Customer to confirm CIDR and KMS contact. We'll aim to schedule canary week of Apr 13.
[06:42] Monica: Sounds good. Thanks everyone.
[06:46] Benji: Thanks all, I'll ping the spreadsheet and upload logs.

title: Inference Capacity Staircase & Rollbacks Playbook
preview:
Header:
Date: 2026-03-18
Start: 11:00 UTC
Duration: ~75 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen, Mateo Alvarez, Priya Nair, Liam O'Connor, Jordan Blake, Elena Park, Noah Wright, Sana Mir, Greg Foster

Summary:
Quick internal workshop to align on a concrete 'capacity staircase' (phased ramps), explicit rollback criteria for model and capacity regressions, and a short playbook for dry-run/drill execution. Focus was on launch readiness for the next private deployment wave and a plan to avoid cost surprises while keeping SLOs.

Topics:
- Capacity staircase definition (levels, triggers)
- Canary and dry-run plan
- Rollback criteria: quality regressions vs infra regressions
- Observability needs and runbook edits
- Security/audit notes for private deployments

Action items (captured live): see metadata section above.

Transcript body:
[00:00] Ava: alright, uh thanks everyone for squeezing this in, I know the calendars are tight. the goal is to leave with a concrete, like, staircase thresholds doc and the first dry-run scheduled.
[00:18] Elena: yep, from exec side want clear guardrails — if latency crosses 220ms p95 for more than 5min we need an automatic step down, or at least an alert that triggers rollback.
[00:30] Noah: I think 220 as a hard number might be tough on the 8k contexts, we should scope by model size. maybe per-model thresholds, and then a global SLO budget.
[00:45] Mateo: agreed. we have telemetry split by model+region already but we need a staircase with 3 levels: baseline, stress, and surge. baseline is normal autoscale, stress is reserved burst, surge is emergency cap (manual). each level has triggers: latency, error rate, token throughput.
[01:05] Priya: can we call them v1/v2/v3? the names matter less than the actions — for v2 the plan is increase batch window slightly and enable KV caching for prefixes, for v3 we should auto-fallback to a quantized variant or smaller model.
[01:25] Liam: caution on auto-fallback — there's user-affecting quality regressions. we need rollback criteria both for infra failures and for quality regressions. that means an observable that's not just latency: e.g. eval prompt set failure rate or hallucination proxy.
[01:40] Sana: on the security side, any autoscaling or rollbacks in private deployments must be logged with the instance id and KMS key usage. audit trail is mandatory for some customers. also, VPC egress events need to be captured.
[02:00] Jordan: ok capturing, also note that cost must be part of the staircase. if burst > X token-minutes we trigger a cost review window and possibly step-down. otherwise finance will freak.
[02:15] Greg: for dry-run we should pick a low-traffic weekend slot and run a canary that ramps traffic using replay plus synthetic tails to exercise long-context paths. the sim should include KV cache warmup so we don't get skewed results.
[02:35] Ava: agreed — Mateo you own the sim plan?
[02:40] Mateo: yep, i'll do a 4-hour sim with three ramps, I'll include latency, p95/p99, tail lat, token throughput, and error rate. spreadsheet will have columns for thresholds and pass/fail criteria.
[02:55] Elena: what's the rollback mechanism we're comfortable with? full traffic cut or model variant fallback?
[03:05] Priya: prefer staged: first fallback model variant (quantized), if QA metric drops below threshold then step to capacity step-down (move traffic to smaller region or dedicated pool), last resort cut traffic. incorporate vote from oncall.
[03:25] Liam: that needs clear criteria: e.g., quality proxy > 2% fail on eval set OR latency p99 > 500ms for 10 minutes leads to vote. we should automate the vote if it's infra-exit (OOMs, GPU errors).
[03:45] Noah: on capacity accounting: the staircase should expose reserved burst buckets per customer so we can bill and also stop accidental overrun. we should include a strap on the console that shows current staircase stage and remaining burst minutes.
[04:05] Priya: we talked about KV cache being mis-heard in prior calls as 'KV cash' — note: caching warms cost but helps latency; we'll measure cost per token with cache on/off.
[04:20] Jordan: heh yep, recorded.
[04:25] Ava: operational playbook edits — Liam, can you propose amendments to runbook for staged rollback, including who has the unilateral authority to move to v2/v3?
[04:40] Liam: i'll propose that oncall can move to v2 (stress) but v3 (surge) requires approval from capacity lead + product (Noah + Elena). final traffic cut needs VP ok.
[04:55] Greg: and we'll add a checklist for the dry-run: prechecks (metrics sane), smoke test endpoints, KMS audit hook enabled, and roll-forward plan.
[05:10] Ava: timeline check: draft stairs doc by Monday (Ava), sim by Wednesday (Mateo), rollback hook prototype by next Friday (Priya), dry-run targeted end of next week (Greg).
[05:25] Elena: quick question — how do we handle private deployments where customers want full manual control? do we still enforce staircases?
[05:35] Sana: for privacy customers we ship the scaffolding; by default it's opt-in for automatic steps, but we still require audit logs and a manual approval path. the defaults will be conservative.
[05:50] Mateo: technical gotcha — when we do model variant fallback we need to ensure KV cache format compatibility or else cache miss storms will happen. we should add a cache compatibility check in the rollback hook.
[06:05] Priya: yep, that's on my prototype scope. i'll include a quick latency-safe migration that invalidates prefixes older than N seconds to avoid cold-miss avalanche.
[06:25] Jordan: can we add a short 'blast radius' diagram to the staircase doc that maps components affected (frontend, router, runtime, kv cache, billing)?
[06:40] Ava: good idea, Jordan — that'll help execs understand risk.
[06:50] (crosstalk)
[07:00] Ava: ok smaller notes: include specific SLO windows, escalation path, and the exact telemetry dashboards we'll need. Mateo, please list dashboard IDs so we can pair them to thresholds.
[07:18] Mateo: will do — i'm also going to include an 'emergency capacity token' concept that is separate from reserved burst and requires manual approval.
[07:30] Noah: one more ask — instrument the console to show last rollback reason: quality/infrastructure/cost. that's critical for postmortems.
[07:45] Priya: agreed. building that into trace logs is straightforward but we need UX work to surface it. I'll coordinate with Jordan for the wire.
[07:58] Ava: wrapping — any final blockers?
[08:05] Elena: none here. just want the doc to be crisp.
[08:10] Liam: need to validate SSO and audit hooks for private customers before dry-run. Sana, can you sign off once done?
[08:20] Sana: yes i'll sign off once I see KMS audit logs and VPC egress hooks in place.
[08:30] Ava: cool. recap next steps in chat and i'll own the doc.
[08:35] (meeting ends)

Post-call quick notes (auto):
- Some speech mis-recognition around 'KV cache' and 'canary sim' replaced with 'KV cash' and 'candy sim' in earlier auto transcript; corrected above.
- A few timestamps drift because of overlapping talkers; manual cleanup recommended if used for legal/audit purposes.

Recap (final):
- Deliverables: staircase thresholds doc (Ava), canary sim results spreadsheet (Mateo), rollback hook prototype (Priya), dry-run schedule and checklist (Greg), runbook edits (Liam).
- Dry-run target: weekend slot week of 2026-03-29.
- Acceptance criteria: per-model thresholds pass in sim, KV cache compatibility verified, security audit hooks present, and rollback behavior tested with postmortem template filled.

References shared during call:
- \"staircase-spec-v0\" doc link (to be attached in calendar)
- canary-spreadsheet template (sheet link)
- runbook PR template (repo link)

End of transcript.

Security and on-prem constraints discussed; next steps include experiments on Helio dev cluster and sharing low-level perf traces.

title: Strata prefetch heuristics weekly POC sync
preview:
Meeting header
Date: 2026-03-11 14:00 PT
Duration: 42 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Diego Morales (Redwood SE), Maya Patel (ML Eng, StrataOps), Tom Rivers (Platform Eng), Sara Kim (CTO)

Summary:
- Weekly POC sync focusing on prefetch/kv cache hit-rate improvements and prompt reuse strategy. Reviewed last week's changes, current numbers, and tuned eviction/prefetch thresholds. Agreed next steps for 24h experiment and docs.

Topics:
- Current KV hit-rate baseline and measurement noise
- Prefetch heuristics (time-based vs frequency-based)
- Prompt trimming / canonicalization for reuse
- VPC/private deployment constraints and data residency
- Pilot pricing and success criteria

Action items listed separately below.

Transcript:
[00:00] Ava: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Quick roll call and then we'll jump into the metrics. Diego, you up for the runbook recap?
[00:08] Diego: Yeah, thanks Av— uh Ava. Hi Maya, Tom, Sara. So last week we changed the prefetch window from 2s to 1s and lowered the eviction threshold for low-frequency prefixes, we also added a canonicalization pass for prompts that strips user metadata, so small changes.
[00:25] Maya: Yeah, we saw a... like immediate bump in prefix reuse on the mid-tier traffic, roughly, um, 6 to 9% depending on the cohort. But the KV hit-rate stayed kinda flat for the cold set.
[00:37] Tom: To add, our tails still spike at 95th; saw a lot of extra AOk latency when the cache warmed up. Might be due to our batching sizes too.
[00:46] Diego: Right, I think the prefetch heuristics helped warm hot prefixes faster but we didn't tune frequency-weighted eviction, so low-frequency prefixes keep evicting and not coming back.
[00:55] Ava: Can you remind me of the exact numbers? Maya, you had the dashboard open?
[01:00] Maya: Yeah, baseline— before changes we had KV hit-rate at 21.4% overall. After prefetch tweak the overall jumped to 23.1, prefix reuse increased from 12.7 to 18.9 for our 'session' cohort, but the cold cohort remained around 8.3.
[01:18] Sara: So is that enough? Our success criteria was a 15% delta in latency or 20% cost reduction, right?
[01:24] Ava: Right, goal is end-to-end token cost improvement and tail latency. Diego, what's the latency delta on average?
[01:30] Diego: P50 improved by about 7%, p95 about 3% but with higher variance. The variance comes from large prompts where canonicalization didn't apply— the user metadata in our app still varies a lot.
[01:43] Tom: That's the thing— our prompt surface has a bunch of session IDs and random UUIDs injected, so reuse is low unless you normalize.
[01:50] Maya: We've been trying a regex strip but it's noisy — sometimes we remove relevant context.
[01:55] Diego: Right, so we prototyped a canonicalizer that keeps entity tokens and strips noise— it's heuristic-based. On a sample set it improved reuse by about 11% without hurting quality on our eval prompts.
[02:07] Sara: Any risk to accuracy/regressions? We can't degrade NPS on assistant answers.
[02:12] Diego: We ran a small eval (n=500) with human check— no statistically significant regressions. There are edge cases; we flagged 12 prompts where semantics changed slightly.
[02:21] Ava: Good call. Let's add those to the prompt exclusion list. Also, are we comfortable with the measurement? I noticed the team uses two definitions of hit-rate across dashboards.
[02:31] Maya: Sorry yeah, there's metric drift— the infra dashboard counts prefix hits only, while the model side counts KV hits post-merge. We need a unified definition.
[02:39] Tom: We can align to the 'post-merge KV hit' since that's what affects token generation cost.
[02:43] Ava: Perfect. Diego, can you update the instrumentation? Also include token-level cost delta per hit vs miss.
[02:49] Diego: Yep, will add a column for estimated per-token cost savings and roll it into the next export.
[02:53] Maya: Quick question, prefetch policy— time-based vs frequency-based: what are the tradeoffs again? We saw time-based warms predictable hot sessions but frequency-based catches recurring patterns across sessions.
[03:04] Diego: Exactly. Time-based is simpler and lower overhead, good for session-heavy workloads. Frequency-based requires tracking prefix counts across a sliding window, more state but better for cross-session reuse.
[03:14] Ava: For StrataOps use-case, Tom, you said most reuse appears across users in a 12-24 hour window, right?
[03:20] Tom: Yeah, especially for the 'alerts' flow— lots of similar prompts across users but not in same session. So frequency-based prefetch might be the win.
[03:27] Sara: Does frequency prefetch increase memory or network overhead significantly?
[03:31] Diego: It increases bookkeeping— we maintain counts and perform background prefetches. Memory impact depends on thresholds; we propose a conservative threshold to limit prefetch candidates to top 5k prefixes.
[03:41] Maya: How about eviction— LRU vs LFU hybrid? We tried pure LRU and it thrashes when many short-lived prefixes appear.
[03:48] Diego: LFU hybrid with recency decay seems to work best— it preserves frequently used prefixes while letting transient ones fall out. Our pilot used a decay factor of 0.6 and it looked stable.
[03:58] Ava: Any production security considerations— for private deployment, KMS, logs, PII stripping?
[04:04] Tom: We'll need KMS integration, we also have strict audit log retention policies. StrataOps compliance team will want SOC2 documentation and encryption at rest.
[04:13] Ava: We'll send the SOC2 summary and our private deploy checklist after the call.
[04:17] Sara: Good. Also want to see how data residency works for EU customers— we might need region pinning.
[04:22] Ava: Noted— we'll include region options in the follow-up doc.
[04:26] Maya: Back to tuning: Diego, you mentioned a 24-hour KV-only experiment. Walk us through expected datasets and what you'll measure.
[04:34] Diego: Sure. We'll run with frequency-based prefetch enabled, canonicalization on by default, and LFU-decay eviction. We'll sample traffic from production using a 1% mirror; measure per-prefix hit-rate, aggregate KV hit, latency p50/p95, and token cost estimate. We'll produce a CSV and dashboard snapshot.
[04:48] Tom: Mirror— okay we can enable that next Tuesday's window. We need to confirm PII filtering before shipping samples.
[04:54] Maya: I'll scrub and provide the samples. Expect them by Monday.
[04:58] Ava: Great. Any blockers on running the mirror from Redwood side?
[05:02] Diego: We need a short-lived ingest endpoint and IAM role for the mirrored traffic. Tom, can you provision?
[05:08] Tom: Yes, I can do that— will ping infra to open a peering and create role.
[05:12] Sara: Also want to set a clear success gate for the 24h run— what's acceptable?
[05:17] Diego: From our side we'd look for a net KV hit-rate improvement >=5 percentage points and tail latency improvement of at least 5% without quality regressions.
[05:25] Maya: That's reasonable. We'll also track cost per 1k requests.
[05:29] Ava: Ok, next topic: prompt reuse strategy— any new thoughts on canonicalization thresholds?
[05:35] Maya: On our last batch we saw that aggressive trimming (removing entire 'context' sections) reduced quality for tasks that rely on that context, so we think we should do per-template rules.
[05:45] Diego: We can implement template-aware canonicalization— the idea is to parse by template ID and apply rules per template. That requires template metadata though.
[05:53] Tom: We do have template IDs for the alerts flow, but legacy endpoints don't tag them. Might need a rollout over time.
[05:58] Ava: For the pilot, can we focus on top 20 templates that represent 70% of traffic?
[06:03] Maya: Yes, we can extract that.
[06:05] Diego: We'll build a lightweight mapping and run canonicalization only for mapped templates; fallback to conservative regex for others.
[06:12] Sara: What about model variants— fallback policies? If model A is quantized, does KV cache behavior change?
[06:18] Diego: The runtime normalizes keys across compatible model variants, but quantization can affect latency. Prefetch still helps; the cost delta is model-specific so we'll include model tags in exports.
[06:28] Ava: Any competitor concerns— were you evaluating Anthropic or others?
[06:32] Maya: We looked at Anthropic earlier but their hosted pricing plus slower tail made them less attractive. We're still evaluating multiple vendors for redundancy.
[06:40] Ava: Noted.
[06:42] Diego: Quick gotcha— when we canonicalize we add a trace header so we can attribute hits back to original prompt, helps debugging.
[06:49] Tom: Helpful. Also can we get a fail-open on canonicalization if the tracer flags a potential semantic drift?
[06:56] Diego: Yep, we can add a safety check— when the canonicalizer confidence score < threshold, skip.
[07:02] Maya: Let's talk numbers— Diego, can you share the current CSV snapshot now?
[07:06] Diego: Sure, I'll paste a small excerpt. (audio share) "prefix,hit_count,miss_count,kv_hit_rate,p50_ms,p95_ms"
[07:14] Diego: "alerts:summary,1345,1023,0.568,18,112"
[07:19] Diego: "session:reply,423,1878,0.183,8,96"
[07:24] Maya: That session:reply line explains the low overall hit-rate— sessions are low reuse.
[07:28] Ava: For the CSV, we'll add an estimated tokens-saved column.
[07:32] Diego: Agreed.
[07:34] (crosstalk)
[07:36] Tom: Quick infra note— we have a maintenance window Friday; don't schedule heavy profiling then.
[07:41] Ava: Good to know.
[07:42] Sara: Any pricing impact? If we drive hit-rate up 5pp what's the expected bill delta?
[07:47] Diego: Ballpark, depends on model and per-token cost; for your mix we'd expect 8-12% reduction in inference cost if KV hit-rate increases 5pp.
[07:56] Maya: That's huge.
[07:57] Ava: Ok we'll include a cost model spreadsheet. I'll share it after the call.
[08:02] Diego: One more ask— can StrataOps enable a short canary for private deployment? We can demo VPC peering and KMS in a sandbox.
[08:10] Tom: Yes, we can open a sandbox VPC.
[08:12] Ava: Great. Let's recap next steps.

Action items:
[08:20] - Diego Morales: run 24h KV prefetchexperiment, produce CSV + dashboard, due 2026-03-18
[08:28] - Ava Chen: share pilot pricing + SOC2 summary + private deploy checklist, due 2026-03-13
[08:34] - Maya Patel: scrub and upload traffic sample, due 2026-03-16
[08:39] - Tom Rivers: provision ingest endpoint and IAM role for mirror, due 2026-03-15

Next meeting: 2026-03-18 14:00 PT (tentative)

Shared artifacts referenced:
- pilot_metrics_snapshot.csv (drive link)
- prefetch-runbook-v1.md (internal doc)
- pilot-pricing-v2.xlsx (pricing spreadsheet)

Notes on transcription: some names may be mis-attributed due to overlapping talk; timestamps are approximate.

End of transcript.

title: MercuryBank QBR / reliability review
preview:
Meeting: MercuryBank QBR / reliability review
Recorded: 2026-03-10 16:00 UTC
Duration: 42 minutes
Attendees: Ben Carter, Ava Chen, Dev Patel, Jordan Wells, Sana Iqbal

Summary:
- Overall satisfaction good; throughput is meeting targets.
- One open pain point: streaming sometimes cuts out during peak load (partial answers).

Topics:
1) Reliability metrics
2) Streaming behavior under load
3) Next quarter roadmap

Action items:
- Ben to send retry guidance + best practices for SSE clients
- Ava to coordinate support ticket if request_ids confirm a platform issue

Transcript
[00:00] Ben Carter: cool, thanks everyone. wanted to start with last quarters uptime and latency.
[00:38] Jordan Wells: overall good. the one thing that still bites us is streaming. sometimes the response just stops mid-way. no clear error.
[01:05] Ava Chen: to clarify you mean you stop receiving events? like it stalls?
[01:10] Sana Iqbal: yeah. the connection doesnt always close. we just stop getting chunks. our client times out and then we retry. it happens more during our peak traffic window.
[02:02] Dev Patel: is this hosted or dedicated?
[02:05] Jordan Wells: hosted in us-east.
[02:22] Ben Carter: ok. weve been tracking similar reports where SSE gets buffered and it looks like it freezes. we put a keepalive thing in one region; need to confirm rollouts.
[03:10] Ava Chen: can you share a couple request ids? we can pull logs on our side.
[03:18] Sana Iqbal: yes, we can grab a few from our traces.
[40:10] Ben Carter: recap well send retry guidance, and once we have request ids well open an escalation ticket if it looks platform-side.

title: Ops Admin: maintenance, budget & security rendezvous
preview:
HEADER:
Date: 2026-03-05
Start: 15:00 UTC
Duration: ~45 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen, Liam Morgan, Priya Nair, Carlos Reyes, Jules Park, Marta Velasquez, Devon Li

SUMMARY:
Quick sync across IT, Finance, and Security to reconcile planned maintenance windows, budget impacts for Q2 reserved capacity, and outstanding compliance evidence for an upcoming audit. Goal: agree on next steps for maintenance scheduling and ensure KMS rotation + SOC2 evidence are on-track.

TOPICS:
- Q2 maintenance windows and blackout periods
- Reserved capacity billing implications and reforecast
- KMS rotation + key custody (on-prem vs cloud HSM)
- SOC2 evidence gap list and owners
- Procurement timeline for additional GPUs / dedicated capacity

ACTION ITEMS (captured verbally, also in metadata):
- Ava to draft maintenance runbook + send to attendees
- Priya to pull and annotate SOC2 evidence list; call out any missing items
- Liam to reforecast committed instance usage and budget impact
- Devon to confirm downstream infra constraints for maintenance windows

TRANSCRIPT:
[00:00:00] Ava: alright good afternoon everyone, thanks for jumping on. i think we've got about 45 minutes. quick round robin — can we do updates 2 minutes each?
[00:00:12] Liam: yeah that works, i just wanna flag the Q2 numbers, we've got an uptick in reserved usage, the committed instances line is a little higher than forecasted so i'll share a reforecast later.
[00:00:28] Priya: quick from security, SOC two evidence pull is in progress, but KMS rotation is pending and it depends on whether we go HSM or cloud-kms again — couple of vendors ask for HSM certs which we need to validate.
[00:00:45] Carlos: facilities note, planned datacenter maint on 3/20 might affect network tiering, but i'm waiting on the vendor window.
[00:01:02] Jules: procurement is tracking the PO for extra dedicated nodes, timeline is two to three weeks once legal signs off.
[00:01:18] Marta: legal is reviewing the contracts, mostly standard, a couple of redlines on data processing addendum for private deployments.
[00:01:34] Devon: infra side: VPC peering and routing for the private stack — if we do a 2-hour maintenance window we need to ensure KV cache warmup after restart, otherwise latency spike.
[00:01:50] Ava: great; ok let's go item by item. first, maintenance windows. proposed date 3/20 as Carlos mentioned, 2 hours during off-peak 02:00-04:00 UTC. any blockers?
[00:02:05] Devon: that time looks ok but i need to confirm on-call rotation. also the auto-scaling polices might create a fan-out of cold starts, we should pre-warm critical services.
[00:02:21] Liam: cost question — if we pre-warm, that's extra minutes on dedicated, do we bill that as reserved or on-demand? i need the accounting treatment for Q2 forecast.
[00:02:36] Priya: for security, pre-warm means we must also ensure keys are valid post-rotate. if K M S isn't rotated before the window we could have auth failures.
[00:02:52] Ava: ok action: Priya will confirm KMS rotation timing. Liam will model pre-warm cost into forecast. Devon will outline the warmup playbook.
[00:03:07] Priya: quick clarification, when you say KMS rotation do you mean full key roll or just rekeying material? we have to coordinate with the HSM vendor if we go that route.
[00:03:22] Ava: good point, we should define scope. for this maintenance we prefer a rekey with a gradual rollover, not full immediate key replacement.
[00:03:36] Devon: noted. also KV cache persistence — the runtime supports prefix caching but if nodes restart the cache loses hot tokens, we might see a 200-300ms median increase briefly.
[00:03:52] Liam: 200-300ms is material for SLAs, finance will need to account for potential incident cost credit. is there a fallback to a lower-cost model variant during the warm-up?
[00:04:07] Ava: yes we can route to a smaller model for non-critical routes using routing policies; but product teams will push back, so we need comms.
[00:04:21] Jules: procurement question — if we delay the PO sign-off till after 3/20 maintenance, will lead time slip? vendor said 10 business days once PO is in.
[00:04:36] Marta: legal can expedite DPA sign-off but need a final vendor appendix. i'll loop in vendor counsel.
[00:04:50] Priya: SOC two: we have about 7 evidence items outstanding — change control logs, KMS rotation attestations, and a recent pen test report. i'm compiling a list.
[00:05:04] Ava: can you read out the top three missing so we can assign owners?
[00:05:12] Priya: sure — change control tickets for the runtime deploys (owner: infra), KMS rotation policy doc (owner: security), and updated endpoint access matrix for the private deployment (owner: product ops).

title: Acme  Private upgrade escalation (rollback keeps re-triggering)
preview:
Meeting header
- Date: 2026-02-14
- Time: 1:00 PM PT
- Duration: 41 minutes
- Attendees: Ava Chen, Ethan Park, Nina Shah, Omar Raza

Summary
Customer reports that the Private upgrade is stuck and rollback keeps re-triggering. Redwood shares a workaround involving deleting a rollback lock ConfigMap and restarting the installer controller. Discussed guardrails and confirmation steps.

Transcript
[00:00] Ava Chen: Thanks for jumping on. We saw your email thread. Want to make sure we get you unblocked today.
[00:18] Nina Shah: Yeah, its been uh rolling back since last night. The UI just says rollback in progress, and it never ends.
[00:35] Ethan Park: Got it. Weve seen a similar failure mode. In logs do you see it repeating like scheduling rollback retry?
[00:52] Omar Raza: Exactly. Its like it fails, waits thirty seconds, and then does it again.
[01:10] Ava Chen: Okay. There is a workaround but it involves a couple kubectl commands. Are you comfortable running those?
[01:22] Nina Shah: Yep, thats fine, just need to be sure its safe.
[01:35] Ethan Park: The key is: only delete the specific configmap called `installer-rollback-lock` in the `redwood-private` namespace. Dont touch anything else.
[02:01] Omar Raza: And then restart the controller deployment?
[02:05] Ethan Park: Yeah, `rollout restart deploy/installer-controller`. That should allow it to complete and stop the loop.
[02:24] Nina Shah: Is this a known bug?
[02:30] Ethan Park: Yes, tracked internally. Sometimes youll hear us call it a rollback loop or stuck rollback. Were working on a patch so it cant happen again.
[03:10] Ava Chen: After you run it, can you paste back the log lines around the time it transitions?
[03:22] Nina Shah: Sure.
[05:10] Omar Raza: Just to confirm, were currently on 2.8.0 trying 2.9.0.
[05:18] Ethan Park: Thats consistent with what weve seen.
[38:40] Ava Chen: Recap: run the two commands, send us the logs, and well confirm patch timing for 2.9.1. Thanks everyone.

title: Arcadia Health - weekly check-in (streaming issue follow-up)
preview:
Meeting Header
Date: 2026-02-03
Time: 2:00pm PT
Duration: 44 min
Attendees: Ava Chen, Alex Martinez (Redwood) | Nina Patel, Samir Desai (Arcadia Health)

Summary (auto)
- Discussed recent streaming freeze where responses stopped mid-generation.
- Reviewed gateway change (disable gzip for SSE, periodic flush).
- Agreed on client-side timeouts and retry behavior.

Transcript
[00:00] Ava Chen: Hey Nina, Samir. Thanks for hopping on. Wanted to do a quick follow-up on the streaming freeze you saw last week.
[00:18] Nina Patel: Yeah, thanks. It was pretty bad. The stream would just, like, stop halfway through. No done event, and our UI spinner would spin forever.
[00:36] Alex Martinez: Right, that matches what we saw. On our side it looked like the connection stayed open but no bytes were being flushed.
[00:55] Samir Desai: Exactly. We werent getting an error, so our retry logic didnt trigger. We just sat there.
[01:14] Ava Chen: Since the change went out, have you seen any of those mid-generation freezes?
[01:22] Nina Patel: Since Monday, no. We ran a bunch of tests  like hundreds  and it hasnt frozen.
[01:40] Alex Martinez: Great. For context, the gateway was gzipping the event stream, which can buffer. We turned that off for text/event-stream and added a periodic heartbeat flush.
[02:05] Samir Desai: Makes sense. We also added a client read timeout so we dont hang forever if something like this happens again.
[02:22] Ava Chen: Good. Well keep monitoring and also document the recommended client-side behavior for streaming.
[03:01] Nina Patel: Cool. The main thing is we cant have the stream freeze mid-answer. That was killing agent productivity.
[43:10] Ava Chen: Recap: well watch metrics for a week and youll keep the timeout + idempotency retries. Well follow up next Tuesday.

preview:

Summary (auto-generated, brief):
Northbridge is evaluating Redwood Private/Dedicated for a genomi.cs POC where data must remain region-locked and not leave their VPC. Primary concerns: VPC peering vs PrivateLink, egress restrictions, KMS/HSM integration, benchmark workloads (peak inference rps, batch size, context lengths), and timeline for a 4-week pilot. Next steps: share diagrams, benchmark plan, and SOC2/KMS docs; schedule follow-up for egress test.

Topics Covered:
- POC success criteria and acceptance tests
- Networking options: VPC Peering vs AWS PrivateLink vs on-prem appliance
- Region locking & residency controls (EU vs US regions)
- Egress constraints and firewall/ACL checks
- Workload sizing: tokens per request, concurrency, batch strategies
- Benchmarks: latency SLOs, throughput targets, test harness
- Timeline and stakeholders

Transcript:
[00:00] Ava Chen: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Quick0 reconfirm, I'm Ava, AE on Redwood Inference, with Marco, our SE, and Priya. Jamie, Riley — thanks for making time.
[00:10] Dr. Jamie Patel: Thanks, Ava. We wanna run this POC because our research pipelines are generating sensitive genome sequences. The ask is we need inference to run strictly inside our VPC, no egress to public internet, and data residency in us-west-2.
[00:22] Riley Gomez: Yeah and also we have strict egress rules — only specific egress endpoints allowed via our firewall.
[00:28] Marco Alvarez: Understood. From Redwood side we can do a couple of patterns. VPC peering is the simplest for low-latency private connectivity, PrivateLink is more managed but has some limits on IPs and endpoints, and we also support private deployments on dedicated hardware if you need an on-prem box or air-gapped.
[00:42] Samir Das: Quick q: does PrivateLink avoid route table changes on our side? We prefer not to change our main route tables for compliance.
[00:49] Marco Alvarez: PrivateLink uses an ENI in your VPC — you don't need broad route table changes, you create an interface endpoint and your traffic to the endpoint goes via the private network. But there are egress considerations if the inference runtime reaches out for telemetry or model pulls; by default Redwood hosted control plane may pull models unless we configure pinned artifacts or push to your private registries.
[01:10] Dr. Jamie Patel: Model artifacts must be pulled from within our VPC, we cannot allow outbound to an external S3. Is there a push option?
[01:17] Priya Kapoor: Yes — for Private deployments we can either pre-stage model artifacts into your S3 or we can set up an SFTP transfer. For Dedicated it's typical to pre-stage into your S3 bucket then we configure the runtime to read from that bucket with a customer KMS key.
[01:30] Riley Gomez: What's the difference for latency and scaling between VPC Peering vs PrivateLink?
[01:35] Marco Alvarez: Peering is L3 and generally slightly lower latency and less throttling overhead. PrivateLink introduces a network hop through the endpoint, which is usually 1–4 ms extra but it's more controlled. The tradeoff is peering requires CIDR management and potential route table changes; PrivateLink is easier operationally.
[01:51] Lina Park: Our workload is mix of small queries (single gene lookup, ~200 tokens) and long assembly prompts (~2k tokens). We also want batch scoring for nightly jobs.
[02:03] Marco Alvarez: Ok that's helpful. For sizing we'll need rough QPS for both interactive and batch. For interactive, what's target p95 latency that you'd consider acceptable?
[02:11] Dr. Jamie Patel: p95 under 150 ms for the short queries is ideal, anything below 300 ms acceptable. For the 2k token requests, p95 under 2s would be great.
[02:22] Marco Alvarez: Good — those are achievable but will depend on model and quantization. If we use a 8-bit quantized variant and 16GB GPU backends, the small queries will be very fast. For the 2k tokens you should expect higher load per request — we'll recommend offloading those into batch workers or use longer-context optimized kernels.
[02:39] Riley Gomez: Can we run benchmarks with our actual payloads? I don't want synthetic tokens that don't represent our prompts.
[02:45] Marco Alvarez: Absolutely. The POC plan we propose: 1) baseline cold-start single request latency using your real prompts; 2) scale up to your peak concurrent interactive users; 3) run nightly batch profile (e.g., 50k seqs at 2k tokens) and measure throughput and cost. We'll provide a harness that can replay requests preserving tokenization.
[03:07] Dr. Jamie Patel: Sounds good. Timeline? We were hoping for a 4 week window to validate success criteria.
[03:13] Ava Chen: 4 weeks is feasible. Week 1: networking + docs + artifact staging, Week 2: initial benchmarks and small-scale tests, Week 3: scale tests and egress verification, Week 4: analysis and handoff.
[03:26] Samir Das: Egress verification is the blocker for us. We need to ensure no telemetry or logs leave our VPC. How do we validate logs and telemetry are off?
[03:35] Marco Alvarez: We can turn off outbound telemetry on the runtime, and run a packet capture test with you during Week 3. Also we can configure the runtime to ship logs to your CloudWatch/S3 with your KMS key rather than Redwood's telemetry backend. We'll share a telemetry switch guide.
[03:50] Riley Gomez: If we do peering, are there CIDR overlap concerns? Our infra team is conservative about changing CIDRs.
[03:55] Marco Alvarez: We'll need a non-overlapping CIDR for the Redwood side, or use PrivateLink to avoid that. For peering we can pick a /28 to /24 range — standard approach. We'll provide a sample peering proposal.
[04:09] Dr. Jamie Patel: OK and region locking — we need everything in us-west-2. Does Redwood support dedicated nodes in us-west-2?
[04:17] Ava Chen: Yes — we support us-west-2 with Dedicated pools. For Private we can deploy into a VPC in us-west-2 controlled by you, or run dedicated GPU pool in that region.
[04:27] Lina Park: Cost — can you ballpark expected cost for a pilot with 1 dedicated GPU for 4 weeks?
[04:33] Ava Chen: I'll send a ballpark in the follow-up, but rough order: depending on instance type, expected spend ~ $8k–$16k for one high-memory GPU for 4 weeks including data transfer and storage. We'll break that down in the pricing doc.
[04:46] Marco Alvarez: For benchmarks we should define success criteria explicitly. Proposed acceptance tests: 1) Interactive p95 < 300ms for 200-token prompts at 100 concurrent users; 2) Long-context p95 < 2s for 2k tokens at 10 concurrent users; 3) Nightly batch throughput > X seqs/hr (we'll compute X once you share batch size); 4) No outbound connections to non-allowed endpoints during packet-capture test; 5) KMS integration for model artifacts and logs.
[05:16] Riley Gomez: For #3 the nightly batch target we expect 50k sequences in a 6 hour window.
[05:22] Marco Alvarez: Great — that gives us throughput target (~8,333 seqs/hr) — we'll size GPU hours accordingly.
[05:30] Dr. Jamie Patel: On encryption — we require customer-managed KMS keys and HSM-backed key storage for any model material. Is that supported?
[05:38] Priya Kapoor: Yes, we support reading artifacts encrypted with your CMK; for Dedicated/Private we can integrate with your KMS or HSM. We'll document exact IAM roles and policies.
[05:48] Samir Das: Are there any background model version checks that could phone home? We need to ensure automatic updates are disabled.
[05:55] Marco Alvarez: Automatic model pulls can be disabled. We can pin a model version and operate in a fully offline mode. For security we also recommend running with audit logging to your own S3 bucket.
[06:05] Riley Gomez: If PrivateLink is chosen, what IP space and scaling limitations are there for many endpoints? Could we hit ENI limits?
[06:12] Marco Alvarez: ENI limits are per AZ and per account — PrivateLink will create one interface per AZ. For extremely large scale we might recommend peering or a dedicated deployment. We'll add a doc section covering ENI and throughput limits and recommended request routing across AZs.
[06:29] Dr. Jamie Patel: Testing plan: We want Redwood to run initial tests, then we'll run our security team to do a pen-test/packet capture to confirm no egress. After pass, we'll move to a 3-month pilot.
[06:41] Ava Chen: Sounds good — we'll treat the POC as gating to the pilot. Marco will send the benchmark harness and test plan and we'll coordinate the packet capture window in Week 3.
[06:52] Marco Alvarez: On instrumentation — when you share the sample prompts and tokenization we'll run token-level timing and show token per second numbers and KV-cache behavior. For long-context cases we'll show how recomputation and attention kernels impact latency.
[07:06] Lina Park: Quick note — some of our prompts include PHI-like metadata. We need assurances on retention policies.
[07:12] Samir Das: Right — retention: we require logs and request material to be zero-retention unless explicitly stored in our S3 with CMK.
[07:19] Marco Alvarez: We can push logs only to your S3 and set zero-retention at Redwood side. We will also provide a signed attestation for delete-on-ingest patterns for the POC.
[07:30] Dr. Jamie Patel: Any gotchas you usually see?
[07:34] Marco Alvarez: The common ones are CIDR overlap, default telemetry not being disabled, and forgetting to pin model versions which can cause outbound pulls. We'll provide a pre-checklist to avoid these.
[07:45] Ava Chen: Ok let's recap next steps: (1) Redwood sends the network diagram and peering vs PrivateLink pros/cons + checklist; (2) Redwood sends benchmark harness and expected metrics template; (3) Northbridge shares VPC details, allowed egress list, and sample prompts; (4) schedule Week 3 packet capture; (5) Redwood sends SOC2/KMS/HSM docs.
[08:05] Dr. Jamie Patel: Agreed. One last question — what happens if we need an air-gapped appliance?
[08:11] Priya Kapoor: We have an on-prem package — it requires more lead time (4–6 weeks for appliance provisioning and security review). If your compliance requires it we can start that in parallel but for the POC we'd try VPC-based approach first.
[08:24] Riley Gomez: Let's try VPC peering first. If we run into CIDR issues we'll fall back to PrivateLink or the on-prem route.
[08:30] Ava Chen: Great. We'll follow up with the docs and propose calendar times for the benchmark runs. Thanks everyone.
[08:35] Dr. Jamie Patel: Thanks, appreciate it.

Auto-generated Action items (from transcript):
- Ava to circulate peering diagram and PrivateLink checklist.
- Marco to draft benchmark harness using sample prompts.
- Riley to provide VPC and firewall details.
- Samir to verify legal/data residency and confirm retention policy constraints.

Closing notes: call ended 10:26 PST (approx).

Notes on transcription: occasional mis-hear: "privatelink" transcribed variably as "private link" or "private-link"; acronyms like KMS/HSM sometimes appear as km-s or h-sm in auto transcript. Natural crosstalk present.
