title: Hybrid packing & fallback strategy — KV cache + continuous batching bench
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-12-08 14:30 UTC
Duration: ~62 minutes
Location: Zoom / Fireflies capture
Attendees: Ava (Redwood AE), Marcus (Redwood SE), Denise (Orchid Head of Platform), Ravi (Orchid SRE), Lina (Orchid ML infra)

Summary (auto): quick deep-dive on throughput knobs — exploring hybrid packing policies, fallback strategies when KV hit-rate drops, and the interaction between max_num_seqs and token caps. Agreed to run a bench on Orchid traces and reconvene.

[00:00:05] Ava Chen: all right everyone, thanks for joining. quick intro — i'm ava, i'll run the convo and make sure we capture action items. marcus will drive the perf bits.
[00:00:20] Marcus Reed: hey hi, good to meet you folks. we got the brief from denise, we wanted to focus on throughput tuning for bursts and steady state, particularly how continuous batching + k v cache behaves.
[00:00:32] Denise Park: great, thanks. our main pain is unpredictable tail latency and big cost spikes when traffic bursts hit. we use a 16k seq model for summaries and a 4k for search rerank, mixed load.
[00:00:45] Ravi Patel: yes and importantly we run inside a VPC/on-prem proxy, so network egress and chasing caches across nodes is a concern.
[00:00:55] Lina Gomez: plus we're interested in quantization profiles — 8-bit vs 4-bit — what breaks in terms of KV cache behavior when we quantize aggressively.

[00:01:10] Marcus Reed: cool. high-level: continuous batching pools requests into buckets and packs them by sequence length while respecting max_num_seqs and per-seq token caps. the k v cache is shared across sequences on the same GPU instance — hit-rate matters more for long-context models.
[00:01:30] Ava Chen: quick note, some of the auto-transcript will spell KV as K V or K V cache — apologies, we'll be explicit in the notes.

[00:01:42] Denise Park: maybe start with a concrete failure mode — when we've seen tail latency spikes it's usually after a cold-start or a large burst where the kv hit drops to like 10-15% and then we pay the full token decode cost.
[00:01:58] Marcus Reed: yes that's common — two knobs to think about: aggressive pre-warming (keep KV warm for hot prefixes) and adaptive max_num_seqs. if max_num_seqs is too high you can pack more sequences but you also increase per-batch latency because long contexts need more GPU time.

[00:02:20] [00:02:35] Ravi: we've got two priorities — keep 95th below 600ms on short requests and avoid >3x cost spikes on heavy traffic. can the scheduler do per-route priority shaping?
[00:02:46] Marcus Reed: scheduler supports priority queues; we can set per-route target latency and max_num_seqs per queue. common pattern: high-priority short requests go to low max_num_seqs, smaller batch targets, plus a fast-path model variant (quantized) for graceful degradation.
[00:03:10] Denise: okay but how do we avoid oscillation? e.g., auto-scaling sees high p95, scales up, k v warms, then scale down and hit again.
[00:03:22] Marcus: recommend hysteresis and a rolling warm-window — keep a tiny dedicated pool pinned for hot prefixes (like 2-3 GPUs for critical routes) so you don't entirely rely on reactive scale-up. also routing fallback: if k v hit-rate below threshold, automatically route to a lower-cost quantized but lower-accuracy variant that has faster per-token throughput.

[00:03:44] [00:04:00] Lina: how does quantization affect the KV hit logic? we saw weird cache evictions when using 4-bit on the same instance.
[00:04:12] Marcus: great question. quantization changes kernel layouts and memory usage so effective number of sequences that fit into the KV cache changes. 4-bit reduces activation size but sometimes the runtime uses different eviction heuristics, so hit-rate can be non-linear. in practice we characterize by running a memory packing bench.
[00:04:35] Ava: note: transcripts sometimes show 'quan-tiz' for quantization.

[00:04:46] Denise: can you explain hybrid packing?
[00:04:52] Marcus: hybrid packing is where we combine seq-packing and prefix-based single-seq handling: short requests get packed aggressively (seq packing) up to a configured max_num_seqs; long-context requests preferentially go alone or in small batches with KV sharing. hybrid tries to maximize throughput for short ones without degrading long-tail. we couple that with an adaptive token limit: if a batch exceeds a per-GPU token cap, the scheduler splits it or defers lower-priority seqs.
[00:05:20] Ravi: what's a sensible max_num_seqs for mixed 4k/16k workloads?
[00:05:26] Marcus: empirically, for 4k heavy traffic you can set max_num_seqs 8-12 on A100-like hardware; for 16k long contexts you'd reduce to 1-3 for those routes. our scheduler supports per-model and per-route overrides.
[00:05:45] Denise: and how about token limits — do you use per-request caps or global per-batch caps?
[00:05:52] Marcus: both. per-request caps protect against runaway tokens; per-batch/cell caps protect GPU memory/time for the whole batch. you should tune per-batch token cap to fit within your target latency SLO and GPU memory budget.

[00:06:10] [00:06:30] Lina: we saw increased network traffic when many clients hit short requests and the runtime rebalances across nodes. in VPC this becomes problematic due to egress charges. any way to reduce cross-node kv traffic?
[00:06:41] Marcus: two levers: affinity routing (bind hot prefixes to specific nodes) and local-pref caching where a node keeps a soft-copy of recent kv entries to reduce cross-node fetches. both reduce inter-node traffic but increase memory footprint. for VPC/on-prem we recommend affinity plus smaller soft-copies.
[00:07:02] Ava: also worth noting if you run private (on-prem) you can control NIC tuning and RDMA to reduce latency — firewall/proxy in VPC can add jitter.

[00:07:15] Denise: tradeoffs with affinity — if you pin too much you lose global burst capacity.
[00:07:22] Marcus: right — use a two-tier strategy: a small pinned pool for top-n prefixes and a shared pool for all other traffic. pinned pool handles critical SLOs, shared pool is elastic. fallback policy: if pinned pool overloaded, route to shared pool but with a quantized variant and degraded SLO.

[00:07:50] [00:08:10] Ravi: can you walk through a real benchmark you run to decide thresholds? curious about methodology.
[00:08:18] Marcus: sure — we run a trace replay bench: take 24-48h of anonymized requests, replay with same inter-arrival distribution into isolated cluster, measure KV hit-rate, p50/p95/p99 latencies, per-token GPU cost, and eviction churn. then sweep knobs: max_num_seqs, per-batch token cap, kv-keep-window, and quantization profile. we produce a heatmap and recommend operating points.
[00:08:45] Lina: how long does a sweep take?
[00:08:48] Marcus: depends on traces, but a full sweep across 4-6 knob combinations can be done in ~8-12 hours on a modest cluster. we start with targeted sweeps for the most impactful knobs.

[00:09:05] Denise: for us, minimizing cost while keeping p95 < 600ms for short routes is main objective. can we simulate the cost hit when we lower kv hit-rate to 20%?
[00:09:15] Marcus: yes — bench will produce per-token cost numbers. rough back-of-envelope: if hit-rate drops from 80% to 20% for long contexts, token decode ops rise ~3-4x, so GPU token cost jumps accordingly. short requests are less affected, but packing inefficiency can increase.

[00:09:40] [00:10:05] Ava: do you have any constraints about maintenance windows for bench runs?
[00:10:10] Ravi: we prefer non-prod runs isolated from our clusters; we'll provide traces and a staging VPC. we'd like Redwood to run bench in their sandbox and give raw metrics.

[00:10:22] Marcus: no problem. for the bench, we'll need the anonymized trace, preferred model version (you said 16k and 4k), and a list of critical prefix patterns to pin.
[00:10:34] Denise: i'll get that.

[00:10:40] Marcus: couple of deeper notes: 1) token limits per-request should be conservative to avoid outlier long runs affecting batches, 2) max_num_seqs should be tuned with observed seq-length distribution quantiles (e.g., 90th percentile), and 3) KV eviction policy can be LRU or LFU-like; LFU smoother for bursty repeated prefixes.
[00:11:10] Lina: LFU sounds promising because our queries repeat many system prompts.

[00:11:20] Ava: any questions about on-prem specifics?
[00:11:25] Ravi: yes, our cluster uses mixed GPUs and some nodes are older. does Redwood runtime support mixed GPU pools?
[00:11:33] Marcus: yes — runtime can tag pools by GPU class and you'll set routing policies. heavier loads go to A100 pool; fallback to T4-like for non-critical dev traffic. mixed pools complicate packing because throughput profiles differ; scheduler accounts for model-device perf matrices.

[00:11:55] Denise: are there known failure modes when using very high max_num_seqs?
[00:12:00] Marcus: with very high max_num_seqs you can see increased latency for long sequences due to head-of-line blocking and larger GPU kernel durations. also more variability in queuing delay. mitigations: priority preemption (remove lower-priority seqs mid-assembly) and per-route quotas.

[00:12:20] [00:12:45] Ava: sounds like we have a plan. recap: redwood runs a 24h bench with orcid traces, benchmark will sweep max_num_seqs, per-batch token cap, and a few quant profiles. action items recorded.
[00:12:58] Denise: good. one last practical — can you share the runbook if we need to rollback scheduler changes?
[00:13:05] Marcus: yes, i'll prepare a rollback runbook showing knob defaults and safe values, plus commands to revert.
[00:13:12] Ravi: thanks.
[00:13:20] Ava: okay we'll follow up with links: benchmark spreadsheet, runbook draft, and a shared S3. expected follow-up week of 2025-12-15. thanks all.

Auto-generated topics: throughput tuning, packing, cache eviction, quantization impact, vpc affinity.

End of transcript.
text:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-12-08 14:30 UTC
Duration: ~62 minutes
Location: Zoom / Fireflies capture
Attendees: Ava (Redwood AE), Marcus (Redwood SE), Denise (Orchid Head of Platform), Ravi (Orchid SRE), Lina (Orchid ML infra)

Summary (auto): quick deep-dive on throughput knobs — exploring hybrid packing policies, fallback strategies when KV hit-rate drops, and the interaction between max_num_seqs and token caps. Agreed to run a bench on Orchid traces and reconvene.

[00:00:05] Ava Chen: all right everyone, thanks for joining. quick intro — i'm ava, i'll run the convo and make sure we capture action items. marcus will drive the perf bits.
[00:00:20] Marcus Reed: hey hi, good to meet you folks. we got the brief from denise, we wanted to focus on throughput tuning for bursts and steady state, particularly how continuous batching + k v cache behaves.
[00:00:32] Denise Park: great, thanks. our main pain is unpredictable tail latency and big cost spikes when traffic bursts hit. we use a 16k seq model for summaries and a 4k for search rerank, mixed load.
[00:00:45] Ravi Patel: yes and importantly we run inside a VPC/on-prem proxy, so network egress and chasing caches across nodes is a concern.
[00:00:55] Lina Gomez: plus we're interested in quantization profiles — 8-bit vs 4-bit — what breaks in terms of KV cache behavior when we quantize aggressively.

[00:01:10] Marcus Reed: cool. high-level: continuous batching pools requests into buckets and packs them by sequence length while respecting max_num_seqs and per-seq token caps. the k v cache is shared across sequences on the same GPU instance — hit-rate matters more for long-context models.
[00:01:30] Ava Chen: quick note, some of the auto-transcript will spell KV as K V or K V cache — apologies, we'll be explicit in the notes.

[00:01:42] Denise Park: maybe start with a concrete failure mode — when we've seen tail latency spikes it's usually after a cold-start or a large burst where the kv hit drops to like 10-15% and then we pay the full token decode cost.
[00:01:58] Marcus Reed: yes that's common — two knobs to think about: aggressive pre-warming (keep KV warm for hot prefixes) and adaptive max_num_seqs. if max_num_seqs is too high you can pack more sequences but you also increase per-batch latency because long contexts need more GPU time.

[00:02:20] [00:02:35] Ravi: we've got two priorities — keep 95th below 600ms on short requests and avoid >3x cost spikes on heavy traffic. can the scheduler do per-route priority shaping?
[00:02:46] Marcus Reed: scheduler supports priority queues; we can set per-route target latency and max_num_seqs per queue. common pattern: high-priority short requests go to low max_num_seqs, smaller batch targets, plus a fast-path model variant (quantized) for graceful degradation.
[00:03:10] Denise: okay but how do we avoid oscillation? e.g., auto-scaling sees high p95, scales up, k v warms, then scale down and hit again.
[00:03:22] Marcus: recommend hysteresis and a rolling warm-window — keep a tiny dedicated pool pinned for hot prefixes (like 2-3 GPUs for critical routes) so you don't entirely rely on reactive scale-up. also routing fallback: if k v hit-rate below threshold, automatically route to a lower-cost quantized but lower-accuracy variant that has faster per-token throughput.

[00:03:44] [00:04:00] Lina: how does quantization affect the KV hit logic? we saw weird cache evictions when using 4-bit on the same instance.
[00:04:12] Marcus: great question. quantization changes kernel layouts and memory usage so effective number of sequences that fit into the KV cache changes. 4-bit reduces activation size but sometimes the runtime uses different eviction heuristics, so hit-rate can be non-linear. in practice we characterize by running a memory packing bench.
[00:04:35] Ava: note: transcripts sometimes show 'quan-tiz' for quantization.

[00:04:46] Denise: can you explain hybrid packing?
[00:04:52] Marcus: hybrid packing is where we combine seq-packing and prefix-based single-seq handling: short requests get packed aggressively (seq packing) up to a configured max_num_seqs; long-context requests preferentially go alone or in small batches with KV sharing. hybrid tries to maximize throughput for short ones without degrading long-tail. we couple that with an adaptive token limit: if a batch exceeds a per-GPU token cap, the scheduler splits it or defers lower-priority seqs.
[00:05:20] Ravi: what's a sensible max_num_seqs for mixed 4k/16k workloads?
[00:05:26] Marcus: empirically, for 4k heavy traffic you can set max_num_seqs 8-12 on A100-like hardware; for 16k long contexts you'd reduce to 1-3 for those routes. our scheduler supports per-model and per-route overrides.
[00:05:45] Denise: and how about token limits — do you use per-request caps or global per-batch caps?
[00:05:52] Marcus: both. per-request caps protect against runaway tokens; per-batch/cell caps protect GPU memory/time for the whole batch. you should tune per-batch token cap to fit within your target latency SLO and GPU memory budget.

[00:06:10] [00:06:30] Lina: we saw increased network traffic when many clients hit short requests and the runtime rebalances across nodes. in VPC this becomes problematic due to egress charges. any way to reduce cross-node kv traffic?
[00:06:41] Marcus: two levers: affinity routing (bind hot prefixes to specific nodes) and local-pref caching where a node keeps a soft-copy of recent kv entries to reduce cross-node fetches. both reduce inter-node traffic but increase memory footprint. for VPC/on-prem we recommend affinity plus smaller soft-copies.
[00:07:02] Ava: also worth noting if you run private (on-prem) you can control NIC tuning and RDMA to reduce latency — firewall/proxy in VPC can add jitter.

[00:07:15] Denise: tradeoffs with affinity — if you pin too much you lose global burst capacity.
[00:07:22] Marcus: right — use a two-tier strategy: a small pinned pool for top-n prefixes and a shared pool for all other traffic. pinned pool handles critical SLOs, shared pool is elastic. fallback policy: if pinned pool overloaded, route to shared pool but with a quantized variant and degraded SLO.

[00:07:50] [00:08:10] Ravi: can you walk through a real benchmark you run to decide thresholds? curious about methodology.
[00:08:18] Marcus: sure — we run a trace replay bench: take 24-48h of anonymized requests, replay with same inter-arrival distribution into isolated cluster, measure KV hit-rate, p50/p95/p99 latencies, per-token GPU cost, and eviction churn. then sweep knobs: max_num_seqs, per-batch token cap, kv-keep-window, and quantization profile. we produce a heatmap and recommend operating points.
[00:08:45] Lina: how long does a sweep take?
[00:08:48] Marcus: depends on traces, but a full sweep across 4-6 knob combinations can be done in ~8-12 hours on a modest cluster. we start with targeted sweeps for the most impactful knobs.

[00:09:05] Denise: for us, minimizing cost while keeping p95 < 600ms for short routes is main objective. can we simulate the cost hit when we lower kv hit-rate to 20%?
[00:09:15] Marcus: yes — bench will produce per-token cost numbers. rough back-of-envelope: if hit-rate drops from 80% to 20% for long contexts, token decode ops rise ~3-4x, so GPU token cost jumps accordingly. short requests are less affected, but packing inefficiency can increase.

[00:09:40] [00:10:05] Ava: do you have any constraints about maintenance windows for bench runs?
[00:10:10] Ravi: we prefer non-prod runs isolated from our clusters; we'll provide traces and a staging VPC. we'd like Redwood to run bench in their sandbox and give raw metrics.

[00:10:22] Marcus: no problem. for the bench, we'll need the anonymized trace, preferred model version (you said 16k and 4k), and a list of critical prefix patterns to pin.
[00:10:34] Denise: i'll get that.

[00:10:40] Marcus: couple of deeper notes: 1) token limits per-request should be conservative to avoid outlier long runs affecting batches, 2) max_num_seqs should be tuned with observed seq-length distribution quantiles (e.g., 90th percentile), and 3) KV eviction policy can be LRU or LFU-like; LFU smoother for bursty repeated prefixes.
[00:11:10] Lina: LFU sounds promising because our queries repeat many system prompts.

[00:11:20] Ava: any questions about on-prem specifics?
[00:11:25] Ravi: yes, our cluster uses mixed GPUs and some nodes are older. does Redwood runtime support mixed GPU pools?
[00:11:33] Marcus: yes — runtime can tag pools by GPU class and you'll set routing policies. heavier loads go to A100 pool; fallback to T4-like for non-critical dev traffic. mixed pools complicate packing because throughput profiles differ; scheduler accounts for model-device perf matrices.

[00:11:55] Denise: are there known failure modes when using very high max_num_seqs?
[00:12:00] Marcus: with very high max_num_seqs you can see increased latency for long sequences due to head-of-line blocking and larger GPU kernel durations. also more variability in queuing delay. mitigations: priority preemption (remove lower-priority seqs mid-assembly) and per-route quotas.

[00:12:20] [00:12:45] Ava: sounds like we have a plan. recap: redwood runs a 24h bench with orcid traces, benchmark will sweep max_num_seqs, per-batch token cap, and a few quant profiles. action items recorded.
[00:12:58] Denise: good. one last practical — can you share the runbook if we need to rollback scheduler changes?
[00:13:05] Marcus: yes, i'll prepare a rollback runbook showing knob defaults and safe values, plus commands to revert.
[00:13:12] Ravi: thanks.
[00:13:20] Ava: okay we'll follow up with links: benchmark spreadsheet, runbook draft, and a shared S3. expected follow-up week of 2025-12-15. thanks all.

title: Northbridge Analytics - technical deep dive: safe apply + rollback for optimization configs
preview:
Fireflies.ai Meeting Transcript

Date: 2025-11-19
Start time: 10:00 AM PT
Duration: 56 minutes
Title: Northbridge Analytics - technical deep dive: safe apply + rollback for optimization configs

Attendees
- Redwood Inference: Ava Chen, Benji Okafor, Sean Gallagher, Marcus Lin
- Northbridge Analytics: Priya Nair, Sam Whitaker, Elena Petrova, Jason Kim

---
Summary (auto-generated)
Northbridge asked detailed questions about change safety for Redwood Optimize configuration changes (batching, caching, quantization profiles). Redwood walked through immutable config versions, staged rollout/canary, gating on latency and 5xx signals, audit log events, and one-click rollback semantics. Customer emphasized enterprise requirements: “who changed what, when,” SIEM export, RBAC separation of duties, and clear guarantees for rollback propagation.

---
[00:00] Ava Chen: Cool, hi everyone. Thanks for making the time. Today we wanted to go deeper on the safe apply and rollback mechanism we’ve been building for optimization configs. Priya, you had a bunch of specific questions in email.

[00:15] Priya Nair: Yep. Thanks Ava. We’re basically blocked on change management. We can’t have a config tweak take down production and then we’re stuck hunting around. So we want to understand what “rollback” means in your system, how fast, and how auditable.

[00:32] Ava Chen: Makes sense. I pulled in Benji from platform, Sean from SRE, and Marcus from security. Jason, Sam, Elena, anything you want to set context on before we jump in?

[00:44] Sam Whitaker: Just that we run a fairly strict SRE gate. If it’s not measurable and reversible it’s not going to pass. Also we run multi-region and we’ve been burned by partial config applies.

[00:55] Elena Petrova: And on my side: audit logs. Not “we have logs somewhere,” but specific events, actor, scope, correlation ids, retention. We export to Splunk.

[01:05] Jason Kim: Plus, quantization. We’re experimenting with lower precision profiles. A regression in quality is harder to catch than latency. So gating there is important.

[01:18] Ava Chen: Great. Benji, do you want to start with the conceptual model?

[01:22] Benji Okafor: Sure. So, today optimization settings like batching heuristics, cache toggles, and quant profiles are moving toward an explicit object we call an Optimization Config. The key change is: configs are versioned and immutable. You don’t “edit config X,” you create version N+1 with metadata, and then you can apply that version to a scope.

[01:45] Priya Nair: Scope meaning tenant, model, region?

[01:48] Benji Okafor: Exactly. The minimum scoping dimensions are tenant, model, region. Some customers also do environment like staging vs prod, but under the hood it’s basically separate scopes/pointers.

[02:02] Sam Whitaker: So there’s like a pointer to “active version” for that scope?

[02:06] Benji Okafor: Yeah. We treat “active config for scope” as a pointer that can move forward (promote) or backwards (rollback). The versions themselves never mutate.

[02:18] Marcus Lin: And each movement of that pointer is an auditable action with RBAC controls, which I’m sure we’ll get into.

[02:25] Ava Chen: Before we go into security, Sean can you explain the safe apply flow and gates?

[02:31] Sean Gallagher: Yep. The workflow is: validate, stage, observe, then promote. Validation is both schema and semantic. For example, you can’t set batching max tokens above what your worker type supports, or enable a cache mode that’s incompatible with a given model family.

[02:53] Jason Kim: Semantic validation includes quant profile compatibility?

[02:56] Benji Okafor: Yes. Like, quant profile “int4-kv” might only be valid for certain architectures. And we’ll reject it at validation time rather than letting it roll.

[03:05] Sean Gallagher: Then apply is staged. You can do a canary percent. Under the covers, we roll that config to a subset of worker shards, or a percent of traffic depending on the deployment mode.

[03:19] Sam Whitaker: Is it request-based percentage or instance-based percentage?

[03:23] Sean Gallagher: Good question. In hosted and dedicated it’s typically shard-based first, which effectively becomes traffic-based because the router sends to those shards. We try to avoid per-request randomized routing because it makes debugging messy, you end up with mixed behavior on the same worker.

[03:40] Priya Nair: And your gates are what, exactly? p95, error rate?

[03:44] Sean Gallagher: Default gates we recommend: 5xx rate, p95 latency, optionally p99. Also saturation signals like GPU utilization and queue depth. We’ve seen cases where latency looks fine until queue depth spikes, so we gate on that too for higher blast radius.

[04:05] Ava Chen: And for quantization there’s an eval gate. Benji, do you want to frame it?

[04:10] Benji Okafor: For quant profile changes, safe apply can be configured to require a quality signal from the eval harness. That can be a prompt set run that compares to baseline and checks thresholds. It’s not “prove it’s good,” but “it’s not significantly worse” based on your thresholds.

[04:27] Jason Kim: Is that synchronous? Like the rollout waits for eval to finish?

[04:31] Benji Okafor: It can. Usually the canary phase pauses until the eval signal posts. In practice it’s a job that runs during canary, then the controller reads the result.

[04:42] Sam Whitaker: What if the eval is noisy? We’ve had flaky tests cause unnecessary rollbacks.

[04:48] Sean Gallagher: Totally. We’re building in “pause” vs “fail” states. If the gate is ambiguous or the signal is missing, the controller can pause and require human confirmation rather than auto failing.

[05:02] Priya Nair: Okay, let’s talk rollback. When you say “one-click rollback,” what is guaranteed? Because we had a vendor where rollback meant “we set a flag” but half the fleet still had the bad config cached.

[05:18] Benji Okafor: Yeah, so we define rollback as: we move the active pointer back to a previous version for that scope, and then we actively converge the fleet. The runtime apply pipeline is idempotent and checkpointed.

[05:33] Priya Nair: Converge meaning…

[05:34] Benji Okafor: Each worker periodically fetches the desired version, but we also push invalidations to speed it up. The worker applies config as a transaction with a “last_applied_version” marker. If it sees desired != last_applied, it applies; if it’s already there, it’s a no-op.

[05:56] Sam Whitaker: And if apply is in-flight and rollback triggers, race condition?

[06:01] Benji Okafor: That’s exactly why we have transaction checkpoints. Rollout controller also uses a monotonic operation id. So an older apply operation can’t “win” after a rollback. The worker compares operation epoch, basically.

[06:18] Sean Gallagher: In the SRE view, we expect rollback convergence within a couple minutes for hosted. Dedicated can be similar. For private, it depends on how their control plane is wired, but we still provide the same version semantics.

[06:34] Sam Whitaker: Couple minutes is okay. But do you expose progress? Like “80% of shards rolled back”?

[06:41] Sean Gallagher: Yes, that’s part of the rollout health dashboard. It shows rollout state, percent applied, gate status, and it annotates the metrics charts with “apply version X” and “rollback to version Y.”

[06:56] Ava Chen: Also in the Console there’s a history timeline view. You can click a version, see diff, see who applied it.

[07:04] Elena Petrova: Who is “who”? Is that a user id, or…

[07:08] Marcus Lin: It’s the authenticated principal. In hosted/dedicated, that’s typically your SSO user, service account, or API key identity. We store actor_id, actor_type, plus source_ip if available, request_id, and the scope.

[07:25] Elena Petrova: And audit events, do you log validate? Apply? Rollback? Promote? Or only some.

[07:31] Marcus Lin: The taxonomy includes: optimization_config.version_created, version_validated, apply_started, apply_paused, apply_failed, apply_completed, promoted, rollback_started, rollback_completed. Names may slightly differ, but that’s the shape.

[07:52] Elena Petrova: We need “attempted rollback but denied by RBAC” as well.

[07:56] Marcus Lin: Good call. We emit authz_denied events for sensitive actions, including rollback and promote, with the requested scope and reason code.

[08:06] Priya Nair: RBAC separation is important. We want people to be able to draft configs but not apply them to prod.

[08:12] Marcus Lin: Yup. Common pattern is: “Optimization Config Author” can create versions and run validations in staging, but only “Optimization Change Operator” can apply to production scopes. And you can have “Approver” for promotion depending on policy. The Console respects those permissions too.

[08:29] Jason Kim: Can a service account do rollback via API? We have automation for incident response.

[08:35] Marcus Lin: Yes, if granted permission. We also support requiring a second factor or a ticket id in the request payload for some orgs. That’s optional policy.

[08:48] Sam Whitaker: Do you have a cooldown? Like we can’t flip versions back and forth and thrash.

[08:53] Sean Gallagher: There’s a safeguard we call a rollback cooldown. By default you can’t re-apply the same “bad” version immediately without explicitly overriding, to prevent oscillation. Also the rollout controller can be set to pause after rollback to force review.

[09:12] Priya Nair: I’m going to ask the most annoying question: what if rollback itself causes impact? Like cache invalidation and you get a latency spike.

[09:21] Omar? (unidentified speaker): Yeah, cache flush can…

[09:23] Sean Gallagher: That’s real. If a config change toggles caching behavior, rolling back may invalidate caches. We recommend gating on p95 and also doing “gradual rollback” in some cases, where you rollback canary-first, rather than instant 100% revert. But the one-click path defaults to fastest safe convergence.

[09:43] Benji Okafor: Also, not all rollbacks require cache flush. The runtime decides based on the diff. For example if you only changed a batching threshold, there’s no cache invalidation.

[09:57] Jason Kim: You said diff. Do you show that diff and do you classify risky fields?

[10:02] Ava Chen: Yes, the diff view highlights changes like quant profile selection, cache enablement, max batch tokens, concurrency caps, that sort of thing. We label them higher risk in UI.

[10:14] Priya Nair: Okay, now audit log details. Elena wants required fields. Can you list what you include?

[10:20] Marcus Lin: Typical fields: event_name, event_time, actor_id, actor_email if known, actor_type, org_id/tenant_id, scope (tenant/model/region), config_version_id, previous_version_id when applicable, request_id, client_ip, user_agent for console, and outcome. For SIEM, we support export with JSON lines.

[10:46] Elena Petrova: Retention?

[10:48] Marcus Lin: Default is 90 days in-console, longer via export. For enterprise we can do longer retention in our system depending on contract, but we generally encourage streaming to your SIEM.

[11:02] Elena Petrova: We need at least one year on our side, so export is fine. But we need stable schema.

[11:08] Marcus Lin: Understood. We version the event schemas. Changes are backward compatible or announced.

[11:16] Sam Whitaker: Do you tag runtime metrics with config version? Because for incident response, we want to correlate p95 with version.

[11:24] Sean Gallagher: Yes, that’s a core piece. Metrics are tagged with tenant, model, region, and config_version. There’s a tradeoff with cardinality, so we do it specifically for optimization-config-related metrics and for request tracing sampling.

[11:41] Jason Kim: Can we also get it in response headers? Like when we call the inference API, we want to know which version served the request.

[11:48] Ava Chen: We can optionally surface an “x-redwood-config-version” header. It’s off by default because some customers don’t want extra metadata, but for debugging it’s super helpful.

[12:03] Priya Nair: That’s great. We’ve asked others for that and they said no.

[12:07] Ava Chen: It’s a feature flag but yeah.

[12:10] Sam Whitaker: Let’s talk about guarantees again. If I click rollback in Console, what does “completed” mean? Completed in control plane, or completed fleet wide?

[12:20] Benji Okafor: “Rollback started” is when pointer moves and convergence begins. “Rollback completed” means the controller has observed all targeted shards reporting desired version and gates are green for a minimum window. If we can’t observe all shards—for example in a degraded state—we’ll mark as “incomplete” with details.

[12:45] Sam Whitaker: So it’s not just “we sent the command.” Good.

[12:49] Priya Nair: And in a partial rollout, can I rollback only the canary?

[12:54] Benji Okafor: Yep. You can rollback to previous for the same scope, but because canary is essentially a sub-scope/shard set, you can target just that stage. Console offers “rollback canary” vs “rollback full” depending on current state.

[13:12] Elena Petrova: Is that captured in audit logs? Like “blast radius”?

[13:16] Marcus Lin: We include “target_percent” or “target_shards” as fields. The Console also forces a confirmation step where you see scope + percent + impacted regions.

[13:27] Ava Chen: It’s the “type to confirm” modal for production.

[13:30] Sam Whitaker: Nice.

[13:32] Jason Kim: For quantization gating, you mentioned eval harness. Is that your harness or ours?

[13:39] Benji Okafor: Both options. Redwood can run a standardized harness with your prompt set, if you upload it or connect it. Some customers run their own and post a gate signal back. The rollout controller just needs a pass/fail/score with a version id.

[13:58] Jason Kim: We likely want to run ours. We have internal evals.

[14:02] Benji Okafor: That’s common.

[14:04] Priya Nair: How do private deployments work? We might do VPC, maybe on-prem later.

[14:10] Ava Chen: VPC and private deployment support the same version model. The difference is the operator workflow: you can pin a version via helm values or terraform, and rollback is an operator command that flips the pinned version and triggers reconcile.

[14:26] Sam Whitaker: Does that still have audit logs?

[14:29] Marcus Lin: In Private, audit logging can be dual: local audit logs inside the customer environment plus optional export to Redwood support plane if enabled. Some regulated customers keep it fully local.

[14:42] Elena Petrova: As long as we can get the events locally to Splunk, that’s fine.

[14:46] Sean Gallagher: And operationally, the runbook is the same: check dashboards, confirm regression, click rollback, verify error rate and p95 returns, and then write the incident note.

[15:01] Priya Nair: Okay. I want to pressure test with an example. Suppose we change batching from max 8 to max 32, and it tanks tail latency. What happens?

[15:11] Sean Gallagher: You’d validate first, then apply canary say 5%. During canary, we monitor p95 and 5xx. If p95 breaches threshold for a sustained window, the gate will fail and the controller pauses. You can then rollback canary or rollback full.

[15:32] Sam Whitaker: Does it auto rollback?

[15:34] Sean Gallagher: Not generally, in early versions we prefer human confirmation. Internally we do some auto rollback for our own accounts. For enterprise, you can opt-in later.

[15:47] Priya Nair: That’s fine. We actually prefer confirmation.

[15:50] Jason Kim: Another example: quant profile changes quality but latency improves. The gate might not catch quality if it’s only latency.

[15:58] Benji Okafor: Right, that’s why quant profile changes can require the eval gate. The controller will not promote without it if the policy requires it.

[16:08] Elena Petrova: Are policy settings themselves auditable? Like who changed gating thresholds?

[16:14] Marcus Lin: Yes. Policy changes are separately audited. And we recommend restricting policy edits to security/admin roles.

[16:24] Priya Nair: I think this is mostly good. I do want to know how it compares to, say, SageMaker endpoints. We can do blue/green, but we lose the “config version” traceability.

[16:37] Ava Chen: That’s consistent with what we hear. Our focus is that “optimization knobs” are first-class and traceable, and rollbacks are a standard operation with dashboards and audit trail.

[16:48] Sam Whitaker: On dashboards, do we get alerts when a gate fails? Like paging?

[16:53] Sean Gallagher: Yes. Gate failures can generate alerts. In our system, we can integrate with PagerDuty or webhook. Also we annotate the charts with the event so on-call sees “apply started” right where the p95 jumped.

[17:10] Priya Nair: That’s helpful.

[17:12] (crosstalk)
Elena Petrova: One more—
Sam Whitaker: Sorry go ahead.

[17:15] Elena Petrova: We need to know if audit logs include the diff, or just references.

[17:20] Marcus Lin: We include references: config_version_id and previous_version_id. The diff can be fetched by authorized users, but we don’t embed full config content in audit events by default because of size and potential sensitive fields. Some customers ask for “diff hash” which we can include.

[17:39] Elena Petrova: Diff hash is good.

[17:41] Priya Nair: I’m going to jump to next steps. Ava, can you send us sample audit payloads and the dashboard spec? We want to run it by our security and SRE teams.

[17:52] Ava Chen: Yes, I’ll send that.

[17:54] Sam Whitaker: And we’d like to see the UI in action. Like a demo where you intentionally break latency and rollback.

[18:01] Ava Chen: We can do that in sandbox. Sean can drive.

[18:05] Sean Gallagher: Happy to.

[18:07] Jason Kim: For quant, can we test gating with a small eval?

[18:11] Benji Okafor: Yes, we can simulate a failing eval to see how the controller pauses.

[18:17] Priya Nair: Great.

[18:19] Ava Chen: Okay, we’ll set up a follow-up. Before we end, any open concerns?

[18:26] Sam Whitaker: Only that we want clarity on convergence times and what happens if a shard is unhealthy during rollback. Like do you block completion.

[18:35] Benji Okafor: If a shard is unhealthy, we’ll mark rollback as “degraded” and show which shards didn’t report. But the pointer is still moved, so new healthy workers come up with the correct version.

[18:49] Sean Gallagher: From ops perspective, you’d treat that as a separate incident: why is that shard unhealthy. But rollback won’t get stuck forever waiting.

[18:58] Elena Petrova: And RBAC: we’ll need to map to our roles. We use Okta groups.

[19:04] Marcus Lin: That’s supported. We can map groups to roles.

[19:08] Ava Chen: Cool. We’ll follow up with docs and schedule the security review and sandbox demo.

[19:14] Priya Nair: Thanks everyone.

[19:16] Sam Whitaker: Thanks.

[19:17] Elena Petrova: Thank you.

[19:18] Jason Kim: Thanks.

text:
Fireflies.ai Meeting Transcript

Date: 2025-11-19
Start time: 10:00 AM PT
Duration: 56 minutes
Title: Northbridge Analytics - technical deep dive: safe apply + rollback for optimization configs

Attendees
- Redwood Inference: Ava Chen, Benji Okafor, Sean Gallagher, Marcus Lin
- Northbridge Analytics: Priya Nair, Sam Whitaker, Elena Petrova, Jason Kim

---
Summary (auto-generated)
Northbridge asked detailed questions about change safety for Redwood Optimize configuration changes (batching, caching, quantization profiles). Redwood walked through immutable config versions, staged rollout/canary, gating on latency and 5xx signals, audit log events, and one-click rollback semantics. Customer emphasized enterprise requirements: “who changed what, when,” SIEM export, RBAC separation of duties, and clear guarantees for rollback propagation.

---
[00:00] Ava Chen: Cool, hi everyone. Thanks for making the time. Today we wanted to go deeper on the safe apply and rollback mechanism we’ve been building for optimization configs. Priya, you had a bunch of specific questions in email.

[00:15] Priya Nair: Yep. Thanks Ava. We’re basically blocked on change management. We can’t have a config tweak take down production and then we’re stuck hunting around. So we want to understand what “rollback” means in your system, how fast, and how auditable.

[00:32] Ava Chen: Makes sense. I pulled in Benji from platform, Sean from SRE, and Marcus from security. Jason, Sam, Elena, anything you want to set context on before we jump in?

[00:44] Sam Whitaker: Just that we run a fairly strict SRE gate. If it’s not measurable and reversible it’s not going to pass. Also we run multi-region and we’ve been burned by partial config applies.

[00:55] Elena Petrova: And on my side: audit logs. Not “we have logs somewhere,” but specific events, actor, scope, correlation ids, retention. We export to Splunk.

[01:05] Jason Kim: Plus, quantization. We’re experimenting with lower precision profiles. A regression in quality is harder to catch than latency. So gating there is important.

[01:18] Ava Chen: Great. Benji, do you want to start with the conceptual model?

[01:22] Benji Okafor: Sure. So, today optimization settings like batching heuristics, cache toggles, and quant profiles are moving toward an explicit object we call an Optimization Config. The key change is: configs are versioned and immutable. You don’t “edit config X,” you create version N+1 with metadata, and then you can apply that version to a scope.

[01:45] Priya Nair: Scope meaning tenant, model, region?

[01:48] Benji Okafor: Exactly. The minimum scoping dimensions are tenant, model, region. Some customers also do environment like staging vs prod, but under the hood it’s basically separate scopes/pointers.

[02:02] Sam Whitaker: So there’s like a pointer to “active version” for that scope?

[02:06] Benji Okafor: Yeah. We treat “active config for scope” as a pointer that can move forward (promote) or backwards (rollback). The versions themselves never mutate.

[02:18] Marcus Lin: And each movement of that pointer is an auditable action with RBAC controls, which I’m sure we’ll get into.

[02:25] Ava Chen: Before we go into security, Sean can you explain the safe apply flow and gates?

[02:31] Sean Gallagher: Yep. The workflow is: validate, stage, observe, then promote. Validation is both schema and semantic. For example, you can’t set batching max tokens above what your worker type supports, or enable a cache mode that’s incompatible with a given model family.

[02:53] Jason Kim: Semantic validation includes quant profile compatibility?

[02:56] Benji Okafor: Yes. Like, quant profile “int4-kv” might only be valid for certain architectures. And we’ll reject it at validation time rather than letting it roll.

[03:05] Sean Gallagher: Then apply is staged. You can do a canary percent. Under the covers, we roll that config to a subset of worker shards, or a percent of traffic depending on the deployment mode.

[03:19] Sam Whitaker: Is it request-based percentage or instance-based percentage?

[03:23] Sean Gallagher: Good question. In hosted and dedicated it’s typically shard-based first, which effectively becomes traffic-based because the router sends to those shards. We try to avoid per-request randomized routing because it makes debugging messy, you end up with mixed behavior on the same worker.

[03:40] Priya Nair: And your gates are what, exactly? p95, error rate?

[03:44] Sean Gallagher: Default gates we recommend: 5xx rate, p95 latency, optionally p99. Also saturation signals like GPU utilization and queue depth. We’ve seen cases where latency looks fine until queue depth spikes, so we gate on that too for higher blast radius.

[04:05] Ava Chen: And for quantization there’s an eval gate. Benji, do you want to frame it?

[04:10] Benji Okafor: For quant profile changes, safe apply can be configured to require a quality signal from the eval harness. That can be a prompt set run that compares to baseline and checks thresholds. It’s not “prove it’s good,” but “it’s not significantly worse” based on your thresholds.

[04:27] Jason Kim: Is that synchronous? Like the rollout waits for eval to finish?

[04:31] Benji Okafor: It can. Usually the canary phase pauses until the eval signal posts. In practice it’s a job that runs during canary, then the controller reads the result.

[04:42] Sam Whitaker: What if the eval is noisy? We’ve had flaky tests cause unnecessary rollbacks.

[04:48] Sean Gallagher: Totally. We’re building in “pause” vs “fail” states. If the gate is ambiguous or the signal is missing, the controller can pause and require human confirmation rather than auto failing.

[05:02] Priya Nair: Okay, let’s talk rollback. When you say “one-click rollback,” what is guaranteed? Because we had a vendor where rollback meant “we set a flag” but half the fleet still had the bad config cached.

[05:18] Benji Okafor: Yeah, so we define rollback as: we move the active pointer back to a previous version for that scope, and then we actively converge the fleet. The runtime apply pipeline is idempotent and checkpointed.

[05:33] Priya Nair: Converge meaning…

[05:34] Benji Okafor: Each worker periodically fetches the desired version, but we also push invalidations to speed it up. The worker applies config as a transaction with a “last_applied_version” marker. If it sees desired != last_applied, it applies; if it’s already there, it’s a no-op.

[05:56] Sam Whitaker: And if apply is in-flight and rollback triggers, race condition?

[06:01] Benji Okafor: That’s exactly why we have transaction checkpoints. Rollout controller also uses a monotonic operation id. So an older apply operation can’t “win” after a rollback. The worker compares operation epoch, basically.

[06:18] Sean Gallagher: In the SRE view, we expect rollback convergence within a couple minutes for hosted. Dedicated can be similar. For private, it depends on how their control plane is wired, but we still provide the same version semantics.

[06:34] Sam Whitaker: Couple minutes is okay. But do you expose progress? Like “80% of shards rolled back”?

[06:41] Sean Gallagher: Yes, that’s part of the rollout health dashboard. It shows rollout state, percent applied, gate status, and it annotates the metrics charts with “apply version X” and “rollback to version Y.”

[06:56] Ava Chen: Also in the Console there’s a history timeline view. You can click a version, see diff, see who applied it.

[07:04] Elena Petrova: Who is “who”? Is that a user id, or…

[07:08] Marcus Lin: It’s the authenticated principal. In hosted/dedicated, that’s typically your SSO user, service account, or API key identity. We store actor_id, actor_type, plus source_ip if available, request_id, and the scope.

[07:25] Elena Petrova: And audit events, do you log validate? Apply? Rollback? Promote? Or only some.

[07:31] Marcus Lin: The taxonomy includes: optimization_config.version_created, version_validated, apply_started, apply_paused, apply_failed, apply_completed, promoted, rollback_started, rollback_completed. Names may slightly differ, but that’s the shape.

[07:52] Elena Petrova: We need “attempted rollback but denied by RBAC” as well.

[07:56] Marcus Lin: Good call. We emit authz_denied events for sensitive actions, including rollback and promote, with the requested scope and reason code.

[08:06] Priya Nair: RBAC separation is important. We want people to be able to draft configs but not apply them to prod.

[08:12] Marcus Lin: Yup. Common pattern is: “Optimization Config Author” can create versions and run validations in staging, but only “Optimization Change Operator” can apply to production scopes. And you can have “Approver” for promotion depending on policy. The Console respects those permissions too.

[08:29] Jason Kim: Can a service account do rollback via API? We have automation for incident response.

[08:35] Marcus Lin: Yes, if granted permission. We also support requiring a second factor or a ticket id in the request payload for some orgs. That’s optional policy.

[08:48] Sam Whitaker: Do you have a cooldown? Like we can’t flip versions back and forth and thrash.

[08:53] Sean Gallagher: There’s a safeguard we call a rollback cooldown. By default you can’t re-apply the same “bad” version immediately without explicitly overriding, to prevent oscillation. Also the rollout controller can be set to pause after rollback to force review.

[09:12] Priya Nair: I’m going to ask the most annoying question: what if rollback itself causes impact? Like cache invalidation and you get a latency spike.

[09:21] Omar? (unidentified speaker): Yeah, cache flush can…

[09:23] Sean Gallagher: That’s real. If a config change toggles caching behavior, rolling back may invalidate caches. We recommend gating on p95 and also doing “gradual rollback” in some cases, where you rollback canary-first, rather than instant 100% revert. But the one-click path defaults to fastest safe convergence.

[09:43] Benji Okafor: Also, not all rollbacks require cache flush. The runtime decides based on the diff. For example if you only changed a batching threshold, there’s no cache invalidation.

[09:57] Jason Kim: You said diff. Do you show that diff and do you classify risky fields?

[10:02] Ava Chen: Yes, the diff view highlights changes like quant profile selection, cache enablement, max batch tokens, concurrency caps, that sort of thing. We label them higher risk in UI.

[10:14] Priya Nair: Okay, now audit log details. Elena wants required fields. Can you list what you include?

[10:20] Marcus Lin: Typical fields: event_name, event_time, actor_id, actor_email if known, actor_type, org_id/tenant_id, scope (tenant/model/region), config_version_id, previous_version_id when applicable, request_id, client_ip, user_agent for console, and outcome. For SIEM, we support export with JSON lines.

[10:46] Elena Petrova: Retention?

[10:48] Marcus Lin: Default is 90 days in-console, longer via export. For enterprise we can do longer retention in our system depending on contract, but we generally encourage streaming to your SIEM.

[11:02] Elena Petrova: We need at least one year on our side, so export is fine. But we need stable schema.

[11:08] Marcus Lin: Understood. We version the event schemas. Changes are backward compatible or announced.

[11:16] Sam Whitaker: Do you tag runtime metrics with config version? Because for incident response, we want to correlate p95 with version.

[11:24] Sean Gallagher: Yes, that’s a core piece. Metrics are tagged with tenant, model, region, and config_version. There’s a tradeoff with cardinality, so we do it specifically for optimization-config-related metrics and for request tracing sampling.

[11:41] Jason Kim: Can we also get it in response headers? Like when we call the inference API, we want to know which version served the request.

[11:48] Ava Chen: We can optionally surface an “x-redwood-config-version” header. It’s off by default because some customers don’t want extra metadata, but for debugging it’s super helpful.

[12:03] Priya Nair: That’s great. We’ve asked others for that and they said no.

[12:07] Ava Chen: It’s a feature flag but yeah.

[12:10] Sam Whitaker: Let’s talk about guarantees again. If I click rollback in Console, what does “completed” mean? Completed in control plane, or completed fleet wide?

[12:20] Benji Okafor: “Rollback started” is when pointer moves and convergence begins. “Rollback completed” means the controller has observed all targeted shards reporting desired version and gates are green for a minimum window. If we can’t observe all shards—for example in a degraded state—we’ll mark as “incomplete” with details.

[12:45] Sam Whitaker: So it’s not just “we sent the command.” Good.

[12:49] Priya Nair: And in a partial rollout, can I rollback only the canary?

[12:54] Benji Okafor: Yep. You can rollback to previous for the same scope, but because canary is essentially a sub-scope/shard set, you can target just that stage. Console offers “rollback canary” vs “rollback full” depending on current state.

[13:12] Elena Petrova: Is that captured in audit logs? Like “blast radius”?

[13:16] Marcus Lin: We include “target_percent” or “target_shards” as fields. The Console also forces a confirmation step where you see scope + percent + impacted regions.

[13:27] Ava Chen: It’s the “type to confirm” modal for production.

[13:30] Sam Whitaker: Nice.

[13:32] Jason Kim: For quantization gating, you mentioned eval harness. Is that your harness or ours?

[13:39] Benji Okafor: Both options. Redwood can run a standardized harness with your prompt set, if you upload it or connect it. Some customers run their own and post a gate signal back. The rollout controller just needs a pass/fail/score with a version id.

[13:58] Jason Kim: We likely want to run ours. We have internal evals.

[14:02] Benji Okafor: That’s common.

[14:04] Priya Nair: How do private deployments work? We might do VPC, maybe on-prem later.

[14:10] Ava Chen: VPC and private deployment support the same version model. The difference is the operator workflow: you can pin a version via helm values or terraform, and rollback is an operator command that flips the pinned version and triggers reconcile.

[14:26] Sam Whitaker: Does that still have audit logs?

[14:29] Marcus Lin: In Private, audit logging can be dual: local audit logs inside the customer environment plus optional export to Redwood support plane if enabled. Some regulated customers keep it fully local.

[14:42] Elena Petrova: As long as we can get the events locally to Splunk, that’s fine.

[14:46] Sean Gallagher: And operationally, the runbook is the same: check dashboards, confirm regression, click rollback, verify error rate and p95 returns, and then write the incident note.

[15:01] Priya Nair: Okay. I want to pressure test with an example. Suppose we change batching from max 8 to max 32, and it tanks tail latency. What happens?

[15:11] Sean Gallagher: You’d validate first, then apply canary say 5%. During canary, we monitor p95 and 5xx. If p95 breaches threshold for a sustained window, the gate will fail and the controller pauses. You can then rollback canary or rollback full.

[15:32] Sam Whitaker: Does it auto rollback?

[15:34] Sean Gallagher: Not generally, in early versions we prefer human confirmation. Internally we do some auto rollback for our own accounts. For enterprise, you can opt-in later.

[15:47] Priya Nair: That’s fine. We actually prefer confirmation.

[15:50] Jason Kim: Another example: quant profile changes quality but latency improves. The gate might not catch quality if it’s only latency.

[15:58] Benji Okafor: Right, that’s why quant profile changes can require the eval gate. The controller will not promote without it if the policy requires it.

[16:08] Elena Petrova: Are policy settings themselves auditable? Like who changed gating thresholds?

[16:14] Marcus Lin: Yes. Policy changes are separately audited. And we recommend restricting policy edits to security/admin roles.

[16:24] Priya Nair: I think this is mostly good. I do want to know how it compares to, say, SageMaker endpoints. We can do blue/green, but we lose the “config version” traceability.

[16:37] Ava Chen: That’s consistent with what we hear. Our focus is that “optimization knobs” are first-class and traceable, and rollbacks are a standard operation with dashboards and audit trail.

[16:48] Sam Whitaker: On dashboards, do we get alerts when a gate fails? Like paging?

[16:53] Sean Gallagher: Yes. Gate failures can generate alerts. In our system, we can integrate with PagerDuty or webhook. Also we annotate the charts with the event so on-call sees “apply started” right where the p95 jumped.

[17:10] Priya Nair: That’s helpful.

[17:12] (crosstalk)
Elena Petrova: One more—
Sam Whitaker: Sorry go ahead.

[17:15] Elena Petrova: We need to know if audit logs include the diff, or just references.

[17:20] Marcus Lin: We include references: config_version_id and previous_version_id. The diff can be fetched by authorized users, but we don’t embed full config content in audit events by default because of size and potential sensitive fields. Some customers ask for “diff hash” which we can include.

[17:39] Elena Petrova: Diff hash is good.

[17:41] Priya Nair: I’m going to jump to next steps. Ava, can you send us sample audit payloads and the dashboard spec? We want to run it by our security and SRE teams.

[17:52] Ava Chen: Yes, I’ll send that.

[17:54] Sam Whitaker: And we’d like to see the UI in action. Like a demo where you intentionally break latency and rollback.

[18:01] Ava Chen: We can do that in sandbox. Sean can drive.

[18:05] Sean Gallagher: Happy to.

[18:07] Jason Kim: For quant, can we test gating with a small eval?

[18:11] Benji Okafor: Yes, we can simulate a failing eval to see how the controller pauses.

[18:17] Priya Nair: Great.

[18:19] Ava Chen: Okay, we’ll set up a follow-up. Before we end, any open concerns?

[18:26] Sam Whitaker: Only that we want clarity on convergence times and what happens if a shard is unhealthy during rollback. Like do you block completion.

[18:35] Benji Okafor: If a shard is unhealthy, we’ll mark rollback as “degraded” and show which shards didn’t report. But the pointer is still moved, so new healthy workers come up with the correct version.

[18:49] Sean Gallagher: From ops perspective, you’d treat that as a separate incident: why is that shard unhealthy. But rollback won’t get stuck forever waiting.

[18:58] Elena Petrova: And RBAC: we’ll need to map to our roles. We use Okta groups.

[19:04] Marcus Lin: That’s supported. We can map groups to roles.

[19:08] Ava Chen: Cool. We’ll follow up with docs and schedule the security review and sandbox demo.

[19:14] Priya Nair: Thanks everyone.

[19:16] Sam Whitaker: Thanks.

[19:17] Elena Petrova: Thank you.

[19:18] Jason Kim: Thanks.

---

title: Commercial gate breakout - KMS/finance preflight - AtlasRx
preview:
Meeting header
Date/Time: 2025-11-16 16:00 UTC | Duration: 52 mins
Attendees: Priya Nambiar (Redwood AE), Samir Patel (Redwood SE), Ava Chen (Redwood Solutions), Marta Ruiz (AtlasRx - Procurement), Liam O'Connor (AtlasRx - Infosec), Devin Brooks (AtlasRx - Finance), Nisha Rao (AtlasRx - Legal)

Summary:
Quick commercial gate breakout focused on MSA/DPA redlines, KMS approach options, SLA tiers and pricing path for pilot. Agreed to circulate redlines and a cost/latency model; customer to validate KMS policy.

Topics:
- MSA/DPA principal redline walk-through
- SLA/uptime and latency targets for pilot
- Pricing and billing path for hosted vs dedicated vs private
- KMS (BYOK vs Redwood-managed) and key escrow concerns
- Audit evidence (SOC2 / ISO) and data residency
- Next steps and stakeholders for procurement approval

Action items (covered above in metadata)

Transcript body:
[00:00] Priya Nambiar: ok everyone, thanks for joining, i'm Priya with Redwood, and this is Samir our SE, ava's on for solutions. quick roll call?
[00:08] Marta Ruiz: Marta here from procurement.
[00:11] Liam O'Connor: Liam, infosec.
[00:13] Devin Brooks: Devin for finance.
[00:15] Nisha Rao: Nisha, legal.
[00:16] Samir Patel: hey all. thanks Marta. i'll kick us off with the tech constraints then hand to priya for commercial.
[00:23] Samir Patel: so from atlasrx notes we saw two big asks: one, ability to enforce key ownership and not have key material offloaded, and two, predictable latency under 250ms p95 for the patient-facing flows. correct me if i'm wrong.
[00:35] Liam O'Connor: yes that's right. plus we need audit trails for key usage and per-tenant access logs.
[00:40] Marta Ruiz: procurement also flagged egress costs and how those map to internal chargebacks.
[00:45] Priya Nambiar: great — we'll address each. on the MSA/DPA — we've sketched typical enterprise redlines, i can share a redline today; there are a couple of Atlas-specific asks we want to confirm, like data retention limits and breach notification windows.
[00:58] Nisha Rao: on retention, we typically want 90 days for logs, but the model output we want to be able to set a shorter TTL. also we need explicit clauses on derivative data — how you treat model outputs with customer data mixed in.
[01:12] Ava Chen: quick note, on outputs our platform supports configurable retention per-route, so that can be surfaced in the DPA. there is often ambiguity in derivative clauses, happy to draft language we've used elsewhere.
[01:25] Devin Brooks: can i ask about pricing path? our finance approvals need an estimate for a three-month pilot: roughly 1M monthly tokens, expected mix of short and long context requests. we need hosted vs dedicated delta.
[01:37] Samir Patel: sure, short answer: hosted is faster to start, lower upfront, but per-token is slightly higher; dedicated gives better unit economics at steady state but requires commit or reserved instance and planning. private/on-prem has the highest integration lift. i'll produce a cost/latency comparison spreadsheet.
[01:52] Devin Brooks: what's the typical commit size for dedicated?
[01:55] Priya Nambiar: it depends on throughput — many customers start with a 3-6 month reserved capacity to hit their internal forecast; we can make a pilot-friendly short commit with an escape clause. i'll include options in the pricing sheet.
[02:07] Marta Ruiz: about KMS, atlasrx legal has strict policy: keys stored in our HSM, no copy retained by vendor, and we need key rotation controls integrated into our inventory. is BYOK possible?
[02:19] Samir Patel: yes BYOK is supported for Redwood Private and our Dedicated VPC option. for Hosted we offer a customer-managed KMS integration for keys at rest, but not full in-memory BYOK for model runtime unless it's Private deployment. slight nuance there.
[02:34] Liam O'Connor: that nuance is important. we need to understand whether the model runtime will ever have plaintext keys in a place outside our control. if yes, that may be a blocker.
[02:42] Samir Patel: understood. for private / VPC we can run with your HSM and ensure keys never leave your control; for Dedicated there's an integration with customer KMS where envelope keys are in your HSM and ephemeral keys live only in memory on host and are zeroed after session. we'll document exact flow.
[02:58] Nisha Rao: will you provide SOC2, pen test results and artifact trail? security team will evaluate.
[03:04] Ava Chen: yes, we maintain SOC2 Type II reports and regular pen tests; we can provide a redacted report and a SOC attestation. we'll also share our infra architecture diagram for the private path.
[03:15] Marta Ruiz: and SLA — we need a 99.95% uptime for production and a clear SLO for latency. for pilot we can be flexible. what's your standard?
[03:26] Priya Nambiar: hosted comes with a baseline SLA of 99.9%, dedicated/enterprise tiers support 99.95% plus financial credits. for pilot, we usually do a 99.5% test window but the contract will reflect production SLOs. we can include automatic fallback policies in the rollout.
[03:44] Devin Brooks: if we accept 99.95% SLA, what are the typical credits? we'd need to model the P&L.
[03:50] Priya Nambiar: credits are tiered by outage duration; i'll capture the exact table in the redline and pricing doc. commitments affect credit levels.
[03:58] Liam O'Connor: any issues with data residency? our PHI workflows must remain in eu-west-1 for european patients.
[04:04] Samir Patel: you can pin routing to region — hosted supports region pinning for EU; private gives you full control. we'll mark the routes in the spreadsheet.
[04:12] Marta Ruiz: procurement wants to know the exec sign-off path — who in Redwood signs for the MSA exceptions?
[04:18] Priya Nambiar: small exceptions handled by AE legal; anything material gets escalated to our commercial counsel, cc'ing our Head of Revenue — i'll flag that in the notes.
[04:26] Nisha Rao: on derivative data again — we need a sink clause to ensure model outputs that reference PHI are treated as controlled data. can you propose specific wording?
[04:35] Ava Chen: will do; i'll draft two variants: one conservative (retain outputs as customer data) and one permissive (standard SaaS model outputs clause).
[04:43] Devin Brooks: about billing — how granular is usage reporting? we need token-level breakdown per route for chargeback to product lines.
[04:50] Samir Patel: token-level, route-level, and token-type (prompt vs completion) rundown — plus tooling to export into your billing system. we'll include sample CSV schema.
[05:00] Marta Ruiz: timelines — procurement target is internal approval by end of month, ideally sign in early december. is that realistic?
[05:06] Priya Nambiar: yes, if we move fast on redlines this week and you return comments early next week. that's why i'm aiming for 2025-11-20 to get redlines out.
[05:14] Liam O'Connor: we will try to get SOC2 and KMS policy before 2025-11-19 so samir can confirm compatibility.
[05:20] Priya Nambiar: perfect. any final questions?
[05:23] Marta Ruiz: just one: are there any data escrow requirements? some of our legal ops ppl want an escrow for model weights if critical service is discontinued.
[05:32] Priya Nambiar: we don't typically escrow model weights for open models; for customer-trained models or private custom stacks we can discuss escrow, but that'll be a special clause. i'll note it as an exception to discuss with our counsel.
[05:45] Nisha Rao: ok please provide the change log for MSA redlines and an explicit list of clauses you refuse to accept so we can prepare internal briefing.
[05:54] Priya Nambiar: yes, we'll include a redline with commentary and call out any no-go items.
[06:00] Ava Chen: i'll attach the infra diagram and a short note on key flow for BYOK.
[06:05] Samir Patel: i'll start the cost/latency comparison and sample CSV for billing.
[06:09] Devin Brooks: i'll circulate internal approver list and budget slot by 2025-11-25.
[06:13] Marta Ruiz: thanks all. recap of next steps?
[06:16] Priya Nambiar: recap: Priya to send MSA/DPA redline and summary (due 11/20), Samir to send cost/latency spreadsheet (due 11/21), Ava to share infra/KMS diagram and two derivative-clauses drafts (due 11/20), AtlasRx to provide SOC2/KMS policy and approver list (due 11/19 and 11/25 respectively). schedule exec readout for 11/24.
[06:38] All: thanks, bye.

End of transcript.
text:
Meeting header
Date/Time: 2025-11-16 16:00 UTC | Duration: 52 mins
Attendees: Priya Nambiar (Redwood AE), Samir Patel (Redwood SE), Ava Chen (Redwood Solutions), Marta Ruiz (AtlasRx - Procurement), Liam O'Connor (AtlasRx - Infosec), Devin Brooks (AtlasRx - Finance), Nisha Rao (AtlasRx - Legal)

Summary:
Quick commercial gate breakout focused on MSA/DPA redlines, KMS approach options, SLA tiers and pricing path for pilot. Agreed to circulate redlines and a cost/latency model; customer to validate KMS policy.

Topics:
- MSA/DPA principal redline walk-through
- SLA/uptime and latency targets for pilot
- Pricing and billing path for hosted vs dedicated vs private
- KMS (BYOK vs Redwood-managed) and key escrow concerns
- Audit evidence (SOC2 / ISO) and data residency
- Next steps and stakeholders for procurement approval

Action items (covered above in metadata)

Transcript body:
[00:00] Priya Nambiar: ok everyone, thanks for joining, i'm Priya with Redwood, and this is Samir our SE, ava's on for solutions. quick roll call?
[00:08] Marta Ruiz: Marta here from procurement.
[00:11] Liam O'Connor: Liam, infosec.
[00:13] Devin Brooks: Devin for finance.
[00:15] Nisha Rao: Nisha, legal.
[00:16] Samir Patel: hey all. thanks Marta. i'll kick us off with the tech constraints then hand to priya for commercial.
[00:23] Samir Patel: so from atlasrx notes we saw two big asks: one, ability to enforce key ownership and not have key material offloaded, and two, predictable latency under 250ms p95 for the patient-facing flows. correct me if i'm wrong.
[00:35] Liam O'Connor: yes that's right. plus we need audit trails for key usage and per-tenant access logs.
[00:40] Marta Ruiz: procurement also flagged egress costs and how those map to internal chargebacks.
[00:45] Priya Nambiar: great — we'll address each. on the MSA/DPA — we've sketched typical enterprise redlines, i can share a redline today; there are a couple of Atlas-specific asks we want to confirm, like data retention limits and breach notification windows.
[00:58] Nisha Rao: on retention, we typically want 90 days for logs, but the model output we want to be able to set a shorter TTL. also we need explicit clauses on derivative data — how you treat model outputs with customer data mixed in.
[01:12] Ava Chen: quick note, on outputs our platform supports configurable retention per-route, so that can be surfaced in the DPA. there is often ambiguity in derivative clauses, happy to draft language we've used elsewhere.
[01:25] Devin Brooks: can i ask about pricing path? our finance approvals need an estimate for a three-month pilot: roughly 1M monthly tokens, expected mix of short and long context requests. we need hosted vs dedicated delta.
[01:37] Samir Patel: sure, short answer: hosted is faster to start, lower upfront, but per-token is slightly higher; dedicated gives better unit economics at steady state but requires commit or reserved instance and planning. private/on-prem has the highest integration lift. i'll produce a cost/latency comparison spreadsheet.
[01:52] Devin Brooks: what's the typical commit size for dedicated?
[01:55] Priya Nambiar: it depends on throughput — many customers start with a 3-6 month reserved capacity to hit their internal forecast; we can make a pilot-friendly short commit with an escape clause. i'll include options in the pricing sheet.
[02:07] Marta Ruiz: about KMS, atlasrx legal has strict policy: keys stored in our HSM, no copy retained by vendor, and we need key rotation controls integrated into our inventory. is BYOK possible?
[02:19] Samir Patel: yes BYOK is supported for Redwood Private and our Dedicated VPC option. for Hosted we offer a customer-managed KMS integration for keys at rest, but not full in-memory BYOK for model runtime unless it's Private deployment. slight nuance there.
[02:34] Liam O'Connor: that nuance is important. we need to understand whether the model runtime will ever have plaintext keys in a place outside our control. if yes, that may be a blocker.
[02:42] Samir Patel: understood. for private / VPC we can run with your HSM and ensure keys never leave your control; for Dedicated there's an integration with customer KMS where envelope keys are in your HSM and ephemeral keys live only in memory on host and are zeroed after session. we'll document exact flow.
[02:58] Nisha Rao: will you provide SOC2, pen test results and artifact trail? security team will evaluate.
[03:04] Ava Chen: yes, we maintain SOC2 Type II reports and regular pen tests; we can provide a redacted report and a SOC attestation. we'll also share our infra architecture diagram for the private path.
[03:15] Marta Ruiz: and SLA — we need a 99.95% uptime for production and a clear SLO for latency. for pilot we can be flexible. what's your standard?
[03:26] Priya Nambiar: hosted comes with a baseline SLA of 99.9%, dedicated/enterprise tiers support 99.95% plus financial credits. for pilot, we usually do a 99.5% test window but the contract will reflect production SLOs. we can include automatic fallback policies in the rollout.
[03:44] Devin Brooks: if we accept 99.95% SLA, what are the typical credits? we'd need to model the P&L.
[03:50] Priya Nambiar: credits are tiered by outage duration; i'll capture the exact table in the redline and pricing doc. commitments affect credit levels.
[03:58] Liam O'Connor: any issues with data residency? our PHI workflows must remain in eu-west-1 for european patients.
[04:04] Samir Patel: you can pin routing to region — hosted supports region pinning for EU; private gives you full control. we'll mark the routes in the spreadsheet.
[04:12] Marta Ruiz: procurement wants to know the exec sign-off path — who in Redwood signs for the MSA exceptions?
[04:18] Priya Nambiar: small exceptions handled by AE legal; anything material gets escalated to our commercial counsel, cc'ing our Head of Revenue — i'll flag that in the notes.
[04:26] Nisha Rao: on derivative data again — we need a sink clause to ensure model outputs that reference PHI are treated as controlled data. can you propose specific wording?
[04:35] Ava Chen: will do; i'll draft two variants: one conservative (retain outputs as customer data) and one permissive (standard SaaS model outputs clause).
[04:43] Devin Brooks: about billing — how granular is usage reporting? we need token-level breakdown per route for chargeback to product lines.
[04:50] Samir Patel: token-level, route-level, and token-type (prompt vs completion) rundown — plus tooling to export into your billing system. we'll include sample CSV schema.
[05:00] Marta Ruiz: timelines — procurement target is internal approval by end of month, ideally sign in early december. is that realistic?
[05:06] Priya Nambiar: yes, if we move fast on redlines this week and you return comments early next week. that's why i'm aiming for 2025-11-20 to get redlines out.
[05:14] Liam O'Connor: we will try to get SOC2 and KMS policy before 2025-11-19 so samir can confirm compatibility.
[05:20] Priya Nambiar: perfect. any final questions?
[05:23] Marta Ruiz: just one: are there any data escrow requirements? some of our legal ops ppl want an escrow for model weights if critical service is discontinued.
[05:32] Priya Nambiar: we don't typically escrow model weights for open models; for customer-trained models or private custom stacks we can discuss escrow, but that'll be a special clause. i'll note it as an exception to discuss with our counsel.
[05:45] Nisha Rao: ok please provide the change log for MSA redlines and an explicit list of clauses you refuse to accept so we can prepare internal briefing.
[05:54] Priya Nambiar: yes, we'll include a redline with commentary and call out any no-go items.
[06:00] Ava Chen: i'll attach the infra diagram and a short note on key flow for BYOK.
[06:05] Samir Patel: i'll start the cost/latency comparison and sample CSV for billing.
[06:09] Devin Brooks: i'll circulate internal approver list and budget slot by 2025-11-25.
[06:13] Marta Ruiz: thanks all. recap of next steps?
[06:16] Priya Nambiar: recap: Priya to send MSA/DPA redline and summary (due 11/20), Samir to send cost/latency spreadsheet (due 11/21), Ava to share infra/KMS diagram and two derivative-clauses drafts (due 11/20), AtlasRx to provide SOC2/KMS policy and approver list (due 11/19 and 11/25 respectively). schedule exec readout for 11/24.
[06:38] All: thanks, bye.

End of transcript.
