title: Pilot scoping: credits, SLA, and contracting path
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-09-23
Start: 15:00 PT
Duration: 55m
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Ravi Kapoor (Redwood SE), Maya Li (Redwood CSM), Daniel Park (BlueWave Head of ML), Lena Ortiz (BlueWave Security), Tom Reed (Procurement), Samira Khan (Data Eng)
Shared links: Pilot terms draft: https://redwood.example/pilot-terms, Perf template: https://redwood.example/pilot-benchmarks, Security pack: https://redwood.example/security-pack.pdf

[00:00] Ava Chen (AE): Hey everyone, thanks for joining, appreciate you taking the time. Quick intros then I'll hand over to Ravi to go through the perf/POC scoping details.
[00:18] Daniel Park (BlueWave): Thanks Ava. Daniel here — we run the consumer-facing triage assistant, want to lower latency and cost while staying HIPAA-aligned.
[00:28] Lena Ortiz (BlueWave): Lena — security and privacy, we'll want SOC2+ and KMS integration, data residency notes early on.
[00:35] Tom Reed (BlueWave): Tom — procurement, will listen for credit/term expectations and PO path.
[00:40] Ravi Kapoor (SE): Great, thanks. High-level goal: 4-week pilot to validate two things: accuracy of entity extraction/intent classification on BlueWave data and end-to-end latency/throughput under expected production load.
[00:55] Ava Chen: Quick note on commercial approach — we typically provide trial credits for pilot usage and a short pilot agreement. Today we want to nail the credit sizing and the path to contracting assuming successful pilot.
[01:10] Samira Khan (BlueWave): Our main constraint is that we can't send PHI to third-party hosts without proper BAAs and enclave controls. We're evaluating both Redwood Private (VPC) and Redwood Dedicated.
[01:22] Maya Li (CSM): Noted — we'll flag the security pack and BAA process in the follow-up.
[01:28] Ravi Kapoor: For workload sizing can you confirm throughput? Samira, you mentioned peak of ~80 concurrent user requests, avg 1.8k tokens per request?
[01:40] Samira Khan: Yes avg 1.8k tokens, peak concurrency around 80 during morning surge, baseline steady at 20 qps later in day.
[01:49] Ravi Kapoor: Ok that maps to roughly 144k tokens per minute peak (1.8k * 80), which is about 2.4k tokens/sec peak. For pilot we usually run steady-state tests at 30% and a couple of 10-minute peak blasts to validate autoscaling.
[02:05] Daniel Park: And cost — how do pilot credits translate to production unit costs? We need to estimate per-1k-token cost for our CFO.
[02:12] Ava Chen: We'll include a modeled cost sheet. For the pilot we provide credits that cover the expected benchmark runs plus a safety buffer (typically 20%). We'll show both hosted and dedicated cost projections so you can compare.
[02:25] Tom Reed: On contracting, how long are pilot terms and what's the usual procurement path? We have to map to our PO process.
[02:33] Ava Chen: Typical pilot term is 30 days. Pilot agreement is limited to liability and usage of credits; it doesn't lock you into long-term. If pilot meets success criteria, we'll escalate to an MSA and SOW for Dedicated or Private deployment; procurement often wants an SO or simple purchase order referencing the MSA. We'll share a sample path in the follow-up.
[02:55] Lena Ortiz: On security I'm hearing a few things — do you support customer-managed KMS and key rotation? Also need audit logs retention and SSO (SAML).
[03:05] Ravi Kapoor: Yes — Redwood Private supports KMS integrations (AWS KMS, Google CMEK), audit logs export to Cloud Storage, and SAML SSO. We'll include exact retention options in the security pack.
[03:18] Daniel Park: Success criteria — let's be explicit. We propose: F1 >= 0.92 on entity extraction on a 1k-sample gold set, 95th percentile latency < 300ms for short responses (<256 tokens) and maintain cost < $0.02 per 1k tokens. Thoughts?
[03:36] Ava Chen: Those are reasonable. From Redwood's side we'll run benchmark harness and measure both latency and token-cost. One ask: can you provide the 1k sample gold set (de-identified) or a synthetic equivalent?
[03:46] Samira Khan: We can provide a de-id set, will need a short DPA addendum.
[03:52] Tom Reed: Procurement note — we'll need vendor info, W-9, and standard indemnity language.
[03:58] Ava Chen: We'll include those in the draft pilot package.
[04:05] [05:00] Ravi Kapoor: Walkthrough of the benchmark plan — step 1: baseline single-request latency tests with sample prompts; step 2: sustained throughput tests at 30%/60%/100% of peak; step 3: end-to-end application test running the triage pipeline with prefix caching enabled and KV reuse; step 4: quality evals on the gold set. We instrument cost-per-token and cost-per-inference.
[05:35] Daniel Park: How long does an end-to-end run take? We need to schedule a time window.
[05:40] Ravi Kapoor: A full pass on a 5k sample in our harness takes ~45 minutes to an hour depending on concurrency profile; we'll break into smaller runs for iteration.
[05:50] Lena Ortiz: If we do Private deployment, what's the timeline to get a VPC instance up with Redwood Private?
[05:57] Maya Li: Typical onboarding for Private (VPC) is 4-6 weeks depending on approvals and network access; Dedicated (hosted reserved capacity) can be faster, like 2-3 weeks. We can run the initial perf tests against Hosted first if you want a quicker turnaround.
[06:14] Samira Khan: We'll prefer VPC but can start with hosted for benchmarks if BAAs are in place.
[06:20] Tom Reed: For credits, what is the quantum? We don't want to overcommit on pilot credits that go unused.
[06:27] Ava Chen: We size credits based on expected benchmark runs plus 20% buffer. For your workload (estimated ~1.5M tokens for the benchmark suite), we'd propose ~2M tokens of credits for the 30-day pilot. We can convert tokens to $ equivalent in the credit sheet.
[06:42] Daniel Park: If pilot yields success criteria, what's the path to reserved capacity pricing? Any discount assumptions we should plan on?
[06:50] Ava Chen: We typically move to committed capacity with a 6–12 month reserved node agreement for Dedicated; discounts are a function of commit size and term. We'll provide a sample pricing table for 6 vs 12 month commitments.
[07:03] [07:30] Ravi Kapoor: Technical nuance — with your average request size (1.8k tokens) quantization can help reduce cost. For the benchmark we'll try FP16 and an 8-bit quantized variant to compare latency and accuracy tradeoffs.
[07:45] Samira Khan: Any risk of quality degradation with quantization?
[07:49] Ravi Kapoor: Small measurable differences on generation, but for extraction/classification tasks it's usually negligible. We'll show delta metrics in the benchmark.
[07:58] Daniel Park: On the legal side, can pilot credits be invoiced if unused?
[08:04] Ava Chen: We provide credits free for the pilot period; if you convert to a paid contract we can convert unused pilot credits into a dollar credit against the first invoice — subject to contract terms. We'll put that option in the draft pilot agreement.
[08:19] Tom Reed: What are the cancellation terms of the pilot? We need clarity on termination and data retention after pilot end.
[08:25] Ava Chen: Pilot agreement standard: 30-day term, either party can end with 10-day notice, Redwood retains aggregated logs for 60 days unless you request immediate deletion; any raw customer data will be handled per the DPA you sign. We'll capture this in the draft.
[08:42] Lena Ortiz: Important: audit logs export and timestamp integrity for regulatory review.
[08:47] Ravi Kapoor: We'll include audit log export steps and formats — JSON lines and S3-compatible export detailed in the security pack.
[08:55] [09:30] Daniel Park: A couple more success criteria items: model stability — no more than 5% variance on F1 across three runs, and autoscaling should not increase 95th latency by more than 30% during scale events.
[09:48] Ava Chen: We'll add those to the acceptance criteria list. Good call.
[09:55] Ravi Kapoor: For instrumentation we'll provide a shared dashboard with token-level cost, latency histograms, and per-route QPS, and we'll drop CSVs into the shared perf sheet after each run.
[10:12] Samira Khan: We'll need network egress costing too; if you run hosted tests with our data there could be egress.
[10:19] Ava Chen: We'll surface egress numbers in the cost model; if you go Private, egress is internal.
[10:25] Tom Reed: Procurement question — when do you need a signed PO to start the pilot?
[10:31] Ava Chen: If the pilot uses only credits and is under a mutual pilot agreement we can start once the pilot agreement is signed; a PO is not required for the credit-funded pilot. However procurement often wants the pilot agreement countersigned for internal tracking — we'll support that.
[10:44] Daniel Park: Risk question — are there any throttles or soft-limits we should expect in hosted that would affect the benchmark?
[10:51] Ravi Kapoor: On hosted we may have rate limits per account; for pilot runs we typically lift rate limits or apply a temporary burst policy. We'll document this and provision the account accordingly.
[11:03] [11:45] Ava Chen: Let's recap next steps: 1) Redwood to issue draft pilot agreement + credit schedule; 2) BlueWave to provide de-identified 1k gold set and network contacts; 3) Redwood SE to run a 5k benchmark baseline and share results; 4) Procurement to share PO process. Any objections?
[11:23] Tom Reed: Sounds good. We'll aim to get our procurement checklist to you by 2025-10-06.
[11:29] Lena Ortiz: We'll look at the DPA and confirm data residency requirements.
[11:33] Samira Khan: I'll drop firewall rules and VPC peering contacts into the shared doc.
[11:37] Ava Chen: Perfect. We'll send the package by end of day Friday (2025-09-26).
[11:44] Daniel Park: Quick question — benchmarking cadence during pilot, how often will you run?
[11:48] Ravi Kapoor: We'll do an initial baseline run within the first week, then two iteration runs after any tuning — about 3-4 benchmark runs total across the pilot. We'll timestamp and note config changes in the results.
[11:59] Ava Chen: Anything else?
[12:03] Daniel Park: No, that's it from me. Appreciate the clarity.
[12:07] Ava Chen: Thanks everyone. We'll follow up with the docs and book the technical deep dive.
[12:12] [12:45] Meeting closed.

Auto-summary (note: auto-gen may be imperfect):
- Purpose: Scope a 30-day pilot to validate extraction accuracy and performance for BlueWave Health's triage assistant; discuss pilot credits, security/DPA, and contracting path.
- Key success criteria: F1 >= 0.92 on 1k gold set, 95p latency < 300ms for short responses, cost target < $0.02/1k tokens, stability variance < 5% across runs, autoscale latency increase < 30%.
- Workload sizing: avg 1.8k tokens/request, peak concurrency ~80, pilot credit recommendation ~2M tokens.
- Timeline: Draft pilot agreement by 2025-09-26; baseline benchmark by 2025-09-30; on-prem deep dive target 2025-10-02; procurement timeline confirmation by 2025-10-06.
- Commercial notes: Credits provided for pilot with option to convert unused credits to invoice credit if converting to paid contract; pilot does not obligate long-term commitment; path to MSA/SOW for Dedicated or Private if pilot successful.

References/attachments mentioned in call:
- Pilot terms draft: https://redwood.example/pilot-terms
- Performance template: https://redwood.example/pilot-benchmarks
- Security pack: https://redwood.example/security-pack.pdf

End of transcript.

[08:04] Ava Chen: We provide credits free for the pilot period; if you convert to a paid contract we can convert unused pilot credits into a dollar credit against the first invoice — subject to contract terms. We'll put that option in the draft pilot agreement.

[08:25] Ava Chen: Pilot agreement standard: 30-day term, either party can end with 10-day notice, Redwood retains aggregated logs for 60 days unless you request immediate deletion; any raw customer data will be handled per the DPA you sign. We'll capture this in the draft.
[08:42] Lena Ortiz: Important: audit logs export and timestamp integrity for regulatory review.
[08:47] Ravi Kapoor: We'll include audit log export steps and formats — JSON lines and S3-compatible export detailed in the security pack.
[08:55] [09:30] Daniel Park: A couple more success criteria items: model stability — no more than 5% variance on F1 across three runs, and autoscaling should not increase 95th latency by more than 30% during scale events.
[09:48] Ava Chen: We'll add those to the acceptance criteria list. Good call.
[09:55] Ravi Kapoor: For instrumentation we'll provide a shared dashboard with token-level cost, latency histograms, and per-route QPS, and we'll drop CSVs into the shared perf sheet after each run.
[10:12] Samira Khan: We'll need network egress costing too; if you run hosted tests with our data there could be egress.
[10:19] Ava Chen: We'll surface egress numbers in the cost model; if you go Private, egress is internal.
[10:25] Tom Reed: Procurement question — when do you need a signed PO to start the pilot?
[10:31] Ava Chen: If the pilot uses only credits and is under a mutual pilot agreement we can start once the pilot agreement is signed; a PO is not required for the credit-funded pilot. However procurement often wants the pilot agreement countersigned for internal tracking — we'll support that.
[10:44] Daniel Park: Risk question — are there any throttles or soft-limits we should expect in hosted that would affect the benchmark?
[10:51] Ravi Kapoor: On hosted we may have rate limits per account; for pilot runs we typically lift rate limits or apply a temporary burst policy. We'll document this and provision the account accordingly.
[11:03] [11:45] Ava Chen: Let's recap next steps: 1) Redwood to issue draft pilot agreement + credit schedule; 2) BlueWave to provide de-identified 1k gold set and network contacts; 3) Redwood SE to run a 5k benchmark baseline and share results; 4) Procurement to share PO process. Any objections?
[11:23] Tom Reed: Sounds good. We'll aim to get our procurement checklist to you by 2025-10-06.
[11:29] Lena Ortiz: We'll look at the DPA and confirm data residency requirements.
[11:33] Samira Khan: I'll drop firewall rules and VPC peering contacts into the shared doc.
[11:37] Ava Chen: Perfect. We'll send the package by end of day Friday (2025-09-26).
[11:44] Daniel Park: Quick question — benchmarking cadence during pilot, how often will you run?
[11:48] Ravi Kapoor: We'll do an initial baseline run within the first week, then two iteration runs after any tuning — about 3-4 benchmark runs total across the pilot. We'll timestamp and note config changes in the results.
[11:59] Ava Chen: Anything else?
[12:03] Daniel Park: No, that's it from me. Appreciate the clarity.
[12:07] Ava Chen: Thanks everyone. We'll follow up with the docs and book the technical deep dive.
[12:12] [12:45] Meeting closed.

Auto-summary (note: auto-gen may be imperfect):
- Purpose: Scope a 30-day pilot to validate extraction accuracy and performance for BlueWave Health's triage assistant; discuss pilot credits, security/DPA, and contracting path.
- Key success criteria: F1 >= 0.92 on 1k gold set, 95p latency < 300ms for short responses, cost target < $0.02/1k tokens, stability variance < 5% across runs, autoscale latency increase < 30%.
- Workload sizing: avg 1.8k tokens/request, peak concurrency ~80, pilot credit recommendation ~2M tokens.
- Timeline: Draft pilot agreement by 2025-09-26; baseline benchmark by 2025-09-30; on-prem deep dive target 2025-10-02; procurement timeline confirmation by 2025-10-06.
- Commercial notes: Credits provided for pilot with option to convert unused credits to invoice credit if converting to paid contract; pilot does not obligate long-term commitment; path to MSA/SOW for Dedicated or Private if pilot successful.

References/attachments mentioned in call:

- Performance template: https://redwood.example/pilot-benchmarks

title: Autumn Townhall: Roadmap, Ops & Ask Me Anything
preview:
Header:
Date: 2025-09-10
Start: 16:00 UTC
Duration: ~75 minutes
Attendees: Sanjay Rao, Leah Kim, Jonah Miles, Ava Chen, Marco Diaz, Priya Singh + all company (townhall)

Summary (auto-gen, may be imperfect):
- Leadership reviewed Q3 performance, runway and hiring pause lift plan.
- Product walked through prioritization of model hosting work, Dedicated capacity optimizations, and private deployment milestones.
- Ops/infra talked about latency SLOs, KV cache improvements, and a migration plan for a critical kernel selector that will reduce 95p latency.
- Low-level Q&A included questions on SOC two, VPC options, on-prem packaging, and hiring priorities.

00:00 Sanjay (CEO): Hey everyone, good afternoon and thanks for joining, um, our autumn townhall. I'm gonna kick it off with a quick update on numbers and then pass to Leah and Jonah for product and infra. We wanted to—uh—make this a bit of an ops deep dive and keep a long Q and A at the end so grab coffee.

00:34 Sanjay: Quick bullets — revenue up 14% quarter-over-quarter, usage growth in hosted API about 20%, churn stable, enterprise pipeline healthy. We did see some bookings push into next quarter on a couple of big deals which means we have to be careful about runway math, but the burn is trending better thanks to cost optimizations.

01:02 Sanjay: Hiring pause is being lifted for priority roles — we will still be deliberate. Priya will cover the hiring cadence shortly. Um, thanks to Eng and Product for hitting a good set of deliveries this quarter.

01:25 Leah (CTO): Hi folks. Quick engineering update — we shipped the first pass of the quantized kernel selector for Llama variants last month. Early internal numbers show about 22% reduction in token cost on our dedicated fleet when using the new path, and it shrinks 95p latency by around 8 to 12 percent. Caveat: measurement noise and the usual, um, benchmark drift.

02:00 Leah: We're also changing how the runtime does prefix caching — there's an experimental feature we call "prefix-K" in the branch, which is intended to reduce repeated prefix compute for multi-turn chat. It's still noisy; we need more telemetry. Side note: some of the internal logs mis-typed it once as "KV cash" in last week's deploy notes, so ignore that in dashboards.

02:28 Jonah (VP Product): Thanks Leah. Product priorities for the next 8–12 weeks — (1) stabilize Dedicated autoscaler and make burst capacity configurable per-tenant, (2) ship private on-prem packaging with a hardened control plane option, (3) make Optimize suggestions available in the Console as a first-class tab. The rationale: customers keep telling us they need predictable throughput and easier ops for on-prem.

03:05 Jonah: About rollouts — we want to give teams the ability to pin model variants per-route and set graceful fallbacks; that'll be a canary + auto-fallback pattern. Goal is to avoid noisy neighbor issues and avoid surprises on latency SLOs.

03:46 Ava (Head Eng): A couple of infra things — we're planning a rolling upgrade of CUDA on the Dedicated fleet in mid-October. We'll do it region by region, low-traffic windows, and there will be a short capacity dip that Marco's team will coordinate with the sales ops. Also, there is a plan to add per-tenant KV cache metrics to the Console so customers can tune cache TTLs.

04:22 Ava: Note — the running test for the new kernel path showed an edge case for seq length = 4096 on some older drivers, it causes a small memory regression; we have a mitigation but it needs more validation.

04:46 Marco (Head Sales): From GTM — enterprise interest in Dedicated and Private is increasing. We closed two pilots for regulated industries with dedicated reservations. Pricing feedback: customers want clearer tradeoffs between quantization, throughput and observed hallucination rates; the word I keep hearing is "predictable." We'll share an updated pilot pack.

05:12 Priya (Head of People): Quick update — we lifted hiring pause selectively for SWE II and above, SRE and security hires prioritized, and we'll run two all-hands hiring Q&As next Tuesday and Thursday for interviewers. Also reminder: we are rolling out updated parental leave policy next month.

05:40 Sanjay: Alright, I'm gonna switch gears to roadmap context and the bets we're making. We've had a strategic thesis for a while: the advantage moves to serving. That hasn't changed. We're doubling down on making inference predictable and cost-efficient across hosted, dedicated, and private. This quarter that means: autoscaling, KV prefix caching improvements, and the private control plane hardening.

06:18 Sanjay: We also want to be disciplined about scope. Some feature asks are delightful but don't move the needle on unit economics or reliability. We'll prioritize accordingly.

07:00 Audience Q&A (open mic)

07:12 Employee 1 (Design): Hi, quick question on the private on-prem timeline — is the initial on-prem deliverable gonna be air-gapped ready or a follow-up?

07:22 Jonah: Short term it's a private VPC control plane with an on-prem optional data plane — air-gap is a follow-up. We want a safe, manageable first release; air-gapped customers have additional compliance glue and that's a second milestone.

07:40 Employee 2 (SWE): Are we planning to support specialized tensor cores for some of the newer chips?

07:48 Leah: Yes, kernel selection will include hardware-specific kernels for tensor cores. That work is in progress; timeline depends on upstream vendor drivers but it's in our internal backlog. Expect a preview in the next quarter.

08:12 Employee 3 (Ops): Any change to SLOs during CUDA upgrades?

08:20 Ava: We'll maintain our public SLOs and communicate windows; the only impact is temporary capacity reduction during each region's brief maintenance window. We'll publish a schedule.

08:48 Employee 4 (Security): Status on SOC two and ISO? We had a customer ask about continuous audit logs and KMS integration.

09:00 Leah: SOC two Type II is in progress; audit readiness checklist nearly complete. KMS integrations for Private are prioritized — HSM/KMS support is in the roadmap and we expect initial partners for cloud KMS by Q4. Note: sometimes the compliance docs auto-generated reports refer to "SOC two" in prose which is odd but it's the same thing.

09:32 Employee 5 (Sales Engineer): Any guidance for customers asking about per-token cost measurement and variances across models?

09:45 Marco: We recommend sharing per-route token breakdowns from the token-level cost dashboard. Also, Recommend using Optimize suggestions (when available) to tune batch sizes and cache TTLs. We'll create a small playbook and sample spreadsheet to share.

10:16 Sanjay: Okay, a couple of specific operational asks — (1) we must reduce our public infra spend by 6% without impacting reliability; (2) ship the private control plane MVP by end of Q4 with the on-prem data plane alpha. Those are our top company-level deliverables this quarter.

11:00 Jonah: To hit those we'll be prioritizing model families that give the best throughput/cost tradeoffs for our largest customers. We'll pause some smaller exploratory integrations to free engineering cycles.

11:20 Priya: Quick note on org changes — we are creating a small Platform Reliability team that will sit between infra and product to drive SLOs and escalations. It'll be about 6 people initially.

11:43 Sanjay: Questions about runway — yes, we trimmed discretionary spend and are closely watching enterprise close timing. We're comfortable with the plan as long as the pipeline continues to convert at current rates. We'll provide an updated financial slide deck in the follow-up email.

12:10 Employee 6 (New Hire): Hi, I'm new — how should I think about docs and onboarding for our runtime primitives?

12:20 Ava: Welcome. Two things — there's a runtime playbook in the internal docs, and we'll add a runtime fundamentals session to the new hire curriculum next month. Also we'll publish a short "how to run a benchmark against Dedicated" guide.

12:54 Sanjay: Alright I'm gonna call on Leah to show a quick demo clip — Leah, are you sharing your screen?

13:05 Leah: Yep, I can share. This is a short recording from the internal perf dashboard showing the kernel selector flipping between reference and quantized kernels. You'll see the 95th percentile latency bucket move a bit. Warning: audio in this clip is rough, it's from an internal run.

13:40 Leah: [demo narration omitted in transcript artifact; clip played for attendees]

15:05 Sanjay: Thanks Leah. There's a common thread — customers want predictability and clear tradeoffs; we plan to make those tradeoffs explicit in the Console and in our pricing materials.

15:30 Employee 7 (Support): There was a ticket about mis-attributed model names in logs; is that being tracked?

15:40 Ava: Yes, there's an issue to normalize model name aliases coming from the model catalog. That's in the infra backlog with a priority of P2.

16:05 Employee 8 (Legal): Any update on contract template changes for Private deployments? Some customers want stronger indemnity language.

16:20 Marco: Yes, legal and sales are collaborating on a Private deployment playbook and contract addendum. We'll standardize the addendum with templated SLAs.

16:50 Open Mic Rapid-Fire Questions

17:00 Employee 9: Are we doing more community benchmarks?

17:05 Jonah: Yes, but we'll be more careful — publish curated workloads where we can show real-world latencies rather than synthetic bursty numbers.

17:30 Employee 10: Any changes to the PLG funnel?

17:38 Marco: We're testing a faster onramp for self-serve Dedicated trials (limited capacity) and improved onboarding templates. Expect some experiments next month.

18:05 Sanjay: Ok, some smaller items before we wrap — retention bonuses for early ICs were discussed earlier in leadership; we'll share the eligibility matrix this week.

18:30 Priya: Also, remember Friday is team offsite check-in day; managers please share agendas by Wednesday.

18:50 Sanjay: Final Q: What keeps me up — making sure we cross the reliability threshold customers require while staying capital-efficient. We need excellent execution; we will keep communicating tradeoffs transparently.

19:20 Final wrap and explicit next steps recap

19:30 Sanjay: Recap: Share Q3 financial slide deck by Friday, product to publish internal roadmap doc, infra will post maintenance windows schedule for October CUDA upgrades, People to run hiring Q&A sessions. Action items will be tracked in Asana.

19:55 Sanjay: Thanks everyone — we'll keep these townhalls regular. If you have follow-ups drop them in #townhall-questions or email leadership.

End of transcript.

Notes on transcription artifacts: speaker overlap and some brand names are occasionally mis-transcribed (e.g., "KV cash" for KV cache, "SOC two" typed out). Some timestamps approximate the start of remarks.

title: POC timeline, checkpoints, and benchmark SLA discussion - Quantio Health POC
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-09-02
Start time: 15:05 UTC
Duration: ~42 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Marcus Patel (Redwood SE), Liam Ortega (Quantio HoML), Priya Rao (Quantio Eng Mgr), Derek Mills (Quantio Security), Sana Ahmed (Quantio Product)

Summary:
Quick scoping call to align on POC timeline, success criteria, workload sizing and benchmark plan. Primary goals: define kickoff and access gating, agree mid-point review metrics, and set expectations for exec readout. Some security constraints (VPC/KMS/SOC2) and latency SLOs discussed.

Agenda / Topics:
- POC objectives & success criteria
- Workload sizing and sample collection
- Benchmark definitions (latency, throughput, quality)
- Access and network setup (VPC, IP allowlist, KMS)
- Timeline and checkpoints: kickoff, access window, mid-point review, exec readout
- Next steps and owners

Transcript:
00:00:03 Ava: Hey everyone, thanks for joining, uh we'll kick off, quick intros?
00:00:10 Liam: Yep Liam here, Head of ML at Quantio. We're focused on putting Redwood behind a patient-facing summarization flow, latency and cost are the big ones for us.
00:00:22 Priya: Priya, eng manager, I'll own integration side.
00:00:26 Derek: Derek, security. I'll vet the VPC/KMS stuff.
00:00:30 Sana: Sana, product - focus on exec readout metrics.
00:00:35 Marcus: Marcus from Redwood, I'll be SE for the POC - running benchmarks and helping with access.
00:00:41 Ava: Cool, thanks. Quick framing - we're aiming for a 4-week POC with clear checkpoints: kickoff, access setup, a mid-point performance review, then exec readout. But want to lock what success looks like.
00:01:02 Liam: For us, success = < 300ms p95 on short responses, overall cost < $0.018 per 1k tokens equivalent, and accuracy/ROUGE-type metric no worse than our current fine-tuned baseline.
00:01:18 Marcus: Noted. For latency, we should capture p50/p95/p99 and tail behavior under load. We'll define concurrency and sequence length distributions.
00:01:30 Priya: Sequence lengths vary a lot - 128 tokens typical, 512 for long form. Our 90th percentile is around 360.
00:01:40 Ava: Good to know. Liam, can you share a 24-48 hour trace of real requests? Even aggregated token length histogram helps.
00:01:48 Liam: Yes we can, but some PII concerns - will anonymize.
00:01:54 Derek: We'll need to ensure redaction is solid, and also specifics around data residency. We're US-only for now.
00:02:03 Marcus: We can run against anonymized payloads, or use representative synthetic prompts. For performance tests, synthetics are fine as long as they preserve length and prompt complexity distributions.
00:02:15 Sana: For the exec readout, we want a simple dashboard: avg latency, p95, cost per 1k tokens, and a short quality sample set.
00:02:25 Ava: Ok we'll provide a standard dashboard template.

00:02:42 Marcus: On the technical side - access. Two deployment flavors you asked about: Hosted API vs Dedicated vs Private VPC. I know Derek leaned toward private or dedicated?
00:02:52 Derek: Privacy + audit logs + KMS integration, so private is preferred but we can consider dedicated if private is too heavy for POC. Need to see SOC2 artifacts too.
00:03:03 Ava: We'll share SOC2 cert and the compliance pack. For KMS we support customer-managed keys for private installs; for hosted we can negotiate a key escrow pattern, but Derek you'll want customer KMS.
00:03:17 Derek: Yep. Also IP allowlist and SAML SSO for admin access are must-haves.
00:03:24 Marcus: Noted. For ingress we usually request a peered VPC or IP allowlist; we'll provide runbook. For peered VPC we'll need a window to test connectivity and endpoint firewall rules.
00:03:36 Priya: How long does peering typically take?
00:03:39 Marcus: Usually 2-3 business days if we have the network contacts and IP ranges. We can also do a temporary SSH jump or VPN for the POC to speed it up, but that needs security approval.
00:03:51 Derek: VPN is likely easier for initial benchmark runs. We'll confirm.

00:04:05 Ava: Back to benchmarks - what workloads do you want measured? Latency under 1, 5, 10 concurrent users? Throughput in tokens/sec? Quality evaluation?
00:04:14 Liam: Let's do: single-request latency, burst of 10 concurrent, sustained 100 req/min baseline. Throughput: tokens/sec at 128/256/512 sequences. Quality: a holdout of 150 prompts scored against our gold summaries.
00:04:30 Marcus: Good. We'll run cold start and warm cache scenarios. Note: our runtime supports KV cache and prefix caching which will significantly change p95 depending on cache hit rate.
00:04:43 Priya: How easy to simulate cache hit patterns?
00:04:46 Marcus: We can parameterize the harness to force certain KV cache hit rates; we'll document assumptions.

00:05:02 Sana: Timeline specifics? You mentioned 4 weeks earlier - can you break that into gates?
00:05:09 Ava: Proposed: week 0 = kickoff and access; week 1 = baseline benchmarks + integration; week 2 = mid-point review with initial numbers and tuning; week 3 = final runs and prep; week 4 = exec readout. But we can compress if access is fast.
00:05:23 Liam: If access is delayed, can we do initial hosted runs to at least get numbers?
00:05:28 Marcus: Yes, we can run hosted tests with synthetic data to validate config. But because your compliance needs customer-managed KMS, final numbers should be on private/dedicated. Hosted is for early signal.
00:05:40 Derek: Hosted is fine for pre-checks but not for final acceptance.

00:05:52 Ava: Milestone names: Kickoff (this week once we sign off), Access window (network & KMS ready), Mid-point review (2 weeks after access), Finalization (1 week), Exec readout (presentation). Exec readout is demo + topline metrics + a couple of representative transcripts.
00:06:10 Liam: Who owns the exec slides?
00:06:12 Sana: I'd like Redwood to provide a first-pass slide deck and we'll adapt.
00:06:16 Ava: We'll draft the exec deck after mid-point with topline numbers; Redwood will include an appendix with raw benchmark charts and the benchmark spreadsheet link.
00:06:25 Marcus: Speaking of spreadsheets, I'll post the benchmark template and the harness instructions to the shared drive. Link will be included in the follow-up.

00:06:40 Priya: On sizing: our production peak is ~60 concurrent users hitting summarization flows, translating to approx 14 tokens/sec average per user, but bursts up to 5x during morning syncs.
00:06:52 Marcus: So plan for sustained 60 concurrent with 5x burst tests. We'll run step load tests.
00:06:59 Liam: Cost model concern: we'll need Redwood to show cost with quantization toggles. We want rough delta between fp16 and int8 for cost/latency tradeoffs.
00:07:09 Marcus: We'll include quantization profiles and expected token/sec improvements. Note that int8 may change output behavior slightly; we'll include quality checks.

00:07:25 Derek: One more security note - audit logs must be exportable and retained for 90 days for the POC and potentially longer. Also, SSO for console access and RBAC are required.
00:07:36 Ava: We support audit log export and RBAC; Marcus will share the retention config options and default schemas.
00:07:43 Marcus: I'll include the log schema and sample JSONs.

00:07:55 Sana: For the mid-point review, what's the acceptance criteria? We need gating to decide whether to continue to exec readout.
00:08:02 Ava: Proposed mid-point acceptance criteria: 
- Performance: p95 within 1.25x of target (so if target 300ms p95, mid-point should be <= 375ms)
- Stability: no more than 3 incidents during runs
- Quality: holdout set quality drop <= 5% relative to baseline
- Security: connectivity and KMS validated
00:08:30 Liam: That seems reasonable. If mid-point fails we agree to an extension window to tune and rerun.
00:08:36 Ava: Correct. We'll agree on an extension trigger and a max one-week extension.

00:08:45 Priya: How do we handle experiment reproducibility? We'll need the exact model version, quantization, and runtime settings captured.
00:08:53 Marcus: We'll output an immutable run manifest with model commit id, kernel selection, batch configuration, and assertion of cache state. We'll attach manifests to the spreadsheet.
00:09:05 Liam: Great. Marcus, do you anticipate any special infra requirements for your end?
00:09:09 Marcus: For private deployment we'll ask for an initial test cluster with at least 4x A100-class GPUs for the runs, but for the POC we can use smaller instance types depending on scale. We'll size based on the workload traces you share.
00:09:21 Priya: Cost center - who pays for the infra during POC?
00:09:24 Ava: Typically Redwood absorbs baseline infra for hosted tests; for private/dedicated POC there's a shared cost model— we'll include pricing in the follow-up.
00:09:33 Derek: We'll need explicit billing caps and a way to cap spend.
00:09:36 Ava: We'll include a spend cap in the POC runbook.

00:09:45 Marcus: I'll outline the benchmark matrix: scenarios (cold/warm), sequence lengths (128/256/512), concurrency (1/10/60 with 5x burst), metrics (p50/p95/p99, tokens/sec, cost/1k tokens, quality scores), and gating criteria.
00:10:02 Liam: Please also include the thermal of model switching to a smaller variant if latency exceeds targets. We may accept small model fallback if quality drop is minimal.
00:10:12 Marcus: We'll add fallback policy tests.

00:10:22 Sana: Anything else for the exec readout? C-suite will ask about reliability, cost savings, and next steps to production.
00:10:29 Ava: We'll craft the slide to call out those items with recommended roadmap for production (Dedicated or Private, estimated costs, SLA expectations).

00:10:38 Priya: On instrumentation - what telemetry do we need to integrate? We already have Datadog and internal logging.
00:10:44 Marcus: We can emit metrics to Datadog and provide a tracing hook. We'll provide sample dashboards and the probe endpoints.
00:10:52 Derek: Need encryption in transit and rest; KMS handles rest. Confirmed.
00:10:58 Marcus: Confirmed.

00:11:05 Ava: Ok let's recap actions — I'll send the benchmark template and a proposed 4-week calendar. Marcus will circulate the harness and runbook. Derek provide KMS/IP details. Liam will provide traces and holdout sets.
00:11:21 Liam: We'll get those by end of next week.
00:11:24 Derek: I'll try to get SOC2 and KMS details by Sep 5.
00:11:28 Marcus: I'll try to have the harness ready and preliminary hosted tests by Sep 9.
00:11:34 Ava: We'll set a tentative kickoff for Sep 10 with an access window from Sep 10-12. Mid-point review around Sep 24. Exec readout target Oct 1. Everyone ok?
00:11:46 Priya: Works for us as calendar rough dates.
00:11:49 Sana: Works.
00:11:51 Derek: Works, pending network windows.

00:12:00 Ava: Great. We'll follow up with links and the spreadsheet. Thanks all.

(Transcription quality note: medium — some proper nouns may be off; e.g., 'KV cache' sometimes transcribed as 'K V cash', 'quantization' rendered correctly but occasional filler words retained.)

End of meeting.

00:03:39 Marcus: Usually 2-3 business days if we have the network contacts and IP ranges. We can also do a temporary SSH jump or VPN for the POC to speed it up, but that needs security approval.
00:03:51 Derek: VPN is likely easier for initial benchmark runs. We'll confirm.

00:04:05 Ava: Back to benchmarks - what workloads do you want measured? Latency under 1, 5, 10 concurrent users? Throughput in tokens/sec? Quality evaluation?
00:04:14 Liam: Let's do: single-request latency, burst of 10 concurrent, sustained 100 req/min baseline. Throughput: tokens/sec at 128/256/512 sequences. Quality: a holdout of 150 prompts scored against our gold summaries.
00:04:30 Marcus: Good. We'll run cold start and warm cache scenarios. Note: our runtime supports KV cache and prefix caching which will significantly change p95 depending on cache hit rate.
00:04:43 Priya: How easy to simulate cache hit patterns?
00:04:46 Marcus: We can parameterize the harness to force certain KV cache hit rates; we'll document assumptions.

00:05:02 Sana: Timeline specifics? You mentioned 4 weeks earlier - can you break that into gates?
00:05:09 Ava: Proposed: week 0 = kickoff and access; week 1 = baseline benchmarks + integration; week 2 = mid-point review with initial numbers and tuning; week 3 = final runs and prep; week 4 = exec readout. But we can compress if access is fast.

00:05:40 Derek: Hosted is fine for pre-checks but not for final acceptance.

- Performance: p95 within 1.25x of target (so if target 300ms p95, mid-point should be <= 375ms)

- Quality: holdout set quality drop <= 5% relative to baseline

title: Content Safety: Sidecar vs Inline Filter Placement — Latency & Cost Deep Dive
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-08-07
Start: 15:30 UTC
Duration: ~52 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Samir Patel (Redwood SE), Priya Rao (BlueBridge CTO), Miguel Torres (ML Engineer), Janelle Kim (Security Lead)

Summary:
Quick technical deep dive to decide placement of content safety checks (inline in model runtime vs sidecar pre/post filters) for BlueBridge Health PII-sensitive assistant. Focus on runtime overhead, KV cache/streaming implications, batching, quantization, and VPC/on-prem constraints. Planning to establish guardrails for latency-sensitive paths and to run benchmarks.

Topics:
- inline vs sidecar (pre-filter, post-filter)
- streaming moderation (early-abort vs post-hoc flagging)
- KV cache interactions and prefix caching when sidecar alters tokens
- batching, quantization and CPU/GPU tradeoffs
- VPC/on-prem constraints for safety tooling
- rollback/fallback strategies for safety pipeline

Transcript:
[00:00] Ava (Redwood AE): thanks everyone for joining, we'll keep this pretty technical—Samir will cover runtime tradeoffs and bench numbers. Priya, want to give 30s context on BlueBridge's constraints?
[00:17] Priya (BlueBridge CTO): yeah thanks Ava. We're building an assistant for patient intake and triage, lots of PII, must be auditable, and latency targets are tight — sub 600ms tail for interactive flows. We're evaluating whether to run moderation inline on model host, or as a sidecar in front or behind. Security wants minimal data in transit and clear audit trails.
[00:38] Samir (Redwood SE): cool. high level, three common placements: inline (safety logic embedded in the generation path), sidecar pre-filter (filter input before it hits the model), and sidecar post-filter (check outputs, potentially streaming). each has different latency/cost/resilience profiles.
[00:54] Miguel (BlueBridge ML Eng): can you quantify overhead? like tokens/sec or ms per request; we need ballpark to make decisions.
[01:04] Samir: sure, ballparks depend on model size, quantization, and whether safety runs on GPU or CPU. for an Llama2-70b-ish style quantized model on A100, model generation cost is X ms per token (we gave bench numbers in the deck). safety inline adds ~10-40% additional latency depending on complexity — a single classifier head run on-accelerator is cheap, maybe +5-15ms per request, but multi-stage policy checks or regex/Pii detectors on CPU can add more.
[01:30] Janelle (Security): what do you mean by 'classifier head on-accelerator'? are you talking about doing a forward pass through a small safety model attached to the same runtime?
[01:39] Samir: yes, two patterns. one is a tiny transformer classifier that runs inline on the GPU using the same inference engine — that leverages the already-warmed context and KV cache so marginal cost is lower. the other pattern is hooking an external microservice (on CPU) for checks — that's sidecar and often incurs network hop and serialization.
[01:58] Priya: And what about streaming? We do real-time typing assistance and can't wait for full sequence. If we do inline safety after each token, that seems expensive.
[02:08] Samir: exact. token-level inline checks are heavy. we usually recommend either partial streaming + background safety scoring, or pre-filtering inputs aggressively. for streaming output you can do an early-abort policy: run a lightweight on-GPU classifier on prefixes to catch high-probability violations, then if flagged, stop and run heavier post-filter.
[02:28] Miguel: how does this interact with KV cache? if sidecar post-filters modify/replace tokens, does that invalidate cache and cause re-generation
[02:36] Samir: good question. yes, if your post-filter alters the sequence you risk KV cache mismatch and must either re-generate (expensive) or apply deterministic edits client-side. inline moderation that masks disallowed tokens before they emit preserves cache consistency because model never stores the forbidden tokens in KV. sidecar pre-filter also preserves cache because it doesn't touch model outputs; it only changes inputs.
[02:58] Ava: to be clear: pre-filter = examine prompt and block/transform before send to model; inline = safety sits inside the model loop and prevents forbidden emissions; post-filter = check outputs and then possibly redact or re-generate.
[03:10] Priya: what's the cost delta? if we pick inline on GPU vs sidecar CPU what hits our bill more?
[03:17] Samir: sidecar CPU services are cheaper compute-per-second but add latency and complexity at scale. inline on GPU increases GPU cycles slightly but keeps one hop and benefits from batching. rough rule: a lightweight GPU inline check costs less at high QPS when you can batch safety checks with generation; at low QPS, CPU sidecars can be more cost-effective.
[03:36] Janelle: our compliance folks want an immutable audit trail. Sidecar pre or post has clearer logs, but inline — how do we get auditable traces if checks run inside the model process?
[03:46] Samir: you can emit structured safety events to the control plane from inline checks — we log classifier decisions, token positions, hashed contexts for integrity. inline implementation needs to surface hooks to the observability stack. Redwood Console can capture token-level safety decisions and store them in the audit logs with retention controls.
[04:06] Priya: any difference for private/VPC/on-prem? We prefer VPC for PHI.
[04:12] Samir: on-prem or VPC means you control network so sidecar patterns are simpler (local network hop). but resource constraints matter: many customers don't have spare GPUs for safety models, so run safety on CPU or smaller GPU. On-prem with strict egress rules sometimes forces both model and safety to be co-located; that tends to favor inline or local sidecar.
[04:31] Miguel: can quantization break our safety models? we plan to use 4-bit quantization for generator to save cost.
[04:38] Samir: quantization mostly affects the main model; small safety classifiers are usually tiny and run in fp16 or int8 and are robust. the main risk is when you use quantized generation with aggressive kernel optimizations that change tokenization/outputs distribution — that can alter safety classifier calibration. so we recommend re-calibrating safety thresholds when switching quantization profiles.
[04:57] Ava: another nuance is batching. Redwood does continuous batching; if safety is inline and per-request, we can include safety work in same batch. Sidecar pre-filter may be synchronous and block batching opportunities.
[05:11] Miguel: example numbers? say 32ms average per token generation, how much added by inline classifier vs sidecar call?
[05:18] Samir: example: base token gen 32ms. inline lightweight classifier amortized in batch might add 2-6ms per token. a sidecar roundtrip on local VPC might add 8-25ms median depending on serialization and CPU load; worst-case remote sidecar (cross-region) could add 100+ms.
[05:37] Priya: streaming again: can we do 'optimistic streaming' — stream tokens downstream and then if post-filter finds violation, retract?
[05:44] Samir: retracting is messy for UX and cache. pattern is optimistic stream: show tokens with low-confidence labels and mark segments as provisional; if a later heavier check flags content, you either send a correction, an apology, or in critical flows suspend the session and re-render. For healthcare, retracting might be unacceptable; better to prevent emission for high-risk categories.
[06:03] Janelle: so for us prevention is preferred. we need high recall on PII detection.
[06:08] Samir: for prevention you want checks as early as possible (pre-filter or inline prefix checks). pre-filter is great for PII extraction before it ever gets into logs, which helps reduce PII exposure. inline prevention is stronger for hallucinated PII or model-suggested unsafe outputs.
[06:24] Miguel: what about hybrid? pre-filter for known PII patterns, inline for hallucinations, and post-filter for paraphrase checks?
[06:30] Samir: yes hybrid is common. e.g., pre-filter strip SSNs/emails, inline classifier on GPU for semantic safety, and post-filter for final QA and human review workflows. you get best of both worlds but pay engineering to maintain pipelines.
[06:43] Priya: what are the operational risks? complexity increases Surface area for bugs and latency spikes.
[06:48] Samir: main ops risks: cascading failures if sidecar blocks or times out (we recommend circuit-breakers and fallbacks to lower-safety but higher-availability modes with audit annotation), cache invalidation bugs if edits are applied post-hoc, and misconfiguration across environments (prod vs staging). Redwood recommends automated canary tests and synthetic prompt sets to monitor regressions.
[07:07] Ava: let's go through a 3-scenario benchmark we propose for you: (A) inline on-GPU classifier with 1-stage token-level checks; (B) sidecar pre-filter on CPU + model generation; (C) post-filter sidecar with optimistic streaming. we'll measure p50/p95 latency for 128/512/2k tokens under QPS 1, 10, 50, and measure cost per 1M tokens.
[07:31] Miguel: sounds good. can you also include quantized vs non-quantized delta?
[07:35] Samir: will do, we'll test 4-bit quantized generator + fp16 safety classifier vs fp16 generator + fp16 classifier.

[07:45] -- periodic interjection while Samir pulls up a slide --
[08:01] Samir: sharing screen — rough bench from our internal lab: inline adds +8ms median for small classifier and +22ms p95 under batching load; sidecar pre on local CPU adds +18ms median and +45ms p95; sidecar post-streaming optimistic adds lower median but higher tail because of rechecks (+30-150ms tails).
[08:22] Priya: those tails worry us. our SLA is 600ms tail end-to-end.
[08:27] Samir: with those numbers, inline is safest for keeping tail low, but you need to ensure classifier is small and can be batched. Also consider using prefix/KV caching to reduce model generation time — if your prompts are repetitive the cache reduces tokens generated and hence decreases the absolute safety overhead.
[08:45] Janelle: reference architecture — do you have examples of sidecar deployment on VPC with private endpoints and KMS for logging?
[08:52] Ava: yes we'll share an architecture note — typical: model hosts in private subnets, sidecar services co-located on same subnet with mutual TLS, logs forwarded to customer-controlled SIEM via encrypted channel, KMS for key management. Redwood Private can be deployed with no egress and with HSM integrations.
[09:12] Miguel: one more question — does inline safety require retraining or fine-tuning the main LLM to be "safety-aware"?
[09:19] Samir: not necessarily. inline classifier can be a separate head that uses hidden states; you can also use logit filtering (banlist of tokens) without retraining. for semantic checks, a small fine-tuned detector might help but is optional.
[09:31] Priya: okay, let's talk failure modes: if inline classifier false-positives block valid content, what's the UX and ops path?
[09:39] Samir: you need override policies and audit trails. typical pattern: for high-risk searches block and send to human review; for low-risk allow with annotation. We support per-route policies and rollout with canaries to tune thresholds.
[09:52] Ava: I'll note that in action items — Samir will run the bench, we'll share threshold tuning tips, and we'll provide config examples for sidecar and inline setups.

[10:02] -- longer discussion about telemetry and synthetic tests --
[10:22] Janelle: one more compliance ask — we need the ability to redact PII from logs before stored. how does that fit with sidecar vs inline?
[10:31] Samir: easiest is pre-filter redaction before logging — sidecar pre-filter can strip PII and only forward hashed or redacted context to the model and logs. inline can also redact before any persistent logging if you hook the inspection pipeline before the logging call; that's an engineering discipline but supported.
[10:50] Priya: we prefer pre-filter redaction then inline semantic checks. keep raw PII out of logs entirely.
[10:58] Ava: make sense.

[11:04] -- near end recap --
[11:06] Ava: recap: BlueBridge prefers prevention-first. recommended path: implement pre-filter redaction for clear PII, inline lightweight classifier on GPU for semantic safety to keep latency low, and post-filter human review for edge cases. Redwood will run the three-scenario benchmark including quantization delta, share results and a sample cost spreadsheet, and send security pack.
[11:28] Priya: sounds good. timeline: we want bench results and configs by Aug 12 so we can present to compliance on Aug 15.
[11:35] Ava: we'll hit that.

Closing:
[11:40] Miguel: thanks everyone. quick note — can you include example SAML/SO maybe details in the security pack?
[11:47] Janelle: and please include log retention options and redaction examples.
[11:50] Ava: will do. thanks all.

End of transcript.
text:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-08-07
Start: 15:30 UTC
Duration: ~52 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Samir Patel (Redwood SE), Priya Rao (BlueBridge CTO), Miguel Torres (ML Engineer), Janelle Kim (Security Lead)

Summary:
Quick technical deep dive to decide placement of content safety checks (inline in model runtime vs sidecar pre/post filters) for BlueBridge Health PII-sensitive assistant. Focus on runtime overhead, KV cache/streaming implications, batching, quantization, and VPC/on-prem constraints. Planning to establish guardrails for latency-sensitive paths and to run benchmarks.

Topics:
- inline vs sidecar (pre-filter, post-filter)
- streaming moderation (early-abort vs post-hoc flagging)
- KV cache interactions and prefix caching when sidecar alters tokens
- batching, quantization and CPU/GPU tradeoffs
- VPC/on-prem constraints for safety tooling
- rollback/fallback strategies for safety pipeline

Transcript:
[00:00] Ava (Redwood AE): thanks everyone for joining, we'll keep this pretty technical—Samir will cover runtime tradeoffs and bench numbers. Priya, want to give 30s context on BlueBridge's constraints?
[00:17] Priya (BlueBridge CTO): yeah thanks Ava. We're building an assistant for patient intake and triage, lots of PII, must be auditable, and latency targets are tight — sub 600ms tail for interactive flows. We're evaluating whether to run moderation inline on model host, or as a sidecar in front or behind. Security wants minimal data in transit and clear audit trails.
[00:38] Samir (Redwood SE): cool. high level, three common placements: inline (safety logic embedded in the generation path), sidecar pre-filter (filter input before it hits the model), and sidecar post-filter (check outputs, potentially streaming). each has different latency/cost/resilience profiles.
[00:54] Miguel (BlueBridge ML Eng): can you quantify overhead? like tokens/sec or ms per request; we need ballpark to make decisions.
[01:04] Samir: sure, ballparks depend on model size, quantization, and whether safety runs on GPU or CPU. for an Llama2-70b-ish style quantized model on A100, model generation cost is X ms per token (we gave bench numbers in the deck). safety inline adds ~10-40% additional latency depending on complexity — a single classifier head run on-accelerator is cheap, maybe +5-15ms per request, but multi-stage policy checks or regex/Pii detectors on CPU can add more.
[01:30] Janelle (Security): what do you mean by 'classifier head on-accelerator'? are you talking about doing a forward pass through a small safety model attached to the same runtime?
[01:39] Samir: yes, two patterns. one is a tiny transformer classifier that runs inline on the GPU using the same inference engine — that leverages the already-warmed context and KV cache so marginal cost is lower. the other pattern is hooking an external microservice (on CPU) for checks — that's sidecar and often incurs network hop and serialization.
[01:58] Priya: And what about streaming? We do real-time typing assistance and can't wait for full sequence. If we do inline safety after each token, that seems expensive.
[02:08] Samir: exact. token-level inline checks are heavy. we usually recommend either partial streaming + background safety scoring, or pre-filtering inputs aggressively. for streaming output you can do an early-abort policy: run a lightweight on-GPU classifier on prefixes to catch high-probability violations, then if flagged, stop and run heavier post-filter.
[02:28] Miguel: how does this interact with KV cache? if sidecar post-filters modify/replace tokens, does that invalidate cache and cause re-generation
[02:36] Samir: good question. yes, if your post-filter alters the sequence you risk KV cache mismatch and must either re-generate (expensive) or apply deterministic edits client-side. inline moderation that masks disallowed tokens before they emit preserves cache consistency because model never stores the forbidden tokens in KV. sidecar pre-filter also preserves cache because it doesn't touch model outputs; it only changes inputs.
[02:58] Ava: to be clear: pre-filter = examine prompt and block/transform before send to model; inline = safety sits inside the model loop and prevents forbidden emissions; post-filter = check outputs and then possibly redact or re-generate.
[03:10] Priya: what's the cost delta? if we pick inline on GPU vs sidecar CPU what hits our bill more?
[03:17] Samir: sidecar CPU services are cheaper compute-per-second but add latency and complexity at scale. inline on GPU increases GPU cycles slightly but keeps one hop and benefits from batching. rough rule: a lightweight GPU inline check costs less at high QPS when you can batch safety checks with generation; at low QPS, CPU sidecars can be more cost-effective.
[03:36] Janelle: our compliance folks want an immutable audit trail. Sidecar pre or post has clearer logs, but inline — how do we get auditable traces if checks run inside the model process?
[03:46] Samir: you can emit structured safety events to the control plane from inline checks — we log classifier decisions, token positions, hashed contexts for integrity. inline implementation needs to surface hooks to the observability stack. Redwood Console can capture token-level safety decisions and store them in the audit logs with retention controls.
[04:06] Priya: any difference for private/VPC/on-prem? We prefer VPC for PHI.
[04:12] Samir: on-prem or VPC means you control network so sidecar patterns are simpler (local network hop). but resource constraints matter: many customers don't have spare GPUs for safety models, so run safety on CPU or smaller GPU. On-prem with strict egress rules sometimes forces both model and safety to be co-located; that tends to favor inline or local sidecar.
[04:31] Miguel: can quantization break our safety models? we plan to use 4-bit quantization for generator to save cost.
[04:38] Samir: quantization mostly affects the main model; small safety classifiers are usually tiny and run in fp16 or int8 and are robust. the main risk is when you use quantized generation with aggressive kernel optimizations that change tokenization/outputs distribution — that can alter safety classifier calibration. so we recommend re-calibrating safety thresholds when switching quantization profiles.
[04:57] Ava: another nuance is batching. Redwood does continuous batching; if safety is inline and per-request, we can include safety work in same batch. Sidecar pre-filter may be synchronous and block batching opportunities.
[05:11] Miguel: example numbers? say 32ms average per token generation, how much added by inline classifier vs sidecar call?
[05:18] Samir: example: base token gen 32ms. inline lightweight classifier amortized in batch might add 2-6ms per token. a sidecar roundtrip on local VPC might add 8-25ms median depending on serialization and CPU load; worst-case remote sidecar (cross-region) could add 100+ms.
[05:37] Priya: streaming again: can we do 'optimistic streaming' — stream tokens downstream and then if post-filter finds violation, retract?
[05:44] Samir: retracting is messy for UX and cache. pattern is optimistic stream: show tokens with low-confidence labels and mark segments as provisional; if a later heavier check flags content, you either send a correction, an apology, or in critical flows suspend the session and re-render. For healthcare, retracting might be unacceptable; better to prevent emission for high-risk categories.
[06:03] Janelle: so for us prevention is preferred. we need high recall on PII detection.
[06:08] Samir: for prevention you want checks as early as possible (pre-filter or inline prefix checks). pre-filter is great for PII extraction before it ever gets into logs, which helps reduce PII exposure. inline prevention is stronger for hallucinated PII or model-suggested unsafe outputs.
[06:24] Miguel: what about hybrid? pre-filter for known PII patterns, inline for hallucinations, and post-filter for paraphrase checks?
[06:30] Samir: yes hybrid is common. e.g., pre-filter strip SSNs/emails, inline classifier on GPU for semantic safety, and post-filter for final QA and human review workflows. you get best of both worlds but pay engineering to maintain pipelines.
[06:43] Priya: what are the operational risks? complexity increases Surface area for bugs and latency spikes.
[06:48] Samir: main ops risks: cascading failures if sidecar blocks or times out (we recommend circuit-breakers and fallbacks to lower-safety but higher-availability modes with audit annotation), cache invalidation bugs if edits are applied post-hoc, and misconfiguration across environments (prod vs staging). Redwood recommends automated canary tests and synthetic prompt sets to monitor regressions.
[07:07] Ava: let's go through a 3-scenario benchmark we propose for you: (A) inline on-GPU classifier with 1-stage token-level checks; (B) sidecar pre-filter on CPU + model generation; (C) post-filter sidecar with optimistic streaming. we'll measure p50/p95 latency for 128/512/2k tokens under QPS 1, 10, 50, and measure cost per 1M tokens.
[07:31] Miguel: sounds good. can you also include quantized vs non-quantized delta?
[07:35] Samir: will do, we'll test 4-bit quantized generator + fp16 safety classifier vs fp16 generator + fp16 classifier.

[07:45] -- periodic interjection while Samir pulls up a slide --
[08:01] Samir: sharing screen — rough bench from our internal lab: inline adds +8ms median for small classifier and +22ms p95 under batching load; sidecar pre on local CPU adds +18ms median and +45ms p95; sidecar post-streaming optimistic adds lower median but higher tail because of rechecks (+30-150ms tails).
[08:22] Priya: those tails worry us. our SLA is 600ms tail end-to-end.
[08:27] Samir: with those numbers, inline is safest for keeping tail low, but you need to ensure classifier is small and can be batched. Also consider using prefix/KV caching to reduce model generation time — if your prompts are repetitive the cache reduces tokens generated and hence decreases the absolute safety overhead.
[08:45] Janelle: reference architecture — do you have examples of sidecar deployment on VPC with private endpoints and KMS for logging?
[08:52] Ava: yes we'll share an architecture note — typical: model hosts in private subnets, sidecar services co-located on same subnet with mutual TLS, logs forwarded to customer-controlled SIEM via encrypted channel, KMS for key management. Redwood Private can be deployed with no egress and with HSM integrations.
[09:12] Miguel: one more question — does inline safety require retraining or fine-tuning the main LLM to be "safety-aware"?
[09:19] Samir: not necessarily. inline classifier can be a separate head that uses hidden states; you can also use logit filtering (banlist of tokens) without retraining. for semantic checks, a small fine-tuned detector might help but is optional.
[09:31] Priya: okay, let's talk failure modes: if inline classifier false-positives block valid content, what's the UX and ops path?
[09:39] Samir: you need override policies and audit trails. typical pattern: for high-risk searches block and send to human review; for low-risk allow with annotation. We support per-route policies and rollout with canaries to tune thresholds.
[09:52] Ava: I'll note that in action items — Samir will run the bench, we'll share threshold tuning tips, and we'll provide config examples for sidecar and inline setups.

[10:02] -- longer discussion about telemetry and synthetic tests --
[10:22] Janelle: one more compliance ask — we need the ability to redact PII from logs before stored. how does that fit with sidecar vs inline?
[10:31] Samir: easiest is pre-filter redaction before logging — sidecar pre-filter can strip PII and only forward hashed or redacted context to the model and logs. inline can also redact before any persistent logging if you hook the inspection pipeline before the logging call; that's an engineering discipline but supported.
[10:50] Priya: we prefer pre-filter redaction then inline semantic checks. keep raw PII out of logs entirely.
[10:58] Ava: make sense.

[11:04] -- near end recap --
[11:06] Ava: recap: BlueBridge prefers prevention-first. recommended path: implement pre-filter redaction for clear PII, inline lightweight classifier on GPU for semantic safety to keep latency low, and post-filter human review for edge cases. Redwood will run the three-scenario benchmark including quantization delta, share results and a sample cost spreadsheet, and send security pack.
[11:28] Priya: sounds good. timeline: we want bench results and configs by Aug 12 so we can present to compliance on Aug 15.
[11:35] Ava: we'll hit that.

Closing:
[11:40] Miguel: thanks everyone. quick note — can you include example SAML/SO maybe details in the security pack?
[11:47] Janelle: and please include log retention options and redaction examples.
[11:50] Ava: will do. thanks all.

End of transcript.

title: Hosted Chat Quickstart + Console & Cost Controls Demo
preview: Intro demo of Redwood hosted chat generative API with console tour and cost controls. Focus on streaming behavior, key parameters (temperature, max_tokens, stop sequences), retry/backoff patterns, concurrency limits, and cost-saving levers (batching, caching, quantization recommendations). Customer raised HIPAA/data residency concerns and wanted examples for SSO, audit logs, and private deployment tradeoffs.

title: Weekly POC sync — model compatibility & hot fallback planning
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-07-21
Time: 14:30 UTC
Duration: ~52 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Marco Silva (Redwood SE), Priya Nair (Cobalt - Head of ML), Jamal Ortega (Cobalt - Platform Eng), Helen Cho (Cobalt - Product)

Auto-summary (auto-gen, may be patchy):
- Quick review of last week's smoke tests: slight latency spike when using pinned quantized models under burst load.
- Agreement to run additional throughput bench and share traces.
- Discussed fallback strategy: soft fallback to smaller model family, plus region-based routing.
- Action items assigned (see bottom).

Topics:
- Model pinning vs soft routing
- Quantization profile compatibility across infra variants
- Rollback / escape hatch playbook for canary cutover
- Data residency and audit logging for dedicated pool

Transcript:
[00:00] Ava Chen: alright let's kick off, thanks everyone for making time, uh this is the weekly poc sync for cobalt—Cobalt systems, sorry, morning/afternoon depending on where you are.
[00:10] Priya Nair: Hey Ava, hi Marco. We had an incident the other day where a batch of inference requests started timing out in the EU region after the pin. Wanted to go over that and make sure the rollback plan is solid.
[00:22] Marco Silva: Got it. Quick context: we deployed a pinned model variant (v1.2-quant) on the dedicated pool for the pilot. Under steady traffic it's fine but under burst we saw queueing and token-generation slowdowns. We suspect batching interaction with the K V cache and the continuous batching pipeline.
[00:38] Jamal Ortega: Do you mean KV cache as in the key-value cache, right? Our ops team saw a lot of memory churn. It looked like K V cash growing — uh — apologies for the typo.
[00:48] Marco Silva: yeah KV cache. Also kernel selection differences between the GPU nodes in EU vs US — some nodes picked a fallback kernel that isn't as optimized for our int8 path.
[00:58] Helen Cho: can you remind us what the fallback is supposed to do — when it failsover does it go to a smaller model or to the hosted API?
[01:05] Ava Chen: good question. By default our soft fallback policy in the pilot was set to route to a smaller compatible model family in-region. We also configured a cross-region fallback to the hosted API as an extra escape hatch if regional capacity or perf is degraded.
[01:18] Priya Nair: we worried about data egress to hosted. We need audit logs and proof that payloads don't leak to a different tenant.
[01:26] Ava Chen: understood. For the private/dedicated route we already include KMS-encrypted transport and audit logs with request hashes. We'll send the security brief but short answer: hosted cross-region fallback can be disabled, or you can set it to anonymize/pseudonymize inputs first.
[01:42] Jamal Ortega: ok that helps. Back to the root cause — is it model quantization compatibility? Our infra uses a mix of A100s and L4s in staging and EU is L4-heavy.
[01:54] Marco Silva: yes L4s behave differently on certain int8 kernels — we saw mismatch in per-token compute time. The quantized model was compiled with a kernel tuned for A100, so when L4 picked the alternative kernel it increased latency.
[02:07] Priya Nair: hmm so how do we prevent that? We want pinned versions because they guarantee reproducibility, but we also need resilience to infra heterogeneity.
[02:15] Marco Silva: two options: one, pin to a model variant that we certify across the catalog for both A100 and L4 (we call that cross-kernel compatibility build), or two, use soft pin with an escape to the non-quantized or smaller model under degradation thresholds.
[02:29] Ava Chen: realistically many customers pick option two during canary — soft pin with transparent metrics and an automatic rollback if p90 latency crosses threshold for X minutes. That gives you safety without losing determinism entirely.
[02:42] Helen Cho: is that automatic rollback configurable per route? Like web search vs inbox summarization have different SLOs.
[02:49] Marco Silva: yes, per-route policy. You can set p50/p90 thresholds and the duration window, and it will either shift traffic to the fallback model or to a different capacity pool.
[02:59] Priya Nair: we want an escape hatch that doesn't change output quality too much — the smaller family sometimes hallucinate more. Any suggestion for quality-preserving fallbacks?
[03:08] Marco Silva: we can prioritize fallbacks to models that preserve the same tokenizer & architecture family — e.g., move from v1.2-quant to v1.1-float instead of moving to a cheap gpt-ish family. That reduces regression risk. Also we can enable shadowing: route a small sample to the fallback to compare outputs before switching.
[03:26] Jamal Ortega: shadowing sounds helpful. But will that double cost during the test window?
[03:30] Ava Chen: minimal sample sizes only, so cost impact should be small; Redwood Optimize can suggest the sampling rate and estimate cost delta. We'll attach the cost/latency tradeoffs doc.
[03:40] Helen Cho: earlier you mentioned kernel selection. Can we force kernel selection or bake compatibility at build time?
[03:45] Marco Silva: we don't force kernel selection at runtime, but we do a compilation/profile pass and create model artifacts per hardware profile. For pilot we can produce an L4-validated artifact and push that to the dedicated pool image so it picks optimized kernels.
[03:58] Priya Nair: that might be the simplest. But it means more artifacts to manage — pinning across artifacts then becomes trickier.
[04:05] Marco Silva: yep it's a management tradeoff. For production we recommend a small matrix of artifacts (A100-optimized, L4-optimized, and a safe float fallback). Redwood Console can keep track of these and label them as compatible variants in the model catalog.
[04:20] Ava Chen: quick check — are there any compliance blockers for generating multiple artifacts? e.g., do you need different approvals?
[04:26] Priya Nair: no approvals needed, but we have to record hashes and change events for audit.
[04:30] Ava Chen: perfect — that's standard.
[04:32] [05:00] Marco Silva: moving on — rollout plan for next week: run a 2x throughput bench with pinned v1.2-quant on a mirror of EU nodes, collect traces, and test soft fallback thresholds.
[04:45] Jamal Ortega: can you run it on the same mixed node profile? Our staging isn't identical to EU production.
[04:49] Marco Silva: we'll replicate the EU mix for the bench. Also we'll run a canary cutover in staging with a 5% traffic shift for 30 minutes to validate no regressions.
[05:02] Priya Nair: timeline? we need to present to leadership on Monday.
[05:07] Ava Chen: we'll aim to have the bench results + the latency heatmap by Thursday (2025-07-24). That matches your ask.
[05:13] Helen Cho: regarding the fallback to hosted API — Priya wanted to disable that for PII routes. What's the recommended config?
[05:21] Marco Silva: you can set a route-level policy: block cross-region hosting for PII, allow only in-region model family fallbacks, and enforce pseudonymization before any cross-region path. We'll send sample policy snippets.
[05:34] Priya Nair: good. Also one minor thing: sometimes the transcript we saw earlier said \"RWD-flex\" in logs — what's that?
[05:39] Ava Chen: that's an internal alias in the pilot for the flexible routing plugin; it's not a separate model. Bad transcription sometimes merges it.
[05:47] Jamal Ortega: competitor question: one of our infra vendors suggested using SageMaker multi-model endpoints instead of dedicated pools — any quick thoughts?
[05:55] Marco Silva: if you need tightly controlled SLAs and predictable per-token cost, dedicated pools are better. SageMaker can work but you'll need additional orchestration and you might lose some of the runtime optimizations (continuous batching, KV cache tuning) out-of-the-box.
[06:07] Priya Nair: final checklist for us: 1) get bench + heatmaps, 2) receive artifact options (A100/L4/float), 3) draft rollback playbook, 4) confirm policy for hosted fallback.
[06:18] Ava Chen: noted. I'll send the pricing + dedicated capacity options tomorrow. Marco will run the bench and share traces. Priya to share failing prompts. Helen to coordinate product acceptance criteria.
[06:27] Jamal Ortega: small nit: can you include the request IDs from the timeout incident? We captured them in logs.
[06:33] Marco Silva: yes include stack traces. Also we'll include a short snippet showing where the kernel switch occurred.
[06:39] Priya Nair: great. thanks everyone.

Recap / Agreed next steps:
- Redwood: run 2x throughput bench on EU-mixed nodes and produce latency heatmap + traces by 2025-07-24. (Marco)
- Redwood: produce A100-validated, L4-validated, and float-safe artifacts for model v1.2 and publish to model catalog; mark compatibility in Console (Marco/Ava) due 2025-07-25.
- Cobalt: share failing prompt examples and incident request IDs by 2025-07-23. (Priya/Jamal)
- Redwood: send POC pricing + dedicated capacity options and sample route policies (Ava) due 2025-07-22.

Action items (as recorded):
- \"Ava Chen | Send POC pricing & dedicated capacity options | 2025-07-22\"
- \"Marco Silva | Run 2x throughput benchmark with pinned v1.2 quantized model | 2025-07-25\"
- \"Priya Nair | Share failing prompt examples and recent evals | 2025-07-23\"

References mentioned / links: 
- Latency heatmap template (link: internal)
- Sample route policy snippets (link: console.redwood/docs/policies)
- Cost/latency tradeoff doc (link: redwood/optimize/cost-latency.pdf)

Notes about transcript quality: auto-generated captions had a few mis-attributions and kernel names (e.g., \"K V cash\" instead of KV cache, \"RWD-flex\" alias).

[06:18] Ava Chen: noted. I'll send the pricing + dedicated capacity options tomorrow. Marco will run the bench and share traces. Priya to share failing prompts. Helen to coordinate product acceptance criteria.
