title: Hydra Sealed Vault - offline artifact delivery, driver activation & kv warmup sync
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-06-25
Start time (UTC): 15:00
Duration: 72 minutes (approx)
Location: Zoom
Recording id: fireflies-20250625-8f3b
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Diego Marquez (Redwood SE), Priya Singh (Redwood SE), Marta Ruiz (Atlas - ML Infra), Evan Cho (Atlas - Platform), Rakesh Gupta (Atlas - Security), Sofia Li (Atlas - SRE)
__Auto-generated summary (may be fuzzy):__
Discussed on-prem sealed artifact delivery for air-gapped Hydra cluster, offline license activation workflow, GPU driver packaging/compatibility, KV warmup / prefix caching strategy to meet latency SLOs, batching and quantization tradeoffs for throughput vs memory. Agreed action items and follow-up install session.
---
Topics: offline packaging, transfer media, driver signing, license activation, KV cache pre-warm, batching window config, quantization profiles, V P C and firewall implications.
---
Transcript:
[00:00] Ava Chen: ok hi everyone thanks for joining, i'm Ava from Redwood, i'll kick us off — Diego and Priya from engineering are here too. Marta, do you want to quick intro?
[00:12] Marta Ruiz: yeah sure, hi all, i'm marta ruiz i run the ml infra team at atlas, we are trying to run redwood on a sealed hydra cluster behind our airgapped network. we have some compliance windows and need offline license activation.
[00:25] Ava Chen: perfect, yeah that's what we want to cover. quick agenda: sealed artifact transport, driver activation & signing, and then a deep dive on kv warmup and batching for latency. any thing to add?
[00:36] Evan Cho: maybe also network firewall ports and what goes outbound for the activations, that was one of our legal asks.
[00:42] Ava Chen: good call, we'll cover that.
[00:45] Diego Marquez: and i'll show some numbers from our internal kv-warmup script and how much p95 it helps for cold-starts.
[00:51] Sofia Li: just flag — our transfer window only allows offline media A through vendor X, so we need the artifact in that format.
[00:57] Ava Chen: noted.
[01:05] Diego Marquez: ok jumping in — artifact packaging: we provide a sealed tarball signed with our release key, plus a detached signature and a verification tool. for airgap we also produce a channel bundle — it's basically the model artifact, model codecs (if we ship quantized binaries), runtime container images as OCI tarballs, and the driver bundle.
[01:30] Marta Ruiz: when you say driver bundle is that the nv driver or the runtime shim drivers? we have a vendor that supplies their own kernel module and the nvidia driver for their DGX clusters.
[01:40] Diego Marquez: good q. we ship both the runtime drivers (cuda/cuDNN-compatible shims) and a recommended tested nvidia driver version, but we do not ship kernel modules for vendor-specific custom kernels. you'll have to use vendor kernel modules; we validate against a matrix. we'll provide a compatibility matrix and a signed driver manifest.
[02:02] Marta Ruiz: okay okay, that helps. can you share the matrix today?
[02:05] Diego Marquez: i'll include it in the follow-up, yes.
[02:08] (overlap) Priya Singh: and we also include a small helper that does the driver sanity checks and suggests sysctl knobs — it's in the tools tarball.
[02:19] Rakesh Gupta: on the licensing, our security team needs offline activation that doesn't require outbound connections. how does your offline license flow look?
[02:26] Ava Chen: brief high-level: we provide a one-time activation token that you can import via our offline activation utility; the utility generates a signed activation request file that you transfer to a connected machine controlled by your security team, we then create a license artifact signed by our licensing authority and it gets pulled back in via transfer media. no outbound from the sealed cluster required.
[02:45] Rakesh Gupta: so there's a mediate step that touches an internet-connected machine? do we have to expose any endpoints?
[02:51] Ava Chen: yes the mediate machine must have outbound to our license service or we can do a manual signed license file exchange. for high security customers we perform a manual signing step and provide the license file over the sealed transfer channel — no node in your V P C needs outbound.
[03:06] Rakesh Gupta: ok good. what about KMS keys — can the license reference our HSM?
[03:10] Priya Singh: you can either inject a public key and we'll encrypt the license blob to your key, or we can ship a license that uses an activation token bound to machine-id. for H S M we prefer you provide a wrapping key; we'll document the exact steps.
[03:22] Marta Ruiz: is there any behavioral telemetry that leaves the cluster during normal operation? we need to make sure telemetry is off by default.
[03:29] Diego Marquez: telemetry is off by default in the airgap builds. only the offline-activation helper may contact our license server unless you do the manual signed license exchange.
[03:38] Marta Ruiz: perfect.
[03:40] Diego Marquez: switching to kv warmup — the problem we see is cold KV cache for large context models causes the first few requests to spike p95; our recommendation is a two-stage warmup: 1) prefix-level KV prefetch for common prompt prefixes, and 2) on-disk kv checkpoints that load hot key ranges on boot.
[04:02] Evan Cho: prefix-level — can you explain how that interacts with quantized weights and memory pressure? we have 512GB GPU but host memory is limited.
[04:10] Diego Marquez: yeah so prefix prefetching only loads the KV entries for the recent tokens sequence patterns, it's much smaller than full kv. quantized weights affect GPU memory but KV cache lives in GPU memory too if you pin it. We offer two modes: gpu-resident kv (best latency) or host-resident kv with async fetch (lower memory). you can set thresholds in the runtime conf.
[04:33] Sofia Li: is gpu-resident kv supported on your quantized kernels? we've seen some kernels not play nicely with int8 memory layouts.
[04:39] Diego Marquez: we've optimized the kernel to keep kv owners in a quantization-friendly layout, but there are edge cases. if you're on a very aggressive 4-bit quantization profile you may need host-resident kv. i'll note that in the compatibility matrix.
[04:53] Marta Ruiz: can we pre-warm KV on a staging cluster and then snapshot to the sealed cluster?
[04:58] Diego Marquez: yes, we support kv checkpoint exports — you can warmup using our benchmark harness, export a serialized kv file and include it in the artifact. during init the runtime will validate the checkpoint signature and load the hot ranges.
[05:12] Evan Cho: what's the format size for a common warmup set — like for a 7B open model?
[05:17] Diego Marquez: depends on prompt length distribution. for a 7B with typical 128 token prompts, a warmup of 10k prefixes is roughly 4-6GB of serialized kv. for long context workloads it's larger.
[05:28] Marta Ruiz: for our workload we expect a lot of short queries, so that seems doable.
[05:33] Ava Chen: quick note about batching: we recommend dynamic batching with a 5-15ms accumulation window to reduce tokens cost without adding perceptible latency. you can specify a max batch size per GPU.
[05:44] Evan Cho: 5-15ms — that's tight, our infra jitter might add 3-4ms. is there an adaptive mode?
[05:49] Priya Singh: yes there's an adaptive batching policy that monitors inter-arrival times and adjusts the window. it tries to keep p99 below your configured SLO.
[05:56] Rakesh Gupta: what telemetry does that policy need? will it send anything out?
[06:00] Priya Singh: no, it runs locally; we do not require outbound for the policy. metrics can be ingested to your internal prometheus if you want.
[06:08] (short pause)
[06:15] Marta Ruiz: driver signing — you mentioned signed driver manifests, do you also provide a way to verify driver binaries in the kernel? our security needs an attestation step.
[06:23] Diego Marquez: we provide an shasum manifest and a detached signature; additionally we can produce an optional binary attestation using a signing H S M we control. for deep attestation we can coordinate with your vendor to embedded signed certs into the kernel module load flow. this is heavier lifting but we've done it with a couple of large customers.
[06:43] Sofia Li: are there any kernel versions not supported? we run RHEL 8 with a custom vendor kernel.
[06:50] Diego Marquez: we validate against the common enterprise kernels; the vendor kernel may introduce ABI differences. we'll run our driver sanity helper on your kernel during the install window. if we find incompatibilities we'll provide patch recommendations.
[07:03] Marta Ruiz: ok. next — runtime tuning for latency: you mentioned early layer pruning and kv sharding?
[07:10] Diego Marquez: yes, early layer activation (not pruning in the model sense) — we can do a lightweight attention cache that avoids recomputing certain layers for prefix-heavy workloads. kv sharding allows us to spread KV across multiple GPUs so bootload is parallel. both help with tail latency.
[07:27] Evan Cho: any impact on throughput?
[07:29] Diego Marquez: slight, usually <5% throughput loss for majority of workloads; p95 improves more significantly. it's a tradeoff you can toggle per route.
[07:36] Ava Chen: sounds like we should map which routes need p95 vs token cost saving.
[07:40] Marta Ruiz: yes and we need per-route RBAC as well — only certain services can use dedicated high-cost paths.
[07:47] Ava Chen: Redwood Console supports per-route config, and for private it's all config-as-code in the control plane. offline builds ship a route manifest you can apply.
[07:56] Rakesh Gupta: we need audit logs for who applied route changes, can that be integrated with our S S O and audit pipeline?
[08:03] Priya Singh: yes we integrate with SAML/SSO and push audit logs to your syslog or SIEM. in airgap setups we also include a signed audit bundle that you can ingest.
[08:15] Marta Ruiz: good.
[08:18] Diego Marquez: small demo note — i'll run through a lightweight kv warmup script now, it's on my laptop. (screen share)
[08:25] Diego Marquez: so we're running the warmup with 1k prefixes, you can see time to load, then measure p50/p95. the script will produce a checkpoint.
[08:38] (typing sounds)
[08:45] Diego Marquez: hmm looks like my live environment hit an env var i didn't set — apologies, this is a quick local. but typical run shows warmup reduced first request p95 from ~1.1s to ~220ms on this 8xA100 equivalent sim.
[09:02] Marta Ruiz: those are promising numbers, our SLA target is 300ms p95.
[09:06] Diego Marquez: so you're within target with warmup and gpu-kv resident.
[09:10] Evan Cho: can you provide the measurement harness as a standalone binary for offline execution?
[09:15] Diego Marquez: yes, it will be in the tools tarball.
[09:18] Ava Chen: quick security follow-up — Marta, Rakesh — any additional blockers from a compliance standpoint?
[09:24] Rakesh Gupta: we need attestation for drivers, audit trails for license activations, and no outbound by default. also need to understand how updates are applied in sealed setups.
[09:33] Priya Singh: updates in sealed setups: you prepare a new sealed artifact, we provide differential artifact deltas to reduce transfer size, you validate on staging, then apply. we also support signed rollback artifacts.
[09:46] Marta Ruiz: delta artifacts sound important given our slow transfer window. can you quantify typical delta sizes?
[09:52] Diego Marquez: for smaller model weight changes it's often a few hundred meg; for full model replacements it's the full artifact. deltas work better for runtime patches and driver updates.
[10:02] Evan Cho: what about quantization—if we want int4 for cost, does that change the artifact format?
[10:08] Diego Marquez: quantized artifacts include a separate weight pack and small runtime shim. we version the quantization profile and embed it in the manifest. you'll need to ensure the runtime supports that profile — again in the compatibility matrix.
[10:22] Marta Ruiz: can we do an initial pilot with a 7B quantized and then evaluate 13B later?
[10:28] Ava Chen: absolutely, that's a common path — start with 7B int8 or int4 depending on latency and cost, then evaluate bigger models once driver and kernel validation is complete.
[10:36] (brief side conversation)
[10:46] Sofia Li: transfer media question: vendor X only accepts signed checksums in a particular format — can you include both sha256 and sha512?
[10:53] Diego Marquez: yes we produce both and can package md5 if you need legacy.
[10:58] Marta Ruiz: ok thanks.
[11:02] Ava Chen: i've been taking notes — i'll recite the agreed next steps so we have alignment.
[11:08] Ava Chen: 1) Diego will send driver compatibility matrix and the signed driver manifest. 2) Priya will upload sealed artifact packaging checklist and the offline license activation utility. 3) Diego will include the kv-warmup harness and instructions to produce a checkpoint for import. 4) Atlas (Marta) will provide transfer constraints and target transfer media. 5) Rakesh to confirm H S M/KMS boundary and whether we should encrypt license blobs to your key. 6) schedule a hands-on install week of 2025-07-07.
[11:36] Marta Ruiz: yes that matches. can we get a sample activation token for us to test in staging?
[11:41] Ava Chen: we'll provide a one-time sample token for staging, yes.
[11:45] Rakesh Gupta: on the legal side, can we get the offline activation workflow and SLA in writing?
[11:50] Ava Chen: sure i'll include that in the follow-up email along with the runbook.
[11:54] Evan Cho: last question — do you have a recommended sysctl list for kernel tuning?
[11:59] Priya Singh: yes it's in the helper tool and the checklist; it includes network nic settings, hugepages, file descriptor limits.
[12:07] Marta Ruiz: great. thanks everyone.
[12:10] Ava Chen: thanks all. we'll follow up with artifacts and timeline.
---

Notes & links referenced:

title: Cartograph POC - economics & sizing discussion
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-03-12
Start time: 16:00 PDT
Duration: 58 minutes
Recording ID: ff-20250312-8347
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Diego Ramos (Redwood SE), Priya Patel (Solutions), Nina Ortiz (CSM), Mark Levin (CTO - Cartograph), Sana Iqbal (Lead ML Eng), Ravi Menon (Infra), Elena Cho (Product), Jordan (Finance)

Auto Summary (auto-generated - may be incomplete):
- Goal: scope POC to validate per-token unit economics for Cartograph's conversational analytics workload and determine Dedicated vs Hosted recommendation.
- Key success criteria discussed: p95 latency < 250ms for short queries, sustained throughput of 2000 tokens/sec, inference cost target ~ $0.00010 - $0.00015 per token, data residency and SOC2 requirements.
- Next steps: share cost model, run 48h benchmark on dedicated capacity, customer to provide traces.

Topics covered:
- Success criteria and timelines
- Workload sizing: tokens per request, concurrency, burst patterns
- Cost modeling: per-token estimates, batching and caching knobs
- Benchmarks and pass/fail thresholds
- Security and deployment constraints (VPC, KMS)

Transcript:
[00:00] Ava Chen: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. quick intro round, I'll kick off—i'm Ava, i'm the AE on this, with Diego our SE.
[00:12] Mark Levin: Hi all, Mark here, CTO at Cartograph. We mostly want to confirm economics before we commit to pilot.
[00:20] Sana Iqbal: Sana from ML. we'll share traces but high-level: mix of short queries (10-50 tokens) and long context summarizations (~400-800 tokens).
[00:33] Diego Ramos: Great. quick clarification — are the long requests mostly offline or part of the interactive path?
[00:38] Sana Iqbal: Interactive, sometimes users paste long transcripts and expect near realtime summarization. so latency matters.
[00:45] Ava Chen: On the economics front, we usually model per-token cost and then add infra overhead for dedicated. we'll run through a sample model.
[00:52] [00:52] Speaker 6: (mumbled) // transcription glitch
[01:05] Mark Levin: Our finance target is roughly $12k/month for inference initially—scoped to a pilot. we need to know if that's feasible.
[01:15] Diego Ramos: okay to ground that—could you share expected monthly active users and average tokens per session? even ballparks help.
[01:23] Elena Cho: Ballpark: 10k MAU for pilot, with peak concurrency of around 120 simultaneous sessions in a US region.
[01:33] Jordan - Finance: And seasonality we might see spikes to 300 concurrency during campaigns.
[01:38] Diego Ramos: Noted. for cost modeling we need avg tokens per request and requests per user — even distributions help.
[01:46] Sana Iqbal: Avg tokens per interactive request: 180 tokens. For the long summarizations, 600 tokens on average, but those are 20% of calls. Rest are 50-200 tokens.
[01:58] Ava Chen: Okay, so rough weighted avg per call: (0.2 * 600) + (0.8 * 180) = ~252 tokens per call.
[02:08] Mark Levin: That math looks right. our expected requests per user per month: 4 actions. so 40k requests/month roughly.
[02:16] Diego Ramos: That comes to ~10.1M tokens/month (40k * 252). Is that consistent with your telemetry?
[02:24] Sana Iqbal: yes, close. earlier we underestimated because we didn't include system prompts and metadata tokens.
[02:31] Ava Chen: Important to include system tokens — we usually add ~30-60 tokens of prompt overhead per call for safety. let's add 50 tokens -> new avg ~302 tokens -> ~12.1M tokens/month.
[02:46] Mark Levin: Right, so our finance target of $12k/month implies an allowable per-token cost of about $0.00099? wait math — hold on.
[02:55] [pause, typing]
[03:05] Ava Chen: Quick calc: $12,000 / 12.1M tokens = ~$0.00099 per token — that seems high compared to typical hosted per-token rates. Did you mean $1.2k?
[03:17] Jordan - Finance: Sorry typo—target is $12k total infra incl infra and engineering support. open to reallocations—main ask is per-token unit economics not exceed $0.00015 ideally.
[03:29] Diego Ramos: Good to know. So target per-token ~ $0.00012 - $0.00015. We'll show two scenarios: hosted (pay-as-you-go) and Redwood Dedicated (reserved capacity with lower per-token compute).
[03:40] Sana Iqbal: Important: our compliance team requires SOC2 and VPC option. That pushes us toward Dedicated or Private.
[03:47] Ava Chen: Understood. Dedicated can be deployed in customer VPC or a private plane—both options available. We'll outline the delta in monthly reservation costs vs per-token compute.
[03:55] Diego Ramos: On the technical knobs: batching, prefix/KV cache and quantization can move unit costs a lot. For example, auto-batching across concurrent requests reduces per-token compute by up to 30-60% depending on seq length distribution.
[04:08] Sana Iqbal: Do you have example numbers for batching impact on short vs long requests?
[04:12] Diego Ramos: Yes — short requests (<=50 tokens) benefit less from batching because overhead dominates, but concurrency helps. long requests see better amortization of kernel overhead. We'll put concrete throughput numbers in the model.
[04:24] [04:24] Speaker 6: crosstalk (someone off)
[04:35] Ava Chen: For planning, we recommended a 2-week smoke test, then a 48-hour benchmark under production-like load on Redwood Dedicated to measure p95 and cost. That will give you real per-token billing numbers with your actual traces.
[04:47] Mark Levin: Sounds good. timeline-wise we want benchmarks before end of month — is that realistic?
[04:51] Diego Ramos: Yes if you share traces by Friday (3/14) we can spin up and run benchmarks the following week.
[04:58] Sana Iqbal: We'll aim to share traces by 3/14.
[05:03] Ava Chen: Great. let's walk through a sample cost model quickly—this is high level.
[05:09] Ava Chen: Scenario A: Hosted model (pay-as-you-go): assume per-token price $0.0002 (gen+context combined). For 12.1M tokens that's $2,420/month purely compute. Add network and service margin ~20% -> $2.9k.
[05:23] Ava Chen: Scenario B: Dedicated reservation: reservation = $7k/month for 4 reserved GPUs (example), compute effective per-token could be $0.00008 after utilization of 65%. But you'll have minimum commit and some ops fees.
[05:36] Mark Levin: So dedicated looks cheaper per token but requires higher up-front commitment. We need to figure utilization assumptions carefully.
[05:44] Diego Ramos: Exactly. fill rate is critical. If you hit 65% steady-state utilization you'll get the $0.00008 effective; if you only use 20% you'd be worse off. We'll model break-even utilization.
[05:56] Sana Iqbal: How do caching and KV store change this? For example, session continuation where a user calls model again with same prefix—can Redwood cache the prefix KV to reduce compute?
[06:06] Diego Ramos: Yes — prefix/KV caching reduces recomputed tokens. If you have repeated prompts or multi-turn sessions, we can preserve KV cache and serve subsequent calls faster and cheaper. Caching generally reduces total billed token compute if reuse is high; we'll add a caching reuse rate in the model (e.g., 20-40%).
[06:20] Elena Cho: Do you support hybrid strategies like some routes going to hosted and others to dedicated to handle spikes?
[06:26] Ava Chen: Yes — Smart routing can send latency-critical routes to dedicated and fall back to hosted for overflow. That helps keep commit small but maintain SLA.
[06:34] Mark Levin: Can you show sample per-token math with our numbers? maybe a 3-line calc.
[06:39] Diego Ramos: Sure. Example — Dedicated reserved cost $7,000/mo for capacity X (we'll supply X in GPU-hours). If we divide by 12.1M tokens that's $0.00058/token if utilization is 20%. If utilization is 65% the same reservation costs drop to $0.00018/token. With kernel/quantization améliorations might reach $0.00008. Realistic hosted price used for comparison $0.0002.
[06:58] Jordan - Finance: So our target $0.00015 looks achievable only with decent utilization and caching.
[07:04] Ava Chen: Correct. We'll model optimistic/median/conservative scenarios.
[07:08] Sana Iqbal: For benchmarks, we need p95 under 250ms for short queries and under 800ms for long summarizations. Is that doable on Dedicated?
[07:17] Diego Ramos: Yes — Dedicated with auto-scaling + batching tuned for low-latency can hit p95 ~120-200ms for short queries depending on model size and seq length. Long summarizations are more compute-bound — p95 depends on model selection and quantization. We'll propose a 2-model strategy: smaller quantized model for chat, larger model for long summaries.
[07:33] Mark Levin: What's the work to test quantized variants — will that be part of the POC?
[07:37] Diego Ramos: We can include quantized variants in the benchmark — they just require model packaging. We'll test fp16, int8 variants and measure quality delta — Redwood Optimize can recommend a sweet spot.
[07:48] Ava Chen: Also worth calling out tokenization differences between model variants; sometimes token counts change up to 5-7% and we should normalize on that.
[07:56] Sana Iqbal: Good point, we'll provide sample texts so you can token-count consistently.
[08:01] [08:01] Speaker 6: (laughs)
[08:10] Ava Chen: On the POC success criteria, can we align now? I'm proposing:
- Pass criteria: p95 latency < 250ms for short queries and < 800ms for long summaries under pilot load; cost per useful token <= $0.00015 in median scenario; SOC2 and VPC connectivity validated; and quality delta within X (we'll define eval metrics).
[08:30] Mark Levin: Agreed but want cost per active user number too — finance wants a monthly per-active-user cost.
[08:36] Ava Chen: We'll add that metric.
[08:40] Sana Iqbal: Also need a failure mode: in case latency or cost misses, define rollback — for example, continue hosted or scale down model size.
[08:49] Diego Ramos: We'll include fallback policies in the runbook.
[08:52] Ava Chen: For the benchmark itself, we'll ask for a representative traffic generator, ideally replaying your traces with preserved timing to reproduce bursts.
[08:59] Ravi Menon: For network, we might need VPC peering; we'll start internal approvals. key management: we use AWS KMS, will that work?
[09:08] Diego Ramos: Yes, we support customer KMS integration for Dedicated and Private. We'll include the KMS doc in the security packet.
[09:15] Ava Chen: I'll drop the SOC2 and KMS docs in the follow-up email.
[09:19] [09:19] Speaker 6: background noise, faint
[09:28] Mark Levin: For the 48-hour benchmark, what's the expected Redwood involvement? Full provisioning and monitoring?
[09:34] Nina Ortiz: From CSM side, we typically provision, monitor, and run the runbook with you. Diego will configure the workload, and we'll review logs and cost dashboards together.
[09:44] Ava Chen: We'll also provide a shared dashboard—token breakdown, per-route latency, batching stats, cache hit rate—so you can see unit economics in near real time.
[09:55] Elena Cho: That dashboard is key for product and finance.
[09:58] Ava Chen: We'll flag cost drivers: warm vs cold cache, average batch size, cache reuse rate. Those will be columns in the spreadsheet.
[10:07] Sana Iqbal: Quick question — how do you count tokens for multi-turn contexts with trimmed history?
[10:12] Diego Ramos: We count all tokens the model executes. If you trim history before sending, that's your optimizer. We can simulate different retention policies in the benchmark so you can see tradeoffs.
[10:22] Mark Levin: Any concerns about data residency? all pilot traffic is US-only.
[10:26] Ava Chen: We can pin region to a US region and provide audit logs. Dedicated deployments can be placed in your VPC to meet policy.
[10:34] Jordan - Finance: Regarding pricing, do you have a short note on how overages or burst capacity are billed with Dedicated?
[10:40] Diego Ramos: Typically reserved capacity has base commit and a burst pool at higher rates. We'll capture a sample pricing breakdown in the spreadsheet and show per-token cost at 50/65/80% utilization.
[10:51] Ava Chen: We'll also show a hybrid route example to keep commit low and cover spikes with hosted fallback.
[10:55] [10:55] Speaker 6: (unclear)
[11:05] Sana Iqbal: For quality evaluation, we want an automatic metric plus a small human eval set. can Redwood help with that?
[11:12] Diego Ramos: Yes—we can run evals on the output of each model variant and quantify drop in rouge/semantic-bert scores; human eval recommended for subjective quality. Redwood Optimize has built-in tooling to manage regression alerts.
[11:24] Mark Levin: How long does it take to ship a benchmark environment?
[11:27] Diego Ramos: If traces arrive by Friday, we can provision and run smoke tests within 48 hours and schedule the 48-hour benchmark early next week. End-to-end ~7 business days to results depending on scheduling.
[11:39] Ava Chen: Let's talk about risk: biggest risk is underutilization of Dedicated commit — we'll show a break-even chart. second risk is quality regression from quantization — we'll measure that.
[11:50] Ravi Menon: We need to know typical warm-up times — how long before cache warms sufficiently to see amortized benefits?
[11:57] Diego Ramos: Warm-up depends on reuse patterns; for session-based reuse you often see benefits within minutes for hot content; for broader prefix caches maybe hours. The 48-hour benchmark should capture steady-state behavior.
[12:10] Mark Levin: Can you include network egress estimates? cloud egress is a surprise cost for us.
[12:16] Ava Chen: We'll include egress based on token sizes and average response size. we'll need a sample payload size to be accurate.
[12:24] Elena Cho: Samples will be included in the traces.
[12:27] [12:27] Speaker 6: (phone buzzing)
[12:36] Ava Chen: On the contract side, we'll prepare options for a 3-month pilot with an early-termination clause if metrics don't meet pass criteria.
[12:45] Jordan - Finance: good idea. finance wants clear exit criteria.
[12:50] Ava Chen: We'll put that in the proposal.
[12:53] Sana Iqbal: For the step to share traces—any PII concerns? we'll anonymize tokens and replace names with placeholders.
[12:59] Diego Ramos: That's fine; as long as token distribution and timing are preserved. We can accept anonymized traces.
[13:06] Mark Levin: Any required network changes on our side for Dedicated?
[13:10] Ravi Menon: We'll probably need VPC peering and ip allowlist for the cluster. I'll confirm with infra.
[13:16] Ava Chen: Great. We'll include a network checklist in the security packet.
[13:22] [13:22] Speaker 6: (paper rustle)
[13:30] Elena Cho: For the per-user metric, can you show monthly cost per MAU as a function of utilization?
[13:36] Diego Ramos: Yes, the model will output per-MAU cost under different utilization and caching assumptions.
[13:42] Ava Chen: Let's commit to dates: Sana to send traces by 3/14; Ava to send cost-model spreadsheet and security docs by 3/14 as well; Diego to schedule benchmark for week of 3/17. Does that work?
[13:55] Sana Iqbal: Yes.
[13:57] Jordan - Finance: Works for finance.
[14:00] Mark Levin: OK. A few technical clarifications — are you able to instrument per-route cost in the dashboard?
[14:07] Diego Ramos: Yes. We surface token counts, compute time, batch size, cache-hit rate per route. That will help attribute costs to product features.
[14:18] Ava Chen: We'll also include a sample runbook for rollback and alert thresholds.
[14:23] Ravi Menon: For the benchmark, we need to test cold start behavior too — some of our use cases have infrequent users.
[14:29] Diego Ramos: We'll include cold-start patterns in the workload generator. Cold start penalizes latency and might increase per-token cost slightly; we'll show that impact.
[14:39] [14:39] Speaker 6: (keyboard clicking)
[14:46] Mark Levin: Any final pricing notes? for Dedicated, is there a minimum term?
[14:50] Ava Chen: For Dedicated we usually discuss monthly vs annual commitments — pilot can be monthly reservation. We'll propose a trial-friendly pricing for pilot.
[14:59] Jordan - Finance: OK, that helps.
[15:02] Ava Chen: Alright, we'll recap next steps: I'll email the spreadsheet + security pack by Friday. Diego will provision benchmark infra once traces arrive. We'll reconvene with benchmark results and pricing in ~2 weeks.
[15:16] Mark Levin: Thanks, appreciate it.
[15:19] Sana Iqbal: Thanks all.
[15:22] Ava Chen: Thanks everyone — we'll follow up.
[15:25] [END]

Post-call notes (auto):
- Key assumption to validate: avg tokens/call including system overhead = ~302; monthly tokens ~12.1M.
- Recommended immediate steps: send traces, run 48h benchmark on Redwood Dedicated with quantized variants, measure caching impact.
- Risk items to track: utilization, cache-hit rate, quantization quality delta, network egress.

Referenced artifacts/links: 
- Cost-model template (to be attached)
- SOC2 + KMS integration doc (to be attached)
- Benchmark runbook (link will be provided)

[06:34] Mark Levin: Can you show sample per-token math with our numbers? maybe a 3-line calc.
[06:39] Diego Ramos: Sure. Example — Dedicated reserved cost $7,000/mo for capacity X (we'll supply X in GPU-hours). If we divide by 12.1M tokens that's $0.00058/token if utilization is 20%. If utilization is 65% the same reservation costs drop to $0.00018/token. With kernel/quantization améliorations might reach $0.00008. Realistic hosted price used for comparison $0.0002.
[06:58] Jordan - Finance: So our target $0.00015 looks achievable only with decent utilization and caching.
[07:04] Ava Chen: Correct. We'll model optimistic/median/conservative scenarios.
[07:08] Sana Iqbal: For benchmarks, we need p95 under 250ms for short queries and under 800ms for long summarizations. Is that doable on Dedicated?
[07:17] Diego Ramos: Yes — Dedicated with auto-scaling + batching tuned for low-latency can hit p95 ~120-200ms for short queries depending on model size and seq length. Long summarizations are more compute-bound — p95 depends on model selection and quantization. We'll propose a 2-model strategy: smaller quantized model for chat, larger model for long summaries.
[07:33] Mark Levin: What's the work to test quantized variants — will that be part of the POC?
[07:37] Diego Ramos: We can include quantized variants in the benchmark — they just require model packaging. We'll test fp16, int8 variants and measure quality delta — Redwood Optimize can recommend a sweet spot.
[07:48] Ava Chen: Also worth calling out tokenization differences between model variants; sometimes token counts change up to 5-7% and we should normalize on that.
[07:56] Sana Iqbal: Good point, we'll provide sample texts so you can token-count consistently.
[08:01] [08:01] Speaker 6: (laughs)
[08:10] Ava Chen: On the POC success criteria, can we align now? I'm proposing:
- Pass criteria: p95 latency < 250ms for short queries and < 800ms for long summaries under pilot load; cost per useful token <= $0.00015 in median scenario; SOC2 and VPC connectivity validated; and quality delta within X (we'll define eval metrics).
[08:30] Mark Levin: Agreed but want cost per active user number too — finance wants a monthly per-active-user cost.
[08:36] Ava Chen: We'll add that metric.
[08:40] Sana Iqbal: Also need a failure mode: in case latency or cost misses, define rollback — for example, continue hosted or scale down model size.
[08:49] Diego Ramos: We'll include fallback policies in the runbook.
[08:52] Ava Chen: For the benchmark itself, we'll ask for a representative traffic generator, ideally replaying your traces with preserved timing to reproduce bursts.
[08:59] Ravi Menon: For network, we might need VPC peering; we'll start internal approvals. key management: we use AWS KMS, will that work?
[09:08] Diego Ramos: Yes, we support customer KMS integration for Dedicated and Private. We'll include the KMS doc in the security packet.
[09:15] Ava Chen: I'll drop the SOC2 and KMS docs in the follow-up email.
[09:19] [09:19] Speaker 6: background noise, faint
[09:28] Mark Levin: For the 48-hour benchmark, what's the expected Redwood involvement? Full provisioning and monitoring?
[09:34] Nina Ortiz: From CSM side, we typically provision, monitor, and run the runbook with you. Diego will configure the workload, and we'll review logs and cost dashboards together.
[09:44] Ava Chen: We'll also provide a shared dashboard—token breakdown, per-route latency, batching stats, cache hit rate—so you can see unit economics in near real time.
[09:55] Elena Cho: That dashboard is key for product and finance.
[09:58] Ava Chen: We'll flag cost drivers: warm vs cold cache, average batch size, cache reuse rate. Those will be columns in the spreadsheet.
[10:07] Sana Iqbal: Quick question — how do you count tokens for multi-turn contexts with trimmed history?
[10:12] Diego Ramos: We count all tokens the model executes. If you trim history before sending, that's your optimizer. We can simulate different retention policies in the benchmark so you can see tradeoffs.
[10:22] Mark Levin: Any concerns about data residency? all pilot traffic is US-only.
[10:26] Ava Chen: We can pin region to a US region and provide audit logs. Dedicated deployments can be placed in your VPC to meet policy.
[10:34] Jordan - Finance: Regarding pricing, do you have a short note on how overages or burst capacity are billed with Dedicated?
[10:40] Diego Ramos: Typically reserved capacity has base commit and a burst pool at higher rates. We'll capture a sample pricing breakdown in the spreadsheet and show per-token cost at 50/65/80% utilization.
[10:51] Ava Chen: We'll also show a hybrid route example to keep commit low and cover spikes with hosted fallback.
[10:55] [10:55] Speaker 6: (unclear)
[11:05] Sana Iqbal: For quality evaluation, we want an automatic metric plus a small human eval set. can Redwood help with that?
[11:12] Diego Ramos: Yes—we can run evals on the output of each model variant and quantify drop in rouge/semantic-bert scores; human eval recommended for subjective quality. Redwood Optimize has built-in tooling to manage regression alerts.
[11:24] Mark Levin: How long does it take to ship a benchmark environment?
[11:27] Diego Ramos: If traces arrive by Friday, we can provision and run smoke tests within 48 hours and schedule the 48-hour benchmark early next week. End-to-end ~7 business days to results depending on scheduling.
[11:39] Ava Chen: Let's talk about risk: biggest risk is underutilization of Dedicated commit — we'll show a break-even chart. second risk is quality regression from quantization — we'll measure that.
[11:50] Ravi Menon: We need to know typical warm-up times — how long before cache warms sufficiently to see amortized benefits?
[11:57] Diego Ramos: Warm-up depends on reuse patterns; for session-based reuse you often see benefits within minutes for hot content; for broader prefix caches maybe hours. The 48-hour benchmark should capture steady-state behavior.
[12:10] Mark Levin: Can you include network egress estimates? cloud egress is a surprise cost for us.
[12:16] Ava Chen: We'll include egress based on token sizes and average response size. we'll need a sample payload size to be accurate.
[12:24] Elena Cho: Samples will be included in the traces.
[12:27] [12:27] Speaker 6: (phone buzzing)
[12:36] Ava Chen: On the contract side, we'll prepare options for a 3-month pilot with an early-termination clause if metrics don't meet pass criteria.
[12:45] Jordan - Finance: good idea. finance wants clear exit criteria.
[12:50] Ava Chen: We'll put that in the proposal.
[12:53] Sana Iqbal: For the step to share traces—any PII concerns? we'll anonymize tokens and replace names with placeholders.
[12:59] Diego Ramos: That's fine; as long as token distribution and timing are preserved. We can accept anonymized traces.
[13:06] Mark Levin: Any required network changes on our side for Dedicated?
[13:10] Ravi Menon: We'll probably need VPC peering and ip allowlist for the cluster. I'll confirm with infra.
[13:16] Ava Chen: Great. We'll include a network checklist in the security packet.
[13:22] [13:22] Speaker 6: (paper rustle)
[13:30] Elena Cho: For the per-user metric, can you show monthly cost per MAU as a function of utilization?
[13:36] Diego Ramos: Yes, the model will output per-MAU cost under different utilization and caching assumptions.
[13:42] Ava Chen: Let's commit to dates: Sana to send traces by 3/14; Ava to send cost-model spreadsheet and security docs by 3/14 as well; Diego to schedule benchmark for week of 3/17. Does that work?
[13:55] Sana Iqbal: Yes.
[13:57] Jordan - Finance: Works for finance.
[14:00] Mark Levin: OK. A few technical clarifications — are you able to instrument per-route cost in the dashboard?
[14:07] Diego Ramos: Yes. We surface token counts, compute time, batch size, cache-hit rate per route. That will help attribute costs to product features.
[14:18] Ava Chen: We'll also include a sample runbook for rollback and alert thresholds.
[14:23] Ravi Menon: For the benchmark, we need to test cold start behavior too — some of our use cases have infrequent users.
[14:29] Diego Ramos: We'll include cold-start patterns in the workload generator. Cold start penalizes latency and might increase per-token cost slightly; we'll show that impact.
[14:39] [14:39] Speaker 6: (keyboard clicking)
[14:46] Mark Levin: Any final pricing notes? for Dedicated, is there a minimum term?
[14:50] Ava Chen: For Dedicated we usually discuss monthly vs annual commitments — pilot can be monthly reservation. We'll propose a trial-friendly pricing for pilot.
[14:59] Jordan - Finance: OK, that helps.
[15:02] Ava Chen: Alright, we'll recap next steps: I'll email the spreadsheet + security pack by Friday. Diego will provision benchmark infra once traces arrive. We'll reconvene with benchmark results and pricing in ~2 weeks.
[15:16] Mark Levin: Thanks, appreciate it.
[15:19] Sana Iqbal: Thanks all.
[15:22] Ava Chen: Thanks everyone — we'll follow up.
[15:25] [END]

Post-call notes (auto):
- Key assumption to validate: avg tokens/call including system overhead = ~302; monthly tokens ~12.1M.
- Recommended immediate steps: send traces, run 48h benchmark on Redwood Dedicated with quantized variants, measure caching impact.
- Risk items to track: utilization, cache-hit rate, quantization quality delta, network egress.

Referenced artifacts/links: 
- Cost-model template (to be attached)
- SOC2 + KMS integration doc (to be attached)
- Benchmark runbook (link will be provided)

title: Stream-first Chat API Intro: params, fallbacks and retry patterns
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-03-05
Start: 16:00 UTC
Duration: ~42 minutes
Attendees: Samir Patel (Redwood AE), Ava Chen (Redwood SE), Jules Martinez (Redwood Product), Dr. Mira Lang (Quell Eng Lead), Carlos Vega (Quell CTO), Priya Nair (Quell SRE), Alex Zhu (Quell PM)

Summary: quick hosted API demo focused on streaming-first chat generation. Covered real-time streaming behavior, tuning params (temperature, top_p, max_tokens, stop sequences), practical retry/backoff strategies, fallback to cheaper model variants and console-based traces. Discussed Quell's privacy concerns (PHI), VPC/private deployment options, and next steps to run a short pilot.

Topics: streaming behavior, partial outputs, idempotency, early-exit / stop tokens, latency vs throughput tradeoffs, prefix caching, retry heuristics, auth and encryption.

Action items (captured in metadata above).

Transcript body:
[00:00] Samir: hey everyone, thanks for joining, i'm samir with redwood, ava and jules are with me. quick intro?
[00:08] Dr. Mira: hi all, mira here, lead eng at quell health. thanks, we wanted a practical look at streaming and retry behavior, and how to keep PII/PHI safe.
[00:16] Carlos: yeah, i want latency numbers and how we can failover to cheaper models when the main one is busy.
[00:22] Samir: perfect, that's exactly what we'll cover. quick agenda: quick API demo with streaming, then param tuning recommendations, then retries/fallbacks and console traces. questions as we go.
[00:33] Ava: i'll share my screen, you'll see our hosted endpoint hitting a medium-size LLM, streaming tokens as they arrive. note: transcription might flip product names sometimes in the notes.
[00:43] [screen share starts]
[00:50] Ava: ok, request started, you can see the client opens a http/2 stream, server sends data frames with partial tokens, and the client progressively renders the message. this is useful for chat UIs and typing indicators.
[01:12] Alex: is the stream chunk size fixed or adaptive?
[01:15] Ava: good q. chunking is influenced by model decode speed and batching—so it's not strictly fixed. we batch requests on the runtime side; if you have low qps the server still flushes smaller chunks to avoid large latencies.
[01:30] Dr. Mira: what about partial hallucinations — like, we show a partial answer and it's wrong then the final is different, how to handle that in UI?
[01:40] Samir: common. two UX patterns: show a typing indicator + partial text with a small confidence badge, or wait for final token for critical content (e.g., PHI). you can also post-process final output to replace or redact fields.
[01:56] Priya: we need strong guarantees on data residency and no logs stored by default — can you speak to retention?
[02:03] Jules: by default hosted keeps short retention for troubleshooting but enterprise can opt-in to zero-retention or use private deployment. we support KMS for encryption and VPC peering for private control plane. we also have SOC 2 docs — we'll send them.
[02:20] Carlos: ok good, can you show param effects — temp, top_p, max_tokens?
[02:25] Ava: yup. switching to a small prompt, i'll demo three calls: temp 0.0 (deterministic), temp 0.8 (creative), and top_p 0.9.
[02:40] [demo runs]
[02:48] Ava: temp 0.0 gives shorter, repetitive replies — good for deterministic templates. temp 0.8 produces more diverse completions. top_p adjusts nucleus sampling; it has similar effect but sometimes better for control when combined with a low temp.
[03:05] Alex: any latency difference for higher temp?
[03:08] Ava: negligible — sampling parameters don't materially change per-token compute. main latency drivers are model size, seq length, and batching.
[03:20] Dr. Mira: how do retries behave for streaming? if a stream fails at token 73, do we re-request and replay from scratch?
[03:28] Samir: good one. streaming introduces partial-state issues. our recommended pattern: 1) use idempotency keys on requests so the backend can deduplicate; 2) if the stream disconnects, you can re-request with the same key and the service will attempt to resume where possible or return a final stable response. for high-safety use cases we recommend detecting incomplete responses and re-asking with a short prefix plus context.
[03:52] Ava: also consider saving the final delta on the client side; if you need strict at-most-once semantics you can switch to non-streaming sync calls for critical writes.
[04:04] Priya: is there a built-in retry/backoff library in your SDKs?
[04:08] Ava: yes, our SDKs come with a recommended backoff strategy (exponential with jitter) and automatic idempotency header handling; you can override thresholds in config.
[04:18] Carlos: can you show what a transient 503 looks like and how the client recovers?
[04:22] Ava: sure, i'll simulate a transient server error — see client logs: first attempt returns a 503 mid-stream, SDK waits per backoff schedule and retries; the runtime may route to a warm fallback model if the primary is overloaded. in our demo it retried once and then used a smaller quantized variant.
[04:46] [simulated 503]
[04:53] Ava: you can see in the console trace the routing change: primary model timed out, fallback invoked, output resumed. latency for fallback is slightly higher at the start due to cold-starts but overall end-to-end still within SLO for many apps.
[05:10] Dr. Mira: cold-starts worry me — on a sudden spike can we avoid big latency blips?
[05:15] Samir: we have burst pools and warm pools for Dedicated customers; hosted has warm tiers and warm-up heuristics. also recommend queuing client-side with token budget and backpressure UI.
[05:28] Alex: what's the best practice for batching vs low-latency single-user flows?
[05:32] Ava: if you're latency-sensitive per request, disable batching at the client where possible or use smaller batch windows. if you have many concurrent users, batching improves token throughput and lowers cost. Redwood Optimize can surface suggestions for batch window and quantization per workload.
[05:48] Carlos: show me the console traces that map tokens to cost.
[05:52] Ava: switching to console, here is a per-request trace: token counts, model variant, cost estimate, latency breakdown (queueing, decode, network). you can filter by route and by token type (prompt vs completion).
[06:08] [console demo]
[06:18] Ava: note the optimize tab recommends a quantized model for long conversations with many short prompts to reduce cost by ~30%. it also points out opportunities for prefix caching when you have repeated system prompts.
[06:32] Dr. Mira: prefix caching — can you explain?
[06:35] Samir: prefix or KV caching stores key/value states for common conversation beginnings (system prompts, user profile context) at runtime so the model doesn't re-decode that context on every request. it reduces compute and latency for repeated flows.
[06:47] Priya: about PHI, can we redact at the model boundary? we want to avoid any PHI persisting in logs or in vector DBs downstream.
[06:55] Jules: two paths: 1) do client-side redaction before sending; 2) use our optional on-infer hooks that scrub or hash sensitive fields. for enterprise we support a private deployment with customer-controlled KMS and no-host retention. we'll share the architecture doc.
[07:12] Carlos: any compliance certifications?
[07:15] Jules: we have SOC 2 Type II and are working on HITRUST readiness — will include current scope doc.
[07:24] Alex: curious: if we use function/tool calling, how is streaming affected? can you stream partial function arguments?
[07:31] Ava: function calls are emitted as structured tokens; we stream the text parts and then a final function call payload is returned. you typically receive the function name + args at the end, but some SDKs buffer until the whole payload is formed. streaming the arguments incrementally is supported in the protocol but some client implementations choose to wait.
[07:55] Dr. Mira: good to know.
[08:00] Samir: switching to practical patterns — we recommend three tiers: 1) quick UI with optimistic streaming (display partial tokens) for friendliness; 2) safe UI for PHI (wait for final token or validate via redaction); 3) batch mode for offline generation.
[08:16] Priya: what about token accounting for streaming? do partial tokens count immediately?
[08:21] Ava: billing is per final tokens generated; intermediate partial frames don't change billing. what matters is model output length (final).
[08:30] Carlos: can we set hard stop tokens to avoid runaway outputs?
[08:34] Ava: yes, stop sequences and max_tokens control truncation. we also support safety guards in the runtime to cut output for safety categories.
[08:45] Alex: what's an example retry policy you recommend?
[08:48] Samir: recommended: up to 3 retries, initial backoff 300ms, exponential x2 with jitter, give up on 4xx except 429 and 409 where you can retry with increased backoff. for streaming failures prefer a single restart rather than aggressive retries to avoid repeated partial duplicates.
[09:08] Dr. Mira: for idempotency keys, is the key scoped to a conversation? do we need to store mapping?
[09:12] Ava: idempotency keys are per-call; for conversation-level dedupe you can include a conversation id as part of the key format. server dedupe window defaults to 5 minutes but enterprise can extend policies.
[09:24] Carlos: show us an example SDK snippet with streaming and retry hooks.
[09:29] Ava: sure, here's a python snippet — client.stream(..., on_partial=..., idempotency_key=..., retry_policy=...). the on_partial callback gets invoked with each chunk and you can decide to display or buffer.
[09:44] [code snippet shown]
[09:50] Dr. Mira: can we run a 1-hour pilot with representative prompts and measure latency P95 and cost?
[09:55] Samir: absolutely, that's what we usually do. ava will schedule and we just need your sample payloads and desired SLOs.
[10:03] Priya: about private deployment, what's the minimum contract?
[10:07] Jules: private (VPC) typically has a minimum 12-month commitment and setup time ~4-6 weeks with our infra team. Dedicated reserved capacity seats are smaller and faster to start. we'll send details.
[10:21] Carlos: curious — do you recommend quantization for us given our mix of short diagnostic questions and longer summaries?
[10:27] Ava: usually mixed workloads benefit from two model tiers: a smaller quantized variant for short questions and a larger higher-fidelity model for summarization. Optimize can recommend thresholds to auto-route by prompt length.
[10:40] Alex: auto-routing — you mean the platform will pick variant by policy?
[10:44] Samir: yes, smart routing by policy: latency-first, cost-first, or region-first. you can define fallbacks and canary rollouts per route.
[10:55] Dr. Mira: how does the console expose per-route rollout status? can we see per-user impact?
[10:59] Jules: console shows per-route metrics, traces and canary labels. you can drill down by tokens and by token cost; per-user impact is available if you pass user identifiers (with care for PHI).
[11:12] Priya: we'll need to ensure SSO and RBAC for our team. does Redwood integrate with our existing SAML?
[11:18] Samir: yes SAML/SSO, SCIM provisioning, fine-grained RBAC. enterprise plan includes audit logs and longer retention windows.
[11:28] Carlos: ok, let's cover a failure scenario: network partition causes stream disconnect then duplicate finalization. how do you prevent users seeing duplicates?
[11:38] Ava: client-side dedupe is best — track idempotency and last-acknowledged token. server-side we try to resume where possible. if the client sees a duplicate, it can check sequence numbers in the stream frames and ignore repeats. we emit sequence metadata in frames for reliable clients.
[11:58] Dr. Mira: sequence numbers would be helpful.
[12:02] Samir: quick note: some client SDKs are higher-level and hide sequence numbers; others expose them for cases like yours.
[12:12] Alex: what's the observed P95 for a 4k token conversation on hosted west region?
[12:16] Ava: depends on model — for our medium open model ~ P95 is 350-450ms per request for short prompts; for 4k conversation decode time scales, so end-to-end might be 700-1200ms for many tokens. we'll share sample benchmarks.
[12:34] Carlos: can you do per-token rate limits? we need burst protection.
[12:38] Jules: yes, rate limits by token and by concurrent connections; console shows quota consumption and you can set per-route caps.
[12:48] Priya: on encryption — we want envelope encryption with our KMS.
[12:52] Jules: supported — customer KMS for private deployment and hosted enterprise with customer-managed keys is available.
[13:02] [short silence as demo context switches]
[13:12] Ava: one more demo: we'll show a simulated production flow — UX shows partial tokens, then final structured function call. watch how client buffers a bit until the function args are complete.
[13:34] [demo run]
[13:50] Ava: you can see the client opted to display partial text but waited to execute the function until the final call payload arrived. that pattern keeps actions atomic.
[14:05] Alex: good. for our use-cases, we probably want that behavior.
[14:09] Dr. Mira: anything else around observability?
[14:12] Samir: traces, per-token cost, error types, and automatic anomaly alerts tied to your eval sets. Optimize can run periodic checks and flag quality regressions.
[14:24] Carlos: speaking of evals, we have a set of 500 prompts for triage — can you ingest and run them?
[14:29] Jules: yes we can run a benchmark against your prompts and produce an accuracy/latency/cost matrix. that's part of the pilot.
[14:38] Priya: timeline — we want pilot data by next week, ideally.
[14:42] Samir: ava will coordinate.
[14:45] Ava: will do. can you share the prompt set and expected golden responses? also traffic shape (qps, concurrency).
[14:54] Dr. Mira: yes, we'll upload to the shared drive — can you confirm scoping doc?
[14:59] Samir: we'll send a pilot scoping doc with required inputs.
[15:03] Carlos: final question: cost controls during pilot — can we cap spend?
[15:07] Jules: yes, quota caps and alerts, and you can sandbox within a trial account. we'll show how to set a hard spend cap.
[15:16] Alex: ok thanks, that was helpful.
[15:19] Samir: quick recap and next steps: send quickstart + demo code, send SOC2/KMS/VPC docs, schedule a pilot (ava), receive prompt set and traffic profile (quell). any other asks?
[15:34] Dr. Mira: no, that covers it. thanks.
[15:37] Priya: thanks everyone.
[15:40] Samir: thanks all — we'll follow up in email with links and schedule.
[15:47] [meeting ends]

Notes about transcription: some product names and timings may be slightly off; speaker labels occasionally reflect screen-share speaker.

[04:53] Ava: you can see in the console trace the routing change: primary model timed out, fallback invoked, output resumed. latency for fallback is slightly higher at the start due to cold-starts but overall end-to-end still within SLO for many apps.
[05:10] Dr. Mira: cold-starts worry me — on a sudden spike can we avoid big latency blips?
[05:15] Samir: we have burst pools and warm pools for Dedicated customers; hosted has warm tiers and warm-up heuristics. also recommend queuing client-side with token budget and backpressure UI.
[05:28] Alex: what's the best practice for batching vs low-latency single-user flows?
[05:32] Ava: if you're latency-sensitive per request, disable batching at the client where possible or use smaller batch windows. if you have many concurrent users, batching improves token throughput and lowers cost. Redwood Optimize can surface suggestions for batch window and quantization per workload.
[05:48] Carlos: show me the console traces that map tokens to cost.
[05:52] Ava: switching to console, here is a per-request trace: token counts, model variant, cost estimate, latency breakdown (queueing, decode, network). you can filter by route and by token type (prompt vs completion).
[06:08] [console demo]
[06:18] Ava: note the optimize tab recommends a quantized model for long conversations with many short prompts to reduce cost by ~30%. it also points out opportunities for prefix caching when you have repeated system prompts.
[06:32] Dr. Mira: prefix caching — can you explain?
[06:35] Samir: prefix or KV caching stores key/value states for common conversation beginnings (system prompts, user profile context) at runtime so the model doesn't re-decode that context on every request. it reduces compute and latency for repeated flows.
[06:47] Priya: about PHI, can we redact at the model boundary? we want to avoid any PHI persisting in logs or in vector DBs downstream.
[06:55] Jules: two paths: 1) do client-side redaction before sending; 2) use our optional on-infer hooks that scrub or hash sensitive fields. for enterprise we support a private deployment with customer-controlled KMS and no-host retention. we'll share the architecture doc.
[07:12] Carlos: any compliance certifications?
[07:15] Jules: we have SOC 2 Type II and are working on HITRUST readiness — will include current scope doc.
[07:24] Alex: curious: if we use function/tool calling, how is streaming affected? can you stream partial function arguments?
[07:31] Ava: function calls are emitted as structured tokens; we stream the text parts and then a final function call payload is returned. you typically receive the function name + args at the end, but some SDKs buffer until the whole payload is formed. streaming the arguments incrementally is supported in the protocol but some client implementations choose to wait.
[07:55] Dr. Mira: good to know.
[08:00] Samir: switching to practical patterns — we recommend three tiers: 1) quick UI with optimistic streaming (display partial tokens) for friendliness; 2) safe UI for PHI (wait for final token or validate via redaction); 3) batch mode for offline generation.
[08:16] Priya: what about token accounting for streaming? do partial tokens count immediately?
[08:21] Ava: billing is per final tokens generated; intermediate partial frames don't change billing. what matters is model output length (final).
[08:30] Carlos: can we set hard stop tokens to avoid runaway outputs?
[08:34] Ava: yes, stop sequences and max_tokens control truncation. we also support safety guards in the runtime to cut output for safety categories.
[08:45] Alex: what's an example retry policy you recommend?
[08:48] Samir: recommended: up to 3 retries, initial backoff 300ms, exponential x2 with jitter, give up on 4xx except 429 and 409 where you can retry with increased backoff. for streaming failures prefer a single restart rather than aggressive retries to avoid repeated partial duplicates.
[09:08] Dr. Mira: for idempotency keys, is the key scoped to a conversation? do we need to store mapping?
[09:12] Ava: idempotency keys are per-call; for conversation-level dedupe you can include a conversation id as part of the key format. server dedupe window defaults to 5 minutes but enterprise can extend policies.
[09:24] Carlos: show us an example SDK snippet with streaming and retry hooks.
[09:29] Ava: sure, here's a python snippet — client.stream(..., on_partial=..., idempotency_key=..., retry_policy=...). the on_partial callback gets invoked with each chunk and you can decide to display or buffer.
[09:44] [code snippet shown]
[09:50] Dr. Mira: can we run a 1-hour pilot with representative prompts and measure latency P95 and cost?
[09:55] Samir: absolutely, that's what we usually do. ava will schedule and we just need your sample payloads and desired SLOs.
[10:03] Priya: about private deployment, what's the minimum contract?
[10:07] Jules: private (VPC) typically has a minimum 12-month commitment and setup time ~4-6 weeks with our infra team. Dedicated reserved capacity seats are smaller and faster to start. we'll send details.
[10:21] Carlos: curious — do you recommend quantization for us given our mix of short diagnostic questions and longer summaries?
[10:27] Ava: usually mixed workloads benefit from two model tiers: a smaller quantized variant for short questions and a larger higher-fidelity model for summarization. Optimize can recommend thresholds to auto-route by prompt length.
[10:40] Alex: auto-routing — you mean the platform will pick variant by policy?
[10:44] Samir: yes, smart routing by policy: latency-first, cost-first, or region-first. you can define fallbacks and canary rollouts per route.
[10:55] Dr. Mira: how does the console expose per-route rollout status? can we see per-user impact?
[10:59] Jules: console shows per-route metrics, traces and canary labels. you can drill down by tokens and by token cost; per-user impact is available if you pass user identifiers (with care for PHI).
[11:12] Priya: we'll need to ensure SSO and RBAC for our team. does Redwood integrate with our existing SAML?
[11:18] Samir: yes SAML/SSO, SCIM provisioning, fine-grained RBAC. enterprise plan includes audit logs and longer retention windows.
[11:28] Carlos: ok, let's cover a failure scenario: network partition causes stream disconnect then duplicate finalization. how do you prevent users seeing duplicates?
[11:38] Ava: client-side dedupe is best — track idempotency and last-acknowledged token. server-side we try to resume where possible. if the client sees a duplicate, it can check sequence numbers in the stream frames and ignore repeats. we emit sequence metadata in frames for reliable clients.
[11:58] Dr. Mira: sequence numbers would be helpful.
[12:02] Samir: quick note: some client SDKs are higher-level and hide sequence numbers; others expose them for cases like yours.
[12:12] Alex: what's the observed P95 for a 4k token conversation on hosted west region?
[12:16] Ava: depends on model — for our medium open model ~ P95 is 350-450ms per request for short prompts; for 4k conversation decode time scales, so end-to-end might be 700-1200ms for many tokens. we'll share sample benchmarks.
[12:34] Carlos: can you do per-token rate limits? we need burst protection.
[12:38] Jules: yes, rate limits by token and by concurrent connections; console shows quota consumption and you can set per-route caps.
[12:48] Priya: on encryption — we want envelope encryption with our KMS.
[12:52] Jules: supported — customer KMS for private deployment and hosted enterprise with customer-managed keys is available.
[13:02] [short silence as demo context switches]
[13:12] Ava: one more demo: we'll show a simulated production flow — UX shows partial tokens, then final structured function call. watch how client buffers a bit until the function args are complete.
[13:34] [demo run]
[13:50] Ava: you can see the client opted to display partial text but waited to execute the function until the final call payload arrived. that pattern keeps actions atomic.
[14:05] Alex: good. for our use-cases, we probably want that behavior.
[14:09] Dr. Mira: anything else around observability?
[14:12] Samir: traces, per-token cost, error types, and automatic anomaly alerts tied to your eval sets. Optimize can run periodic checks and flag quality regressions.
[14:24] Carlos: speaking of evals, we have a set of 500 prompts for triage — can you ingest and run them?
[14:29] Jules: yes we can run a benchmark against your prompts and produce an accuracy/latency/cost matrix. that's part of the pilot.
[14:38] Priya: timeline — we want pilot data by next week, ideally.
[14:42] Samir: ava will coordinate.
[14:45] Ava: will do. can you share the prompt set and expected golden responses? also traffic shape (qps, concurrency).
[14:54] Dr. Mira: yes, we'll upload to the shared drive — can you confirm scoping doc?
[14:59] Samir: we'll send a pilot scoping doc with required inputs.
[15:03] Carlos: final question: cost controls during pilot — can we cap spend?
[15:07] Jules: yes, quota caps and alerts, and you can sandbox within a trial account. we'll show how to set a hard spend cap.
[15:16] Alex: ok thanks, that was helpful.
[15:19] Samir: quick recap and next steps: send quickstart + demo code, send SOC2/KMS/VPC docs, schedule a pilot (ava), receive prompt set and traffic profile (quell). any other asks?
[15:34] Dr. Mira: no, that covers it. thanks.
[15:37] Priya: thanks everyone.
[15:40] Samir: thanks all — we'll follow up in email with links and schedule.
[15:47] [meeting ends]

Notes about transcription: some product names and timings may be slightly off; speaker labels occasionally reflect screen-share speaker.

title: SLO/Budget Stability Triage & Deploy Gating
preview:
Header: 2025-02-20 10:30am PST | Duration 1h20 | Recording: ZoomCloudAuto | Meeting: SLO/Budget Stability Triage & Deploy Gating
Attendees: Ava Chen, Diego Morales, Priya Nair, Liam O'Connor, Sophie Park, Maya Singh

Summary: quick triage of recurring production SLO burn, prioritization between batching/quantization vs autoscale, and finalizing deploy gating checklist for next Dedicated capacity rollouts. Short incident review of last week's OOM on dedicated-5a.

Topics: SLO alerts, KV cache noise, kernel fallback experiment, deploy gating checklist, budget vs latency tradeoffs, canary strategy

Action items listed in metadata above.

00:00:12 - Ava: okay everyone, uh, thanks for joining, um let's kick off, we've got 80 minutes but I want to be pragmatic - main goals for today are to decide the immediate mitigations for SLO burn, pick the experiments we run this sprint and lock down the gating checklist for the Dedicated roll next week.
00:00:35 - Diego: sounds good. I'll flag the specific regression from last thursday, the OOM we saw on dedicated-5a when the fine-tune started overlapping with a big enterprise inference batch. I pulled logs, and it looked like the KV cache was filling, and then, uh, K V cash, uh, sorry, the KV cache eviction wasn't kicking in fast enough - might be an eviction policy bug.
00:01:02 - Priya: yep I saw those alerts early morning, and we had a cascade: autoscaler spun up nodes but the serving runtime hit a kernel-level oom before the new nodes got warm. The alert group is noisy — a few of the alerts are duplicates and the pager was paged twice. Alert fatigue is real.
00:01:25 - Liam: we need to distinguish between short-term firefighting and longer-term platform changes. Short-term: alert throttling and runbook updates. Longer-term: kernel fallback and batching policy changes. Diego, can you estimate effort for a kernel fallback toggle that we can ship as an experiment?
00:01:46 - Diego: I can scope a minimal fallback — basically we enable an alternative attention kernel if memory pressure observed and fall back to smaller KV cache retention window. I want to run perf comparison though, because fallback might increase tokens latency by 8-20% depending on sequence length. Roughly, 2-3 engineer-days for POC plus an SRE day for canaries.
00:02:10 - Sophie: from QA perspective, we need clear acceptance criteria: latency < 250ms p95 for 512 tokens in hosted tests, and no regressions on throughput for Dedicated baseline. If fallback pushes p95 over threshold, we mark it as mitigated but not allowed for canary.
00:02:33 - Ava: good. Also we have to consider cost impact versus SLO. Our SLO burn this month is primarily from long-tail high-concurrency customer runs. If we push cheaper kernels or quantize aggressively we save budget but might lose tail latency. What's the delta if we relax p95 by 20ms?
00:02:55 - Priya: small delta in per-token cost but the bigger win is better caching and batching; if we can reduce cold start rate by 15% we save more. We need improved prefix caching instrumentation — current telemetry doesn't expose per-prefix hit/miss cleanly; the dashboard is too coarse.
00:03:22 - Maya: I'll add that dashboard request to action items. We should also update the deploy gating checklist to require cache telemetry smoke tests.
00:03:35 - Liam: on the gating checklist, proposed items: runbook updated, SLO budget check green, canary on Dedicated with 10% traffic for 2 hours, fallback metrics observed, rollback plan documented. Anything else?
00:03:53 - Diego: include kernel fallback feature flag and a safety timeout so if the fallback kicks in for >5% of tokens we auto-roll back.
00:04:06 - Sophie: plus automated QA smoke that runs synthetic burst tests to catch queueing degeneracy.
00:04:18 - Priya: and we should lock on alert groups — ensure SRE owns the primary alert channel and that duplicates are suppressed. I can prototype suppression rules.
00:04:31 - Ava: ok actionable. Now the incident review: Diego, can you walk through the timeline?
00:04:40 - Diego: timeline: 02/13 03:10am - first kv-cache saturation alert, 03:12 - autoscaler triggered, 03:14 - serving runtime OOM, 03:15 - multiple upstream retries triggered, 03:20 - customer-facing errors spiked. Root cause looks like combined load from a scheduled batch job and a high-concurrency customer test. The eviction heuristics didn't prioritize the small keyspaces, and the fallback kernel wasn't enabled for that model. There's also a K8s node provisioning delay of roughly 90 seconds in that zone—contrib to the observed SLO burn.
00:05:15 - Priya: provisioning delay is known in us-west-2 zone; we have a mitigations doc but it's manual. We need automated warm pools for Dedicated to avoid the slow node startup hitting the SLO. Warm pools cost budget though.
00:05:33 - Liam: cost vs reliability again. How much do warm pools add to monthly committed costs?
00:05:40 - Diego: rough estimate: a small warm pool per cluster adds about 6-8% to baseline committed GPU hours; we can trade that against expected SLO breach penalties or credits.
00:05:52 - Maya: finance will want numbers. Ava, can you take the SLO burn and map to projected credits if we don't mitigate?
00:06:00 - Ava: yes I'll pull the last 3 months of SLO burn and simulate dollar impact.
00:06:07 - [crosstalk, inaudible]
00:06:20 - Sophie: one more QA note — our perf harness missed the scenario because the synthetic workload didn't include overlapping fine-tune patterns. We need to expand the synthetic profiles.
00:06:35 - Diego: agree. I'll add a new perf profile that combines long-running fine-tunes with short live inference bursts to surface KV pressure.
00:06:47 - Priya: back to alerts: I propose two-tier suppression: group duplicates into a single incident and suppress noisy follow-ups for 10 minutes unless severity escalates. That should reduce pager noise immediately.
00:07:05 - Liam: fine, but make sure suppression doesn't hide real multi-region failures. We still want multi-region alerting.
00:07:12 - Priya: yes, region-aware suppression.
00:07:17 - Ava: let's talk experiment prioritization — we have three candidates: 1) kernel fallback POC, 2) alert suppression + runbook automation, 3) warm pool warmup + cache instrumentation. Which are highest impact?
00:07:32 - Diego: kernel fallback could stop OOMs quickly but risks latency regressions; alert suppression is low-effort high-impact for ops; warm pools are expensive but reduce SLO risk. I'd prioritize suppression and kernel fallback POC in parallel, warm pools as follow-on contingent on cost analysis.
00:07:53 - Sophie: QA needs time to validate kernel fallback; tentative earliest canary mid next week if Diego's POC aligns.
00:08:03 - Ava: ok assign Priya to suppression, Diego to fallback, Sophie to canary QA, Maya to update gating checklist. Timeline: suppression POC by next Thu, fallback POC by following Fri, gating doc updated by EOD tomorrow.
00:08:20 - [pause]
00:08:30 - Diego: on fallback implementation note — we must be careful with quantization interactions. If the model is running in int8 path and we swap kernels, the kernel might not support that quantized op; we need compatibility checks. Also, the compilation layer sometimes picks kernels based on sequence length heuristics; we'll need to make the selection overrideable via flag.
00:08:58 - Priya: good call. Add a pre-flight that verifies kernel compatibility and a flag to force the safe kernel.
00:09:07 - Liam: this is why we have the compile & kernel selection infra — take advantage of it. Make sure the operator UX (console flag) is clear for on-call to flip fallback quickly.
00:09:22 - Maya: I'll propose an operator runbook snippet for the console UI that shows fallback toggle and a one-click rollback.
00:09:31 - Ava: about canaries: Sophie, what's your canary proposal?
00:09:36 - Sophie: two-stage canary: first internal traffic synthetic for 2 hours against Dedicated cluster; if metrics look good (p95, error rate, memory pressure) we ramp to 10% real traffic for 2 hours. We'll gate full rollout on zero fallback rollbacks and stable error budget.
00:09:58 - Diego: add a metric to detect fallback frequency — if we see fallback >2% of tokens during canary we abort.
00:10:07 - Priya: also ensure tracing spans include kernel selection metadata so we can correlate slow requests with fallback events.
00:10:16 - Ava: good. Anything else?
00:10:20 - Liam: one operational ask: put a temporary cap on some long-running cron jobs next week during the rollout window to reduce chance of overlapping runs. Who owns that?
00:10:30 - Diego: infra jobs are tagged; I can flip caps once we have the schedule from Maya.
00:10:36 - Maya: I'll publish the maintenance window and coordinate with product teams.
00:10:40 - [brief silence]
00:10:48 - Ava: okay final recap: Priya builds suppression POC & runbook updates; Diego scopes and starts kernel fallback POC; Sophie prepares canary QA plan; Maya updates gating checklist and schedules maintenance window; Liam will approve any warm pool spend once Ava sends the cost projection. We'll all reconvene in a week to review results.
00:11:12 - Diego: quick question — are we including dedicated autoscaling policy changes as part of this ticket? There's a small policy tweak that could add warm-start behavior.
00:11:22 - Priya: I can prototype a scale-in delay so nodes aren't killed too fast; that lowers churn and helps warm pools.
00:11:31 - Ava: add it to Diego's scope list.
00:11:35 - [crosstalk]
00:11:45 - Sophie: also, we should run a small A/B on quant profiles — one group with aggressive quant + fallback, one with conservative to compare perf and cost.
00:11:58 - Diego: yup we'll include a perf matrix.
00:12:05 - Ava: excellent. Anything else from the group?
00:12:10 - Liam: just one more: make sure legal/enterprise knows about canary traffic to ensure no contractual surprises for dedicated customers in the canary cohort. Maya please loop in customer success.
00:12:22 - Maya: will do.
00:12:25 - Ava: thanks everyone. We'll post notes and action items. Meeting adjourned.

Notes on transcription: occasional speaker overlap and minor mis-hearings (e.g., "K V cash" for KV cache, "kernel fallback" sometimes heard as "kernel fall back") are preserved for authenticity.

References mentioned: internal runbook link (doc: /docs/runbooks/kv-cache-eviction), gating checklist draft (link: /docs/deploy/gating-checklist-draft), perf harness config (link: /internal/perf/profiles/overlap-finetune-burst), canary plan (link: /playbooks/canary/dedicated-10pct).

End of transcript.

End of transcript.

title: Northstar Bank x Redwood  Private Security Review (Audit Logs)
preview:
Meeting header
Date: 2025-02-14
Time: 1:00 PM PT
Duration: 46 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood), Diego Fernandez (Redwood), Aisha Rahman (Redwood), Morgan Lee (Northstar), Samir Gupta (Northstar), Priya Desai (Northstar)

Summary (auto)
Customer requested details on exporting Redwood Private audit logs to customer-owned S3 including auth model, encryption, and whether log content includes prompts.

Topics (auto)
- Audit log event schema
- S3 bucket policy / permissions
- Encryption (SSE-KMS)
- Delivery semantics and buffering

Action items (auto)
- Redwood: Provide sample IAM role policy (prefix-scoped) and confirm no ListBucket requirement
- Customer: Provide KMS key requirements and desired retention period

Transcript
[00:00] Ava Chen: cool, thanks everyone. today we wanted to focus just on audit logs for the Private deployment.
[00:18] Morgan Lee: yep. we need to prove we can get audit events out of the cluster into our AWS account. retention is 1 year minimum.
[00:35] Diego Fernandez: makes sense. the current plan is an in-cluster exporter that writes JSON lines into S3 under a deterministic prefix.
[00:49] Samir Gupta: what does deterministic mean here?
[01:01] Diego Fernandez: like `redwood-audit/v1/tenant_id=.../cluster_id=.../date=YYYY-MM-DD/part-000001.jsonl.gz`. easy to lifecycle and ingest.
[01:20] Priya Desai: and the events include what exactly?
[01:27] Aisha Rahman: admin + control plane actions. actor, action, resource, result, request_id. importantly no prompt or completion payloads in audit logs.
[01:44] Morgan Lee: ok good. auth is the big concern. we do not allow access keys.
[01:56] Diego Fernandez: same  were using IRSA in the EKS cluster to assume a role in your account. short-lived creds. we require ExternalId.
[02:18] Samir Gupta: do you need ListBucket? our bucket policies are strict.
[02:24] Diego Fernandez: no. we can do just PutObject. if we use multipart we might need AbortMultipartUpload, but not list.
[02:38] Aisha Rahman: from security side, were going to fail closed if ExternalId isnt set. no silent fallback to keys.
[03:02] Morgan Lee: encryption?
[03:05] Diego Fernandez: supports SSE-S3 and SSE-KMS. you can provide your KMS key ARN.
[03:17] Priya Desai: do you support GCS too? we have a secondary environment.
[03:24] Diego Fernandez: yes in the same exporter were adding GCS via Workload Identity Federation.
[03:40] Morgan Lee: ok, timeline?
[03:44] Ava Chen: were doing a design partner rollout this month. well coordinate enablement and testing with you.
[04:02] Samir Gupta: delivery semantics  any gaps?
[04:08] Diego Fernandez: at-least-once. we buffer locally on disk; if S3 is down we pause consumption once buffer hits a watermark. youll have metrics like last success timestamp and buffer size.
[04:29] Morgan Lee: ok. can you send the formal requirements in writing?
[04:33] Aisha Rahman: yes. we have an internal requirements page and we can share a customer-friendly summary + example policy.
[44:50] Ava Chen: recap: well send IAM + KMS examples, youll share role arn/external id and prefix, and well schedule a follow-up on the installer config. sound good?
[45:05] Morgan Lee: yep.
