title: Structured JSON Mode & Function-Calling Runtime Deep Dive
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-02-11
Start: 15:00 PST
Duration approx 66 minutes
Recorded by Fireflies
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Diego Morales (Redwood SE), Priya Nair (Redwood Product), Jared Thompson (CTO, Artemis), Maya Singh (ML Eng), Luca Romano (Infra), Nia Patel (Data Privacy)
Agenda: runtime perf for JSON mode + function/tool calling; constrained decoding overhead; KV cache impacts for structured outputs; batching/quantization tradeoffs in VPC/on-prem deployments

[00:00:00] Ava Chen: Hey everyone thanks for joining, quick roll call?
[00:00:06] Jared Thompson: Jared here, Maya, Luca and Nia on my side.
[00:00:10] Maya Singh: hi all.
[00:00:12] Diego Morales: Diego here, plus Priya. Good to see you.
[00:00:18] Ava Chen: Great, we put together a focused deck — we'll jump into a runtime-first deep dive. Feel free to interrupt, this is a lab-style call.

[00:01:12] Priya Nair: Quick context from product: Artemis mentioned they want to move heavy structured-tool outputs (JSON mode) into production and are seeing higher-than-expected latency and parse failures, especially when tool-calling schemas get nested. We're going to talk about why that happens and tradeoffs.

[00:02:05] Maya Singh: yep that's right. Our main pain: when we ask the model to emit a specific schema (function call), sometimes it adds extra keys or escapes quotes weirdly, or returns nearly-valid JSON that our parser rejects. Also end-to-end p95 latency ballooned when we enforced deterministic decoding constraints.

[00:02:40] Diego Morales: ok that's classic constrained-decoding + tokenization mismatch plus small prefill overhead. Two big axes: 1) how you constrain decoding (e.g., token bias / allowed token set / prefix-tree matcher) and 2) runtime effects on KV cache and batching. We'll demo numbers.

[00:03:20] Luca Romano: can you give the TL;DR on why constrained decoding slows things down? We expected only marginal overhead.

[00:03:33] Diego Morales: TL;DR: constrained decoding introduces per-step extra compute and memory overhead. If you use naive javascript-side regex post-filtering, you do retries or re-queries; if you use token-level masking (vocab-level ban/allow lists), the model still computes logits for the full vocabulary but then you apply the mask — small overhead. More expensive is when you run a prefix-tree (trie) that requires per-token lookups and bookkeeping, sometimes implemented inside the runtime as dynamic token remapping which can break fused kernels and slow the inner loop.

[00:04:23] Maya Singh: we were using a server-side filter that post-processes completions then retries when invalid — that multiplied request counts.

[00:04:30] Diego Morales: right, retries are the worst for latency on p95 because you re-run prefill and decode again (unless you cache prefill). Redwood's runtime has a couple mitigations: prefix-KV caching for prefill segments, and a constrained-decoding mode that integrates allowed-token masks with the decoding loop to avoid re-prefills. But it still adds decode-time overhead — we have numbers later.

[00:05:02] Jared Thompson: you mentioned prefill caching — how does that work with function-calling where the prompt and schema can change per request?

[00:05:12] Diego Morales: Good question. We compute a fingerprint for the prompt template + static context and store the KV cache after the prefill. For per-request variable fields (user input, call args) we keep them in a short prefix so prefill length is shorter. For function calling we try to keep the schema as part of the static prompt when possible. If the schema changes a lot, cache hit rate drops.

[00:05:50] Maya Singh: our schemas are dynamic — customers can supply different tool schemas per request.

[00:05:55] Priya Nair: two operational levers: 1) canonicalize schemas (minify, stable key ordering), 2) move heavy validation into post-invoke step but with a good constrained-decoding fallback to avoid retries.

[00:06:20] Luca Romano: about quantization — will INT8 increase parse errors or odd tokenization behavior? We plan on dedicated on-prem GPUs that may use 4-bit/8-bit quant libs.

[00:06:30] Diego Morales: short answer: quantization can slightly change token probabilities altering generation, which can cause more off-by-one or extra-escape issues in tightly constrained JSON. In our experiments, FP16 vs INT8 changed the exact token where closing brace appears ~3-4% of the time for long nested JSON. That said, with constrained decoding (masking) and a small sampling temperature you can largely eliminate the issue. But INT8 may make the decoder logits noisier around low-probability tokens, so we recommend a conservative decoding policy (top-p / low temp) and stronger schema constraints.

[00:07:12] Maya Singh: so we should keep FP16 for prod or use INT8 with stricter constraints?

[00:07:18] Diego Morales: depends on your latency/cost targets. If cost is critical, INT8 with a token-biasing + strong prefix-tree in the runtime yields good cost/latency, but you need to tune thresholds and run regen-on-fail logic. Dedicated nodes with 8-bit kernels can be 2-3x cheaper per-token but may need more SE tuning.

[00:07:55] Ava Chen: we'll include our quantization perf matrix in the follow-up.

[00:08:05] Jared Thompson: show us actual numbers — how much does constrained decoding add to single-request latency?

[00:08:12] Diego Morales: example numbers from a 4k context model, with 256-token generation:
- unconstrained sampling (FP16) p50: 120ms, p95: 260ms
- token-mask constrained decoding (FP16): p50: 140ms, p95: 320ms
- prefix-tree enforced (runtime integrated): p50: 150ms, p95: 360ms
- INT8 quant + integrated constraints: p50: 95ms, p95: 420ms (notice p95 worsens in some quant cases because of retries or longer decode due to low-prob tokens)

[00:09:08] Maya Singh: interesting that INT8 p50 is better but p95 worse. That matches our observations. Can you explain why retries spike p95?

[00:09:22] Diego Morales: retries hit worst-case paths: if you post-validate output and it's invalid you often re-run full decode with a harder constraint or sampling temperature set to 0 for deterministic. That re-run doubles or triples latency. If you can do partial prefill caching or incremental decoding that reuses KV cache you avoid entire re-prefill cost; if not, it's heavy.

[00:09:56] Luca Romano: incremental decoding — is that safe with GPUs and batching? I thought continuous batching requires full control of per-sequence decode steps.

[00:10:05] Diego Morales: Redwood runtime supports continuous batching: it queues tokens to be decoded across many sequences. Incremental decode reuse is orthogonal — you can pin the prefill KV to a session and only decode the residual. The tricky part is mixing batching with sessions that have different allowed-token masks; the runtime needs to do subgroup batching by mask to keep throughput. That reduces batch sizes for constrained flows.

[00:10:40] Maya Singh: we have many short requests (<150 tokens) and function calls with small outputs. Does batching help?

[00:10:48] Diego Morales: yes, batching helps amortize kernel launch and per-token overhead. For short outputs, large batch sizes (32/64) increase throughput and reduce unit cost but can increase tail latency. Redwood allows latency SLO targeting: you can set a max queueing delay so latency stays bounded; beyond that batch is flushed. For strict p95 requirements we recommend small batch sizes and prefix caching to compensate cost.

[00:11:25] Nia Patel: from security/compliance side: if we do Dedicated or on-prem, we need assurance KV cache isn't persisted or is encrypted. How does Redwood handle KV storage?

[00:11:36] Diego Morales: KV cache in the runtime is in-memory by default on inference nodes and not written to disk unless you enable warm-start snapshots. For Dedicated/VPC we support KMS encryption for any persisted snapshot, and audit logs for access. We'll include our security docs.

[00:11:52] Nia Patel: ok please share the exact config we should use.

[00:11:56] Ava Chen: noted, we'll attach a recommended Dedicated config.

[00:12:10] Priya Nair: let's pivot to function/tool calling performance: if you have many functions registered, does it increase latency?

[00:12:20] Diego Morales: registering many functions increases the schema size in the prompt and thus prefill length. Two strategies: 1) only send schemas for the subset likely to be called (function shortlist), 2) send a compressed or indexed schema and let the runtime expand it — but expansion can hurt cacheability. We also support offloading schema resolution to the application (return a short function id and do a separate call).

[00:12:53] Maya Singh: our system has ~40 tool variants per customer, dynamic. We can't always shortlist reliably.

[00:12:59] Diego Morales: in that case, canonicalizing and compressing schemas (e.g., gzipping static parts and keeping small variable blobs inline) helps. Another approach: use structured calling with a 2-step: produce an intent + function id (short text) then call the function with a second request. That splits latency but reduces constrained-decoding complexity.

[00:13:30] Jared Thompson: is there a pattern that keeps one-round call and still stays reliable?

[00:13:35] Diego Morales: yes: heavy-duty constrained decoding integrated into the runtime plus low-temperature sampling. If you can keep the function signatures fairly stable and canonicalized across requests, you get good cache and single-round reliability ~97-99% for simple schemas. For deeply nested schemas it's trickier.

[00:14:05] Maya Singh: about parser issues — sometimes the model emits Unicode escapes like “ which our JSON parser handles, other times unescaped newlines inside strings break stuff.

[00:14:15] Diego Morales: tokenizer boundary effects can cause those escapes. Two fixes: 1) strong closing token bias to prefer final brace tokens, 2) use a post-processor that repairs common patterns (unescaped newline => 
) but be careful — repairing can mask true errors. We actually instrumented common repair heuristics and found a small % of false-positives.

[00:14:48] Priya Nair: we recorded some microbenchmarks with Redwood runtime — Diego can you walk us through the demo numbers you mentioned?

[00:14:55] Diego Morales: Sure, demo setup: 4k context model, 256r/gens, batch sizes varied (1, 8, 32). Workload: function call asking for nested JSON 12 keys deep, ~180 tokens average. I'll skip slides and read numbers:
- Batch=1, no constraints, FP16: latency p95 ~ 320ms; throughput 3.1 req/s/node
- Batch=8, integrated prefix-tree constraints, FP16: p95 ~ 420ms, throughput 18.9 req/s/node
- Batch=32, mask-only constraints, FP16: p95 ~ 620ms (due to queueing), throughput 62 req/s/node
- Batch=8, INT8 quant + integrated constraints: p95 ~ 580ms (retries caused tail), throughput 45 req/s/node

[00:16:02] Luca Romano: those queueing tails are the latency policy tradeoff I guess. For production we need p95 < 500ms.

[00:16:08] Diego Morales: Then I'd recommend: batch target 8, integrated constraint mode, FP16 (or INT8 with tuned logits threshold), and aggressive prefix caching of static schema. That hits p95 around 380-420ms depending on context length.

[00:16:25] Maya Singh: what's the overhead of the integrated prefix-tree in terms of CPU or host-side work? We worry about host CPU become the bottleneck when we run many constrained sessions.

[00:16:36] Diego Morales: good callout. The integrated approach runs additional small CPU work per token for mask maintenance and trie navigation. On our nodes that used to be ~5-10% of CPU overhead for moderate session counts, but if you have many different masks it becomes a bigger percent because you can't batch across masks. For extreme cardinality of masks we recommend precomputing token mask bitsets on the host and using grouped batching by mask id. That reduces CPU load.

[00:17:10] Jared Thompson: networking question: in VPC/on-prem deployments is there more latency between controller and inference nodes that could affect runtime batching?

[00:17:18] Diego Morales: controller round-trip can add a few ms for orchestration; inference path is direct node-to-node for batching. In on-prem setups we tune node placement to keep that <1ms. The bigger issue is when you have multi-AZ routing and fallbacks which add tens of ms. For strict p95 you want single AZ placement for low variance.

[00:17:43] Nia Patel: about logs/audit: if we enable fine-grained JSON-mode debugging with token-level logs, does Redwood persist that to cloud? We need it in our private logging stack.

[00:17:54] Diego Morales: token-level logging is optional and can be restricted to ephemeral storage in Dedicated/VPC. For Private/on-prem we can push logs to your ELK/Splunk endpoint. We'll include those config snippets in the security pack.

[00:18:12] Priya Nair: quick note on function-call alternatives: structured format like YAML is more error-prone for parsing; JSON is easiest if you can guarantee strict quoting and closing.

[00:18:25] Maya Singh: what's Redwood's recommended prompt template for function-calling JSON mode?

[00:18:30] Diego Morales: we recommend: 1) a short instruction that says 'Return ONLY valid JSON matching schema X', 2) include schema in machine-friendly minimized form, 3) set decoding policy to deterministic-ish (temp 0-0.2, top-p 0.7), 4) enable integrated mask that only allows tokens in JSON plus allowed field names, and 5) post-validate with a strict parser and on-fail do a single constrained re-decode rather than unlimited retries. We'll send examples.

[00:19:10] Maya Singh: can you share an example of allowed-token mask for a nested key? I'm worried about tokenization splitting a key name.

[00:19:20] Diego Morales: allowed-token masks operate at token ids, so for long key names that tokenize to multiple subtokens you need to allow the sequence. That's where prefix-tree matching helps: it tracks allowed token sequences not just single tokens. If you instead allow each subtoken independently, you can still generate invalid key splits. The runtime's trie implementation solves this but costs extra per-token checks.

[00:19:57] Jared Thompson: if we move to a 2-step intent+function-id pattern, do we lose semantic accuracy?

[00:20:05] Diego Morales: some loss but often negligible for stable toolsets. You gain robustness and simpler constrained decoding. We measured after splitting the single round into intent+call, overall success rate increased for complex calls because you avoid long nested output decoding.

[00:20:30] Maya Singh: any notes about token budget and cost controls? We do a lot of concurrent low-latency calls and want to cap spend.

[00:20:38] Ava Chen: Redwood Console can give token-level cost per route and you can set per-route quotas and hard caps. Also batching/caching suggestions from Redwood Optimize can reduce tokens by up to 40% on average for typical function-call workloads.

[00:20:55] Luca Romano: will Redwood help us run the on-prem benchmark? We may need a small SE engagement.

[00:21:02] Ava Chen: yes we offer a short SE-led pilot to run benchmarks and produce a perf delta report. We'll outline scope in the next email.

[00:21:12] Diego Morales: final caution: for deterministic JSON you sometimes need to implement token-level forced close braces and explicit stop-token sequences. But stop tokens are fragile with batching; prefer integrated closing-bias in the runtime.

[00:21:35] Maya Singh: what about streaming? we stream partial JSON but that made our parser choke on incomplete streams.

[00:21:43] Diego Morales: streaming partial JSON works if you stream at the granularity of complete fields or use chunk markers. Some customers buffer until a top-level object is closed before parsing. Streaming is great for UX but adds parsing complexity.

[00:22:00] Jared Thompson: ok good talk, can we summarize action items?
[00:22:05] Ava Chen: sure — Diego will do the benchmark, Maya will send sample payloads, and I'll send docs and pricing. We'll schedule a follow-up for next week.

[00:22:18] Priya Nair: one quick question — do you have a recommended default for retries on JSON parse fail? Limit to one? two?

[00:22:25] Diego Morales: limit to one automated retry with a stricter mask and deterministic decode; if still fails, surface for human review or fallback. More than one automatic retry tends to amplify tail latency.

[00:22:40] Maya Singh: makes sense. We'll align internally and share requirements.

[00:22:50] Ava Chen: thanks all, we'll follow up with the benchmark plan and example prompts.

[00:23:00] Jared Thompson: thanks everyone.

[00:23:05] (call ends)

Notes (auto-summary):
- Key issues: constrained-decoding overhead, retries amplify p95, quantization affects p95 reliability for nested JSON, batching vs p95 tradeoffs, KV cache improves prefill reuse but schema churn reduces hit rate.
- Recommended mitigations: canonicalize schemas, integrated constrained-decoding (runtime trie/mask), single retry with stricter decode, prefix-KV caching for static prompt parts, consider 2-step intent+call pattern for very dynamic tool sets.

References shared on call: link to Redwood constrained-decoding guide (redwood.internal/prod-guides/json-mode), example prompt recipes (link), proposed benchmark sheet (link to spreadsheet).

- prefix-tree enforced (runtime integrated): p50: 150ms, p95: 360ms
- INT8 quant + integrated constraints: p50: 95ms, p95: 420ms (notice p95 worsens in some quant cases because of retries or longer decode due to low-prob tokens)

[00:09:08] Maya Singh: interesting that INT8 p50 is better but p95 worse. That matches our observations. Can you explain why retries spike p95?

[00:09:22] Diego Morales: retries hit worst-case paths: if you post-validate output and it's invalid you often re-run full decode with a harder constraint or sampling temperature set to 0 for deterministic. That re-run doubles or triples latency. If you can do partial prefill caching or incremental decoding that reuses KV cache you avoid entire re-prefill cost; if not, it's heavy.

[00:09:56] Luca Romano: incremental decoding — is that safe with GPUs and batching? I thought continuous batching requires full control of per-sequence decode steps.

[00:10:05] Diego Morales: Redwood runtime supports continuous batching: it queues tokens to be decoded across many sequences. Incremental decode reuse is orthogonal — you can pin the prefill KV to a session and only decode the residual. The tricky part is mixing batching with sessions that have different allowed-token masks; the runtime needs to do subgroup batching by mask to keep throughput. That reduces batch sizes for constrained flows.

[00:10:40] Maya Singh: we have many short requests (<150 tokens) and function calls with small outputs. Does batching help?

[00:10:48] Diego Morales: yes, batching helps amortize kernel launch and per-token overhead. For short outputs, large batch sizes (32/64) increase throughput and reduce unit cost but can increase tail latency. Redwood allows latency SLO targeting: you can set a max queueing delay so latency stays bounded; beyond that batch is flushed. For strict p95 requirements we recommend small batch sizes and prefix caching to compensate cost.

[00:11:25] Nia Patel: from security/compliance side: if we do Dedicated or on-prem, we need assurance KV cache isn't persisted or is encrypted. How does Redwood handle KV storage?

[00:11:36] Diego Morales: KV cache in the runtime is in-memory by default on inference nodes and not written to disk unless you enable warm-start snapshots. For Dedicated/VPC we support KMS encryption for any persisted snapshot, and audit logs for access. We'll include our security docs.

[00:11:52] Nia Patel: ok please share the exact config we should use.

[00:11:56] Ava Chen: noted, we'll attach a recommended Dedicated config.

[00:12:10] Priya Nair: let's pivot to function/tool calling performance: if you have many functions registered, does it increase latency?

[00:12:20] Diego Morales: registering many functions increases the schema size in the prompt and thus prefill length. Two strategies: 1) only send schemas for the subset likely to be called (function shortlist), 2) send a compressed or indexed schema and let the runtime expand it — but expansion can hurt cacheability. We also support offloading schema resolution to the application (return a short function id and do a separate call).

[00:12:53] Maya Singh: our system has ~40 tool variants per customer, dynamic. We can't always shortlist reliably.

[00:12:59] Diego Morales: in that case, canonicalizing and compressing schemas (e.g., gzipping static parts and keeping small variable blobs inline) helps. Another approach: use structured calling with a 2-step: produce an intent + function id (short text) then call the function with a second request. That splits latency but reduces constrained-decoding complexity.

[00:13:30] Jared Thompson: is there a pattern that keeps one-round call and still stays reliable?

[00:13:35] Diego Morales: yes: heavy-duty constrained decoding integrated into the runtime plus low-temperature sampling. If you can keep the function signatures fairly stable and canonicalized across requests, you get good cache and single-round reliability ~97-99% for simple schemas. For deeply nested schemas it's trickier.

[00:14:05] Maya Singh: about parser issues — sometimes the model emits Unicode escapes like “ which our JSON parser handles, other times unescaped newlines inside strings break stuff.

[00:14:15] Diego Morales: tokenizer boundary effects can cause those escapes. Two fixes: 1) strong closing token bias to prefer final brace tokens, 2) use a post-processor that repairs common patterns (unescaped newline => 
) but be careful — repairing can mask true errors. We actually instrumented common repair heuristics and found a small % of false-positives.

[00:14:48] Priya Nair: we recorded some microbenchmarks with Redwood runtime — Diego can you walk us through the demo numbers you mentioned?

[00:14:55] Diego Morales: Sure, demo setup: 4k context model, 256r/gens, batch sizes varied (1, 8, 32). Workload: function call asking for nested JSON 12 keys deep, ~180 tokens average. I'll skip slides and read numbers:
- Batch=1, no constraints, FP16: latency p95 ~ 320ms; throughput 3.1 req/s/node
- Batch=8, integrated prefix-tree constraints, FP16: p95 ~ 420ms, throughput 18.9 req/s/node
- Batch=32, mask-only constraints, FP16: p95 ~ 620ms (due to queueing), throughput 62 req/s/node
- Batch=8, INT8 quant + integrated constraints: p95 ~ 580ms (retries caused tail), throughput 45 req/s/node

[00:16:02] Luca Romano: those queueing tails are the latency policy tradeoff I guess. For production we need p95 < 500ms.

[00:16:08] Diego Morales: Then I'd recommend: batch target 8, integrated constraint mode, FP16 (or INT8 with tuned logits threshold), and aggressive prefix caching of static schema. That hits p95 around 380-420ms depending on context length.

[00:16:25] Maya Singh: what's the overhead of the integrated prefix-tree in terms of CPU or host-side work? We worry about host CPU become the bottleneck when we run many constrained sessions.

[00:16:36] Diego Morales: good callout. The integrated approach runs additional small CPU work per token for mask maintenance and trie navigation. On our nodes that used to be ~5-10% of CPU overhead for moderate session counts, but if you have many different masks it becomes a bigger percent because you can't batch across masks. For extreme cardinality of masks we recommend precomputing token mask bitsets on the host and using grouped batching by mask id. That reduces CPU load.

[00:17:10] Jared Thompson: networking question: in VPC/on-prem deployments is there more latency between controller and inference nodes that could affect runtime batching?

[00:17:18] Diego Morales: controller round-trip can add a few ms for orchestration; inference path is direct node-to-node for batching. In on-prem setups we tune node placement to keep that <1ms. The bigger issue is when you have multi-AZ routing and fallbacks which add tens of ms. For strict p95 you want single AZ placement for low variance.

[00:17:43] Nia Patel: about logs/audit: if we enable fine-grained JSON-mode debugging with token-level logs, does Redwood persist that to cloud? We need it in our private logging stack.

[00:17:54] Diego Morales: token-level logging is optional and can be restricted to ephemeral storage in Dedicated/VPC. For Private/on-prem we can push logs to your ELK/Splunk endpoint. We'll include those config snippets in the security pack.

[00:18:12] Priya Nair: quick note on function-call alternatives: structured format like YAML is more error-prone for parsing; JSON is easiest if you can guarantee strict quoting and closing.

[00:18:25] Maya Singh: what's Redwood's recommended prompt template for function-calling JSON mode?

[00:18:30] Diego Morales: we recommend: 1) a short instruction that says 'Return ONLY valid JSON matching schema X', 2) include schema in machine-friendly minimized form, 3) set decoding policy to deterministic-ish (temp 0-0.2, top-p 0.7), 4) enable integrated mask that only allows tokens in JSON plus allowed field names, and 5) post-validate with a strict parser and on-fail do a single constrained re-decode rather than unlimited retries. We'll send examples.

[00:19:10] Maya Singh: can you share an example of allowed-token mask for a nested key? I'm worried about tokenization splitting a key name.

[00:19:20] Diego Morales: allowed-token masks operate at token ids, so for long key names that tokenize to multiple subtokens you need to allow the sequence. That's where prefix-tree matching helps: it tracks allowed token sequences not just single tokens. If you instead allow each subtoken independently, you can still generate invalid key splits. The runtime's trie implementation solves this but costs extra per-token checks.

[00:19:57] Jared Thompson: if we move to a 2-step intent+function-id pattern, do we lose semantic accuracy?

[00:20:05] Diego Morales: some loss but often negligible for stable toolsets. You gain robustness and simpler constrained decoding. We measured after splitting the single round into intent+call, overall success rate increased for complex calls because you avoid long nested output decoding.

[00:20:30] Maya Singh: any notes about token budget and cost controls? We do a lot of concurrent low-latency calls and want to cap spend.

[00:20:38] Ava Chen: Redwood Console can give token-level cost per route and you can set per-route quotas and hard caps. Also batching/caching suggestions from Redwood Optimize can reduce tokens by up to 40% on average for typical function-call workloads.

[00:20:55] Luca Romano: will Redwood help us run the on-prem benchmark? We may need a small SE engagement.

[00:21:02] Ava Chen: yes we offer a short SE-led pilot to run benchmarks and produce a perf delta report. We'll outline scope in the next email.

[00:21:12] Diego Morales: final caution: for deterministic JSON you sometimes need to implement token-level forced close braces and explicit stop-token sequences. But stop tokens are fragile with batching; prefer integrated closing-bias in the runtime.

[00:21:35] Maya Singh: what about streaming? we stream partial JSON but that made our parser choke on incomplete streams.

[00:21:43] Diego Morales: streaming partial JSON works if you stream at the granularity of complete fields or use chunk markers. Some customers buffer until a top-level object is closed before parsing. Streaming is great for UX but adds parsing complexity.

[00:22:00] Jared Thompson: ok good talk, can we summarize action items?
[00:22:05] Ava Chen: sure — Diego will do the benchmark, Maya will send sample payloads, and I'll send docs and pricing. We'll schedule a follow-up for next week.

[00:22:18] Priya Nair: one quick question — do you have a recommended default for retries on JSON parse fail? Limit to one? two?

[00:22:25] Diego Morales: limit to one automated retry with a stricter mask and deterministic decode; if still fails, surface for human review or fallback. More than one automatic retry tends to amplify tail latency.

[00:22:40] Maya Singh: makes sense. We'll align internally and share requirements.

[00:22:50] Ava Chen: thanks all, we'll follow up with the benchmark plan and example prompts.

[00:23:00] Jared Thompson: thanks everyone.

[00:23:05] (call ends)

Notes (auto-summary):
- Key issues: constrained-decoding overhead, retries amplify p95, quantization affects p95 reliability for nested JSON, batching vs p95 tradeoffs, KV cache improves prefill reuse but schema churn reduces hit rate.
- Recommended mitigations: canonicalize schemas, integrated constrained-decoding (runtime trie/mask), single retry with stricter decode, prefix-KV caching for static prompt parts, consider 2-step intent+call pattern for very dynamic tool sets.

References shared on call: link to Redwood constrained-decoding guide (redwood.internal/prod-guides/json-mode), example prompt recipes (link), proposed benchmark sheet (link to spreadsheet).

title: LatticePort VPC architecture and region gating workshop
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-02-07 08:00 PT / 16:00 UTC
Duration: ~52 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Diego Martinez (Redwood SE), Maya Patel (LatticePort VP Eng), Owen Riley (Network Lead), Priya Sharma (Secops)

Summary (auto): Quick architecture walk-through for LatticePort private deployment. Focus areas: VPC peering vs PrivateLink, region gating / data residency, egress IPs and NAT, SSO/KMS concerns, pilot next steps.

--- Transcript ---
[00:00] Ava: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. I'm Ava from Redwood, this is Diego who's going to be our SE contact. How's everyone doing?
[00:07] Maya: Doing well, thanks. Excited to get into the networking side. We've read the docs but want to make sure the private deployment fits with our compliance and network team constraints.
[00:15] Owen: Yeah hi, Owen here. Main concern is how we actually connect—peering, private endpoints, or dedicated transit — and what egress IPs we need to allow.
[00:23] Priya: And from SecOps perspective, encryption at rest, KMS, audit logs and SSO are must-haves. Also region gating — we serve EU customers so we can't leave certain data in US-only regions.
[00:34] Diego: Great. We'll try to map each of those. Quick callout: Redwood's Private product supports a VPC-hosted dataplane with private endpoints, and we also support dedicated pools. Sorry if I mangle names, we sometimes say Redwood Dedicate or Redwood Dedicated — same idea.
[00:47] Ava: For today's workshop, can we start with a high level from you, Owen? What does your current VPC topology look like, and what constraints apply?
[00:54] Owen: Sure. We have per-environment VPCs in us-west-2, eu-west-1, and ap-southeast-1. dev and staging are peered with a transit gateway, prod is isolated with strict outbound egress through a NAT with firewall ACLs. We use an IP allowlist model — minimal open ports.
[01:14] Maya: We also have a couple of on-prem offices that talk to cloud via VPN, but we prefer not to route Redwood traffic back on-prem if possible.
[01:21] Diego: Makes sense. Two main patterns we see: VPC peering/transit gateway to our Private dataplane, or PrivateLink-style private endpoints. Peering is straightforward but needs route table updates and might require cross-account peering if you want isolation. Private endpoints reduce routing complexity but you need to publish DNS and manage private zone links.
[01:42] Owen: For peering, are there any limitations on route table sizes or max prefixes we should watch for? Our transit gateway already has a bunch of routes.
[01:50] Diego: We try to be conservative — there aren't Redwood-specific route table limits beyond cloud provider limits. For big TGW setups, sometimes teams prefer PrivateLink because it avoids propagating a half dozen prefixes. Also PrivateLink lets you keep the dataplane out-of-path of your transit, which some net teams like.
[02:06] Priya: For auditability: does the Private deployment send audit logs to a customer S3? Or does Redwood retain logs? We need full audit trail for model activity and admin ops.
[02:15] Ava: We offer options. The control plane (which is multi-region) keeps minimal operational logs; for deeper audit logs you can forward logs to a customer-managed sink — S3 or an on-prem collector — via our secure log forwarder. We also offer integrations with SIEMs. We'll share the SOC2 and logging doc after the call.
[02:34] Maya: Great. On encryption: KMS? Do you support BYOK (bring your own key)?
[02:40] Diego: Yes, we support KMS integration for model artifacts and at-rest where customer keys are supported (BYOK). For some on-prem packages we can integrate HSMs as well but that's a later-phase conversation.
[02:52] Owen: About egress: we need a stable set of IPs to whitelist in our firewalls. Are the egress IPs per-region and static for the Private deployment?
[03:00] Diego: Short answer: yes, we provide a set of dedicated egress IP ranges for Private deployments per region. For Hosted customers the ranges are dynamic, but Private gives you a predictable set. We'll include the IP ranges in the follow-up.
[03:12] Owen: Cool. Any NAT gateway requirements on our side? We already force all outbound through a NAT and firewall scanners.
[03:18] Diego: You'll still keep your NAT; you just need to allow routes to the Redwood dataplane. If you use PrivateLink endpoints, NAT isn't required for those flows.
[03:26] Maya: On regional constraints: we need model inference and any tokens tied to EU users remain in eu-west-1 or a set of EU regions. Does Redwood let us pin model runs to a region?
[03:36] Ava: Yes, you can pin model versions and route by region. Our routing policy supports region gating and can fallback to a nearby replica if capacity's low, but for strict data residency we can configure policy to reject non-EU routing. Diego, correct me if I'm simplifying incorrectly.
[03:50] Diego: That's right. There's also a config surface for fallbacks — you can disable automatic fallback for strict regulatory constraints. We'll document how that looks in the config YAML.
[04:01] Priya: About SSO, we use SAML-based SSO and want SCIM provisioning. Is that supported?
[04:07] Ava: Yes, SAML SSO + SCIM for provisioning are supported in the Private control plane. We'll share the SSO config guide. Expect standard timeouts and session lifetimes configurable.
[04:16] Owen: One more network question — MTU and fragmentation. Do you support jumbo frames between peered VPCs? Some of our on-prem flows assume 9000 MTU.
[04:25] Diego: That's mostly governed by your cloud provider and transit configuration; Redwood endpoints don't require special MTU, but we don't block jumbo frames. It's worth validating packet sizes in the pilot.
[04:34] Maya: For the pilot we're thinking: one EU region instance, a Private endpoint + peering test from staging VPC, and a perf test for 200 qps with 1-2s model latencies. Is that realistic?
[04:46] Diego: That sounds reasonable. We'll need sample prompts and a baseline from your side. Also, for 200 qps you probably want a dedicated pool rather than a small shared capacity to keep latency predictable.
[04:56] Owen: Cost question — dedicated pools have a commit cost. How do you typically price a small dedicated pool for pilot?
[05:03] Ava: Pilots can use short-term dedicated capacity with a smaller commit for the pilot window; we can also provide burst credits. I'll follow up with pricing examples in the email.
[05:10] Priya: Anything about network encryption in transit — TLS versions, mutual TLS?
[05:16] Diego: Connections are TLS1.2+, and we support mTLS for Private deployments if you opt into stricter authentication. That's useful when you want end-to-end mutual authentication between your callers and the dataplane.
[05:26] Maya: Who would own the Private endpoint? Is it our account or Redwood's? We want the endpoint to live in our VPC for compliance.
[05:33] Diego: For PrivateLink-style endpoints, we create a service that you accept in your account — the endpoint resource lives in your AWS account, and the service is in Redwood's account. For peering the dataplane sits in our managed account but we can cross-account peer. If you need everything in your account we also have an on-prem box or an appliance model but that's more involved.
[05:50] Owen: If the endpoint is in our account, then DNS and private-zone setups become ours to manage, right?
[05:56] Diego: Correct — you'll manage the private zone and conditional forwarding. We can provide DNS configuration templates.
[06:03] (short crosstalk)
[06:06] Maya: Can you walk through the expected flow for a request from an internal app in our prod VPC to Redwood Private endpoint, including how keys and KV cache are handled?
[06:15] Diego: Sure. App -> internal ALB -> route to Lambda/service -> hits private endpoint in your VPC -> request traverses the private link to our dataplane; model KV cache is managed in the dataplane and is scoped per-model/version. If you bring your own key, encryption at rest is handled by your KMS. Note — sometimes folks say K-V cash instead of KV cache in transcripts so don't worry about the phrasing.
[06:36] Owen: About KV cache size — if we have many short-lived sessions, will the cache blow up memory usage?
[06:43] Diego: The runtime implements LRU and pinned caches for certain workloads; we can tune cache sizing per model and workload. For high-concurrency short sessions, batching and prefix caching are key to maintain throughput. We'll surface recommended cache profiles.
[06:59] Priya: Any compliance docs specifically for EU data residency and GDPR?
[07:05] Ava: Yes. We have a GDPR compliance overview and DPA templates. I'll attach them. You may also want to consider dedicated EU-only dataplane depending on the volume.
[07:15] Maya: On RBAC and auditing, can we set role-based limits for who can rollout models or create rollouts? We need separation of duties.
[07:23] Diego: RBAC is supported in Private control plane; you can separate roles for admin, dev, and read-only. Audit trails for deployments are retained and exportable. For strict separation we recommend integrating your IdP and using SCIM groups mapped to roles.
[07:38] Owen: Latency-wise, do you have perf numbers for eu-west-1 to eu-west-1? We want to set an SLA target for 95th percentile under pilot.
[07:46] Ava: We publish benchmarks for various models, but for exact numbers we run a trace from your network. Typical 95p for small models in-region is sub-200ms server-side; end-to-end depends on your app stack. We'll do a pilot trace so we can set realistic SLAs.
[07:59] Maya: Any hard blockers we should be aware of?
[08:02] Diego: The common blockers are IP allowlist approvals (we provide IPs), TLS policy mismatches (we support mTLS), and corporate firewall deep packet inspection that breaks websockets/streams. If you use DPI, we may need a helper to negotiate TLS passthrough.
[08:18] Priya: About retention — how long do you keep logs by default? We need to ensure we can retain longer for incident investigations.
[08:24] Ava: Default logs retention is 90 days but we can arrange longer retention as an add-on or by forwarding to your S3 with your lifecycle policy.
[08:33] Owen: If we run a pilot and it's successful, what's the typical timeline to prod? A month? Two?
[08:39] Ava: Typical small pilots to prod is 4-8 weeks depending on approvals and firewall changes. If you have network people ready and approvals for IP allowlists, it's faster.
[08:47] Maya: Ok that's helpful. Let's confirm next steps: we'll share topology, list of EU controls, and sample prompts. Redwood to share SOC2 + SAML docs, egress IP ranges and pilot pricing.
[08:59] Ava: Yep — we'll send that by Monday. Diego will schedule the SE deep-dive for 2025-02-12 and prepare a sample config for PrivateLink and peering.
[09:08] Diego: I'll also prepare a small testing checklist: DNS, peering routes, firewall rules, IPs, MTU checks, and simple traceroute steps.
[09:16] Priya: One quick Q from SecOps — can you redact model outputs in logs or control PII in outputs?
[09:22] Diego: We have output filtering options and can integrate a post-processing filter in the pipeline. Not a silver bullet, but we can help build a PII stripping step for logs and for the responses if needed.
[09:34] Maya: Cool. Thanks everyone.
[09:37] Ava: Thanks all. We'll follow up with the artifacts.
[09:40] (meeting ends)

--- End transcript ---

App -> internal ALB -> route to Lambda/service -> hits private endpoint in your VPC -> request traverses the private link to our dataplane; model KV cache is managed in the dataplane and is scoped per-model/version. If you bring your own key, encryption at rest is handled by your KMS. Note — sometimes folks say K-V cash instead of KV cache in transcripts so don't worry about the phrasing.
[06:36] Owen: About KV cache size — if we have many short-lived sessions, will the cache blow up memory usage?
[06:43] Diego: The runtime implements LRU and pinned caches for certain workloads; we can tune cache sizing per model and workload. For high-concurrency short sessions, batching and prefix caching are key to maintain throughput. We'll surface recommended cache profiles.
[06:59] Priya: Any compliance docs specifically for EU data residency and GDPR?
[07:05] Ava: Yes. We have a GDPR compliance overview and DPA templates. I'll attach them. You may also want to consider dedicated EU-only dataplane depending on the volume.
[07:15] Maya: On RBAC and auditing, can we set role-based limits for who can rollout models or create rollouts? We need separation of duties.
[07:23] Diego: RBAC is supported in Private control plane; you can separate roles for admin, dev, and read-only. Audit trails for deployments are retained and exportable. For strict separation we recommend integrating your IdP and using SCIM groups mapped to roles.
[07:38] Owen: Latency-wise, do you have perf numbers for eu-west-1 to eu-west-1? We want to set an SLA target for 95th percentile under pilot.
[07:46] Ava: We publish benchmarks for various models, but for exact numbers we run a trace from your network. Typical 95p for small models in-region is sub-200ms server-side; end-to-end depends on your app stack. We'll do a pilot trace so we can set realistic SLAs.
[07:59] Maya: Any hard blockers we should be aware of?
[08:02] Diego: The common blockers are IP allowlist approvals (we provide IPs), TLS policy mismatches (we support mTLS), and corporate firewall deep packet inspection that breaks websockets/streams. If you use DPI, we may need a helper to negotiate TLS passthrough.
[08:18] Priya: About retention — how long do you keep logs by default? We need to ensure we can retain longer for incident investigations.
[08:24] Ava: Default logs retention is 90 days but we can arrange longer retention as an add-on or by forwarding to your S3 with your lifecycle policy.
[08:33] Owen: If we run a pilot and it's successful, what's the typical timeline to prod? A month? Two?
[08:39] Ava: Typical small pilots to prod is 4-8 weeks depending on approvals and firewall changes. If you have network people ready and approvals for IP allowlists, it's faster.
[08:47] Maya: Ok that's helpful. Let's confirm next steps: we'll share topology, list of EU controls, and sample prompts. Redwood to share SOC2 + SAML docs, egress IP ranges and pilot pricing.
[08:59] Ava: Yep — we'll send that by Monday. Diego will schedule the SE deep-dive for 2025-02-12 and prepare a sample config for PrivateLink and peering.
[09:08] Diego: I'll also prepare a small testing checklist: DNS, peering routes, firewall rules, IPs, MTU checks, and simple traceroute steps.
[09:16] Priya: One quick Q from SecOps — can you redact model outputs in logs or control PII in outputs?
[09:22] Diego: We have output filtering options and can integrate a post-processing filter in the pipeline. Not a silver bullet, but we can help build a PII stripping step for logs and for the responses if needed.
[09:34] Maya: Cool. Thanks everyone.
[09:37] Ava: Thanks all. We'll follow up with the artifacts.
[09:40] (meeting ends)

--- End transcript ---

title: Acme AI x Redwood — Dedicated throughput SLO discovery
preview:
Fireflies.ai — Meeting Transcript

Title: Acme AI x Redwood — Dedicated throughput SLO discovery
Date: Wed, Jan 22, 2025
Start time: 9:05 AM PT (17:05 UTC)
Duration: 52 minutes
Recorded by: Fireflies.ai

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Ava Chen, Markus Klein
Acme AI: Jordan Lee (CTO), Priya Shah (ML Eng), Miguel Santos (Infra), Hannah Wu (Product)

Summary (auto-generated)
- Acme described a bursty chat workload with strict latency goals and a desire for a written throughput commitment for Dedicated.
- Team discussed how Redwood measures throughput (tokens/sec) and what inputs are needed to size a dedicated pool.
- Acme is evaluating alternatives (hosted LLM APIs + other inference vendors) and wants clarity on model support, context length, and failover posture.
- Agreed next step is a benchmark using representative prompts to translate “peak QPS” into an enforceable throughput floor.

Topics (auto-generated)
1) Current workload + growth
2) Model mix and context lengths
3) Throughput floors and how to measure (tokens/sec, windows, streaming)
4) Burst patterns and “burst credits” vs committed
5) Dedicated deployment expectations (region, isolation, failover)
6) Benchmark plan + timeline

Action items (auto-generated)
- Acme (Miguel): Share traffic histogram + region preference by Fri Jan 24
- Acme (Priya): Provide prompt set or sanitized samples for benchmark by Mon Jan 27
- Redwood (Markus): Run sizing benchmark and return results by Wed Jan 29
- Redwood (Ava): Send draft SLO language + tier overview by Fri Jan 24


Transcript

[00:00] Ava Chen: Cool, thanks everyone for joining. I know it’s early. Uh, we wanted to use this as a discovery on Dedicated, specifically throughput guarantees, what you actually need, and then we can propose a shape and do a benchmark.

[00:13] Jordan Lee: Yeah, sounds good. We’re basically at the point where hosted is fine for prototyping but the variability is, like, killing us.

[00:22] Markus Klein: Totally. Before we jump in, quick intros? I’m Markus, solutions. I’ll probably ask a bunch of annoying details about tokens and latency.

[00:30] Priya Shah: Priya, ML engineer. I own the model side and prompt pipeline.

[00:35] Miguel Santos: Miguel, infra. I own, like, the Kubernetes stuff and the networking and the thing that pages me at 2 AM.

[00:42] Hannah Wu: Hannah, product. Mostly here to make sure we don’t promise something we can’t ship.

[00:48] Ava Chen: Great. And I’m Ava, account exec on Redwood side.

[00:55] Ava Chen: So Jordan, maybe start with what’s driving Dedicated now. Is it mostly cost, mostly latency, mostly “we need it in writing,” like what’s the forcing function?

[01:07] Jordan Lee: It’s a mix. Latency and predictability first. Cost is second. We’re building an agent-y chat thing, and customers are starting to ask for SLAs. Not just uptime. They ask “can you handle my peak?”

[01:24] Ava Chen: Makes sense.

[01:26] Jordan Lee: And we’ve had a few incidents with [inaudible] provider where at peak we basically got throttled. Like, they said 429 and then we’re dead.

[01:36] Markus Klein: When you say peak, what are we talking—requests per second, tokens per second? Do you have numbers?

[01:42] Miguel Santos: We can share ranges. Today in prod we’re like 40 to 60 RPS sustained on the chat endpoint. Peaks can be 3x that for like five to ten minutes when we push notifications.

[01:57] Priya Shah: And it’s streaming.

[01:58] Markus Klein: Okay. And output length? Like average completion tokens.

[02:03] Priya Shah: Average output maybe 220 tokens. But we have a long tail. Some sessions go to 800 or 1,200 when people ask for like “write a whole policy doc.”

[02:16] Markus Klein: Got it. And prompt length? Context.

[02:19] Priya Shah: Prompt is heavier. We do retrieval plus tool history. Typical prompt around 2k to 4k tokens. Worst case maybe 12k.

[02:29] Markus Klein: Okay, so not 128k, but not tiny.

[02:33] Jordan Lee: Right. We’re not doing huge doc summarization, but we do have some customer support threads that get long.

[02:41] Ava Chen: And model wise, what are you using today?

[02:45] Priya Shah: Primarily Llama 3… uh, 70B. We also have a smaller 8B variant for some cheaper flows.

[02:54] Markus Klein: Llama 3.1 70B or 3.0?

[02:57] Priya Shah: 3.1. Sorry, we keep saying “3” and it’s confusing.

[03:02] Markus Klein: No worries.

[03:04] Ava Chen: Any other endpoints? Embeddings, reranking?

[03:07] Priya Shah: Yes embeddings. We do embedding on ingest and at query time. That’s separate from chat. That’s higher QPS but smaller compute.

[03:15] Miguel Santos: Embedding QPS is like, I don’t know, 300 RPS? But it’s like a small vector model.

[03:21] Markus Klein: Okay. For Dedicated are you thinking only generation, or also embeddings on the same pool?

[03:27] Jordan Lee: Ideally we isolate generation. Embeddings we can keep hosted or even self-run. The pain is generation.

[03:35] Ava Chen: Great, that simplifies.

[03:38] Markus Klein: On throughput floors: what do you want to guarantee? Customers often say “X RPS,” but with LLMs the truth is tokens-per-second is more stable. How are you thinking about it?

[03:49] Jordan Lee: Yeah, we don’t know. We want something like “we can handle 150 RPS at p95 under two seconds.” But I’m aware that’s… not how LLMs work.

[04:02] Priya Shah: Yeah, it depends on how many tokens, the prompt length. Also we batch.

[04:08] Markus Klein: Right. The most common way we do it on Dedicated is commit to a throughput floor measured as output tokens per second (or total tokens/sec) for a pinned model version and a specified max context. Then you can convert that to “rough RPS” given your distributions.

[04:24] Ava Chen: And we’re in the process of formalizing that into tiers so it’s not bespoke. That’s actually why we’re asking these questions.

[04:32] Hannah Wu: So what is the actual thing you’d put in writing? Like “X tokens per second”?

[04:38] Ava Chen: Yes, for Dedicated, the direction is a tokens/sec floor over a measurement window, with some caveats (client-side backpressure, unsupported model changes). Markus can explain the measurement nuances.

[04:50] Markus Klein: The biggest gotcha is streaming. People think “tokens/sec” but if the client doesn’t read the stream fast enough you can undercount or it looks slow. So we usually exclude customer-side throttling or slow-consumer effects.

[05:05] Miguel Santos: That’s interesting, because we did see that. Our gateway sometimes buffers.

[05:10] Markus Klein: Exactly. If your gateway is holding the connection open and reading slowly, the server can’t send tokens, then tokens/sec drops but it’s not the model.

[05:20] Priya Shah: We use Envoy. It can do weird things with streaming.

[05:23] Markus Klein: Yep.

[05:25] Ava Chen: On burst—Miguel you mentioned 3x for 5–10 minutes. Can you describe what “burst” really is? Is it more users at once, or longer responses, or both?

[05:36] Miguel Santos: It’s more concurrent users. We send a push, we get everyone asking questions.

[05:42] Priya Shah: Output length stays about the same. Maybe slightly longer because they ask more “explain” questions.

[05:49] Markus Klein: Okay. So concurrency spikes.

[05:52] Jordan Lee: Our issue is we don’t want to pay for peak 24/7. But we need to survive the peak.

[06:01] Ava Chen: Totally. Our Dedicated tiers are basically: committed floor + defined burst policy. Burst can be “best effort” from a warm pool or reserved headroom depending on tier.

[06:12] Jordan Lee: Warm pool meaning extra GPUs just sitting there?

[06:16] Ava Chen: Depending. For higher tiers we keep spare capacity or faster failover. For entry tiers we may rely more on scaling within a window. But we want to align what you need.

[06:28] Miguel Santos: What is the ramp time? Like if we burst, is it seconds or minutes?

[06:33] Markus Klein: If it’s purely autoscaling on cold nodes, it can be minutes, and with GPUs sometimes longer. So if you truly have five-minute spikes, the best pattern is either reserve enough throughput for that, or have a “burst pool” that’s already warm.

[06:48] Hannah Wu: And cost wise, how do you bill burst?

[06:51] Ava Chen: We’re still finalizing the packaging, but generally you pay for committed capacity, and burst beyond that is either included up to some limit or metered separately. We’ll be very explicit.

[07:05] Jordan Lee: We don’t want surprise bills. We’d rather have a known number.

[07:10] Ava Chen: Understood.

[07:12] Markus Klein: Let’s talk region and data. Are you okay in a shared cloud region, or do you need VPC / private?

[07:20] Miguel Santos: We’re currently in AWS us-east-1. We can do cross-region but prefer us-east. We don’t need full on-prem. VPC would be nice later, but not required to start.

[07:31] Jordan Lee: Our enterprise customers ask “is it isolated?” They don’t necessarily require VPC day one, but Dedicated capacity that’s not shared is important.

[07:41] Ava Chen: That’s exactly what Dedicated is.

[07:44] Markus Klein: Model pinning: will you pin the model version and keep it stable? Throughput guarantees usually assume pinned weights and runtime version.

[07:52] Priya Shah: Yes. We actually prefer stability. We do our own evals. If it changes under us, it’s painful.

[08:01] Markus Klein: Great. Context length—what max do you need to support for the commitment? Like we can say “throughput floor holds for up to 8k context” or 16k.

[08:12] Priya Shah: We can cap at 16k. We don’t need more for this product.

[08:18] Markus Klein: That’s helpful.

[08:20] Ava Chen: How quickly are you trying to move? What’s your timeline?

[08:25] Jordan Lee: Ideally start a pilot in February, and convert by end of Q1. We have a big customer launching late March.

[08:35] Ava Chen: Okay. That’s doable depending on shape availability.

[08:39] Miguel Santos: What do you mean by availability? Like GPU lead time?

[08:43] Ava Chen: Yes. In us-east, some GPU shapes are constrained. We’ll propose options.

[08:50] Markus Klein: I want to sanity check performance expectations. For Llama 3.1 70B, your prompt 2–4k, output 200-ish, streaming. Are you optimizing for time-to-first-token or total completion time?

[09:05] Priya Shah: Both. TTFT matters because it feels responsive. But total time matters too. We’re aiming for TTFT under 700ms p95 if possible.

[09:15] Markus Klein: That’s aggressive but maybe doable with the right shape and batching config. Total time depends on tokens/sec.

[09:23] Jordan Lee: Our customers complain if it takes like 8 seconds.

[09:27] Markus Klein: Right. So you want decent decode throughput.

[09:31] Ava Chen: What are you seeing today on your current provider?

[09:34] Priya Shah: On a good day, TTFT ~500ms p50, but p95 can be 1.8 seconds. Tokens/sec varies wildly. Sometimes 30 tokens/sec, sometimes 12.

[09:46] Miguel Santos: And at peak it collapses.

[09:49] Ava Chen: Yup.

[09:51] Hannah Wu: When you say “throughput SLO,” how do you measure it? Is it like a five-minute average? Hourly?

[09:58] Markus Klein: For Dedicated it’s typically a rolling window—like 5 minutes or 10 minutes—because you want to smooth noise but still catch sustained issues. We’re aligning internally on an official window. The other piece is we measure server-side emitted tokens so it’s not biased by client reading speed.

[10:16] Miguel Santos: So you instrument on the server.

[10:18] Markus Klein: Exactly.

[10:20] Jordan Lee: And if you miss, what happens? Credits?

[10:24] Ava Chen: Usually yes, service credits tied to sustained miss, plus escalation. We can share the proposed language, but the idea is: we own provisioning and routing, so if we fail to provide the floor under agreed conditions, there’s a remedy.

[10:38] Jordan Lee: We need that. Procurement asks for it.

[10:41] Ava Chen: Understood.

[10:43] Priya Shah: I have a question—does caching count? Like prefix cache. If you’re caching, tokens/sec goes up, but is that “real” throughput?

[10:53] Markus Klein: Great question. It’s real in the sense you’re getting outputs faster, but we try to define it so you can’t game the metric by sending the same request. For Dedicated, we generally measure actual produced tokens per second, but we also look at request mix. If the workload becomes mostly cache hits, your app is happy anyway. The key is we’ll benchmark with your real pattern.

[11:13] Priya Shah: Okay.

[11:14] Miguel Santos: Another question—if you batch, does that hurt latency?

[11:18] Markus Klein: There’s always a tradeoff. We do continuous batching so the added queueing is small, but at high load you might accept slightly higher p50 for much better p95 and cost. We can tune.

[11:34] Ava Chen: If it’s helpful, we can also talk about dedicated “shapes.” They’re basically standardized GPU configs so we don’t reinvent the wheel each deal.

[11:43] Jordan Lee: Yeah, what are the likely options for us? H100? A100?

[11:47] Markus Klein: For 70B, H100 is the best for latency and throughput. A100 can work but might not hit your TTFT goal. Another option is multiple smaller GPUs, but for 70B you usually want enough memory and good interconnect.

[12:03] Miguel Santos: We don’t want something exotic.

[12:06] Ava Chen: We’ll keep it simple.

[12:08] Markus Klein: Can you share whether you’re doing tool calls or function calling? That changes patterns.

[12:14] Priya Shah: Yes. We do tool calls. We do a “plan” step then a “final” step. Sometimes 2–3 calls per user message.

[12:23] Markus Klein: So effectively your RPS multiplies.

[12:26] Priya Shah: Kind of.

[12:28] Jordan Lee: That’s part of why we get spikes.

[12:32] Ava Chen: So we should size for total inference calls, not user messages.

[12:36] Miguel Santos: Exactly.

[12:38] Markus Klein: Any guardrails on max tokens out? Like do you cap at 1,024?

[12:43] Priya Shah: We cap at 1,500.

[12:46] Markus Klein: Okay.

[12:48] Hannah Wu: On the product side, we need to know what we can promise to our customers. Are you suggesting we promise tokens/sec too?

[12:55] Ava Chen: Most of our customers translate the tokens/sec commitment into “we can serve X concurrency at Y latency” using their known distributions. But the written SLO is the tokens/sec floor because it’s objective.

[13:10] Jordan Lee: That’s acceptable as long as you help us map it.

[13:13] Markus Klein: That’s what the benchmark is for.

[13:16] Ava Chen: Can I ask—are you considering other vendors? Just so we know what you’re comparing against.

[13:21] Jordan Lee: Yeah. We’re looking at Together, Fireworks, maybe Bedrock, and obviously OpenAI. But the issue is we want an open model and dedicated capacity.

[13:33] Ava Chen: Got it.

[13:34] Markus Klein: Bedrock dedicated is… complicated.

[13:37] Miguel Santos: Yeah.

[13:39] Priya Shah: Also, we need some observability. Like per-route latency, token usage. Our current thing is a black box.

[13:48] Ava Chen: Redwood Console has that—token-level cost and latency breakdowns, plus per-deployment metrics.

[13:55] Markus Klein: On Dedicated we can give you per-deployment throughput dashboards. We can also export metrics.

[14:02] Miguel Santos: Prometheus?

[14:03] Markus Klein: Yep, or we can push to Datadog. We’ll align on what you use.

[14:09] Miguel Santos: We’re Datadog.

[14:11] Markus Klein: Great.

[14:13] Ava Chen: Let’s talk about the benchmark. Priya, can you share a set of representative prompts? If you can’t share actual data, sanitized is fine.

[14:22] Priya Shah: We can sanitize. We can also generate synthetic prompts that match token length and structure.

[14:27] Markus Klein: If you can give us distributions (prompt length bins, output length bins), we can also do a proxy benchmark.

[14:35] Jordan Lee: What do you need to produce from us?

[14:37] Markus Klein: Ideally: (1) a sample of 50–200 prompts (or prompt templates) with expected tool-call behavior, (2) your max context and max output, (3) whether you require JSON/structured outputs, and (4) concurrency profile.

[14:55] Miguel Santos: We can send a traffic profile. We log prompt and output token counts (not contents) for privacy.

[15:02] Markus Klein: Perfect.

[15:04] Ava Chen: And then we come back with recommended shape and what we’d commit to as the floor.

[15:10] Jordan Lee: When you say floor, is it per minute? per second?

[15:14] Markus Klein: Usually tokens/sec averaged over a window. Like “in any 5-minute window, throughput will be at least X tokens/sec” given you’re not exceeding agreed constraints.

[15:28] Hannah Wu: Constraints meaning what?

[15:31] Markus Klein: Things like: model pinned, context <=16k, you’re not sending requests above a max concurrency beyond what’s intended, and you’re reading streaming responses normally.

[15:44] Miguel Santos: So if we DDoS ourselves, it’s not your problem.

[15:47] Markus Klein: Exactly.

[15:49] Ava Chen: But we can also set admission controls so you get predictable behavior rather than “everything slows.”

[15:55] Jordan Lee: We’d like that.

[15:57] Priya Shah: One nuance: our burst is not just more QPS; it’s also that we sometimes run eval jobs that hit the model hard.

[16:06] Miguel Santos: Oh yeah.

[16:07] Priya Shah: Like offline evaluation. That’s not customer traffic.

[16:12] Ava Chen: Would you run that on the same dedicated deployment?

[16:15] Priya Shah: Preferably no. But sometimes we’re lazy.

[16:19] Markus Klein: If you mix offline batch eval with prod interactive, the SLO gets messy. Best practice is separate deployments or schedule it.

[16:27] Jordan Lee: We can separate. That’s fine.

[16:31] Ava Chen: Great.

[16:33] Miguel Santos: How does failover work? Like if one node dies.

[16:37] Markus Klein: For Dedicated, depends on tier. At a minimum, we have node-level health checks and we replace. Higher tiers may have N+1 redundancy or multi-zone. But there’s a tradeoff with cost.

[16:52] Jordan Lee: We can accept a short blip as long as it’s rare.

[16:57] Ava Chen: Okay.

[17:00] Hannah Wu: Can you do multi-region? Like fail to us-west?

[17:03] Ava Chen: It’s possible, but generally that’s a Premium posture. We can discuss later. Most customers start single-region Dedicated with defined incident escalation.

[17:15] Miguel Santos: Yeah, cross-region adds latency anyway.

[17:18] Priya Shah: Our users are US-based mostly.

[17:20] Markus Klein: Great.

[17:22] Ava Chen: I want to make sure we capture success criteria. What would make you say “yes” after the pilot?

[17:29] Jordan Lee: 1) p95 latency stable under peak, 2) no throttling surprises, 3) a written throughput commitment we can show procurement, and 4) predictable bill.

[17:43] Ava Chen: Great.

[17:44] Priya Shah: And quality can’t regress. But if we pin the model, it’s fine.

[17:49] Markus Klein: Understood.

[17:51] Miguel Santos: Also, we need to know lead time. If we sign in Feb, can we actually have GPUs by March?

[17:58] Ava Chen: That’s the open question. We should assume some shapes are available, but H100 in us-east can be tight. We can offer alternatives like A100 or L40S depending on your needs. Markus will propose.

[18:14] Jordan Lee: We really want H100 if it’s the only way to hit TTFT.

[18:18] Markus Klein: It likely helps. But we can run a benchmark on both and see.

[18:24] Priya Shah: What about quantization? We’ve tried it and it sometimes changes outputs.

[18:29] Markus Klein: We can run FP16/BF16 first. Quantization is optional and depends on your tolerance. For SLO commitments, we’d specify the profile.

[18:40] Ava Chen: Anything else on security? Do you need SOC2, audit logs, SSO?

[18:46] Jordan Lee: We’ll need SOC2 report, yes. And some audit logging, at least access logs.

[18:52] Ava Chen: We can provide. Dedicated also supports data isolation. Content retention—do you require zero retention?

[19:00] Miguel Santos: Ideally no prompt logging on your side.

[19:03] Ava Chen: We can support zero retention / no training. We’ll send our standard security packet.

[19:11] Markus Klein: For the benchmark, we don’t need raw prompt content if that’s an issue. Token counts and structure is often enough.

[19:19] Priya Shah: We can provide sanitized prompt templates.

[19:22] Ava Chen: Perfect.

[19:25] (crosstalk)

[19:26] Hannah Wu: Sorry, can I ask one more thing—if we exceed committed throughput, do you queue or do you reject?

[19:33] Markus Klein: It’s configurable, but generally we recommend a bounded queue and then shed load with clear errors rather than letting latency explode. Tier-aware admission control basically.

[19:44] Miguel Santos: That’s good. We can handle backpressure.

[19:48] Jordan Lee: As long as it’s predictable.

[19:51] Ava Chen: Great.

[19:53] Ava Chen: Okay, I think we have enough to move. Let me recap next steps.

[20:00] Ava Chen: Miguel, you’ll send a 24-hour traffic profile: RPS, concurrency, token distributions, plus peak/burst patterns and the target region (sounds like us-east).

[20:10] Miguel Santos: Yes.

[20:11] Ava Chen: Priya, you’ll send sanitized prompt set or templates for a benchmark, plus max context and max output.

[20:18] Priya Shah: Yep.

[20:19] Ava Chen: Markus will come back with a recommended Dedicated shape option, and run a benchmark that maps to a throughput floor.

[20:26] Markus Klein: Yep. And I’ll include TTFT estimate ranges.

[20:29] Ava Chen: And I’ll send draft SLO language—how we measure tokens/sec, measurement window, exclusions like client-side throttling—so you can sanity check with procurement.

[20:41] Jordan Lee: Great.

[20:43] Ava Chen: Let’s aim for a follow-up next week, maybe Wednesday?

[20:47] Miguel Santos: Works.

[20:49] Priya Shah: Works.

[20:50] Jordan Lee: Yep.

[20:52] Ava Chen: Cool. Thanks everyone.

[20:55] Markus Klein: Thank you.

[20:56] Jordan Lee: Thanks.

(Transcript ends — remainder of recording contained light scheduling chatter and goodbyes.)
text:
Fireflies.ai — Meeting Transcript

Title: Acme AI x Redwood — Dedicated throughput SLO discovery
Date: Wed, Jan 22, 2025
Start time: 9:05 AM PT (17:05 UTC)
Duration: 52 minutes
Recorded by: Fireflies.ai

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Ava Chen, Markus Klein
Acme AI: Jordan Lee (CTO), Priya Shah (ML Eng), Miguel Santos (Infra), Hannah Wu (Product)

Summary (auto-generated)
- Acme described a bursty chat workload with strict latency goals and a desire for a written throughput commitment for Dedicated.
- Team discussed how Redwood measures throughput (tokens/sec) and what inputs are needed to size a dedicated pool.
- Acme is evaluating alternatives (hosted LLM APIs + other inference vendors) and wants clarity on model support, context length, and failover posture.
- Agreed next step is a benchmark using representative prompts to translate “peak QPS” into an enforceable throughput floor.

Topics (auto-generated)
1) Current workload + growth
2) Model mix and context lengths
3) Throughput floors and how to measure (tokens/sec, windows, streaming)
4) Burst patterns and “burst credits” vs committed
5) Dedicated deployment expectations (region, isolation, failover)
6) Benchmark plan + timeline

Action items (auto-generated)
- Acme (Miguel): Share traffic histogram + region preference by Fri Jan 24
- Acme (Priya): Provide prompt set or sanitized samples for benchmark by Mon Jan 27
- Redwood (Markus): Run sizing benchmark and return results by Wed Jan 29
- Redwood (Ava): Send draft SLO language + tier overview by Fri Jan 24


Transcript

[00:00] Ava Chen: Cool, thanks everyone for joining. I know it’s early. Uh, we wanted to use this as a discovery on Dedicated, specifically throughput guarantees, what you actually need, and then we can propose a shape and do a benchmark.

[00:13] Jordan Lee: Yeah, sounds good. We’re basically at the point where hosted is fine for prototyping but the variability is, like, killing us.

[00:22] Markus Klein: Totally. Before we jump in, quick intros? I’m Markus, solutions. I’ll probably ask a bunch of annoying details about tokens and latency.

[00:30] Priya Shah: Priya, ML engineer. I own the model side and prompt pipeline.

[00:35] Miguel Santos: Miguel, infra. I own, like, the Kubernetes stuff and the networking and the thing that pages me at 2 AM.

[00:42] Hannah Wu: Hannah, product. Mostly here to make sure we don’t promise something we can’t ship.

[00:48] Ava Chen: Great. And I’m Ava, account exec on Redwood side.

[04:08] Markus Klein: Right. The most common way we do it on Dedicated is commit to a throughput floor measured as output tokens per second (or total tokens/sec) for a pinned model version and a specified max context. Then you can convert that to “rough RPS” given your distributions.

[04:24] Ava Chen: And we’re in the process of formalizing that into tiers so it’s not bespoke. That’s actually why we’re asking these questions.

[04:32] Hannah Wu: So what is the actual thing you’d put in writing? Like “X tokens per second”?

[04:38] Ava Chen: Yes, for Dedicated, the direction is a tokens/sec floor over a measurement window, with some caveats (client-side backpressure, unsupported model changes). Markus can explain the measurement nuances.

[04:50] Markus Klein: The biggest gotcha is streaming. People think “tokens/sec” but if the client doesn’t read the stream fast enough you can undercount or it looks slow. So we usually exclude customer-side throttling or slow-consumer effects.

[05:05] Miguel Santos: That’s interesting, because we did see that. Our gateway sometimes buffers.

[05:10] Markus Klein: Exactly. If your gateway is holding the connection open and reading slowly, the server can’t send tokens, then tokens/sec drops but it’s not the model.

[05:20] Priya Shah: We use Envoy. It can do weird things with streaming.

[05:23] Markus Klein: Yep.

[05:25] Ava Chen: On burst—Miguel you mentioned 3x for 5–10 minutes. Can you describe what “burst” really is? Is it more users at once, or longer responses, or both?

[05:49] Markus Klein: Okay. So concurrency spikes.

[05:52] Jordan Lee: Our issue is we don’t want to pay for peak 24/7. But we need to survive the peak.

[06:01] Ava Chen: Totally. Our Dedicated tiers are basically: committed floor + defined burst policy. Burst can be “best effort” from a warm pool or reserved headroom depending on tier.

[06:12] Jordan Lee: Warm pool meaning extra GPUs just sitting there?

[06:16] Ava Chen: Depending. For higher tiers we keep spare capacity or faster failover. For entry tiers we may rely more on scaling within a window. But we want to align what you need.

[06:28] Miguel Santos: What is the ramp time? Like if we burst, is it seconds or minutes?

[06:33] Markus Klein: If it’s purely autoscaling on cold nodes, it can be minutes, and with GPUs sometimes longer. So if you truly have five-minute spikes, the best pattern is either reserve enough throughput for that, or have a “burst pool” that’s already warm.

[06:48] Hannah Wu: And cost wise, how do you bill burst?

[06:51] Ava Chen: We’re still finalizing the packaging, but generally you pay for committed capacity, and burst beyond that is either included up to some limit or metered separately. We’ll be very explicit.

[07:10] Ava Chen: Understood.

[09:58] Markus Klein: For Dedicated it’s typically a rolling window—like 5 minutes or 10 minutes—because you want to smooth noise but still catch sustained issues. We’re aligning internally on an official window. The other piece is we measure server-side emitted tokens so it’s not biased by client reading speed.

For Dedicated, we generally measure actual produced tokens per second, but we also look at request mix. If the workload becomes mostly cache hits, your app is happy anyway. The key is we’ll benchmark with your real pattern.
